papers.title: Repurposing and Evaluating the (In)Feasibility of Dataset Poisoning enabled Watermarking for Contrastive Learning
papers.summary: Repurposing and Evaluating the (In)Feasibility of Dataset Poisoning enabled Watermarking for Contrastive Learning tackles a dataset-IP problem specific to unlabeled contrastive learning: how to prove ownership of web-scraped pretraining data when the owner cannot touch the training loss or downstream task and label-based watermarks do not exist. The authors first reproduce six data-poisoning CL backdoors in a unified setup and show that downstream-task-dependent methods like PoisonedEncoder and CorruptEncoder collapse for encoder-level injection, while SSL-Backdoor, CTRL, BLTO, and NA are the only candidates with task-independent triggers. Their key move is to repurpose the weak backdoor effect itself as a watermark signal by testing whether trigger-bearing queries form a statistically denser output cluster than clean queries, measured with a unified cosine-density metric and hypothesis testing rather than attack success rate. They extend this into a multi-level black-box scheme over feature embeddings, soft labels, and hard labels. On CIFAR10 and ImageNet100 with SimCLR, MoCo v2, BYOL, and SimSiam, BLTO gives the best balance of fidelity, verifiability, and robustness, while NA yields the strongest verification but visibly harms image quality. The paper points toward security-oriented, distribution-level provenance for self-supervised foundation data.
papers.abstract: Contrastive learning (CL) reduces annotation cost via auto-derived supervisory signals. Since large-scale in-house CL datasets are infeasible, reliance on third-party or internet data is common. Recent studies show CL models are vulnerable to data-poisoning backdoor attacks, but their generalization and robustness are underexplored. We systematically evaluate existing data-poisoning backdoor attacks on CL, revealing limitations: poor dataset adaptability, low success rates, limited portability, and restrictive assumptions (e.g., downstream task knowledge). Interestingly, trigger samples exhibit distinguishable statistical divergence from clean samples, which inspires repurposing it as a watermark for dataset IP protection. Direct repurposing is challenging due to low success rates; we overcome this by statistical verification using a unified density metric. We further propose a multi-level watermarking scheme adapting to feature-level, soft-label, or hard-label outputs in CL. Experiments show some backdoor attacks can be repurposed as effective watermarks with trade-offs among fidelity, verifiability, and robustness. This work demonstrates weak backdoor effects become reliable signals for dataset IP protection in challenging CL settings.

papers.title: Learning Koopman operators for coupled systems via information on governing equations of subsystems
papers.summary: Learning Koopman operators for coupled systems via information on governing equations of subsystems tackles the mismatch between classical EDMD and structured nonlinear networks: when the full coupled dynamics are unknown but each subsystem’s differential equation is available, purely data-driven Koopman fitting can be unstable and data-hungry. The paper’s core mechanism is a three-step hybrid construction: first, derive each subsystem’s local Koopman matrix from its governing ODEs using the dual Perron-Frobenius/Koopman formulation and a monomial dictionary expansion; second, assemble these local operators into a block-diagonal global Koopman matrix that encodes no inter-subsystem couplings; third, refine that global matrix online with incoming snapshot pairs via online EDMD, using the prior operator as the initialization. This differs from nearby Koopman work that injects topology or conservation laws, because it exploits partial equation-level physics for only the subsystems, not the whole network. Experiments on three coupled Duffing oscillators and three coupled van der Pol oscillators, with third-order monomial dictionaries, show lower one-step and multi-step prediction error than batch EDMD, especially under limited data, and cleaner separation of decaying versus persistent Koopman eigenvalues. The direction is toward physics-informed, data-efficient operator learning for large coupled dynamical systems.
papers.abstract: Nonlinear coupled systems are ubiquitous in science and engineering. The analysis and modeling of such systems is challenging due to their high dimensionality and complex interactions among subsystems. In recent years, operator-theoretic methods based on the Koopman operator have attracted attention as a powerful tool for analyzing and modeling nonlinear dynamical systems. Extended dynamic mode decomposition (EDMD) is one of the most popular methods to approximate the Koopman operator. However, EDMD is a purely data-driven method, and it could be unstable and inaccurate for coupled systems under limited data availability. In this paper, we propose a method to learn the Koopman operator for coupled systems using the differential equations governing each subsystem. We also demonstrate its effectiveness through numerical experiments on coupled oscillator systems.

papers.title: The Cylindrical Representation Hypothesis for Language Model Steering
papers.summary: The Cylindrical Representation Hypothesis for Language Model Steering tackles why activation steering in LLMs is inconsistent even when contrastive vectors are well aligned: the usual Linear Representation Hypothesis assumes concepts can be orthogonalized for lossless control, but real steering vectors overlap. CRH keeps linear concept directions yet relaxes orthogonality, arguing that each sample induces a cylindrical geometry with a central axis for the target concept, a surrounding normal plane for residual concept mixtures, and phase-sensitive sectors inside that plane that can either facilitate or suppress activation. A key differentiator from nearby steering work is that it explains success/failure as intrinsic sample-specific geometry rather than noisy vectors or simple separability. The paper formalizes predictability claims: the normal-plane magnitude is inferable from axis decomposition, but the sensitive sector is not, so identical-looking difference vectors can still produce opposite outcomes. Experiments probe this structure on Gemma-2B-IT and LLaMA2-7B-Chat across 100 AxBench concepts from text, code, and math, using DiffMean, PCA, mean-centering, and probe-based steering at multiple layers. Results show the predicted tradeoff: shrinking the plane component delays corruption but weakens concept activation, and sector similarity does not transfer reliably. The work points toward sample-aware, geometry-based steering prediction beyond LRH.
papers.abstract: Steering is a widely used technique for controlling large language models, yet its effects are often unstable and hard to predict. Existing theoretical accounts are largely based on the Linear Representation Hypothesis (LRH). While LRH assumes that concepts can be orthogonalized for lossless control, this idealized mapping fails in real representations and cannot account for the observed unpredictability of steering. By relaxing LRH's orthogonality assumption while preserving linear representations, we show that overlapping concept contributions naturally yield a sample-specific axis-orthogonal structure. We formalize this as the Cylindrical Representation Hypothesis (CRH). In CRH, a central axis captures the main difference between concept absence and presence and drives concept generation. A surrounding normal plane controls steering sensitivity by determining how easily the axis can activate the target concept. Within this plane, only specific sensitive sectors strongly facilitate concept activation, while other sectors can suppress or delay it. While the surrounding normal plane can be reliably identified from difference vectors, the sensitive sector cannot, introducing intrinsic uncertainty at the sector level. This uncertainty provides a principled explanation for why steering outcomes often fluctuate even when using well-aligned directions. Our experiments verify the existence of the cylindrical structure and demonstrate that CRH provides a valid and practical way to interpret model steering behavior in real settings: https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/CRH.

papers.title: Do Large Language Models Plan Answer Positions? Position Bias in Multiple-Choice Question Generation
papers.summary: Do Large Language Models Plan Answer Positions? Position Bias in Multiple-Choice Question Generation studies whether MCQ generators choose the correct option uniformly or implicitly “plan” its slot. Across 10 LLMs and 5 VLMs on three controlled generation settings—knowledge-based MCQs, BBC reading-comprehension MCQs, and COCO-based VQA MCQs—the authors find structured, family-specific bias: Llama variants skew toward A, Qwen/DeepSeek toward B, Gemini toward B/C, and D is broadly underused. Removing option identifiers amplifies primacy bias, while a simple instruction to balance A/B/C/D partly flattens distributions, especially for reasoning-tuned models. To test latent planning, they probe token-level hidden states in the question stem with lightweight MLPs and show answer position is predictably encoded before option generation, with stronger signals in later tokens and mid-to-late layers. They then intervene causally using activation steering directions from mean-difference and logistic-regression probes; steering at the penultimate token, especially layers 11–15, shifts A→B rates and can move answer distributions substantially more than random directions. The work points toward controllable, representation-level analysis of generation biases, but also shows steering can induce semantic drift and reasoning errors.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate multiple-choice questions (MCQs), where correct answers should ideally be uniformly distributed across options. However, we observe that LLMs exhibit systematic position biases during generation. Through extensive experiments with 10 LLMs and 5 vision-language models (VLMs) on three MCQ generation tasks, we show that these biases are structured, with similar patterns emerging within model families. To investigate the underlying mechanisms, we conduct probing experiments and find that hidden representations in the question stem encode predictive signals of the correct answer position, suggesting that answer position may be implicitly planned during generation. Building on this insight, we apply activation steering to manipulate internal representations and influence answer position. Our results show that steering can partially control positional preferences and substantially shift answer position distributions. Our findings provide a practical framework for studying implicit positional planning in LLMs and highlight the importance of controllable generation for reliable MCQ construction and evaluation.

papers.title: NeuroState-Bench: A Human-Calibrated Benchmark for Commitment Integrity in LLM Agent Profiles
papers.summary: NeuroState-Bench: A Human-Calibrated Benchmark for Commitment Integrity in LLM Agent Profiles attacks the mismatch between endpoint success and the deeper question of whether an LLM agent preserves the commitments that make a multi-turn solution coherent. Instead of inferring latent state, it probes commitment integrity with benchmark-defined side-query prompts attached to each task, scoring whether elicited commitments stay aligned with the task’s gold bindings, constraints, sources, and repaired beliefs. The released inventory is unusually concrete: 144 deterministic tasks, 306 side-query probes, eight cognitively motivated failure families, clean and distractor variants, and three difficulty bands, then evaluated over 32 profiles spanning a fixed local 16-profile slice and a matched hosted large-model slice. Human calibration is strong, with 104 sampled task units, 216 raw annotations, 108 adjudicated rows, weighted κ=0.977, and ICC(2,1)=0.977. The main result is that success and integrity sharply diverge: 31 of 32 profiles change rank, the success leader is not the integrity leader, and integrity rankings are more stable under distractors. HCCIS-CORE reaches 0.8469 AUC and 0.6992 PR-AUC for failure diagnosis, positioning the paper toward benchmark science for auditable agent statefulness rather than leaderboard-only scoring.
papers.abstract: Outcome-only evaluation under-specifies whether an evaluated agent profile preserves the commitments required to solve a multi-turn task coherently. NeuroState-Bench is a human-calibrated benchmark that operationalizes commitment integrity through benchmark-defined side-query probes rather than inferred hidden activations. The released inventory contains 144 deterministic tasks and 306 benchmark-defined side-query probes spanning eight cognitively motivated failure families, paired clean and distractor variants, and three difficulty bands. The main 32-profile evaluation contains a fixed 16-profile local subset and a matched 16-profile hosted large-model subset evaluated through the same benchmark pipeline. Human calibration uses the final merged reporting scope: 104 sampled task units, 216 raw annotations, and 108 adjudicated task rows, with weighted kappa = 0.977 and ICC(2,1) = 0.977. Empirically, task success and commitment integrity diverge across this expanded grid: the success leader is not the integrity leader, 31 of 32 profiles change rank when integrity replaces task success, and integrity rankings are more stable under distractor perturbation. The primary confidence-free score HCCIS-CORE reaches 0.8469 AUC and 0.6992 PR-AUC for post-probe diagnostic discrimination of terminal task failure; the legacy full heuristic variant HCCIS-FULL reaches 0.7997 AUC and 0.6410 PR-AUC. Probe accuracy and state drift achieve slightly higher ROC-AUC, 0.8587, and better Brier/ECE, while HCCIS-CORE has substantially higher point-estimate PR-AUC and remains more closely tied to the benchmark's intended construct. The exploratory neural-augmented variant HCCIS+N is weaker overall, and a randomized subspace control approaches chance. NeuroState-Bench therefore contributes a calibrated evaluation axis for exposing commitment failures over a broader model grid than the original local-only subset.

papers.title: Disentangled Anatomy-Disease Diffusion (DADD) for Controllable Ulcerative Colitis Progression Synthesis
papers.summary: Disentangled Anatomy-Disease Diffusion (DADD) for Controllable Ulcerative Colitis Progression Synthesis tackles the specific bottleneck of generating longitudinal ulcerative colitis endoscopy at a chosen Mayo Endoscopic Score while preserving the same patient’s anatomy, a task made hard because CLIP-style reference tokens entangle mucosal structure with the source lesion state. DADD fine-tunes Stable Diffusion v1.4 on LIMUC by conditioning on two separate streams: purified anatomy tokens from a frozen CLIP ViT-L/14 image encoder and ordinal disease tokens from an Additive Ordinal Embedder that models MES as cumulative increments. Its Feature Purifier uses cross-attention plus learned gating to erase disease-correlated channels from image embeddings, while Triple-Pathway Cross-Attention injects anatomy, disease, and a delta signal through resolution-dependent U-Net routing so coarse blocks preserve global structure and fine blocks edit pathology. The unusual inference trick is Delta Steering, a training-free, single-pass signed control vector derived from ordinal embedding differences, replacing classifier-free guidance and cutting latency by about 2×. On 10,284 LIMUC images, DADD-H best preserved disease fidelity, reaching 81.53 QWK on a regression judge and improving synthetic-only classification, suggesting a direction toward anatomy-preserving, medically faithful progression editors for scarce longitudinal data.
papers.abstract: Synthesizing longitudinal medical images at controllable disease stages while preserving patient-specific anatomy is hindered by the entanglement of pathological textures and structural features. We address this challenge for ulcerative colitis (UC) endoscopy, where severity follows a continuous ordinal progression along the Mayo Endoscopic Score (MES). Our framework, Disentangled Anatomy-Disease Diffusion (DADD), conditions a latent diffusion model on two complementary embeddings: a pretrained image encoder for patient anatomy and a separately trained ordinal embedder for cumulative disease severity. Since image embeddings inevitably capture disease information, we introduce a Feature Purifier, a cross-attention-based erasure mechanism that identifies and suppresses disease-correlated channels, yielding purified anatomical representations. These cleaned anatomy tokens and target disease tokens are injected into the denoising network via a Triple-Pathway Cross-Attention mechanism with resolution-dependent routing gates. This architecture leverages the U-Net hierarchy, in which different network depths encode global structure versus fine-grained pathological texture. Furthermore, we introduce Delta Steering, a training-free directional signal derived from the ordinal embeddings that enables explicit, single-pass control over disease transitions at inference without requiring additional forward passes. Validated on the LIMUC dataset, our approach produces high-fidelity images across all severity levels and effectively rebalances skewed class distributions, enhancing performance for downstream classification tasks. The dataset is available at zenodo.org/records/5827695 and the code base at github.com/umutdundar99/progressive-stable-diffusion

papers.title: DP-SfM: Dual-Pixel Structure-from-Motion without Scale Ambiguity
papers.summary: DP-SfM: Dual-Pixel Structure-from-Motion without Scale Ambiguity attacks the classic SfM/MVS limitation that geometry and camera poses are only recovered up to an arbitrary global scale unless a known reference object is inserted. The paper’s key insight is that dual-pixel sensors provide an extra physical cue: signed defocus blur from the left/right subaperture views is affinely related to inverse depth and the unknown focus distance, so it can be coupled with up-to-scale MVS depths to solve for absolute scene scale. The method first estimates blur sizes from DP patch alignment, then forms a linear system over many patches and views to jointly recover the global scale and per-view focus distances via robust ℓ1 estimation. It then refines the result with an intensity-based discrete search that reblurs the DP views using parameterized PSFs and minimizes cross-view differences, while rejecting unstable views and low-variation patches. Unlike learning-based metric depth or calibrated depth-from-defocus, it needs no training, prior calibration, or reference object. On six real scenes captured with a Canon EOS 5D Mark IV and a Google Pixel 4 across lenses/apertures, it reduces scale error and improves further with multi-aperture capture, pointing toward sensor-physics-driven metric reconstruction.
papers.abstract: Multi-view 3D reconstruction, namely, structure-from-motion followed by multi-view stereo, is a fundamental component of 3D computer vision. In general, multi-view 3D reconstruction suffers from an unknown scale ambiguity unless a reference object of known size is present in the scene. In this article, we show that multi-view images captured using a dual-pixel (DP) sensor can automatically resolve the scale ambiguity, without requiring a reference object or prior calibration. Specifically, the defocus blur observed in DP images provides sufficient information to determine the absolute scale when paired with depth maps (up to scale) recovered from multi-view 3D reconstruction. Based on this observation, we develop a simple yet effective linear method to estimate the absolute scale, followed by the intensity-based optimization stage that aligns the left and right DP images by shifting them back toward each other using cross-view blur kernels. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach across diverse scenes captured with different cameras and lenses. Code and data are available at https://github.com/lilika-makabe/dp-sfm-tpami.git

papers.title: Spatiotemporal Hidden-State Dynamics as a Signature of Internal Reasoning in Large Language Models
papers.summary: Spatiotemporal Hidden-State Dynamics as a Signature of Internal Reasoning in Large Language Models addresses whether long chain-of-thought traces in LRMs reflect real computation or mere verbosity, by probing hidden states token-by-token and layer-by-layer rather than collapsing them into coarse trajectory summaries. The core idea is Spatiotemporal Amplitude of Latent Transition (StALT), a training-free statistic that averages adjacent-token hidden-state change while weighting each step by within-token layer saliency, so it preserves where and when internal updates concentrate. The distinctive finding is an asymmetry: successful reasoning trajectories show broad temporal motion with sharp layer-local hotspots, especially late in generation, whereas non-reasoning baselines and knowledge-heavy domains show flatter dynamics. The authors test open-weight Qwen3, SmolLM3, gpt-oss-20b, Qwen2.5-Math, and Llama-3.1 on s1K-1.1 integer reasoning, GSM8K, MATH-500, GPQA-Diamond, and MMLU-Pro, and compare StALT against token-count, confidence, entropy, and embedding-space baselines. StALT separates correct from incorrect traces, remains useful under length control, and rises with thinking-mode activation but falls under CoT scaffolding or supervised fine-tuning, pointing toward mechanistic, label-free probes of latent deliberation in reasoning models.
papers.abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) generate extended solutions, yet it remains unclear whether these traces reflect substantive internal computation or merely verbosity and overthinking. Although recent hidden-state analyses suggest that internal representations carry correctness-related signals, their coarse aggregations may obscure the token and layer structure underlying reasoning computation. We investigate hidden-state transitions across decoding steps and layers, and identify a distinct spatiotemporal pattern in LRMs: successful trajectories exhibit broad temporal dynamics with localized layer-wise concentration, while this structure is weaker in non-reasoning models and knowledge-heavy domains. We formalize this characteristic as Spatiotemporal Amplitude of Latent Transition (StALT), a training-free trajectory statistic that summarizes temporal changes between adjacent tokens weighted by within-token layer saliency. Across diverse models and benchmarks, StALT reliably separates correct from incorrect trajectories in reasoning-intensive regimes, providing a competitive label-free correctness signal alongside strong output-space and length-based baselines. Intervention analyses further show that this spatiotemporal amplitude responds systematically to manipulations that increase or reduce the demand for internal reasoning, supporting its association with latent reasoning dynamics in LRMs. These findings provide empirical evidence that LRMs exhibit measurable hidden-state dynamics and offer a practical probe for understanding internal computation beyond output-based evaluation.

papers.title: High-Fidelity Mobile Avatars with Pruned Local Blendshapes
papers.summary: High-Fidelity Mobile Avatars with Pruned Local Blendshapes attacks the bottleneck that state-of-the-art Gaussian human avatars reconstruct rich pose-dependent appearance from multi-view video but are too compute- and memory-heavy for phones. Instead of the global pose-feature-plus-blendshape distillation used by TaoAvatar/SqueezeMe, the method partitions the SMPL-X template into 256 body regions and learns separate tiny MLPs that generate local pose features, which linearly combine local blendshapes for rotation, scale, color, and sparse position correctives interpolated from sampled control-point Gaussians. The distinctive move is exploiting spatial covariance: nearby Gaussians on the same limb or garment are highly correlated, so local linear bases approximate nonlinear appearance changes with less error than a single global basis. It then prunes blendshapes per attribute by collecting corrective variances over training poses, keeping only the top-changing Gaussians and fine-tuning, yielding a minimal sparse representation that removes about 90% of blendshape parameters without a pretrained teacher. Trained end-to-end on AvatarRex, Talking-Body4D, ActorsHQ, and DREAMS-Avatar, and deployed in Rust/WebGPU, it reaches 2K rendering at 120 FPS on Snapdragon 8 Elite while preserving fine wrinkles and text, pointing toward directly trainable, browser-native avatar systems for AR/mobile capture.
papers.abstract: We propose a method to reconstruct high-fidelity human avatars from multi-view video that can run on mobile devices. Many works can model high-quality Gaussian-based full-body avatars from multi-view video. However, these methods require heavy computation to obtain pose-dependent appearance, making deployment on mobile devices very difficult. Recent methods distill from pretrained models and model pose-dependent nonlinear Gaussian attributes by linearly combining global pose features with blendshapes. Although they can run on mobile devices, they suffer some loss of detail. We observe that nearby Gaussians are often highly correlated within a local region of the body, and can be linearly modeled with less error. Therefore, we use local linear blendshapes in small body parts to capture global nonlinear changes of Gaussian attributes. To further reduce computation and model size, we propose to remove blendshapes for Gaussians whose attributes change little, yielding a minimal blendshape representation. Our method is an end-to-end training method without a pretrained model. To make it run on multiple devices, we implement our method using WebGPU. Experiments show that our method can render high-quality human avatars with better details, and can reach 120 FPS at 2K resolution on mobile devices.

papers.title: Stable Blanket with Hidden Variables and Cycles
papers.summary: Stable Blanket with Hidden Variables and Cycles studies when stabilized regression can still find a small predictor set whose relationship to a response survives environment shifts once latent confounding and feedback loops break the usual DAG assumptions. The paper generalizes the stable blanket beyond fully observed acyclic SCMs by splitting the problem into three graphical regimes: hidden variables only, cycles only, and both together. For latent-variable models, it works on ADMGs with m-separation and introduces the intervened sub-district to track how interventions propagate through districts connected to Y; stable sets are built by removing descendants of carefully chosen sub-district colliders. For cyclic models, it replaces local neighborhoods with strongly connected components and uses σ-separation in DGs/DMGs. In the combined setting, it merges both constructions to characterize Markov blankets, stable frontiers, and stable blankets, including the weaker case where interventions hit the district of Y. The main implication is a graphical recipe for invariant prediction under hidden confounding and feedback, with minimality and uniqueness conditions that indicate when a unique stable blanket exists and when multiple stable frontiers remain possible.
papers.abstract: Stabilized regression aims to identify a set of predictors whose conditional relationship with a response variable remains invariant across different environments. Existing graphical characterizations of the stable blanket are mainly developed for structural causal models (SCMs) without hidden variables or causal cycles. However, latent variables and feedback relationships naturally arise in many applications, and they can change both the Markov blanket and the set of predictors that remain stable under interventions. This paper studies stable blankets in graphical causal models with hidden variables, causal cycles, and both features simultaneously. For models with hidden variables, we use acyclic directed mixed graphs (ADMGs) and $m$-separation to characterize the Markov blanket and to construct intervention-stable predictor sets. We introduce the notion of an intervened sub-district and use it to describe how interventions may affect districts connected to the response. For models with cycles, we work with directed graphs (DGs) and directed mixed graphs (DMGs) together with $σ$-separation, treating strongly connected components (SCCs) as the basic graphical units. We then combine these ideas to analyze models with both hidden variables and cycles. The main results give graphical characterizations of Markov blankets, stable frontiers, and stable blankets in these generalized settings. In particular, we identify conditions under which the response is conditionally independent of intervention variables given a suitable predictor set, and we describe when such sets are minimal or unique. These results extend the graphical interpretation of stabilized regression beyond acyclic fully observed models.

papers.title: Decouple and Cache: KV Cache Construction for Streaming Video Understanding
papers.summary: Decouple and Cache: KV Cache Construction for Streaming Video Understanding tackles the mismatch between offline VideoVLLMs and unbounded streaming video, where fixed-memory inference must both preserve long history and faithfully encode newly arriving frames. The paper identifies two failure modes in uniform streaming caches: cumulative interference, in which newly built KV entries inherit too much stale context and lose recency detail, and position overflow/discontinuity when cache eviction pushes tokens beyond training-length positional regimes. Its training-free DSCache mechanism separates a cumulative past KV cache from an on-demand instant cache built from a small FIFO feature buffer, so recent frames are re-encoded without contamination from older caches while historical context is still retained. A second key idea is position-agnostic encoding for RoPE-based models, storing caches before positional injection and reassigning positions at use time; the authors prove this is equivalent to standard encoding when relative distances are preserved. Evaluated on StreamingBench, OVO-Bench, and RVS-Ego/Movie with LLaVA-OneVision and Qwen2.5-VL at 1/0.5 FPS, DSCache reaches state-of-the-art results, averaging 2.5% over prior streaming methods and pointing toward cache-construction-aware streaming multimodal inference.
papers.abstract: Streaming video understanding requires processing unbounded video streams with limited memory and computation, posing two key challenges. First, continuously constructing new and evicting old key-value(KV) caches is required for unbounded streams. Secondly, due to the high cost of collecting and training on unbounded streams, models must learn from short sequences while generalizing to long streams. Existing streaming VideoVLLMs fail to scale to unbounded video streams or focus on cache reuse strategies, leaving the impact of cache construction underexplored. In this paper, we propose Decoupled Streaming Cache(DSCache), a training-free cache construction mechanism that adapts pretrained offline models to streaming settings. DSCache maintains a cumulative past KV cache while constructing a separate instant cache on-demand, decoupled from past caches to preserve the informativeness of recent inputs. To enable position extrapolation beyond the training length, DSCache further incorporates a position-agnostic encoding strategy, ensuring KV caches to support unseen positions and preventing position overflow. Experiments on Streaming Video QA benchmarks demonstrate DSCache's state-of-the-art performance, with an average 2.5% accuracy gains over prior methods.

papers.title: QHyer: Q-conditioned Hybrid Attention-mamba Transformer for Offline Goal-conditioned RL
papers.summary: QHyer: Q-conditioned Hybrid Attention-mamba Transformer for Offline Goal-conditioned RL tackles offline goal-conditioned RL when static datasets are partially observable and history-dependent, so standard RTG-conditioned Decision Transformers fail: sparse binary rewards make RTG nearly non-discriminative for stitching sub-trajectories, and fixed-window local modules in prior hybrids truncate variable-length dependencies. QHyer replaces RTG with a flow-parameterized, state-conditioned goal-reaching Q-estimator, learned by maximum-likelihood normalizing flows and then sharpened through high-τ expectile regression to recover in-distribution maximum Q-values from hindsight relabeled transitions. It also introduces a gated Hybrid Attention-Mamba backbone: attention handles global goal-directed planning while Mamba’s selective state-space dynamics compress history in a content-adaptive way, yielding token-wise memory that expands on non-Markovian “play” data and contracts on Markovian “noisy” data. Unlike value-guided DT variants that keep RTG as the main conditioning signal, QHyer uses Q-values directly as conditioning tokens with concatenated [s;g] inputs. On OGBench manipulation and D4RL AntMaze/Maze2d benchmarks, it reports state-of-the-art success on both non-Markovian and Markovian settings, pointing toward sequence models whose value signal and memory length are both adaptive to dataset heterogeneity.
papers.abstract: Offline goal-conditioned RL (GCRL) learns goal-reaching policies from static datasets, but real-world datasets are often partially observable and history-dependent, exhibiting a mix of Markovian and non-Markovian that violate standard RL assumptions. History-aware sequence models such as Decision Transformer (DT) are a natural fit for long-term dependency modeling, yet pure attention is inefficient and brittle when handling local Markovian structure and long-range context simultaneously. Although recent hybrid architectures (e.g., LSDT) introduce local extractors to improve local dependencies modeling, the fixed-window extraction cannot adapt its effective memory to varying dependency lengths in temporally heterogeneous settings, often truncating long-range context rather than compressing its content adaptively. Moreover, sequential offline GCRL faces a key bottleneck: under sparse rewards, return-to-go (RTG) becomes non-discriminative across sub-trajectories, providing little guidance signal for stitching goal-reaching behaviors from diverse demonstrations. To address these, we propose \textbf{QHyer}, which replaces RTG with a flow-parameterized, state-conditioned goal-reaching Q-estimator to support stitching across demonstrations, and introduces a gated Hybrid Attention-Mamba backbone that performs content-adaptive history compression while preserving local dynamics. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \textbf{QHyer} achieves state-of-the-art performance on both non-Markovian and Markovian datasets, validating its effectiveness for diverse scenarios.

papers.title: Quality-Aware Exploration Budget Allocation for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Quality-Aware Exploration Budget Allocation for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning tackles the brittleness of intrinsic-motivation exploration in cooperative MARL, where a single global exploration weight β can either swamp the team reward and collapse coordination or stay too weak to uncover rare joint strategies. The paper’s key idea is to split exploration control into two layers: a return-conditioned sigmoid schedule (RCB) that lowers β as team return improves, and a per-agent Reward Signal Quality (RSQ) rule that reallocates the exploration budget according to each agent’s intrinsic-reward signal-to-noise ratio. Unlike prior adaptive MARL exploration methods that tune entropy or impose safety constraints, this work explicitly models noisy intrinsic rewards as unreliable channels and approximates the information-optimal water-filling allocation with a clipped affine modulation that preserves ordering while avoiding irreversible zeroing-out of agents. Successor Distance (SD) is chosen because its quasimetric structure yields distinct per-agent signal quality, enabling RSQ to separate reliable explorers from noisy ones. Across seven benchmarks in JaxMARL—MPE corridor/tag, SMAX 3s5z/27m, and MABrax ant_4x2, ant_ball, halfcheetah_6x1—the method achieves top or tied returns, with especially strong stability on corridor and large-scale combat.
papers.abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) requires agents to discover joint strategies in a combinatorially large state-action space, yet effective coordination configurations are exceedingly rare. Intrinsic motivation, which augments task rewards with novelty bonuses, is a popular approach for driving exploration, but its effectiveness hinges on the exploration intensity $β$, where too large a value overwhelms the task signal and causes coordination collapse, while too small a value prevents discovery of rare strategies. We address two complementary challenges: adapting $β$ globally over training, and allocating the exploration budget across agents whose intrinsic reward signals vary in reliability. Our framework combines a return-conditioned sigmoid schedule (RCB) for global intensity control with a per-agent Reward Signal Quality (RSQ) metric that concentrates the exploration budget on agents with reliable signals. The core insight is that agents receiving noisy intrinsic rewards should explore less aggressively, and this allocation can be determined automatically from signal-to-noise statistics. Successor Distance (SD), a quasimetric intrinsic reward, naturally produces distinguishable per-agent signal quality, completing the framework with convergence and ordering preservation guarantees. On seven cooperative benchmarks (MPE, SMAX, MABrax), our method achieves top-tier returns across all environments.

papers.title: ShiftLIF: Efficient Multi-Level Spiking Neurons with Power-of-Two Quantization
papers.summary: ShiftLIF: Efficient Multi-Level Spiking Neurons with Power-of-Two Quantization attacks the core bottleneck in edge-oriented spiking neural networks: standard LIF neurons preserve rich membrane dynamics but collapse them into binary spikes, while prior multi-level neurons typically use uniform integer quantization that both mismatches the heavy-tailed, zero-concentrated membrane-potential distribution and reintroduces costly multiply-accumulate synapses. The paper’s key idea is to quantize membrane potentials onto a logarithmically spaced dyadic spike alphabet {0, 2^-K, …, 1}, so small amplitudes get finer resolution exactly where trained SNN states cluster, yet every nonzero spike remains a power of two and can be implemented as bit-shift plus accumulation with integer weights. ShiftLIF also uses proportional soft reset to preserve residual charge, a straight-through estimator over the full quantization support, and spike-rate regularization to keep activity sparse. Unlike nearby multi-threshold work, its differentiator is the explicit hardware-algorithm co-design and theoretical analysis of quantization error and bit utilization. Evaluated on 10 wireless, acoustic, motion, and visual sensing benchmarks with a shared spiking LeNet and other backbones, ShiftLIF matches or exceeds existing multi-level neurons while keeping synaptic energy close to binary LIF, pointing toward distribution-aware, multiplier-free SNNs for cross-modal edge sensing.
papers.abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are promising for edge sensing due to their event-driven computation and temporal filtering capability. However, standard leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neurons communicate only through binary spikes, which severely limit representational capacity. Existing multi-level spiking neurons improve information transmission, but often rely on uniform quantization that mismatches membrane-potential distributions or introduces costly synaptic multiplications. In this paper, we propose ShiftLIF, a multi-level spiking neuron that maps membrane potentials to a logarithmically spaced power-of-two spike set. This design provides finer representation in the small-amplitude regime, where membrane potentials are densely concentrated, while enabling multiplier-free synaptic computation through bit-shift and accumulation operations. As a result, ShiftLIF improves spike-level expressiveness without sacrificing the hardware-friendly nature of standard SNN computation. We evaluate ShiftLIF on 10 datasets spanning wireless, acoustic, motion, and visual sensing tasks. Results show that ShiftLIF consistently matches or exceeds the accuracy of existing multi-level spiking neurons while maintaining synaptic energy consumption close to standard binary LIF. These results indicate that ShiftLIF provides a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off for cross-modal edge sensing.

papers.title: Robust Conditional Conformal Prediction via Branched Normalizing Flow
papers.summary: Robust Conditional Conformal Prediction via Branched Normalizing Flow attacks a gap in conformal prediction under distribution shift: standard and Wasserstein-regularized methods can preserve marginal coverage while still failing at individual test inputs because conditional score distributions differ. The paper defines conditional coverage gap (CCG) and its expectation over test features, integrated coverage gap (ICG), then proves ICG is controlled by the Wasserstein distance between calibration and test joint distributions, exposing invertible transport as the right primitive for conditional robustness. It turns that theory into Branched Normalizing Flow (BNF), a two-branch normalizing-flow architecture that learns separate, invertible maps for X and Y so a test feature can be normalized to the calibration domain without access to the unknown label, and the resulting conformal set can be mapped back to the shifted test domain with coverage preserved. A stochastic Augmented BNF variant injects Gaussian noise into the Y-branch to improve expressiveness and is paired with conformalized quantile regression. Across nine datasets spanning synthetic shifts, Bike Rental, traffic, MIMIC-IV/eICU, and U.S. ILI, the method achieves the lowest worst-slice coverage gap and consistently tighter conditional reliability, pointing CP research toward transport-based, input-aware uncertainty under joint shift.
papers.abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) constructs prediction sets with marginal coverage guarantees under the assumption that the calibration and test distributions are identical. However, under distribution shift, existing approaches primarily align marginal conformal score distributions, which is sufficient to preserve marginal coverage but does not control the conditional coverage error at individual test inputs. As a consequence, CP can remain unreliable in regions where the conditional score distributions are mismatched. In this work, we bound the conditional invalidity of CP under distribution shift in terms of the Wasserstein distance between the calibration and test distributions. This result highlights the role of invertible transport in mitigating conditional coverage degradation. Motivated by this insight, we introduce Branched Normalizing Flow (BNF), a two-branch architecture that normalizes a test input to the calibration distribution and transforms the prediction set of the normalized input back to the test distribution while preserving conditional guarantees. Empirically, BNF consistently improves conditional coverage robustness on nine datasets across a wide range of confidence levels.

papers.title: Evolving Token Communication with Parametric Memory Network
papers.summary: Evolving Token Communication with Parametric Memory Network tackles the overhead bottleneck in semantic token transmission for wireless image communication: full tokens are still too costly over bandwidth-limited MIMO fading channels, yet naïvely dropping tokens breaks receiver decoding. The paper’s core idea is to transmit only a fixed-length prefix of each semantic token and let the receiver reconstruct the missing suffix with a parametric memory network whose “semantic memory” is stored implicitly in model weights rather than an explicit cache. Full tokens are first quantized into a codebook; truncated tokens are paired with their full-token labels, and kNN-based teacher distributions over these pairs are used to fine-tune a pretrained GPT-2-based recovery module so it predicts the codeword distribution of an incomplete token and recovers the complete one. This differs from prior evolving-memory or semantic-cache work by avoiding online retrieval at inference and instead distilling memory into parameters, then periodically re-evolving the whole system with newly observed test samples. On FFHQ64 image transmission over 2×2 Rayleigh MIMO channels, at CBR 1/40 and 1/20, it consistently beats Swin Token Comm and evolving cache baselines, reaching up to 1.09 dB PSNR gain, pointing toward continual, parameterized semantic communication.
papers.abstract: Token communication has emerged as a promising framework for efficient wireless transmission by representing source data as compact semantic tokens. However, transmitting full semantic tokens still incurs considerable communication overhead. In this paper, we propose an evolving semantic token communication system with a parametric memory network over MIMO fading channels. Specifically, only an equal-length prefix of each semantic token is transmitted, which reduces transmission cost while preserving a consistent token structure for receiver-side recovery. At the receiver, a parametric memory network is introduced to reconstruct the missing suffix information from the received token prefixes, where semantic memory is stored implicitly in the network parameters. To realize this design, full semantic tokens are first organized into a codebook, and truncated tokens are paired with the codeword labels of their corresponding full tokens. Based on these token-label pairs, kNN-based teacher distributions are constructed to fine-tune a pretrained GPT-2-based recovery module, which learns to infer the codeword distribution of each incomplete token and recover the corresponding complete semantic token. In addition, an online evolution strategy is developed to periodically update the parametric memory network and the entire system using newly observed test samples, thereby improving adaptability under distribution shifts. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms the existing evolving memory benchmark under different channel conditions and channel bandwidth ratios, with up to 1.09 dB PSNR improvement.

papers.title: Maistros: A Greek Large Language Model Adapted Through Knowledge Distillation From Large Reasoning Models
papers.summary: Maistros: A Greek Large Language Model Adapted Through Knowledge Distillation From Large Reasoning Models tackles the scarcity of culturally grounded Greek QA training data and the gap between expensive large reasoning models and deployable open-weights systems. The core move is CulturaQA, a 2,700-example synthetic-but-human-curated Greek QA corpus generated with GPT-5 across 11 Greek culture domains, then cleaned for morphology, spelling, hallucinations, and cultural bias. Maistros 8B is built by supervised fine-tuning Ministral-3-8B-Instruct with LoRA adapters on CulturaQA, using masked loss so only answer tokens contribute to training, plus 4-bit quantization and 8-bit AdamW to keep the adaptation memory-efficient enough for commodity hardware. Unlike nearby Greek LLM work that relies mainly on translation or broad multilingual pretraining, this paper distills structured reasoning and local knowledge into a compact open model and pairs it with a standardized evaluation harness for multiple-choice and open-ended QA. Across nine human-curated Greek QA benchmarks, Maistros 8B becomes the strongest open-weights model, with especially large gains on Plutus QA and CulturaQA, while still trailing proprietary models like Gemini 3 Flash, pointing toward practical low-resource language adaptation via curated synthetic distillation.
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have substantially advanced the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP), achieving state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of tasks. These improvements have been attributed, in part, to their emerging reasoning capabilities, which are enabled by large-scale training and increased model capacity. However, existing LLMs can generate erroneous responses when addressing complex queries that fall outside their training distribution, due to limited internal knowledge or the need for multi-step reasoning. To address these limitations, recent work has introduced large reasoning models (LRMs), which incorporate explicit internal reasoning processes to improve response accuracy. Additionally, state-of-the-art LRMs often comprise hundreds of billions of parameters and require several seconds per inference, even on advanced multi-GPU systems. These characteristics limit their practicality for deployment in conventional computing environments. Meanwhile, NLP research on multilingual LLMs continues to prioritize high-resource languages. However, these models exhibit limited performance in under-resourced languages, primarily due to insufficient language- and culture-specific training data. In this paper, we focus on Modern Greek, for which only a limited number of question answering (QA) datasets have been proposed, most of which are intended for model evaluation. To address this research gap in Greek QA, we make the following contributions: (i) CulturaQA, a high-quality LRM-generated and human-curated dataset, for Greek LLM training and evaluation; (ii) a memory-efficient LLM evaluation framework adaptable to diverse languages and QA tasks; (iii) Maistros 8B, a state-of-the-art open-weights Greek LLM developed via knowledge distillation and fine-tuning on CulturaQA; and (iv) a comprehensive evaluation of nine LLMs across nine human-curated Greek QA datasets.

papers.title: Leveraging Data Symmetries to Select an Optimal Subset of Training Data under Label Noise
papers.summary: Leveraging Data Symmetries to Select an Optimal Subset of Training Data under Label Noise studies data-level denoising when noisy labels are unavoidable and the goal is to recover a small training subset that behaves like a clean dataset. The paper formalizes cutstats subset selection and shows that its success is governed by k-NN accuracy: if neighborhood labels are predicted well, the retained subset’s class-flip rates shrink and downstream balanced error approaches the noiseless optimum. The distinctive move is to expose why vanilla k-NN breaks in high dimensions—its Bayes-optimal approximation window collapses as ambient dimension grows—and then restore that window by injecting symmetry knowledge through an invariant representation F. For orthogonal and permutation-invariant tasks, F is chosen to quotient away nuisance variation (e.g., norm or sorting), and when symmetries are only partially known, learned invariant embeddings from group-invariant representation learning or contrastive learning still help. Experiments on synthetic orthogonal/permutation data, rotated MNIST, and Tetris with heavy label noise show markedly higher subset accuracy and downstream classifier performance than vanilla cutstats, entropy filtering, herding, forgetting, and noisy-data baselines, pointing toward symmetry-aware, classifier-agnostic curation as a scalable alternative to robustness-only training.
papers.abstract: The performance of machine learning models often relies on large labeled datasets; however, data collected from diverse sources can contain label noise. Recent work has shown that, in noisy settings, there may exist a subset of the training data on which models can achieve performance comparable to training on a noise-free dataset. A widely used method for identifying such subsets is cutstats, which employs k-nearest neighbors (k-NN) to detect low-noise samples. However, its performance on high-dimensional data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we formally establish that the performance of a classifier trained on a subset of a noisy dataset selected via cutstats is influenced by the accuracy of k-NN. We further demonstrate that, in noisy environments, exploiting data invariance and knowledge of underlying symmetries can significantly enhance the performance of k-NN, bringing it closer to the Bayes optimal classifier even in high-dimensional regimes. Finally, we show that for real-world scenarios, where information about the underlying invariance is only partially known, learnt invariant representations can still facilitate the identification of near-optimal subsets.

papers.title: BadmintonGRF: A Multimodal Dataset and Benchmark for Markerless Ground Reaction Force Estimation in Badminton
papers.summary: BadmintonGRF: A Multimodal Dataset and Benchmark for Markerless Ground Reaction Force Estimation in Badminton addresses the lack of public, lab-grade resources for non-periodic court sports, where markerless load estimation is constrained by the absence of synchronized force-plate targets and realistic multi-view video. The paper’s core contribution is a badminton-specific capture stack with eight fixed RGB cameras at about 120 FPS, four Kistler force plates, and Vicon C3D motion capture, all aligned in software despite no hardware genlock. Rather than assuming perfect timing, it uses human-verified event pairing, automated QA, per-camera time offsets, and uncertainty metadata, then releases a privacy-preserving Tier 1 benchmark that maps 2D pose to time-aligned vertical GRF under leave-one-subject-out splits. The release packages 17,425 impact-segment archives from 156 trials, deduplicated to 1,732 unique impacts, plus preprocessing code and ten baselines across TCN, transformer, recurrent, and skeleton-graph families. Reported LOSO performance tops out around r²≈0.40, with PatchTST and ST-GCN+Transformer strongest, showing that cross-subject variability and impact timing, not just model capacity, dominate. The paper points toward reproducible, metadata-aware biomechanics benchmarks for occlusion-heavy, fatigue-sensitive sports.
papers.abstract: Multimodal resources for non-periodic court sports with laboratory-grade sensing remain scarce: few publicly pair instrumented ground reaction force (GRF) with high-frame-rate multi-view video, limiting markerless load estimation in realistic training settings. BadmintonGRF records eight synchronized RGB views at ~120 FPS, four Kistler force plates, and Vicon motion capture (C3D) without hardware genlock across modalities; alignment combines human-verified events, automated quality assurance, and per-camera time offsets with uncertainty metadata. Tier 1 distributes pose, time-aligned GRF, metadata, and splits under CC BY-NC 4.0, enabling the primary benchmark without raw RGB or C3D; we report a Tier 1 task that maps 2D pose to GRF. Tier 2 provides raw RGB and C3D under controlled access for studies that require appearance or full kinematics. The public release contains 17,425 impact-segment archives in the 10-subject benchmark tree (156 instrumented trials; raw multi-view RGB alone exceeds 1 TB); benchmark loader gates retain 12,867 view-specific instances and 1,732 unique impacts after multi-view deduplication. We are not aware of prior public badminton corpora that combine this sensing layout with audited video--GRF alignment for impact-centric GRF estimation. We distribute preprocessing code, leave-one-subject-out splits, ten reference baselines, and optional late fusion (one deterministic test-time pass per instance; no test-time augmentation), with a within-trial diagnostic in the supplementary material.

papers.title: Sheaf-Theoretic Planning: A Categorical Foundation for Resilient Multi-Agent Autonomous Systems
papers.summary: Sheaf-Theoretic Planning: A Categorical Foundation for Resilient Multi-Agent Autonomous Systems attacks the failure of event-calculus and situation-calculus planning under open-world multi-agent conditions, where unobserved interventions, plan interruptions, and belief-reality divergence break the closed-world assumption. The core move is to replace monolithic timelines with the interval category T equipped with a Grothendieck topology, so histories become sheaves whose local sections capture partial observations and whose gluing axioms encode multi-agent consistency. Actions are modeled as natural transformations on the world sheaf, while abductive diagnosis of surprising observations is recast as a pullback between predicted and observed states, making explanation and plan repair part of the same categorical mechanism. Unlike nearby symbolic planning work, the paper explicitly uses topos semantics, sheaf cohomology, and the sheaf Laplacian to represent obstructions, consensus, and asynchronous diffusion across heterogeneous agents. It grounds the theory in a Prolog/event-calculus implementation on Raspberry Pi 4B and ESP32 robots, with tabling yielding sub-2-second planning over up to 1,000 time points and 50 fluents. The paper points toward certifiable, decentralized, geometry-aware autonomy for resilient swarms.
papers.abstract: The challenge of engineering autonomous agents capable of navigating the stochastic and adversarial nature of the physical world has historically resided at the intersection of symbolic logic and control theory. Traditional multi-agent system (MAS) frameworks have relied heavily on monolithic logical models -- primarily variations of the event calculus and situation calculus -- to represent action, change, and temporal persistence. While these classical systems provide robust solutions to the frame problem through mechanisms like circumscription and successor state axioms, they are inherently limited by a closed-world assumption that fails in the face of unobserved agent interventions, plan interruptions, and divergent belief-reality states. The paradigm of Sheaf-Theoretic Planning (STP) emerges as a transformative alternative, grounding the problem of multi-agent coordination under the mathematical structures of topos theory and sheaf semantics. This report provides an exhaustive analysis, justification, and extension of the STP framework, exploring its categorical foundations, implementation feasibility, and role in the future of resilient autonomous systems.

papers.title: Chart-FR1: Visual Focus-Driven Fine-Grained Reasoning on Dense Charts
papers.summary: Chart-FR1: Visual Focus-Driven Fine-Grained Reasoning on Dense Charts tackles why MLLMs fail on high-information-density charts with multiple subplots, legends, and dense annotations: they miss local cues, drown in redundant visual tokens, and cannot expand reasoning depth adaptively as evidence accumulates. The core idea is a two-stage focused-reasoning pipeline built on Qwen2.5-VL-7B. First, Focus-CoT reconstructs chain-of-thought traces so each reasoning step is explicitly anchored to OCR text and localized image regions via a <focus> tag, bootstrapped by an automated data-generation and GPT-5 reconstruction process. Second, Focus-GRPO replaces standard GRPO with a focus-driven RL objective that combines relaxed numerical accuracy, a format reward, an information-efficiency reward that penalizes redundant OCR/box evidence, and an adaptive KL penalty that loosens constraints when more cues are found, enabling longer reasoning only when justified. Unusually, the paper also introduces HID-Chart, a 734-chart, 1,561-QA benchmark scored by a Chart-ID density metric to stress fine-grained chart understanding. Chart-FR1 improves average performance by 6.1% over the base model on five chart benchmarks and by 10.0% on HID-Chart, pointing toward evidence-grounded, cue-efficient multimodal reasoning.
papers.abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown considerable potential in chart understanding and reasoning tasks. However, they still struggle with high information density (HID) charts characterized by multiple subplots, legends, and dense annotations due to three major challenges: (1) limited fine-grained perception results in the omission of critical visual cues; (2) redundant or noisy visual information undermines the performance of multimodal reasoning; (3) lack of adaptive deep reasoning relative to the amount of visual information. To tackle these challenges, we present a novel focus-driven fine-grained chart reasoning model, Chart-FR1, to improve perception, focusing efficiency, and adaptive deep reasoning on HID charts. Specifically, we propose Focus-CoT, a visual focusing chain-of-thought that enhances fine-grained perception by explicitly linking reasoning steps to key visual cues, such as local image regions and OCR signals. Building on this, we introduce Focus-GRPO, a focus-driven reinforcement learning algorithm with an information-efficiency reward that compresses redundant visual information for efficient focusing, and an adaptive KL penalty mechanism that enables flexible control over reasoning depth as more visual cues are discovered. Furthermore, to fill the gap in benchmarks for HID charts, we build HID-Chart, a challenging benchmark with an information-density metric designed to evaluate fine-grained chart reasoning capabilities. Extensive experiments on multiple chart benchmarks demonstrate that Chart-FR1 outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs in chart understanding and reasoning. Code is available at https://github.com/phkhub/Chart-FR1.

papers.title: AFFormer: Adaptive Feature Fusion Transformer for V2X Cooperative Perception under Channel Impairments
papers.summary: AFFormer: Adaptive Feature Fusion Transformer for V2X Cooperative Perception under Channel Impairments tackles LiDAR-based 3D object detection when intermediate V2X features arrive corrupted by realistic wireless effects—path loss, Rician/Rayleigh fading, AWGN, interference, and bursty degradation that can turn cooperative fusion into a source of false alarms and missed occluded objects. The core idea is to fuse not just across agents, but across time and space while explicitly estimating which spatial regions are trustworthy. Its MATA block stacks an Inter-Agent Transformer and a Temporal-Aware Transformer to recover context from neighboring vehicles and cached history; DualSA replaces expensive global 2D self-attention with separable width/height attention for efficient anisotropic spatial modeling; and UGF converts channel-wise entropy into spatially varying reliability weights that suppress uncertain or corrupted responses. A teacher-student distillation scheme uses an ideal-communication early-collaboration teacher to supervise the impaired student without inference overhead. Evaluated on V2XSet and DAIR-V2X under ideal and impaired channels, AFFormer consistently outperforms V2X-ViT, CoAlign, MKD-Cooper, DSRC, and V2V AM+LCRN, with notably smaller AP drops under low SNR and a strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoff, pointing toward corruption-aware, uncertainty-driven cooperative perception.
papers.abstract: Accurate 3D object detection is essential for ensuring the safety of autonomous vehicles. Cooperative perception, which leverages vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication to share perceptual data, enhances detection but is vulnerable to channel impairments, such as noise, fading, and interference. To strengthen the reliability of intelligent transportation systems, this work improves the robustness of V2X cooperative perception under communication conditions that reflect common channel impairments. This paper proposes an Adaptive Feature Fusion Transformer (AFFormer), a Transformer-based framework that mitigates the adverse effects of corrupted features by modeling temporal, inter-agent, and spatial correlations. AFFormer introduces three key modules: Multi-Agent and Temporal Aggregation for context-aware fusion across agents and over time, Dual Spatial Attention for efficient modeling of spatial dependencies, and Uncertainty-Guided Fusion for entropy-driven refinement of fused features. A teacher-student knowledge distillation strategy further enhances robustness by aligning fused features with reliable early-collaboration supervision. AFFormer is validated on the V2XSet and DAIR-V2X datasets, where it consistently outperforms existing methods under both ideal and impaired communication conditions, demonstrating improved robustness to communication-induced feature degradation while maintaining a competitive efficiency-accuracy trade-off.

papers.title: CyberAId: AI-Driven Cybersecurity for Financial Service Providers
papers.summary: CyberAId: AI-Driven Cybersecurity for Financial Service Providers argues that European finance is bottlenecked less by telemetry than by reasoning capacity, because SIEMs cover only a minority of MITRE ATT&CK techniques, SOCs drown in uninvestigated alerts, and regulatory regimes such as DORA, NIS2, and the EU AI Act demand auditable, timely action. Rather than replacing SIEM/XDR with a monolithic LLM, the paper proposes a model-agnostic, on-premise hybrid multi-agent runtime: a Main Agent/CRA triages events, specialist subagents handle threat intelligence, vulnerability assessment, compliance, behavioral analysis, incident response, forensics, and DevSecOps, and a Reporting capability generates DORA/NIS2/MiFID II artefacts under bounded human-in-the-loop autonomy. Its differentiator is architectural trust: partitioned RAG over telemetry, CTI, and regulatory corpora; federated sharing of accumulated agent knowledge without exporting raw data; append-only audit trails; and optional capability packs for digital-twin adversarial validation, eBPF kernel telemetry, and quantum-token authentication. The platform is framed through four financial use cases—client impersonation, AML, retail incident response, and high-frequency-trading resilience—and points toward skill-based agent adaptation as the path from isolated cybersecurity wins to continuously refined collective defense.
papers.abstract: European financial institutions face mounting regulatory pressure while their security operations centres remain constrained not by data or staffing but by reasoning capacity: enterprise SIEMs cover only a fraction of MITRE ATT&CK techniques, two thirds of SOC teams cannot keep pace with alert volumes, and the majority of breaches are preceded by alerts that are generated but never investigated. Frontier large language models now achieve state-of-the-art results on isolated cybersecurity tasks (one-day vulnerability exploitation, code-level patching, intrusion detection) yet no narrow win constitutes a platform that can compose across functions, persist multi-tenant state, map findings to regulatory regimes and survive an audit. This position paper argues that the right unit of construction is a hybrid multi-agent system in which specialised LLM subagents reason over classical SIEM/XDR telemetry rather than replacing it, share accumulated agent state across institutions through privacy-preserving federation, and can connect to complementary capability packs such as quantum-based authentication, digital twins for adversarial validation, and eBPF-based kernel telemetry. We present CyberAId, a model-agnostic, on-premise-deployable platform in which a Main Agent coordination layer, a Reporting capability, and specialist subagents operate within a shared runtime under bounded human-in-the-loop autonomy, organised around four falsifiable design principles, and aligned with relevant regulations. CyberAId will be validated at four representative financial use cases (client impersonation, anti-money-laundering for payment service providers, retail-banking incident response, and high-frequency-trading resilience) and propose skill-based agent adaptation as the most promising research direction for turning each deployment into a contribution to a continuously refined collective defence.

papers.title: Divide and Conquer: Decoupled Representation Alignment for Multimodal World Models
papers.summary: Divide and Conquer: Decoupled Representation Alignment for Multimodal World Models tackles the failure of multimodal video world models to exploit heterogeneous foundation priors when generating RGB, depth, and mask sequences jointly. The core idea in M^2-REPA is to treat DINOv2, DepthAnythingV2, and SAM2 as complementary modality experts, then split the diffusion model’s intermediate representation into modality-specific subspaces with lightweight MLP decoupling layers before aligning each branch to its matching expert. Unlike prior REPA-style work that aligns one RGB stream to one teacher, this paper shows that naive multi-expert alignment causes feature conflicts and can underperform single-expert guidance. It resolves this with two coupled objectives: a cosine-similarity representation alignment loss and a CKA-based decoupling regularizer that pushes the modality branches toward complementarity rather than redundancy. The method is plugged into both UViT and DiT autoregressive flow-matching video generators and evaluated on RealEstate10K and Minecraft, with pseudo-depth and SAM2 masks. Results report consistently better short- and long-horizon fidelity, stronger depth accuracy, and higher mask mIoU, pointing toward expert-composed, modality-aware world models.
papers.abstract: Emerging multi-modal world models attempt to jointly generate videos across diverse modalities (e.g., RGB, depth, and mask), yet they fail to fully exploit the rich priors of existing foundation models. We propose $M^2$-REPA, the first representation alignment method tailored for multi-modal video generation. Our key insight is that foundation models trained on different modality spaces naturally capture distinct domain-specific priors, acting as complementary "experts." Specifically, we first decouple modality-specific features from the diffusion model's intermediate representations, then align each with its corresponding expert foundation model. To this end, we design two synergistic objectives: a multi-modal representation alignment loss that enforces feature-to-expert matching, and a modality-specific decoupling regularization that encourages complementarity across different modalities. This design enables joint optimization, fully exploiting priors from multiple foundation models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms baselines in visual quality and long-term consistency.

papers.title: How Label Imbalance Shapes Geometry: A General Spectral Analysis of Multi-Label Neural Collapse
papers.summary: How Label Imbalance Shapes Geometry: A General Spectral Analysis of Multi-Label Neural Collapse studies why the familiar “tag-wise averaging” picture of multi-label neural collapse breaks under correlated, long-tailed label co-occurrence. Rather than assuming combinatorial symmetry, the paper analyzes the pick-all-labels loss in a regularized unconstrained-feature setting and shows that terminal geometry is controlled by the centered label covariance spectrum κ_m, the smallest Rayleigh quotient of ΠG_mΠ on the centered subspace. This spectral constant captures the weakest inter-class contrast directions and explains when imbalance makes the collapse geometry ill-conditioned. The main structural result resolves the multiplicity-one imbalance conjecture: multiplicity-one prototypes align with classifier directions after count-aware centering, and every higher-multiplicity prototype is generated by an explicit √r_{1,k}-weighted synthesis of those multiplicity-one directions, not uniform averaging. The classical tag-wise average emerges only in the special orthogonal/balanced case. Experiments on synthetic multi-label MNIST and CIFAR10, built with pad-stacked multiplicity-two samples and imbalance injected only into multiplicity-one counts, confirm persistent NC2/NC3 degradation and scaling-sensitive Gram residuals. The paper points toward a spectral theory of multi-label representation learning that treats co-occurrence statistics and imbalance as first-class geometric operators.
papers.abstract: This work investigates the phenomenon of Neural Collapse (NC) in multi-label classification, extending its conceptual framework from multi-class learning to general correlated and imbalanced multi-label settings. Although recent studies have identified a ''tag-wise averaging'' structure for multi-label features, this view relies on implicit assumptions of label balance and combinatorial symmetry. Consequently, it fails to account for the geometrical distortions caused by intrinsic label correlations and data imbalance, which are common in practice. We resolve the multiplicity-one imbalance conjecture raised by Li et al. (2024), showing that higher-multiplicity prototypes obey a class-frequency-weighted synthesis rule rather than uniform averaging. To address this, we propose a rigorous spectral-control framework to analyze the terminal phase of multi-label learning under general imbalanced conditions. We introduce the label covariance spectrum $κ_m$, a scalar controlling the distribution-dependent lower-bound geometry, derived from the second-order moment matrix of the label distribution. Contrary to the averaging perspective, our analysis reveals that the centered label covariance spectrum controls the stability of terminal geometry by quantifying the weakest centered inter-class contrast directions. We prove that the classical Tag-wise Averaging emerges only as a special case under perfect orthogonality. Numerical experiments on synthetic distributions validate our theoretical bounds. This work resolves the scaled-average aspect of the imbalance conjecture and establishes a unifying theoretical framework that extends Neural Collapse to complex, imbalanced multi-label settings.

papers.title: Disentangling Intent from Role: Adversarial Self-Play for Persona-Invariant Safety Alignment
papers.summary: Disentangling Intent from Role: Adversarial Self-Play for Persona-Invariant Safety Alignment tackles the failure mode where aligned LLMs refuse direct harmful prompts yet comply when the same intent is wrapped in a persuasive persona, showing that safety decisions can be spuriously coupled to role context. The paper proposes Persona-Invariant Alignment (PIA), an adversarial self-play loop with Persona Lineage Evolution (PLE) on the attacker side and Persona-Invariant Consistency Learning (PICL) on the defender side. PLE treats persona search as a lineage graph, adding ancestor credit propagation and a UCB exploration bonus to avoid genetic-algorithm “lineage amnesia” and efficiently surface transferable high-risk personas. PICL operationalizes the structural separation hypothesis by using persona-free outputs as a frozen teacher and applying a unilateral KL constraint so persona-conditioned outputs match the same refusal distribution, then combines this with DPO and SFT. On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, with WildGuard judging and JBB-Behaviors/PKU-SafeRLHF plus OOD jailbreak benchmarks, PLE reaches near-saturated ASR while PICL sharply lowers attack success without materially harming MMLU, GPQA, IFEval, or role-playing, pointing toward mechanism-level safety invariance under adversarial context shifts.
papers.abstract: The growing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have driven their widespread deployment across diverse domains, even in potentially high-risk scenarios. Despite advances in safety alignment techniques, current models remain vulnerable to emerging persona-based jailbreak attacks. Existing research on persona-based jailbreak has primarily focused on attack iterations, yet it lacks systemic and mechanistic constraints on the defense side. To address this challenge, we propose Persona-Invariant Alignment (PIA), an adversarial self-play framework that achieves co-evolution through Persona Lineage Evolution (PLE) on the attack side and Persona-Invariant Consistency Learning (PICL) on the defense side. Theoretically, PICL is grounded in the structural separation hypothesis, using a unilateral KL-divergence constraint to enable the structural decoupling of safety decisions from persona context, thereby maintaining safe behavior under persona-based jailbreak attacks. Experimental results demonstrate that PLE efficiently explores high-risk persona spaces by leveraging lineage-based credit propagation. Meanwhile, the PICL defense method significantly reduces the Attack Success Rate (ASR) while preserving the model's general capability, thereby validating the superiority and robustness of this alignment paradigm. Codes are available at https://github.com/JiajiaLi-1130/PIA.

papers.title: Behavior-Grounded Lane Representation Learning for Multi-Task Traffic Digital Twins
papers.summary: Behavior-Grounded Lane Representation Learning for Multi-Task Traffic Digital Twins addresses the mismatch between traffic digital twins, which usually store lanes as static map geometry, and the operational semantics needed to reason about how a lane actually functions under live traffic. The paper’s GeoLaneRep framework encodes three complementary signals—lane polylines, assigned vehicle tracklets, and interpretable lane descriptors such as lateral rank, edge status, group size, speed, curvature, offset, and density—into a shared 128-D embedding. A cross-lane multi-head attention block injects group-relative context, while training combines structurally mined InfoNCE alignment across cameras with auxiliary role supervision and synthetic temporal anomaly detection; geometry dropout further forces zero-shot transfer when query cameras lack reliable lane maps. Evaluated on 16 roadside cameras, 132 lanes, and 104,415 trajectories from 511 Wisconsin, the full model achieves 0.004 lateral-rank error, edge-role F1 of 1.000, and anomaly AUROC of 0.991, outperforming geometry-only, trajectory-only, and two-stage frozen variants. The same behavioral embedding also conditions a FiLM-modulated diffusion generator to synthesize lane centerlines meeting target specs with 87.9% accuracy, pointing toward lane-level semantic interfaces for constructive, behavior-aware digital twins.
papers.abstract: Traffic digital twins are powerful tools for advanced traffic management, and most systems are built on static geometric representations. However, these representations fail to capture the dynamic functional semantics required for behavior-aware reasoning, such as how a lane operates under complex traffic conditions. To address this gap, we introduce GeoLaneRep, a behavior-grounded lane representation learning framework for traffic digital twins. GeoLaneRep jointly encodes static lane geometry, observed vehicle trajectories, and operational descriptors into a shared, cross-camera semantic embedding. The encoder is trained with a joint objective combining contrastive cross-camera alignment, auxiliary role supervision, and temporal anomaly detection. Across 16 roadside cameras and 132 lanes, the learned embeddings achieve a $0.004$ lateral-rank error and an edge-role F1 of $1.000$ in zero-shot cross-camera matching, and an AUROC of $0.991$ for window-level anomaly detection.
