In this paper, we identify that such joint optimization causes encoders to exploit cross-modal co-occurrences, statistical relationships between modalities that arise from source-specific recording conditions, rather than learning domain-invariant features. We term this failure mode Fusion Overfitting. To address this, we propose Modality-Entropy Regularization for Domain Generalization (MER-DG), which maximizes the entropy of each encoder's feature distribution to preserve feature diversity. MER-DG is architecture-agnostic and integrates into existing multimodal frameworks as an additive loss term. Extensive experiments on EPIC-Kitchens and HAC benchmarks demonstrate average improvements of approximately 5% over standard fusion and approximately 2% over state-of-the-art methods.

papers.title: AdamO: A Collapse-Suppressed Optimizer for Offline RL
papers.summary: AdamO: A Collapse-Suppressed Optimizer for Offline RL targets a failure mode in offline TD learning where bootstrapped critic updates in the deadly triad self-amplify, driving Q-values to explosive collapse; the paper argues this instability is not only caused by the backup rule or network architecture, but by optimizer dynamics themselves. It models frozen offline TD learning as a feedback system under Adam, derives a local linear recurrence for TD errors, and shows stability is equivalent to the spectral radius of an augmented update operator being below one. The key technical move is to translate that criterion into a practical geometric condition: suppress collapse by making the whitened Jacobian features near-isometric via explicit weight orthogonality. AdamO implements this as a decoupled orthogonality correction on selected matrix blocks, scaled by a task-alignment budget so Adam’s moment estimates are not contaminated by the regularizer. Experiments plug AdamO into TD3+BC, IQL, ReBRAC, ACTIVE, PARS, and SQOG on D4RL MuJoCo, AntMaze, Adroit, and FrankaKitchen, where it consistently improves stability and return, especially on collapse-prone sparse-reward tasks. The paper points toward optimizer-level control of RL instability rather than solely policy or critic redesign.
papers.abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) can fail spectacularly when bootstrapped temporal-difference (TD) updates amplify their own errors, driving the critic toward extreme and unusable Q-values. A key counterintuitive insight of this work is that collapse is not only a property of the backup rule or network architecture: optimizer dynamics themselves can directly trigger or suppress instability. From a control-theoretic viewpoint, we model offline TD learning as a feedback system and analyze Adam-based critic updates. This yields a necessary and sufficient condition for stability of the induced local update dynamics: within the regime we analyze, these dynamics are stable if and only if the spectral radius of the corresponding update operator is strictly below one. Further analysis suggests that standard Adam updates can inadvertently distort the parameter geometry, motivating explicit orthogonality constraints to prevent TD error amplification. To this end, we propose AdamO, an Adam-based optimizer with a decoupled orthogonality correction regulated by a strict task-alignment budget. We prove that this design theoretically guarantees worst-case task safety and preserves Adam's continuous-time dissipative dynamics. Empirically, AdamO is broadly compatible with diverse offline RL baselines, improving stability and returns across a broad suite of benchmarks.

papers.title: Trojan Hippo: Weaponizing Agent Memory for Data Exfiltration
papers.summary: Trojan Hippo: Weaponizing Agent Memory for Data Exfiltration studies a realistic confused-deputy attack on persistent LLM-agent memory, where a single untrusted tool interaction such as a crafted email plants a dormant payload into long-term memory that later wakes up only when the user discusses sensitive finance, health, legal, tax, or identity topics and then exfiltrates the user’s message through an outbound tool. The core novelty is an end-to-end, black-box, adaptive red-teaming pipeline built with OpenEvolve that evolves attack emails against a held-out environment, plus the first capability-aware security/utility analysis for memory systems. The authors test a LangChain email assistant over four backends: explicit memory, Mem0 agentic memory, retrieval-augmented memory, and sliding-window context. Against frontier OpenAI and Google models, the attack reaches 85–100% ASR and can persist after 100 benign sessions, showing that memory turns one-shot prompt injection into long-horizon compromise. Simple defenses such as user-only indexing, no-untrusted-write, and memory truncation help but trade off utility sharply; a taint-tracking IFC policy stops exfiltration entirely yet blocks many send workflows. The paper points toward secure memory architectures and deployment-specific security-utility tuning as a central AI systems research direction.
papers.abstract: Memory systems enable otherwise-stateless LLM agents to persist user information across sessions, but also introduce a new attack surface. We characterize the Trojan Hippo attack, a class of persistent memory attacks that operates in a more realistic threat model than prior memory poisoning work: the attacker plants a dormant payload into an agent's long-term memory via a single untrusted tool call (e.g., a crafted email), which activates only when the user later discusses sensitive topics such as finance, health, or identity, and exfiltrates high-value personal data to the attacker. While anecdotal demonstrations of such attacks have appeared against deployed systems, no prior work systematically evaluates them across heterogeneous memory architectures and defenses.We introduce a dynamic evaluation framework comprising two components: (1) an OpenEvolve-based adaptive red-teaming benchmark that stress-tests defenses and memory backends against continuously refined attacks, and (2) the first capability-aware security/utility analysis for persistent memory systems, enabling principled reasoning about defense deployment across different usage profiles. Instantiated on an email assistant across four memory backends (explicit tool memory, agentic memory, RAG, and sliding-window context), Trojan Hippo achieves up to 85-100 percent ASR against current frontier models from OpenAI and Google, with planted memories successfully activating even after 100 benign sessions. We evaluate four memory-system defenses inspired by basic security principles, finding they substantially reduce attack success rates (to as low as 0-5 percent), though at utility costs that vary widely with task requirements. Because of this substantial security-utility tradeoff, the effective real-world deployment of defenses remains an open challenge, which our evaluation framework is specifically designed to address.

papers.title: ProtoFair: Fair Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning via Pseudo-Counterfactual Pairs
papers.summary: ProtoFair: Fair Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning via Pseudo-Counterfactual Pairs tackles a practical weakness in SSL for vision: ResNet-18 representations learned by SimCLR/SupCon still encode gender, age, and ethnicity cues that later drive equalized-odds violations in facial attribute classification. Rather than redesigning the base SSL objective, ProtoFair is a plug-in auxiliary contrastive regularizer, L = LSSL + λLCF, that uses a separate cluster head and momentum-updated prototypes to assign each image to an unsupervised semantic cluster. Samples with the same cluster but different sensitive attributes are treated as pseudo-counterfactual positives and pulled together, while same-cluster same-group pairs are deliberately excluded from the numerator so the method aligns content across groups instead of just smoothing clusters. A FIFO cross-batch queue, inspired by MoCo, enlarges the pool of cross-group pairs beyond the mini-batch and stabilizes training under imbalance. On CelebA and UTKFace, with only sensitive-attribute annotations and no target labels, ProtoFair consistently lowers equalized odds while keeping accuracy competitive, pointing toward modular fairness layers that can be attached to rapidly changing SSL backbones.
papers.abstract: Self-supervised learning methods learn high-quality visual representations, yet recent studies show that these representations often capture demographic biases present in the training data. Existing fairness-aware methods address this by redesigning the self-supervised objective itself, limiting portability across the rapidly evolving landscape of self-supervised learning (SSL) frameworks. We propose ProtoFair, a fairness-aware contrastive loss designed to work alongside existing SSL objectives without modifying them. ProtoFair leverages unsupervised prototype clustering to identify pseudo-counterfactual pairs: samples sharing the same cluster assignment but belonging to different sensitive groups. By pulling these content-matched, cross-group samples together in the embedding space, ProtoFair encourages the encoder to learn representations that are invariant to the sensitive attribute. The method requires only sensitive attribute annotations, no target labels, and integrates seamlessly with both SimCLR and SupCon. Experiments on CelebA and UTKFace demonstrate consistent fairness improvements while maintaining competitive accuracy.

papers.title: Learn-to-learn on Arbitrary Textual Conditioning: A Hypernetwork-Driven Meta-Gated LLM
papers.summary: Learn-to-learn on Arbitrary Textual Conditioning: A Hypernetwork-Driven Meta-Gated LLM tackles the adaptation bottleneck of LLMs under corpus heterogeneity and subtle shifts in task, domain, persona, style, sentiment, or emotion, where full fine-tuning risks catastrophic forgetting and prior meta-learning remains expensive to scale. The key idea is MeGan: freeze the backbone Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and replace each SwiGLU with β-SwiGLU, then use a lightweight hypernetwork to read a textual condition prompt, apply cross-attention bottleneck compression plus layer embeddings, and emit a layerwise β that changes the FFN nonlinearity instead of inserting low-rank weights or adapters. This is unusual relative to LoRA/Text-to-LoRA because it meta-controls activation slope and gain, not parameter updates, giving a neuroscience-inspired “metaplasticity” mechanism. Trained by SFT with an L2 penalty keeping β near 1, MeGan reaches 3.41M trainable parameters and outperforms prompting, SFT, LoRA, MetaICL, CMAML, MLtD, and Text-to-LoRA on Persona-Chat, AdaptSum, CrossFit+UnifiedQA, and SNI, while transferring reasonably to unseen tasks and new condition types.
papers.abstract: Conventional LLMs may suffer from corpus heterogeneity and subtle condition changes. While finetuning can create the catastrophe forgetting issue, application of meta-learning on LLMs is also limited due to its complexity and scalability. In this paper, we activate the meta-signal of $β$ within the SwiGLU blocks, resulting in a meta-gating mechanism that adaptively adjusts the nonlinearity of FFN. A hypernetwork is employed which dynamically produces $β$ on textual conditions, providing meta-controllability on LLMs. By testing on different condition types such as task, domain, persona, and style, our method outperforms finetuning and meta-learning baselines, and can generalize reasonably on unseen tasks, condition types, or instructions. Our code can be found in https://github.com/AaronJi/MeGan.

papers.title: 12 Angry AI Agents: Evaluating Multi-Agent LLM Decision-Making Through Cinematic Jury Deliberation
papers.summary: 12 Angry AI Agents: Evaluating Multi-Agent LLM Decision-Making Through Cinematic Jury Deliberation turns Sidney Lumet’s 12 Angry Men into a controlled benchmark for whether LLMs in a jury-of-agents setting can actually persuade one another rather than merely trade scripted opinions. Twelve persona-conditioned jurors, each faithful to the film’s occupations, biases, and speaking style, deliberate the murder case using Microsoft AutoGen’s SelectorGroupChat, with structured vote checks every 12 turns and early stopping after three no-change rounds. The study compares GPT-4o, representing heavy RLHF alignment, against Llama-4-Scout, representing lighter alignment, under three conditions: baseline 11–1 initial vote, no initial vote, and an “open-minded” prompt that explicitly asks agents to update on sound counterarguments. Across 18 runs, 17 end in hung jury, showing that anchoring dominates and the film’s minority-to-majority cascade almost never materializes. GPT-4o is nearly rigid at 1.0 vote changes per run and never escapes deadlock, while Llama-4-Scout is more responsive, rising to 6.0 changes under the open-minded prompt and producing the only NOT_GUILTY verdict. The paper argues that deliberative flexibility, not raw capability, is the key axis for multi-agent debate and LLM-panel design.
papers.abstract: What if the twelve jurors of Sidney Lumet's 12 Angry Men (1957) were not men, but large language models? Would the one juror who disagrees still be able to change everyone's mind? This paper instantiates that scenario as a multi-agent benchmark for LLM deliberation: twelve agents, each conditioned on a film-faithful persona, debate the film's murder case using multi-agent framework. Two models representing opposite ends of the RLHF spectrum are tested: GPT-4o (closed-source, heavy alignment) and Llama-4-Scout (open-weight, lighter alignment), across three conditions (baseline, open-minded prompt, no initial vote), with N = 3 replications per cell (18 runs total). Three findings emerge. (i) Seventeen of eighteen runs end in a hung jury (a state where the jury fails to reach a unanimous verdict); the film's central event, gradual minority-to-majority persuasion, almost never occurs, indicating that anchoring is the dominant failure mode of current LLMs in this setting. (ii) The two models exhibit sharply different internal dynamics: GPT-4o produces a mean of 1.0 vote changes per run across all conditions, while Llama-4-Scout ranges from 2.0 (baseline) to 6.0 (open-minded prompt), and is the only model to reach a NOT\_GUILTY verdict (1 of 3 runs in the no-initial-vote condition). The same ``open-minded'' instruction is internalized by Llama and ignored by GPT-4o. (iii) This asymmetry suggests that the intensity of RLHF alignment training, not model capability, is the primary determinant of deliberative flexibility in multi-agent settings. Flexibility, not capability, tracks human deliberation. The work is framed as an exploratory study and discusses implications for jury-of-LLMs evaluation and multi-agent debate.

papers.title: Misclassification Rate and Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in Graph Convolutional Networks via Subsampling Stability
papers.summary: Misclassification Rate and Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in Graph Convolutional Networks via Subsampling Stability studies how to make a well-trained GCN differentially private without losing all node-classification signal, using edge-level subsampling as the privacy primitive rather than gradient noise or feature perturbation. The core mechanism, AsampGCN, repeatedly samples subgraphs by retaining each edge with probability p_s, runs a base one-layer GCN on each sampled graph, aggregates the label vectors by majority vote, and then uses a Propose-Test-Release stability check with Laplace noise to release the consensus only when the vote pattern is sufficiently stable. The paper’s distinctive contribution is a first rigorous subsampling-stability analysis for GCNs: it derives an explicit high-probability misclassification bound for a single subsampled graph from a margin condition, Lipschitz activation, and a matrix Bernstein control of the Laplacian deviation, then lifts that to the voting ensemble. It also characterizes a feasible interval for p_s, showing the tension that large p_s weakens stability-based DP guarantees while small p_s destroys utility. This points toward theory-driven parameter selection for private graph learning.
papers.abstract: We study differential privacy (DP) in Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) through the framework of \textit{subsampling stability}. We derive upper bounds on the misclassification rate that depend explicitly on the subsampling probability $p_s$. Furthermore, we characterize the \textit{privacy--utility trade-off} by identifying feasible ranges of $p_s$; if $p_s$ is too large, the stability-based privacy condition becomes difficult to satisfy, yielding vacuous guarantees, whereas if it is too small, accuracy deteriorates. Our results provide the first rigorous theoretical framework for understanding subsampling stability in GCNs under DP.

papers.title: DBLP: Phase-Aware Bounded-Loss Transport for Burst-Resilient Distributed ML Training
papers.summary: DBLP: Phase-Aware Bounded-Loss Transport for Burst-Resilient Distributed ML Training attacks a network-layer bottleneck in large-scale data-parallel training: transient microbursts create tail-latency collapse even when average communication is optimized, and prior bounded-loss schemes treat every gradient and every training phase as equally tolerant. The key idea is a software-only transport protocol that combines semi-reliable UDP/TCP gradient delivery with phase-adaptive loss tolerance: packets carry sequence numbers plus a per-round counter to reject stale arrivals, the receiver returns bitmap/probe/stop control, and the sender stops retransmission once a dynamic loss budget is met. DBLP detects critical learning regimes using relative changes in gradient L2 norm (η=0.5), keeps a low tolerance in these sensitive windows, and raises tolerance by about 40% elsewhere to absorb bursts without harming convergence. Evaluated on a 3-worker/1-server PyTorch all-reduce setup with EfficientNetB0/CIFAR-10, ResNet50 and AlexNet/CIFAR-100, and GPT-2-S/WikiText-2, it withstands injected 70% packet-loss microbursts, reaches up to 5.88× communication speedups, and cuts end-to-end training time by 24.4% on average while preserving accuracy. This points toward transport protocols that are explicitly training-dynamics-aware rather than purely network-agnostic.
papers.abstract: Distributed machine learning (ML) training has become a necessity with the prevalence of billion to trillion-parameter-scale models. While prior work has improved training efficiency from the ML perspective at the application layer, it often fails to address transient congestion events at the network layer that introduce severe tail latency and training-time variability, thereby undermining the quality of service (QoS) of distributed ML training systems. Existing network optimizations treat all gradients equally and thus fail to integrate sufficient model-training insights into communication protocol design. In this paper, we present Dynamic Bounded-Loss Protocol (DBLP), a burst-resilient, training-phase-aware, and hardware-agnostic transport protocol that incorporates model-level tolerance properties into gradient communication. By dynamically adjusting gradient loss tolerance across training phases, DBLP reduces overall training time and mitigates tail-latency collapse during transient high-loss events (i.e., microbursts). Compared to the current state-of-the-art solution (baseline), DBLP tolerates significantly higher loss while achieving comparable test accuracy, and reduces end-to-end training time by an average of 24.4% and a maximum of 33.9%. At microburst events, DBLP achieves up to 5.88x single-round communication latency speedups over the baseline, preventing burst-induced tail-latency spikes and maintaining stable training performance.

papers.title: Real-Time Text Transmission via LLM-Based Entropy Coding over Fixed-Rate Channels
papers.summary: Real-Time Text Transmission via LLM-Based Entropy Coding over Fixed-Rate Channels studies the real bottleneck in streaming predict-then-code systems: when text arrives at a human-like reading pace but bits must traverse a constant-rate channel, variable-length entropy codes create FIFO backlog and latency that depend not just on average compression but on variance and coder structure. The paper separates irreducible algorithmic delay from computational delay and compares Shannon coding, Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS at multiple block sizes, and gzip when the predictor is a causal LLM. Unlike nearby compression work that reports only rate, it evaluates the compression–delay Pareto frontier on five Project Gutenberg books using GPT-2 (124M) and Llama 3.2 (3B) with 10K-token runs. Arithmetic coding is near-Shannon but pays deferred decodability; Huffman has zero algorithmic delay and becomes the practical choice once the channel is over-provisioned; rANS trades buffering for amortized block overhead. The key empirical twist is that scaling from GPT-2 to Llama cuts bits per character by about 38%, pushing the same channel into a safer regime and changing the optimal coder. The paper points toward predictor-centric, latency-aware language transmission and real-time communication design.
papers.abstract: Learning, prediction, and compression are intimately connected: a model that accurately predicts the next symbol in a sequence can be coupled with a source coder to compress that sequence near its information-theoretic limit. When tokenized characters arriving at a fixed reading pace are encoded into variable-length codewords and streamed over a fixed-rate channel, a queue forms whose per-token delay depends on the mean and variance of the bit lengths and on the coder's algorithmic latency. This paper investigates the compression--delay tradeoff that arises when a causal language model serves as the sequential predictor within a predict-then-code architecture for real-time text transmission. Several coding schemes are compared: Shannon (ideal), Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS at various block sizes, and gzip. The analysis separates algorithmic delay, inherent to the coder, from computational delay, which shrinks as hardware improves. Huffman is the practical choice for over-provisioned channels, with zero algorithmic delay and modest compression overhead. Arithmetic coding achieves near-optimal compression at the cost of decodability delay. Findings are validated across two scales: GPT-2 (124M) and Llama~3.2 (3B), a twenty-five-fold parameter range. This scaling yields an approximately 38\% reduction in bits per character, effectively over-provisioning the channel and thereby changing which coder is optimal.

papers.title: From Concept to Capability: Evaluating 3D Gaussian Splatting for Synthetic Scene Editing in Autonomous Driving
papers.summary: From Concept to Capability: Evaluating 3D Gaussian Splatting for Synthetic Scene Editing in Autonomous Driving tackles a safety-critical bottleneck: real driving logs under-sample rare, high-risk scenarios, yet 3DGS-based reconstruction/editing is only useful for ADS verification if its fidelity survives novel viewpoints and partial visibility. The paper builds a dynamic fidelity evaluation framework in CARLA, training four reconstructions (DeformGS, PVG, StreetGS, OmniRe) from calibrated RGB-LiDAR sequences in four scenarios—following, intersection, overtaking, and oncoming—then rendering near/medium/far novel views (0.5 m, 1.6 m, 3.2 m lateral deviation, plus longitudinal/elevation shifts) against simulator ground truth. Unlike nearby work that reports generic render quality, it scores full-image and object-specific PSNR/SSIM, YOLOv10/DETR precision-recall, and SegFormer mIoU/Dice for vehicles and pedestrians, and it demonstrates cross-dataset agent injection using Waymo, Zenseact Open Dataset, and KITTI. OmniRe is consistently best, but fidelity drops sharply with lateral extrapolation, especially for pedestrians; StreetGS and DeformGS cannot model deformable humans well. The main implication is a viewpoint-aware, visibility-tagged reconstruction pipeline for industrial AD V&V, with complementary generative modeling needed for non-rigid road users.
papers.abstract: The perception of an Autonomous Driving System (ADS) critically depends on relevant, comprehensive, and diverse datasets to ensure its safety while operating in the environment. Field data collection lacks completeness with respect to the list of rare but still possible safety-related scenarios needed for the development, verification, and validation of the ADS. 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has shown promising capabilities for the reconstruction and editing of scenes based on data collected by cameras and LiDAR sensors. However, the industrial fidelity evaluation of reconstructions is underexplored, which is crucial when employing such methods in safety-related systems, especially for ADS. This becomes more challenging as ADS operates in a dynamic, uncontrolled environment with limited viewpoints and often partially occluded objects. This paper addresses this gap by proposing and implementing a framework (Fig. 1) to systematically analyze the capabilities and limitations of 3DGS for use in the reconstruction of safety-related scenes. It focuses on the quality of reconstruction for vehicles and pedestrians, which are the two most critical object classes for ADS. Our findings provide industry insights into the fidelity degradation of reconstructions from multiple novel viewpoints, both lateral and longitudinal, enabling the integration of these methods into real-world industrial AD software development and testing pipelines.

papers.title: TumorXAI: Self-Supervised Deep Learning Framework for Explainable Brain MRI Tumor Classification
papers.summary: TumorXAI: Self-Supervised Deep Learning Framework for Explainable Brain MRI Tumor Classification tackles the clinical bottleneck that supervised MRI tumor classifiers need large, balanced annotations despite heterogeneous 17-class tumor appearances and scarce labels. The paper builds a ResNet-50 pipeline that first learns image representations through SSL pretraining with strong augmentations, then compares SimCLR, BYOL, DINO, and MoCo v3 under both linear evaluation and fine-tuning, rather than only reporting a single end-to-end classifier. On a publicly available 4,448-scan brain MRI set, the study finds SimCLR to be the most robust backbone, reaching 99.64% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 in fine-tuning and 97.27% test accuracy, while DINO collapses sharply in linear probing, highlighting method sensitivity in low-label medical settings. Unlike nearby work that stops at accuracy, TumorXAI also adds Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, and EigenCAM to expose tumor-relevant regions and verify that SSL features form cleaner class clusters in t-SNE. The result argues that label-efficient SSL plus XAI is a practical direction for trustworthy radiology support and multimodal medical representation learning.
papers.abstract: Classifying brain tumors using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is crucial for early diagnosis and treatment; however, tumor heterogeneity and a dearth of annotated datasets restrict the use of supervised deep learning approaches. In this work, we use self-supervised learning (SSL) to study multi-class brain tumor classification. Using a ResNet-50 backbone, we evaluate four SSL frameworks including SimCLR, BYOL, DINO, and Moco v3 on a publicly available dataset of 4,448 MRIs with 17 distinct tumor types. On the dataset, SimCLR achieved 99.64% accuracy, 99.64% precision, 99.64% recall, and 99.64% F1-score. The workflow includes preprocessing, fine-tuning, linear evaluation, and SSL pretraining with data augmentations. Results show that, when labels are limited, SSL-pretrained models outperform supervised baselines in terms of F1-score, recall, accuracy, and precision. Additionally, by providing visual insights into model decisions, Explainable AI techniques (Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, EigenCAM) enhance interpretability. These results demonstrate SSL's scalability and dependability in diagnosing brain tumors from unlabeled medical data.

papers.title: RamanBench: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Machine Learning on Raman Spectroscopy
papers.summary: RamanBench: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Machine Learning on Raman Spectroscopy tackles the field’s core bottleneck: Raman ML papers are trapped in fragmented, small, privately hosted datasets with incompatible preprocessing and one-off evaluation, making it impossible to tell whether gains come from better models or better data handling. The paper’s key contribution is a fully reproducible “living benchmark” that standardizes data access, splits, metrics, and code via the raman-data and ramanbench packages, then aggregates 74 real Raman datasets, including 16 newly released ones, into 325,668 spectra spanning 163 classification and regression targets across material science, biotechnology, medical/clinical, and chemical/industrial settings. What differentiates it from nearby tabular or time-series benchmarks is that it preserves Raman-specific structure: ordered wavenumber axes, strong local correlations, low sample/high feature regimes, baseline distortions, and instrument-dependent shifts. Under fixed 80/20 splits and three seeds, 28 models were compared, from PLS and RamanNet to TabPFN/TabICL and ROCKET/Arsenal. Tabular Foundation Models lead overall, time-series methods remain competitive, but no model generalizes across datasets, exposing a large improvability gap and pointing Raman ML toward transfer learning and physics-aware, cross-instrument representation learning.
papers.abstract: Machine Learning (ML) has transformed many scientific fields, yet key applications still lack standardized benchmarks. Raman spectroscopy, a widely used technique for non-invasive molecular analysis, is one such field where progress is limited by fragmented datasets, inconsistent evaluation, and models that fail to capture the structure of spectral data. We introduce RamanBench, the first large-scale, fully reproducible benchmark for ML on Raman spectroscopy, consisting of streamlined data access, evaluation protocols and code, as well as a live leaderboard. It unifies 74 datasets (including 16 first released with this benchmark) across four domains, comprising 325,668 spectra and spanning classification and regression tasks under diverse experimental conditions. We benchmark 28 models under a standardized protocol, including classical methods (e.g., PLS), Raman-specific (e.g., RamanNet), Tabular Foundation Model (TFM) (e.g., TabPFN), and time-series approaches (e.g., ROCKET). TFM consistently outperform domain-specific and gradient boosting baselines, while time-series models remain competitive. However, no method generalizes across datasets, revealing a fundamental gap. Therefore, we invite the community to contribute new approaches to our living benchmark, with the potential to accelerate advances in critical applications such as medical diagnostics, biological research, and materials science.

papers.title: Personalized Digital Health Modeling with Adaptive Support Users
papers.summary: Personalized Digital Health Modeling with Adaptive Support Users tackles the core digital-health personalization bottleneck that user-level signals are sparse, noisy, and highly heterogeneous, making population pretraining or “similar-user only” transfer prone to biased negative transfer. The paper’s distinctive move is to redefine the external data pool as support users spanning both similar and dissimilar people, then train each personal model with a unified objective that combines target-user loss, similarity-weighted knowledge transfer from aligned users, and a margin-based contrastive penalty from dissimilar users to suppress misleading correlations. An iterative alternating optimizer jointly updates model parameters and adaptive support-user weights, so relevance is learned rather than fixed; initial similarity is seeded from cosine distances between user embeddings produced by an auxiliary MLP. The method is tested on six prediction tasks across four real-world datasets, including loneliness, affect, CGMacro postprandial glucose, and Globem sleep forecasting, using both MLP and Transformer backbones under chronological train/test splits. It consistently outperforms population, fine-tuning, clustering, and fixed similarity-weighted baselines, reaching up to 10% lower RMSE on larger datasets and about 25% lower RMSE in low-data regimes, while yielding sparse, interpretable weights that point toward data-efficient, contrast-aware personalized health modeling.
papers.abstract: Personalized models are essential in digital health because individuals exhibit substantial physiological and behavioral heterogeneity. Yet personalization is limited by scarce and noisy user-specific data. Most existing methods rely on population pretraining or data from similar users only, which can lead to biased transfer and weak generalization. We propose a unified personalization framework that trains a personal model using adaptively weighted support users, including both similar and dissimilar individuals. The objective integrates personal loss, similarity-weighted transfer from similar users, and contrastive regularization from dissimilar users to suppress misleading correlations. An iterative optimization algorithm jointly updates model parameters and user similarity weights. Experiments on six tasks across four real-world digital health datasets show consistent improvements over population and personalized baselines. The method achieves up to 10% lower RMSE on large-scale datasets and approximately 25% lower RMSE in low-data settings. The learned adaptive weights improve data efficiency and provide interpretable guidance for targeted data selection.

papers.title: How Can One Choose the Best CAM-Based Explainability Method for a CNN Model?
papers.summary: How Can One Choose the Best CAM-Based Explainability Method for a CNN Model? attacks the practical bottleneck of deciding which saliency-map method actually matches human judgment, rather than relying on IoU between arbitrary boxes and heatmaps. The authors test this on 17 correctly classified Chihuahua images from ImageNet using a pretrained ResNet50, first collecting 50 crowdsourced bounding-box annotations to build weighted “annotation heatmaps,” then generating explanation heatmaps from nine CAM variants spanning gradient-based and activation-based families: CAM, Score-CAM, SS-CAM, IS-CAM, Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, Smooth Grad-CAM++, X-Grad-CAM, and LayerCAM. Instead of thresholding saliency maps, they compare full heatmap vectors with 12 distance metrics, rank methods by each metric, and measure agreement with a separate Prolific crowdsourcing study where participants picked the best overlay per image; ranking similarity is scored with Rank-Biased Overlap. The key finding is that Manhattan and Correlation most closely reproduce human rankings, while LayerCAM, Score-CAM, and IS-CAM are most often preferred by people; plain Grad-CAM performs poorly. The paper points toward human-centered XAI benchmarking that treats evaluation metric choice as a first-class research problem.
papers.abstract: In recent years, several advances have been observed in Deep Learning with surprising results. Models in this area have been increasingly used in numerous applications, including those sensitive to human life, which require clear explanations and justifications. Various explainability methods have been proposed, but not many metrics to evaluate these methods. The most commonly used metric is the Intersection over Union (IoU). However, due to the characteristics of the results of the explainability methods, called saliency maps, which do not have a known shape, we hypothesise that there must be a better metric that allows one to find an explainability method that produces results that best resemble the human perception. We propose using different metrics to assess the similarity between human perception and the explanation saliency maps to find a better metric. An investigation was conducted employing a subset of the Chihuahuas images from ImageNet dataset. Several CAM-based explainability methods were used to generate saliency maps for each chihuahua image. Alignment was measured by applying distance metrics between the bounding box of human annotations and the saliency maps produced by each explainability method. Rankings of the best saliency maps were created using the results of the distance metrics and compared to the ranking obtained using people's choice, collected through crowdsourcing, of the best explanation saliency maps for each selected image. Comparison between rankings was performed using the Rank-Biased Overlap (RBO) metric. The results indicate the feasibility of our method to find the explainability method that best resembles human perception. In our experiments, the two metrics that best resemble human perception corresponded to Manhattan and Correlation. Besides, the best explainability methods regarding human perception were LayerCAM, Score-CAM, and IS-CAM.

papers.title: Benchmarking Wireless Representations: High-Dimensional vs. Compressed Embeddings for Efficiency and Robustness
papers.summary: Benchmarking Wireless Representations: High-Dimensional vs. Compressed Embeddings for Efficiency and Robustness benchmarks whether wireless foundation-model embeddings actually justify their dimensional and runtime cost against compact CSI autoencoders and raw-channel baselines. Using DeepMIMO city_18_denver data with 32-antenna, 32-subcarrier channels, the authors compare LWM transformer outputs (CLS and patch/channel embeddings from masked channel modeling) to two convolutional autoencoder latents, then probe them with fixed downstream heads on LoS/NLoS classification, beam selection, and a newly added multi-user power-allocation task. The study is unusual in treating representation choice as a system-level trade-off, scoring data efficiency, noise robustness, and computational complexity rather than just accuracy. Results show high-dimensional LWM embeddings can be strong in few-shot LoS/NLoS and some low-data settings, but they carry substantial latency, parameter, and transmission overhead and often degrade more under low SNR. The compressed AE latents are consistently more stable, more noise-robust, and cheaper, while matching or approaching LWM performance on beam selection and power allocation. The paper points toward wireless learning that co-designs representation, compression, and deployment constraints.
papers.abstract: Building on recent advances in representation learning for wireless channels, this work investigates the cost-benefit trade-offs of high-dimensional channel embeddings in practical systems. We benchmark multiple wireless representations: high-dimensional learned embeddings from a wireless foundation model, compact autoencoder-based representations with significantly lower dimensionality, and raw data baselines, evaluating their performance across diverse downstream tasks. We then systematically analyze data efficiency, noise robustness, and computational complexity, explicitly characterizing the resource overhead associated with high-dimensional embeddings. Beyond standard tasks such as line-of-sight/non-line-of-sight (LoS/NLoS) classification and beam selection, we introduce power allocation as a new downstream task. Our results reveal clear trade-offs: while high-dimensional embeddings can perform well in few-shot regimes for certain tasks, they incur substantial latency and parameter overhead. In contrast, compressed latent representations learned by autoencoders demonstrate improved noise robustness and more stable performance across tasks, while significantly reducing computational and transmission costs.

papers.title: Reliable AI Needs to Externalize Implicit Knowledge: A Human-AI Collaboration Perspective
papers.summary: Reliable AI Needs to Externalize Implicit Knowledge: A Human-AI Collaboration Perspective argues that the main reliability bottleneck is not missing sources, but unverified tacit competence: LLMs learn both explicit artifacts (papers, docs, databases) and implicit patterns (reasoning traces, debugging heuristics, intermediate steps), yet current safeguards can only check the explicit layer through retrieval, citations, or self-verification. The paper frames this as a verification-economics problem: humans routinely leave tacit know-how undocumented because extraction is too costly, but models absorb those same patterns indiscriminately, including biases, sycophancy, and unfaithful chain-of-thought behavior. Its central proposal is Knowledge Objects (KOs), structured, human-readable artifacts that package a claim or procedure with evidence, scope limits, provenance, and validation metadata so experts can inspect, endorse, correct, and reuse AI-learned know-how. Unlike RAG, RLHF, or agent memory, KOs make validation first-class and cumulative across users. The paper sketches CodeAssist-style software review and high-stakes healthcare/legal deployment as motivating contexts, pointing toward a new research program in validation-aware infrastructure, provenance standards, and reusable trust layers for agentic AI.
papers.abstract: This position paper argues that reliable AI requires infrastructure for human validation of implicit knowledge. AI learns from both explicit knowledge (papers, documentation, structured databases) and implicit knowledge (reasoning patterns, debugging processes, intermediate steps). Implicit knowledge remains unexternalized because documentation cost exceeds perceived value -- yet AI learns from it indiscriminately, acquiring both beneficial patterns and harmful biases. Current reliability methods can only verify explicit knowledge against sources, creating a fundamental gap: the most valuable AI capabilities (reasoning, judgment, intuition) are precisely those we cannot verify. We propose Knowledge Objects (KOs) -- structured artifacts that externalize implicit knowledge into forms humans can inspect, verify, and endorse. KOs transform verification economics: what was previously too costly to verify becomes feasible, enabling accumulated human validation to improve reliability over time.

papers.title: Enhancing Judgment Document Generation via Agentic Legal Information Collection and Rubric-Guided Optimization
papers.summary: Enhancing Judgment Document Generation via Agentic Legal Information Collection and Rubric-Guided Optimization tackles the hard legal drafting bottleneck in which a model must both retrieve the right statutes/precedents from noisy, verbose fact descriptions and then produce a judgment that is formally structured, citation-faithful, and logically valid. The core idea is Judge-R1, a two-stage system that replaces passive RAG with Agentic Legal Information Collection: a dynamic planning agent decomposes a case into multiple legal subqueries, retrieves from multiple sources through a dense retriever plus cross-encoder reranker, and then LLM-selects a compact evidence set, with retrieval itself optimized by GRPO on JuDGE gold evidence labels. On the generation side, the model is first warm-started by SFT and then aligned by rubric-guided GRPO using a legal reward that separately scores statutory/charge correctness, section-level professional structure, and reasoning quality inside <think> traces, penalizing repetition and shallow logic. Evaluated on the JuDGE benchmark with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B-Thinking, Judge-R1 improves evidence recall/precision, citation F1, and reasoning/judgment section quality over SFT, RAG+SFT, and prior Law Retriever baselines, pointing toward agentic, reward-shaped legal document generation.
papers.abstract: Automating the drafting of judgment documents is pivotal to judicial efficiency, yet it remains challenging due to the dual requirements of comprehensive retrieval of legal information and rigorous logical reasoning. Existing approaches, typically relying on standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Supervised Fine-Tuning, often suffer from insufficient evidence recall, hallucinated statutory references, and logically flawed legal reasoning. To bridge this gap, we propose Judge-R1, a unified framework designed to enhance LLM-based judgment document generation by jointly improving legal information collection and judgment document generation. First, we introduce Agentic Legal Information Collection, which employs a dynamic planning agent to retrieve precise statutes and precedents from multiple sources. Second, we implement Rubric-Guided Optimization, a reinforcement learning phase utilizing Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a comprehensive legal reward function to enforce adherence to judicial standards and reasoning logic. Extensive experiments on the JuDGE benchmark demonstrate that Judge-R1 significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both legal accuracy and generation quality.

papers.title: MIRA: A Score for Conditional Distribution Accuracy and Model Comparison
papers.summary: MIRA: A Score for Conditional Distribution Accuracy and Model Comparison tackles the hard validation setting where only one ground-truth draw y* is available for each conditioning input x*, so standard two-sample tests, SBC, or classifier-based diagnostics cannot reliably compare candidate posteriors p(y|x,M) against the true p(y|x,M*). The key move is to build randomized regions R from a candidate sample yr and random center c, exploit the fact that the candidate mass in R is Uniform[0,1], and derive a closed-form Mira statistic p(k|n) via Laplace’s rule of succession from the count n of candidate samples and the indicator k for whether y* falls in R. Averaging this over fiducials and regions yields a scalar score with a theoretical null mean of 2/3 and variance 1/18, plus a lower bound of 1/2, making the score interpretable for ranking models rather than just hypothesis testing. Unlike PQMass or TARP, MIRA is conditional, sample-based, analytic, and does not train a discriminator. It ranks toy posteriors, MNIST conditional image generators, and high-dimensional strong-lensing Bayesian models, detecting overconfidence, bias, and prior/noise misspecification while closely tracking Bayes-factor conclusions.
papers.abstract: We introduce Mira, a sample-based score for assessing the accuracy of a candidate conditional distribution using only joint samples from the true data-generating process. Relying on the principle that distributions coincide if they assign equal probability mass to all regions, we derive an analytic expression for the Mira statistic, whose average defines the Mira score. This formulation further allows us to compute theoretical reference values and uncertainty estimates when the candidate distribution matches the true one. This framework enables model comparison by quantifying the alignment between the conditional distribution of a candidate model and the true data generating process. Consequently, Mira enables Bayesian model comparison through direct posterior validation, bypassing the challenging evidence computation. We demonstrate its effectiveness across several toy problems and Bayesian inference tasks.

papers.title: Robust and Explainable Divide-and-Conquer Learning for Intrusion Detection
papers.summary: Robust and Explainable Divide-and-Conquer Learning for Intrusion Detection tackles the practical mismatch between raw network intrusion traffic and deployable models: payloads are high-dimensional, noisy, class-imbalanced, and too costly for memory- and compute-limited devices, yet global deep or ensemble classifiers remain brittle, opaque, and oversized. The paper’s SCAL (Subproblem Correlation-Aware Learning) method computes per-class fingerprints from lossless zstd compression lengths, builds a class-correlation matrix, and uses hierarchical clustering to split the label space only when correlated classes form beneficial subproblems; otherwise it falls back to a single global task. A three-tier cascade then routes instances through an instance distributor to lightweight local decision trees trained on focused subtasks, rather than forcing one monolithic detector. This is unusual relative to standard mixtures of experts or hierarchical classifiers because decomposition is explicitly correlation-driven and jointly evaluated on accuracy, cost, robustness, and explainability. On UNSW-NB15, CIC-IDS2017, IoT, and VNAT payload benchmarks, SCAL improves local F1 by up to 43.3% and shrinks model size by up to 257× versus global baselines, while also resisting Cube adversarial attacks and enabling aggressive post hoc pruning into compact human-readable trees.
papers.abstract: Machine learning-based intrusion detection requires complex models to capture patterns in high-dimensional, noisy, and class-imbalanced raw network traffic, yet deploying such models remains impractical on resource-constrained devices with limited processing power and memory. In this paper, we present a correlation-aware divide-and-conquer learning technique that decomposes a complex learning problem into smaller, more manageable subproblems. This enables lightweight models as simple as decision trees to be trained on focused subtasks, yielding up to 43.3% higher local accuracy and up to 257 times reduction in model size on real-world network intrusion detection datasets, while also improving adversarial robustness and explainability.

papers.title: Tenability and Weak Semantics: Modeling Non-uniform Defense -- Extended Version
papers.summary: Tenability and Weak Semantics: Modeling Non-uniform Defense -- Extended Version tackles a specific gap in Dung-style abstract argumentation: existing weak semantics still demand one uniform, conflict-free witness that defends an accepted set against all relevant attackers at once, which is too strong when the opponent’s inconsistent threats should be answered differently depending on the line of attack. The paper introduces tenability, a dialogue-based family of semantics built from monotone commitment games over finite conflict-free moves, with three variants: static tenability, strong tenability, and tenability. Their key move is to replace “there exists one stance that answers all attacks” with a strategic condition: for every consistent attack, there exists an adequate reply, possibly different for incompatible challenges. The paper isolates this on three benchmark patterns—self-defeating attack, floating assignment, and disjunctive reinstatement—where tenability diverges from cogency, weak admissibility, and weakly complete semantics. It proves strict implication chains, subset-closure, compatibility with grounded extensions, and finite-model complexity bounds of ΠP2-complete for static tenability and PSPACE-complete for tenability/strong tenability. This points toward argumentation semantics that model dialectical strategy, burden shifting, and non-uniform rebuttal in legal and evidential reasoning.
papers.abstract: In Dung-style abstract argumentation, various semantics capture notions of acceptability of arguments. The admissibility semantics capture the notion that an argument can be consistently defended from any potential counterargument. Weak semantics often relax the demands of admissibility by restricting which counterarguments must be taken seriously (e.g., discounting self-defeating or otherwise incoherent attacks). Many prominent proposals for weak semantics remain extension-based in a stronger sense. While these semantics discount attacks from arguments which are considered unreasonable, they still require a uniform defense against all reasonable arguments, even if they are collectively inconsistent. This uniformity can be too demanding when defensibility is inherently strategic, and thus the appropriate reply depends on the opponent's line of attack. We introduce tenability, a family of dialogue-based semantics that formalize when a designated argument (or a set of arguments) can be maintained in debate by a proponent against any conflict-free attack which the opponent may present. The approach is motivated by three natural benchmark patterns: self-defeating attack, floating assignment, and disjunctive reinstatement, on which tenability behaves differently from all weak semantics previously considered in the literature. We define three variants -- static tenability, tenability, and strong tenability -- via monotone commitment games over finite conflict-free moves, differing in the obligations imposed on the disputants. We establish the relative strength of these notions, prove implications and separations with previously studied weak semantics, and we analyze computational complexity on finite frameworks: deciding static tenability is $Π^P_2$-complete, while deciding tenability and strong tenability is PSPACE-complete.

papers.title: Towards Systematic Generalization for Power Grid Optimization Problems
papers.summary: Towards Systematic Generalization for Power Grid Optimization Problems attacks a core limitation in learning-based power-system optimization: AC Optimal Power Flow and Security-Constrained Unit Commitment are usually modeled separately, so networks relearn the same transmission topology while failing to transfer to coupled formulations. The paper proposes a shared heterogeneous graph transformer encoder over a typed bus-generator-load-shunt graph, paired with task-specific decoders: a static ACOPF head for voltage and generator setpoints, and a temporal SCUC head that injects 36-hour load profiles and time embeddings before Transformer-based commitment/dispatch prediction. Training mixes solver supervision with physics-informed penalties for AC power balance, thermal limits, ramping, and capacity constraints, balanced with GradNorm. The differentiating step is unsupervised UC-ACOPF adaptation: the frozen shared encoder is reused, decoder heads are updated only by a power-dispatch consensus loss plus physics penalties, with effective dispatch enforced by commitment-gated ACOPF output. Evaluated on OPFData and UnitCommitment.jl benchmarks across IEEE case-14/30/57/118, the model improves SCUC accuracy and ACOPF error, transfers zero-shot to unseen grid sizes, and runs 2–3 orders faster than Juniper, pointing toward foundation-style representations for heterogeneous grid optimization.
papers.abstract: AC Optimal Power Flow (ACOPF) and Security-Constrained Unit Commitment (SCUC) are fundamental optimization problems in power system operations. ACOPF serves as the physical backbone of grid simulation and real-time operation, enforcing nonlinear power flow feasibility and network limits, while SCUC represents a core market-level decision process that schedules generation under operational and security constraints. Although these problems share the same underlying transmission network and physical laws, they differ in decision variables and temporal coupling, and prior learning-based approaches address them in isolation, resulting in disjoint models and representations.We propose a learning framework that jointly models ACOPF and SCUC through a shared graph-based backbone that captures grid topology and physical interactions, coupled with task-specific decoders for static and temporal decision-making. Training includes solver supervision with physics-informed objectives to enforce AC feasibility and inter-temporal operational constraints. To evaluate generalization, we assess cross-case transfer on unseen grid topologies for ACOPF and SCUC without retraining, and systematic generalization on the UC-ACOPF problem using unsupervised, physics-based objectives and a power-dispatch consensus mechanism. Experiments across multiple grid scales demonstrate improved performance and transferability relative to existing learning-based baselines, indicating that the model can support learning across heterogeneous power system optimization problems.

papers.title: Large margin classifier with graph-based adaptive regularization
papers.summary: Large margin classifier with graph-based adaptive regularization attacks a specific weakness of Gabriel graph (GG) binary classifiers: their fixed outlier-removal threshold, set from classwise averages of the quality index Q(xi)=Veq/Vdt, can underfit noisy overlaps or worsen imbalance by discarding too many minority patterns. The paper replaces that single cutoff with per-class regularization hyperparameters hclass that scale the quality index before filtering, so the threshold becomes tunable by Bayesian optimization rather than hard-coded. This is unusual relative to nearby graph-based classifiers such as Chipclass, GMM-GG, and RBF-GG, which were designed to be parameter-free and autonomous; here the authors deliberately trade autonomy for a larger, optimizable solution space. The mechanism is interpreted geometrically: removing low-Q support edges prunes outliers, changes the set of structural support vectors, and can expand the margin while shifting the boundary toward the majority class when needed. Experiments on 16 benchmark UCI/KEEL datasets used 10-fold outer testing, 5-fold inner validation, and AUC optimization over 50 Bayesian trials. Flexible thresholds improved average Friedman rank and were statistically competitive with SVM, random forest, and kNN, pointing toward adaptive, graph-structured regularization for margin classifiers in noisy, imbalanced regimes.
papers.abstract: This paper introduces the use of per-class regularization hyperparameters in Gabriel graph-based binary classifiers. We demonstrate how the quality index used for regularization behaves both in the margin region and in the presence of outliers, and how incorporating this regularization flexibility can lead to solutions that effectively eliminate outliers while training the classifier. We also show how it can address class imbalance by generating higher and lower thresholds for the majority and minority classes, respectively. Thus, rather than having a single solution based on fixed thresholds, flexible thresholds expand the solution space and can be optimized through hyperparameter tuning algorithms. Friedman test shows that flexible thresholds are capable of improving Gabriel graph-based classifiers.

papers.title: Counting as a minimal probe of language model reliability
papers.summary: Counting as a minimal probe of language model reliability introduces Stable Counting Capacity (SCC), a fully mechanical assay that strips away semantics, factual knowledge, and tokenization quirks by asking a model to count repeated symbols until it fails. Across more than 100 model variants, the paper shows that exact counting breaks far below advertised context windows, revealing a hard boundary on procedural state maintenance rather than a smooth degradation in reasoning. The distinctive mechanism is an adaptive randomized ladder that estimates the longest sequence length over which the model can still return the exact integer with low error, exposing abrupt collapse into salient guess values once a finite set of count-like internal states is exhausted. Unlike benchmark-style evaluations, SCC is syntax sensitive, uses homogeneous item sequences rather than item-token proxies, and remains brittle even under extra test-time compute or reasoning-tuned variants. Mechanistic probing in open-weight models such as Gemma 3 27B-it and Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B finds linearly decodable progress coordinates and layer-specific activation patching effects, but no single stable counter. The paper argues that fluent benchmark success does not imply reliable rule execution, pointing toward architectures with explicit persistent variables, recurrence, and external memory.
papers.abstract: Large language models perform strongly on benchmarks in mathematical reasoning, coding and document analysis, suggesting a broad ability to follow instructions. However, it remains unclear whether such success reflects general logical competence, repeated application of learned procedures, or pattern matching that mimics rule execution. We investigate this question by introducing Stable Counting Capacity, an assay in which models count repeated symbols until failure. The assay removes knowledge dependencies, semantics and ambiguity from evaluation, avoids lexical and tokenization confounds, and provides a direct measure of procedural reliability beyond standard knowledge-based benchmarks. Here we show, across more than 100 model variants, that stable counting capacity remains far below advertised context limits. Model behavior is consistent neither with open-ended logic nor with stable application of a learned rule, but instead with use of a finite set of count-like internal states, analogous to counting on fingers. Once this resource is exhausted, the appearance of rule following disappears and exact execution collapses into guessing, even with additional test-time compute. These findings show that fluent performance in current language models does not guarantee general, reliable rule following.

papers.title: Conventional Commit Classification using Large Language Models and Prompt Engineering
papers.summary: Conventional Commit Classification using Large Language Models and Prompt Engineering tackles the practical problem of labeling unstructured Git commit changes into Conventional Commits types, which downstream tools need for changelogs and semantic versioning, without paying the cost of training and maintaining a dedicated classifier. Instead of fine-tuning on labels, the paper tests a training-free pipeline that feeds code diffs, not just commit text, into open-source LLMs under three prompt regimes: zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought. The distinctive move is to treat prompt design itself as the main variable, adding representative in-context examples and decision-priority rules derived from the commit specification, then comparing Mistral-7B-Instruct, LLaMA-3-8B, and DeepSeek-R1-32B on a balanced 3,200-commit dataset mined from InfluxDB. The results show few-shot prompting is consistently best overall, chain-of-thought adds no benefit for this structured classification task, and larger models—especially DeepSeek-R1-32B—perform most reliably. The paper points toward software-engineering workflows that rely on promptable foundation models rather than retrained classifiers, especially for evolving repository analytics and commit-aware automation.
papers.abstract: Conventional commits provide a structured format for writing commit messages, which improves readability, software maintenance, and enables automation tools such as changelog generators and semantic versioning systems. Existing approaches to conventional commit classification typically rely on ML/DL models trained on large labeled datasets. In this paper, we investigated a training-free alternative by leveraging large language models (LLMs) through prompt engineering. Rather than building a task-specific classifier, we evaluate three prompting strategies, such as zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought, across three open-source LLMs of varying scale: Mistral-7B-Instruct, LLaMA-3-8B, and DeepSeek-R1-32B. Classification is performed directly on code diffs extracted from a balanced dataset of 3,200 commits mined from the InfluxDB repository, without any model fine-tuning. Our results show that few-shot prompting consistently achieves the highest accuracy, while chain-of-thought prompting does not yield additional gains for this classification task. Among the evaluated models, DeepSeek-R1-32B achieves the strongest overall performance, suggesting that model scale plays a meaningful role in conventional commit classification. These findings provide practical guidance for researchers and practitioners seeking to automate commit classification without the overhead of curating and maintaining labeled training data.

papers.title: A Multimodal Dataset for Visually Grounded Ambiguity in Machine Translation
papers.summary: A Multimodal Dataset for Visually Grounded Ambiguity in Machine Translation addresses a specific weakness in multimodal MT evaluation: existing ambiguity benchmarks often include cases solvable from text alone, use VQA-like formats, or rely on coarse metrics that cannot tell whether an ambiguous span was truly resolved with the image. The paper introduces VIDA (Visually-Dependent Ambiguity), a 2,500-instance English–Chinese dataset built through a three-stage curation pipeline that filters image-caption mismatch, dual-LLM ambiguity verification, GPT-4o disambiguated translation generation, and full human validation; it is split into VIDA-Base, VIDA-Sent, and VIDA-CollN to cover word-level, sentence-level, and collective-noun ambiguities that require visual evidence. For evaluation, the authors propose Disambiguation-Centric Metrics, training a Qwen3-8B LLM-as-judge classifier to score span-level correctness with Disambi-Term and Disambi-Inst. They test Qwen2.5-VL-7B and InternVL3-8B under vanilla inference, SFT, and a six-step CoT-SFT that explicitly grounds, checks, and locally refines ambiguous phrases. Results show SFT boosts overall translation, but CoT-SFT more reliably improves disambiguation, especially on out-of-distribution subsets, pointing toward reasoning-supervised, visually grounded MT rather than surface-fluent translation alone.
papers.abstract: Ambiguity resolution is a key challenge in multimodal machine translation (MMT), where models must genuinely leverage visual input to map an ambiguous expression to its intended meaning. Although prior work has proposed disambiguation-oriented benchmarks that provide supportive evidence for the role of vision, we observe substantial issues in data quality and a mismatch with translation scenarios. Moreover, existing ambiguity-oriented evaluations are not well suited to broader ambiguity types in open-ended translation. To address these limitations, we present VIDA (Visually-Dependent Ambiguity), a dataset of 2,500 carefully curated instances in which resolving an annotated ambiguous source span requires visual evidence. We further propose Disambiguation-Centric Metrics that use an LLM-as-a-judge classifier to verify whether annotated ambiguous expressions are resolved correctly at the span level. Experiments with two state-of-the-art Large Vision Language Models under vanilla inference, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and our chain-of-thought SFT (CoT-SFT) show that while SFT improves overall translation quality, CoT-SFT yields more consistent gains in disambiguation accuracy, especially on out-of-distribution subsets, indicating a stronger generalization for resolving diverse ambiguity types.

papers.title: VILAS: A VLA-Integrated Low-cost Architecture with Soft Grasping for Robotic Manipulation
papers.summary: VILAS: A VLA-Integrated Low-cost Architecture with Soft Grasping for Robotic Manipulation attacks the bottleneck that current vision-language-action (VLA) robots need expensive integrated platforms and explicit force sensing to handle fragile objects reliably. The paper builds a fully modular $8k system around a Fairino FR5 collaborative arm, Jodell RG52-50 electric gripper, GELLO teleoperation leader, and synchronized RealSense D455/D405 cameras, tied together by a ZMQ control layer that supports teleop, dataset logging, fine-tuning, and deployment in one pipeline. Its differentiating mechanism is a kirigami-based PEBA soft extension mounted on the parallel gripper: the cut geometry is tuned to buckle predictably under compression, widening the grasp envelope and cushioning grapes without force-torque sensors or complex impedance control. Using one identical teleoperated dataset of 100 episodes, the authors fine-tune pi0, pi0.5, and GR00T N1.6, then test them on sequential grape grasping at 20 Hz with action chunking. GR00T N1.6 yields the strongest multi-grasp robustness (58%), while pi0.5 leads single-grasp success (84%), showing that accessible hardware can support real VLA deployment and that model choice changes long-horizon stability.
papers.abstract: We present VILAS, a fully low-cost, modular robotic manipulation platform designed to support end-to-end vision-language-action (VLA) policy learning and deployment on accessible hardware. The system integrates a Fairino FR5 collaborative arm, a Jodell RG52-50 electric gripper, and a dual-camera perception module, unified through a ZMQ-based communication architecture that seamlessly coordinates teleoperation, data collection, and policy deployment within a single framework. To enable safe manipulation of fragile objects without relying on explicit force sensing, we design a kirigami-based soft compliant gripper extension that induces predictable deformation under compressive loading, providing gentle and repeatable contact with delicate targets. We deploy and evaluate three state-of-the-art VLA models on the VILAS platform: pi_0, pi_0.5, and GR00T N1.6. All models are fine-tuned from publicly released pretrained checkpoints using an identical demonstration dataset collected via our teleoperation pipeline. Experiments on a grape grasping task validate the effectiveness of the proposed system, confirming that capable manipulation policies can be successfully trained and deployed on low-cost modular hardware. Our results further provide practical insights into the deployment characteristics of current VLA models in real-world settings.

papers.title: What Single-Prompt Accuracy Misses: A Multi-Variant Reliability Audit of Language Models
papers.summary: What Single-Prompt Accuracy Misses: A Multi-Variant Reliability Audit of Language Models attacks the mismatch between leaderboard accuracy and deployment reliability in 1–8B open-weight language models. Rather than scoring one canonical prompt, the authors audit 15 models—focusing on 10 instruct models—across five classification and reasoning benchmarks (SST-2, MNLI, AG News, ARC-Challenge, MMLU-Pro) under five prompt variants each, measuring accuracy, token-probability calibration, verbal-confidence calibration, verbal parse rate, and prompt-perturbation spread at every model×dataset×variant cell. The distinctive contribution is to treat the evaluation pipeline itself as a failure source: switching Expected Calibration Error from raw first-token probability to label-set-normalized probability shifts per-cell calibration by 0.149 on average, and pairing chain-of-thought prompting with a first-character evaluator on ARC-Challenge collapses apparent accuracy by 72–88% before regex re-parsing or constrained decoding recover 93.8% and 102.7% of the lost performance. The paper also finds systematically overconfident verbal self-assessment on MMLU-Pro and no reliable monotonic link between parameter count and prompt robustness. It points toward reliability auditing as a deployment-readiness profile, not a single score.
papers.abstract: Single-prompt accuracy is the dominant way to benchmark language models, but it can miss reliability failures that matter. We evaluate a 15-model open-weight corpus, with the main reliability analyses focused on 10 instruct models across five classification and reasoning benchmarks under five prompt variants each, measuring accuracy, token-probability calibration, verbal-confidence calibration, verbal parse rate, and prompt-perturbation spread for every (model x dataset x variant) cell. We find three broad results. First, evaluation design can materially change the conclusion. Switching Expected Calibration Error (ECE) token from a raw to a label-set-normalised definition changes per-cell calibration by a mean absolute 0.149. More strikingly, pairing a chain-of-thought prompt with a first-character evaluator on ARC-Challenge reduces apparent accuracy by 72-88% across all five primary models; two independent repair procedures recover 93.8% and 102.7% of the lost performance, indicating an evaluator-side rather than model-side failure. Second, confidence signals are fragile. On MMLU-Pro, every primary model verbally reports confidence substantially above both its accuracy and its token-probability confidence on the same rows, and verbal parse rate can collapse for a single model on a single prompt variant. Third, prompt robustness does not track parameter count reliably. Across 10 instruct models, the correlation between model size and prompt-perturbation spread ranges from -0.244 to 0.474 across benchmarks. Taken together, these results show that reliability conclusions for small language models depend not only on the model being evaluated, but also on the evaluation pipeline used to measure it. We argue that calibration definitions, evaluator logic, verbal parseability, and prompt robustness should be reported explicitly when making reliability claims.

papers.title: TIJERE: A Novel Threat Intelligence Joint Extraction Model Based on Analyst Expert Knowledge
papers.summary: TIJERE: A Novel Threat Intelligence Joint Extraction Model Based on Analyst Expert Knowledge tackles the brittle extraction of cyber entities and relations from unstructured threat-intelligence reports, where overlapping triples, ambiguous security jargon, and feature confusion make standard joint models unreliable for knowledge-graph construction. The key idea is to recast joint NER+RE as multisequence labeling representation (MSLR): for each valid entity pair, TIJERE generates a separate labeled copy of the sentence, then injects analyst-derived expert domain features—entity type and entity mask—so the model can distinguish “HackOrg–Tool” or “HackOrg–Time” interactions instead of relying on surface tokens alone. It further uses SecureBERT+, a cybersecurity-finetuned contextual encoder, followed by BiGRU, CRF for NER, and pooled pair representations for relation classification. Unlike nearby cyber-JE work that still depends on relation-matching rules, static dictionaries, or pipeline-like separation, TIJERE keeps the tasks unified while explicitly encoding domain ontology. Evaluated on DNRTI-JE, the first publicly available jointly labeled cybersecurity dataset (13 entity types, 15 relations, 6,592 sentences), it reports F1 above 0.93 for NER and 0.98 for RE, suggesting a data-centric direction for high-precision automated CTI extraction.
papers.abstract: The extraction of entities and relationships from threat intelligence reports into structured formats, such as cybersecurity knowledge graphs, is essential for automated threat analysis, detection, and mitigation. However, existing joint extraction methods struggle with feature confusion, language ambiguity, noise propagation, and overlapping relations, resulting in low accuracy and poor model performance. This paper presents TIJERE, an innovative joint entity and relation extraction framework that formulates joint extraction as a multisequence labeling representation (MSLR) problem. Specifically, separate sequences are generated for each entity pair. Unlike prior tagging schemes, MSLR integrates expert domain features to enrich positional, contextual, and semantic representations of entities, thereby enhancing feature distinction and classification accuracy. Additionally, TIJERE reduces language ambiguity and enhances domain-specific generalization by leveraging SecureBERT+, a contextual language model fine-tuned on cybersecurity text. This improves both named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE). This paper also introduces DNRTI-JE, the first publicly available jointly labeled dataset for cybersecurity entity and RE, filling a crucial gap in cyber threat intelligence automation. Empirical evaluations on the curated DNRTI-JE dataset demonstrate that TIJERE achieves state-of-the-art performance, with F1-scores exceeding 0.93 for NER and 0.98 for RE, outperforming existing methods. Together, TIJERE and the standardized benchmarking DNRTI-JE dataset enable high-performance cybersecurity intelligence extraction, with transferable applications in healthcare, finance, and bioinformatics.

papers.title: Bringing Order to Asynchronous SGD: Towards Optimality under Data-Dependent Delays with Momentum
papers.summary: Bringing Order to Asynchronous SGD: Towards Optimality under Data-Dependent Delays with Momentum studies distributed asynchronous SGD when computation time correlates with sample complexity, so stale gradients systematically overrepresent easy examples. Rather than downweighting or filtering delayed updates, the paper assigns each delayed gradient the exact momentum coefficient β(1−β)^τ it would have in a delay-free trajectory, yielding an ordered-momentum update and a virtual-momentum analysis whose bias is only a transient O(M) missing-update term. In the smooth nonconvex case, this constant-step method attains the first optimal data-dependent-delay rate O(LΔM/T + σ√(LΔ/T)) without the extra bounded-gradient term seen in prior Lipschitz-based analyses. For smooth convex objectives, the authors plug the same ordering principle into µ2-SGD, combining STORM-style gradient correction with Anytime-GD parameter averaging and a double-momentum schedule to get the first optimal bound under data-dependent delays: O(Dσ + D^2σL/√T + D^2LM/T). The result points toward tuning-light, delay-aware optimization that preserves hard examples instead of suppressing them.
papers.abstract: Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (SGD) enables scalable distributed training but suffers from gradient staleness. Existing mitigation strategies, such as delay-adaptive learning rates and staleness-aware filtering, typically attenuate or discard delayed gradients, introducing systematic bias: updates from simpler or faster-to-process samples are overrepresented, while gradients from more complex samples are delayed or suppressed. In contrast, prior approaches to data-dependent delays rely on a Lipschitz assumption that yields suboptimal rates or leave the smooth, convex case unaddressed. We propose a momentum-based asynchronous framework designed to preserve information from delayed gradients while mitigating the effects of staleness. We establish the first optimal convergence rates for data-dependent delays in both convex and non-convex smooth setups, providing a new result for asynchronous optimization under standard assumptions. Additionally, we derive robust learning-rate schedules that simplify hyperparameter tuning in practice.

papers.title: NeuroViz: Real-time Interactive Visualization of Forward and Backward Passes in Neural Network Training
papers.summary: NeuroViz: Real-time Interactive Visualization of Forward and Backward Passes in Neural Network Training attacks the interpretability gap in learning feedforward networks, where students and practitioners can see architectures and loss curves but not the moment-by-moment coupling between activations, gradients, and parameter updates inside an epoch. The paper’s core idea is a browser-based React/TensorFlow.js visualizer that lets users configure architecture, activation function, learning rate, and dataset, then animates training with pulse-based forward passes in green and backward error flow in red, only updating a weight after the corresponding gradient reaches that edge. This step-aligned rendering makes pre-update versus post-update states explicit, adds per-neuron equations that update live, and exposes hoverable weighted-sum formulas, which is more granular than TensorFlow Playground, Netron, CNN Explainer, or GAN/transformer-focused tools.
