In a within-subject study with 31 computer-science and data-science graduate students using Iris and Diabetes tasks, NeuroViz scored the highest SUS (80.97), the lowest workload, and the highest trust (4.20), while over 70% reported substantially greater transparency. The work points toward educational and debugging tools that make training mechanics legible rather than merely visual.
papers.abstract: Training neural networks is difficult to interpret, particularly for newcomers. We introduce NeuroViz, an interactive visualization tool that supports real-time exploration of fully connected neural network training. Users can configure network architecture, activation functions, learning rates, and datasets, then observe activations, weight updates, and loss progression. NeuroViz visualizes weight changes in direct correspondence with activation signals in both forward and backward passes, enabling users to distinguish pre- and post-update states within individual epochs and view dynamically updating per-neuron equations. We conduct a comparative user study with 31 participants against six established visualization tools and we achieved the highest usability score (SUS 80.97, in the 'excellent' range), with mean rankings of 2.47 for clarity and 2.23 for usefulness (lower is better). Over 70% of participants reported that the visualizations substantially increased their perception of neural network training transparency. The implemented instance is accessible at https://neuroviz.org.

papers.title: Optimization of CV-QKD Under Practical Constraints
papers.summary: Optimization of CV-QKD Under Practical Constraints tackles a deployment bottleneck in Gaussian-modulated continuous-variable quantum key distribution: secure key rate collapses when transmitter pulse-shaping and receiver matched filters have only finite FIR length, DAC/ADC quantization is coarse, bandwidth is limited, and the mean photon number is not jointly tuned. The paper formulates the physical link as an environment and uses REINFORCE policy-gradient reinforcement learning to jointly optimize Tx/Rx FIR coefficients and the photon number, maximizing analytically computed SKR through effective transmittance and excess-noise terms that explicitly include intersymbol interference, DAC noise, and ADC noise. Unlike neighboring CV-QKD optimization work that adjusts only one side or depends on gradient-based calibration, this approach is end-to-end, gradient-free, and therefore closer to experimental control loops. Simulations use 4 samples/symbol, a 0.75R low-pass front end, 0.2 dB/km fiber loss, and finite-bit converters. The learned filters suppress mode mismatch and ISI, yielding near-optimal performance with roughly 20–40 taps and 10–11-bit converters, and extending secure distance from about 60 km to nearly 100 km.
papers.abstract: Using reinforcement learning, we optimize for practical hardware constraints, including limited FIR filter taps at the transmitter and receiver, mean photon number and finite DAC/ADC resolution. Under these realistic conditions, the proposed approach achieves significant performance improvements.

papers.title: Principles and Guidelines for Randomized Controlled Trials in AI Evaluation
papers.summary: Principles and Guidelines for Randomized Controlled Trials in AI Evaluation argues that AI “uplift” studies lack a shared experimental standard, especially when researchers randomize human participants to AI access and then overclaim from noisy, non-comparable task scores. The paper repurposes the Shadish-Cook-Campbell framework for construct, internal, external, and statistical conclusion validity, then adds a fifth principle for transparency, repeatability, and verification adapted from TOP. These five principles are operationalized into 33 concrete guidelines with rationales, implementation instructions, and evidence bases, turning an abstract methods manifesto into a checklist for study design, review, and field-wide standard setting. Its differentiating move is to center human performance and causal inference rather than benchmark outputs, while explicitly addressing AI-specific threats such as model version drift, human-AI interaction dynamics, contamination and spillover, unequal AI literacy, and subgroup heterogeneity. The framework recommends pre-registration, pinned model versions, cluster-aware randomization, practical-significance analysis, and a graded disclosure/share/verification/repeatability ladder. It points AI evaluation toward a more clinical-trial-like science of deployment evidence, where claims about assistance, harm, and generalization are disciplined by causal and reproducibility norms.
papers.abstract: This work establishes a foundational framework for standardizing AI evaluation RCTs (sometimes called human uplift studies). Drawing on established experimental practices from disciplines with established RCT traditions, including software engineering, economics, clinical and health sciences, and psychology, we adopt the (Shadish et al., 2002) four-validity framework and extend it with a fifth principle on transparency, repeatability, and verification adapted from the Transparency and Openness Promotion (TOP) Guidelines (Center for Open Science, 2025). We operationalize all five principles into 33 guidelines adapted for AI evaluation RCT contexts, expressed as requirements with rationales, implementation instructions, and evidence bases. We position the principles and guidelines as serving three key roles for AI evaluation RCTs: a design tool for planning studies, an evaluation rubric for assessing existing work, and a blueprint for standard setting as the field converges on norms. Our framework extends prior work by centering evaluation on human performance rather than model output alone, formalizing causal inference through RCT methodology for AI contexts, integrating heterogeneity analysis and practical significance assessment, implementing a graded transparency and repeatability framework, and addressing AI-specific challenges including model versioning, human-AI interaction dynamics, contamination and spillover effects, and equitable impact assessment.

papers.title: Methods, Data, and Conceptual Change: Reflections from Two Quantitative Diachronic Case Studies
papers.summary: Methods, Data, and Conceptual Change: Reflections from Two Quantitative Diachronic Case Studies tackles a core historical-linguistics problem: whether semantic change is something a corpus “contains” or something a method makes observable, given noisy, uneven historical data. Rather than proposing a new algorithm, the paper juxtaposes two worked cases to expose method-data fit. First, quad-based concept modelling from the Linguistic DNA project is applied to EEBO-TCP Early Modern English (c.1470s–1690s; ~765M words), where heavy orthographic variation and genre heterogeneity make syntactic analysis fragile but allow stable discursive “constellations” of four-lemma co-occurrence to emerge, as in the contrasting association networks of liberty and freedom. Second, SynFlow is run on a 1750–1799 Royal Society Corpus subset (~351k tokens) parsed with Stanza, using dependency-slot distributions to track concepts such as air, water, and acid through the Chemical Revolution. The unusual contribution is methodological reflexivity: the authors show that lexical-frequency approaches miss concept structure, while parsing-based models depend on corpus regularity. The paper points toward transparent, context-sensitive diachronic NLP that treats dataset structure as constitutive of semantic evidence.
papers.abstract: This discussion paper reflects on how quantitative approaches to historical linguistics interact with dataset properties. Drawing on two worked examples, we examine English data using quad-based concept modelling of Early Modern English discourse in EEBO-TCP (c. 1470s-1690s; 765M words) alongside SynFlow analysis of scientific writing in Royal Society Corpus 6.0.4 (1750-1799; drawn from a 78.6M-token open corpus). Through parallel comparison, the paper explores how each approach operationalises concepts, the data assumptions they entail, and the diachronic interpretations they support. We argue that comparative methodological reflection clarifies the limits of purely lexical, frequency-based approaches and highlights how dataset structure shapes the kinds of semantic change that quantitative methods can reliably detect.

papers.title: Observability Conditions and Filter Design for Visual Pose Estimation via Dual Quaternions
papers.summary: Observability Conditions and Filter Design for Visual Pose Estimation via Dual Quaternions tackles the failure mode of classical PnP in 6-DOF target tracking: deterministic pose solvers are brittle to noise/outliers and cannot bridge occlusions or measurement dropouts. The paper formulates camera-target relative pose and velocity directly on SE(3) with dual quaternions, then performs a Lie-algebraic nonlinear observability analysis for two sensing models, relative position vectors and unit-vector/bearing measurements. Its distinguishing move is to derive local weak observability from the observability codistribution rank, recovering the P3P collinear-feature degeneracy as a control-theoretic result rather than a purely geometric one, and showing that three non-collinear features suffice when a prior estimate is available. On the estimation side, it builds a dual-quaternion Lie group unscented Kalman filter that propagates full relative rigid-body dynamics without cooperative sensing or slowly varying motion assumptions, unlike earlier EKF/particle-filter formulations. In simulated flyby/interception tracking with OpenCV PnP baselines and staged occlusions, the DQ-UKF maintained covariance through two-marker unobservable intervals and achieved smoother, lower-error pose estimates. The work points toward observability-driven marker placement and manifold-aware sequential pose estimators for vision, SLAM, and visual-inertial navigation.
papers.abstract: This paper presents a dual quaternion framework for 6-DOF visual target tracking that addresses key limitations of perspective-n-point (P$n$P) solvers: sensitivity to noise and outliers, and inability to propagate estimates through measurement dropouts. A nonlinear observability analysis is performed using a Lie algebraic approach, deriving sufficient conditions for local observability under two sensing modalities: relative position vector and unit vector measurements. For the unit vector case, the classical collinear feature point degeneracy of the perspective-three-point problem is recovered through rank analysis of the observability codistribution matrix, providing a control-theoretic interpretation of a previously geometric result. A dual quaternion Lie group unscented Kalman filter is then developed, directly modeling relative dynamics without assumptions about cooperative measurements or slowly-varying motion. Simulations demonstrate improved pose estimation accuracy and robustness to occlusions compared to an off-the-shelf P$n$P solver. Results are broadly applicable to visual-inertial navigation, simultaneous localization and mapping, and P$n$P solver development.

papers.title: DR-SNE: Density-Regularized Stochastic Neighbor Embedding
papers.summary: DR-SNE: Density-Regularized Stochastic Neighbor Embedding targets a specific failure mode of t-SNE-style visualizations: they preserve neighbor relations but warp how probability mass is distributed, so dense regions inflate or collapse and cluster sizes become misleading. The paper reframes dimensionality reduction as jointly aligning conditional structure and relative density structure, then adds a density regularizer to SNE based on normalized kNN log-density estimates in both the original and embedded spaces. Unlike DensMAP and DenSNE, which constrain local scale or bandwidth consistency, DR-SNE directly matches normalized density statistics, making the objective scale-invariant and more interpretable as a penalty on local volume distortion. Experiments span Digits, Fashion-MNIST, MNIST, PBMC single-cell RNA-seq, Shuttle, and a synthetic spiral, with evaluation via trustworthiness plus a density-correlation metric under matched trustworthiness. DR-SNE consistently achieves the best density preservation and competitive neighborhood fidelity, and improves anomaly detection on density-sensitive regimes, especially with LOF/kNN detectors. The work points toward statistical, density-aware embeddings that treat visualization as distribution alignment rather than geometry alone.
papers.abstract: Dimensionality reduction methods such as t-SNE are designed to preserve local neighborhood structure but do not explicitly account for how probability mass is distributed, often leading to distortions of data density. We reformulate dimensionality reduction as the joint alignment of two components: (i) conditional structure, capturing local relationships, and (ii) relative density structure, captured via local density statistics. Based on this perspective, we introduce Density-Regularized SNE (DR-SNE), which augments the stochastic neighbor embedding objective with a density regularization term derived from normalized log-density estimates. Unlike prior approaches such as DensMAP and DenSNE, which rely on local scale consistency, DR-SNE directly aligns normalized density estimates, providing a simple and scale-invariant mechanism for preserving relative density variations. Empirically, DR-SNE improves density preservation while maintaining competitive neighborhood fidelity, and yields gains on density-sensitive tasks such as anomaly detection across multiple datasets. These results suggest that incorporating density information complements geometry-focused objectives in dimensionality reduction.

papers.title: Coopetition-Gym v1: A Formally Grounded Platform for Mixed-Motive Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning under Strategic Coopetition
papers.summary: Coopetition-Gym v1: A Formally Grounded Platform for Mixed-Motive Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning under Strategic Coopetition tackles the benchmark gap between fully cooperative and zero-sum MARL by isolating the harder mixed-motive regime where agents must simultaneously enlarge and divide value. The platform organizes 20 continuous-action environments into four mechanism classes—interdependence/complementarity, trust/reputation, collective action/loyalty, and sequential reciprocity—each inherited from a prior formal report and each defined by closed-form payoffs plus calibrated interdependence matrices. Its differentiating move is a two-layer design that separates environment payoff from the learning reward, exposing private, integrated, and cooperative reward modes so reward-type ablation can test whether algorithm rankings are robust to reward mutuality rather than just task mechanics. The suite is Gymnasium- and PettingZoo-compatible, ships 126 reference policies including learning algorithms, game-theoretic oracles, heuristics, and constant-action baselines, and includes four historically calibrated case studies (Samsung-Sony LCD, Renault-Nissan, Apache HTTP Server, Apple iOS App Store) validated at 98.3%, 81.7%, 86.7%, and 87.3%. A 25,708-run training corpus and 1,116-run behavioral audit corpus underpin the reference study, pointing toward formally grounded, reproducible, mechanism-aware evaluation of coopetitive MARL.
papers.abstract: We present Coopetition-Gym v1, a benchmark platform for mixed-motive multi-agent reinforcement learning under strategic coopetition. The platform comprises twenty environments organized into four mechanism classes that correspond to four foundational technical reports: interdependence and complementarity (arXiv:2510.18802), trust and reputation dynamics (arXiv:2510.24909), collective action and loyalty (arXiv:2601.16237), and sequential interaction and reciprocity (arXiv:2604.01240). Each environment carries a closed-form payoff structure and a calibrated interdependence matrix derived from the corresponding report. Every environment exposes a parameterized reward layer configurable across three structurally distinct modes (private, integrated, cooperative). This separation of payoff from reward enables reward-type ablation, the platform's principal methodological apparatus. Four of the twenty environments are calibrated against historically documented coopetitive relationships and reproduce their outcomes at 98.3, 81.7, 86.7, and 87.3 percent on the validation rubric (Samsung-Sony LCD, Renault-Nissan Alliance, Apache HTTP Server, Apple iOS App Store). The platform exposes Gymnasium, PettingZoo Parallel, and PettingZoo AEC interfaces and ships 126 reference algorithms: 16 learning algorithms, 7 game-theoretic oracles, 2 heuristic baselines, and 101 constant-action policies. A reference experimental study trained the 16 learning algorithms on every environment under every reward configuration with seven random seeds, producing a 25,708-run training corpus and a 1,116-run behavioral audit corpus, both released under CC-BY-4.0 with Croissant 1.0 metadata. Coopetition-Gym v1 is the first platform to combine continuous-action mixed-motive environments, parameterized reward mutuality, calibrated interdependence coefficients, game-theoretic oracle baselines, and validated case studies.

papers.title: Pair2Score: Pairwise-to-Absolute Transfer for LLM-Based Essay Scoring
papers.summary: Pair2Score: Pairwise-to-Absolute Transfer for LLM-Based Essay Scoring tackles the mismatch between easy-to-collect comparative judgments and the absolute rubric scores required by Automated Essay Scoring. The paper’s core idea is a two-stage transfer pipeline on Meta LLaMA-3.2-1B with LoRA: Stage 1 trains a directional Siamese ranker on essay pairs synthesized from absolute trait labels, using an antisymmetric utility difference and pairwise logistic loss; Stage 2 reuses that knowledge for direct score regression via either warm-start initialization or embedding-fusion, rather than prompting. What differentiates it from nearby ranking-plus-regression work is the explicit study of relative-to-absolute transfer as a design space, with controlled variation in pairwise-stage duration and pair-set size under a co-rotated fold/seed five-fold protocol. Evaluation on the Feedback Prize English Language Learning corpus for grammar, vocabulary, and syntax shows the best transfer variant improves QWK over an absolute-only baseline on all three traits, but gains are fragile: one-epoch pairwise training transfers more reliably than longer ranking, and transfer configuration matters more than pairwise accuracy alone. The paper points toward staged comparative supervision for rubric-aligned LLM scoring.
papers.abstract: Many scoring applications require absolute predictions, while pairwise comparisons can provide a simpler learning objective. We present Pair2Score, a two-stage learning framework that transfers pairwise comparisons into absolute scoring with parameter-efficient LLaMA adaptation. Stage 1 trains a directional Siamese ranker on pairwise comparisons derived from absolute trait labels; Stage 2 trains an absolute predictor using configurable transfer strategies (warm-start and embedding-fusion variants). We evaluate on rubric-aligned Automated Essay Scoring (AES) traits (grammar, vocabulary, syntax) under a five-fold protocol that co-rotates held-out fold and random seed. At the trait level, the best-performing transfer variant improves quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) over an absolute-only baseline for all three traits. However, not all transfer configurations help: a one-epoch pairwise stage transfers more reliably than extended pairwise training, and transfer configuration -- not just the inclusion of a pairwise stage -- determines whether downstream scoring benefits.

papers.title: Weight Clipping for Robust Conformal Inference under Unbounded Covariate Shifts
papers.summary: Weight Clipping for Robust Conformal Inference under Unbounded Covariate Shifts tackles the failure mode of weighted conformal prediction when train/test covariate shift yields an unbounded or badly learned density ratio, so a few calibration points dominate the threshold and produce severe undercoverage. The core mechanism is clipped least-squares importance fitting (CLISF): instead of estimating an unconstrained ratio, it optimizes LSIF over a clipped class WB with weights capped at B, trading small bias for much lower variance and provable generalization. A distinctive step is that the clipping bias ∆B is not ignored or post-hoc trimmed; it is estimated from data and then used to inflate the nominal coverage target in clipped weighted conformal prediction (CWCP), restoring both expected and dataset-conditional marginal coverage. The theory gives finite-sample, two-sided guarantees with sample complexity controlled by B and Rademacher complexity rather than higher moments of the true ratio, a key limitation in prior work. Experiments on synthetic heavy-tailed shifts, iWildCam OOD camera-trap classification, and Communities and Crime regression show tighter, more stable coverage than standard WCP under extreme shifts. The paper points toward robust uncertainty quantification for heavy-tailed domain adaptation, especially when exact importance weights are intrinsically unstable.
papers.abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) provides powerful, distribution-free prediction sets, but its guarantees rely on the exchangeability of training and test data, which is often violated in practice due to covariate shifts. While weighted conformal prediction (WCP) is designed to handle such shifts, it can suffer from significant undercoverage when the density ratio between the distributions is unbounded and/or must be learned. This is because of both overfitting in learning the density ratio, and high variance in estimating the nonconformity score threshold. To address this, we introduce clipped least-squares importance fitting (CLISF) as a reduced-variance method for density ratio estimation. Specifically, we show that density ratios learned using CLISF, when plugged into WCP, have bounded expected undercoverage. Furthermore, we show that the undercoverage can be corrected by running WCP with a slightly inflated coverage target; crucially, we are able to estimate the required level of inflation from the data. We provide the first theoretical guarantees for weight clipping in conformal inference, achieving dataset-conditional coverage with a sample complexity that does not blow up with the higher moments of the true density ratio -- a key limitation of prior work. We verify our results on real-world benchmarks and synthetic data.

papers.title: Enhanced LLM Reasoning by Optimizing Reward Functions with Search-Driven Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Enhanced LLM Reasoning by Optimizing Reward Functions with Search-Driven Reinforcement Learning attacks the bottleneck that reasoning-focused RL for LLMs is often limited less by the optimizer than by hand-designed reward code. Keeping the Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct base model fixed, the paper treats the reward specification itself as the search target: Kimi K2 generates candidate Python rewards, each is sandbox-validated, then screened with 500-step GRPO+LoRA runs on GSM8K, and ranked by test F1 before a textual summary feeds the next generation round. Across five rounds, 50 candidates are explored and the search increasingly surfaces process-oriented rewards such as thinking_steps_count, thinking_has_calc, and numeric_reasoning_depth, which score reasoning structure rather than re-checking final answers already covered by three protected base rewards (match_format_exactly, match_format_approximately, check_answer). The best ensemble of discovered rewards reaches F1 = 0.795 and 0.660 accuracy, beating base-rewards-only GRPO by 0.19 F1; a random 5-reward control collapses to F1 = 0.047, showing the ranked-feedback loop is essential. The paper points toward automated, auditable reward discovery as a practical alternative to manual reward engineering for reasoning RL.
papers.abstract: Mathematical reasoning is a key benchmark for large language models. Reinforcement learning is a standard post-training mechanism for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models, yet performance remains sensitive to the design of the reward function that drives policy optimization. This paper introduces a search-driven framework that treats the reward specification itself as an object of optimization. The setting of interest is one in which the base model is held fixed and the reward specification is the primary remaining design lever. Candidate reward functions are generated by a frontier language model, validated automatically, screened through 500-step Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) training runs on a Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct base model with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), and ranked by F1 on the GSM8K test set. Ranked summaries from prior rounds are then fed back into the next round of generation. Over five rounds, the search produces 50 candidate rewards. The mean F1 rises from 0.596 in Round 1 to 0.632 in Round 5, and the top individual reward reaches F1 = 0.787. Seven ensemble configurations of top-ranked rewards are evaluated. The best ensemble achieves F1 = 0.795 (95% bootstrap CI [0.756, 0.832]) and accuracy 0.660 [0.635, 0.686], a 0.19 absolute F1 gain over a base-rewards-only GRPO baseline (F1 = 0.609). Pairwise McNemar tests with Bonferroni correction show all five-or-more-reward configurations are statistically indistinguishable at α = 0.05/21. A three-seed re-training of the best ensemble yields F1 of 0.785. A randomly drawn 5-reward control collapses to F1 = 0.047, which shows that the ranked-feedback loop, not the additive signal of having more rewards, drives the gain.

papers.title: Cripping AI: Reimagining AI Through Lived Disability Experiences
papers.summary: Cripping AI: Reimagining AI Through Lived Disability Experiences reframes the core problem in disability-related AI from “making systems accessible” to exposing how ableist assumptions are baked into task definitions, labels, benchmarks, and deployment norms. Drawing on crip theory, the paper introduces cripping AI as a guiding framework with three linked tenets: expose disability politics, honor cripistemologies, and respect crip labor. Rather than treating disabled users as after-the-fact evaluators or data sources, it argues that disabled bodyminds generate legitimate knowledge about language, perception, and communication, and that access is an ongoing collective practice with real labor costs. The paper differentiates itself from nearby accessible-AI work by challenging the single-ground-truth paradigm and the default authority of able-bodied/sighted/hearing annotators. It concretely applies the framework to three cases: deafness and sign language AI, blindness and visual assistive AI, and stuttering and speech AI, highlighting issues such as interpreter-centric benchmarks, sighted “ground truth” captions, and fluency-optimized ASR that erases disfluency. The result points toward disability-centered AI pipelines, community-led evaluation, and broader justice coalitions across the AI ecosystem.
papers.abstract: Drawing on crip theory, this paper proposes cripping AI as a guiding framework to center lived disability experiences in AI research and development. Moving beyond calls to make AI "accessible" to people with disabilities, cripping AI seeks to: (1) reveal and dismantle ableist assumptions embedded in how AI is imagined, designed, and evaluated; (2) center disabled ways of knowing (i.e., cripistemologies); (3) respect disabled labor in co-creating accessible practices. We demonstrate how to apply our framework with three cases: deafness and sign language AI, blindness and visual assistive AI, and stuttering and speech AI. We end by outlining three directions for future work, including cripping AI with diverse human bodyminds, across the entire AI pipeline and ecosystem, and in collaboration with other justice-oriented AI efforts.

papers.title: EditPropBench: Measuring Factual Edit Propagation in Scientific Manuscripts
papers.summary: EditPropBench: Measuring Factual Edit Propagation in Scientific Manuscripts targets a revision failure mode that ordinary diff checks miss: when a local fact changes in an ML/NLP-style manuscript, qualitative downstream claims become stale and must be cascaded through the text. The benchmark pairs each synthetic manuscript with a targeted edit and a controlled fact graph that labels direct-target sentences, required downstream updates, and protected unrelated text, then evaluates revisions under three protocols—direct edit, patch edit, and plan-then-edit. Its headline metric, Edit-Ripple Adherence (ERA), is built to score implicit propagation rather than just local substitution, with a hard stratum of implicit/free-form dependencies that simple string replacement cannot solve. On 461 hard cells, five LLM editors span ERA 0.148–0.705, and even the best system misses about 30% of required cascade updates; plan-then-edit regresses relative to direct editing on hard cells. The paper validates the scorer with adversarial probes and human calibration, and an audit of 199 recent arXiv cs.CL benchmark/dataset papers finds fact-dependent qualitative claims in 37.2%. The work points toward cascade-aware scientific writing tools with explicit dependency tracking and post-edit consistency checking.
papers.abstract: Local factual edits in scientific manuscripts often create non-local revision obligations. If a dataset changes from 215 to 80 documents, claims such as 'medium-scale' or 'a few hundred items' may also become stale, even though they do not repeat the edited number. We introduce EditPropBench, a benchmark for measuring whether LLM editors propagate factual edits through dependent manuscript claims. Each item contains an ML/NLP-style synthetic manuscript, a targeted edit, and a controlled fact graph with sentence-level labels for direct targets, required downstream updates, and protected unrelated text. EditPropBench provides a controlled manuscript-level benchmark with sentence-level dependency supervision, three editing protocols, adversarial metric probes, stress-test variants, and a metric suite centered on Edit-Ripple Adherence (ERA). On the hard implicit/free-form stratum, five LLM editing systems span ERA 0.148--0.705; even the strongest misses roughly 30% of required cascade updates. A mixed-stratum stress test shows that LLMs retain a positive advantage over deterministic substitution baselines when easy substitution-solvable cases are included. Finally, an audit of recent arXiv cs.CL benchmark and dataset papers finds fact-dependent qualitative claims in 37.2% of papers. EditPropBench shows that current LLM editors can repair many implicit consequences of factual edits, but reliable scientific revision still requires cascade-aware checking.

papers.title: GETA-3DGS: Automatic Joint Structured Pruning and Quantization for 3D Gaussian Splatting
papers.summary: GETA-3DGS: Automatic Joint Structured Pruning and Quantization for 3D Gaussian Splatting tackles the deployment bottleneck that trained 3DGS scenes still occupy hundreds of MB to GB, while prior compressors like HAC++, FlexGaussian, and LP-3DGS rely on separate pruning/quantization stages and scene-specific thresholds. The paper adapts GETA into a 3DGS-native, end-to-end white-box optimizer by treating each Gaussian primitive as the prunable group and each attribute (position, scale, rotation, opacity, SH DC/AC bands) as typed quantization sub-nodes in a quantization-aware dependency graph. Its differentiator is a render-aware saliency score that combines transmittance-weighted contribution, screen-space gradient, and pixel coverage, plus a heterogeneous mixed-precision scheme with projected partial saliency-guided descent and a quality floor constraint. Evaluated on Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending, it operates directly on raw Gaussians rather than anchor representations, yielding about 4.5–5× storage reduction over Vanilla 3DGS and much better PSNR than sequential prune-then-quantize. The key implication is that 3DGS compression is moving toward automatic rate–distortion co-design with explicit budgets, while entropy coding remains a downstream, composable layer.
papers.abstract: 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) is a state-of-the-art representation for real-time photorealistic novel-view synthesis, yet a single high-fidelity scene typically occupies hundreds of megabytes to several gigabytes, exceeding the budgets of mobile, immersive, and volumetric video platforms. Existing 3DGS compression methods (e.g., HAC++, FlexGaussian, LP-3DGS) treat pruning, quantization, and entropy coding as separate stages and rely on hand-tuned heuristics (opacity thresholds, fixed bit-widths, SH truncation), limiting cross-scene generalization and preventing users from specifying a target rate or quality budget. We propose GETA-3DGS, to our knowledge the first end-to-end automatic joint structured pruning and quantization framework for 3DGS. Building on GETA for joint pruning-quantization of deep networks, we contribute: (i) a 3DGS-aware quantization-aware dependency graph (QADG) treating each Gaussian primitive as a group with five attribute sub-nodes and degree-aware SH sub-nodes; (ii) a render-aware saliency fusing transmittance-weighted contribution, screen-space gradient, and pixel coverage into a Gaussian-level importance score; and (iii) a heterogeneous per-attribute mixed-precision scheme co-optimized with structural sparsity under a projected partial saliency-guided (PPSG) descent guarantee. On Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending, GETA-3DGS operates directly on raw Gaussian primitives rather than a post-hoc anchor representation, delivering ~5x storage reduction over Vanilla 3DGS with no per-scene thresholds. Bit-width policy is the dominant rate-distortion lever: a uniform 6-bit cap costs up to -6.74 dB on view-dependent scenes versus our heterogeneous allocation, matching an information-theoretic reverse-water-filling analysis we develop. GETA-3DGS is complementary to existing codecs: entropy coding (HAC++, CompGS) is downstream, so the two can be composed.

papers.title: Model Spec Midtraining: Improving How Alignment Training Generalizes
papers.summary: Model Spec Midtraining: Improving How Alignment Training Generalizes attacks the core weakness of standard alignment fine-tuning: demonstrations of “correct” behavior often underspecify the intended generalization, so models learn shallow compliance that breaks OOD. The paper’s key idea is to insert a midtraining phase after pretraining but before alignment fine-tuning, where the model is trained on synthetic documents that discuss and unpack its Model Spec/Constitution, teaching the “what” and “why” of desired behavior as a prior that later demonstration data can reinforce. Unlike nearby alignment-pretraining or “nice AI stories” work, the synthetic corpus is explicitly spec-derived and designed to shape which latent value or policy the same downstream demonstrations will resolve toward. The authors show this on controlled value-generalization tasks: identical cheese-preference fine-tuning leads to pro-America versus pro-affordability OOD behavior depending on the midtrained spec. On safety, MSM with a self-preservation/goal-guarding spec cuts agentic misalignment on Qwen2.5-32B from 68% to 5% and on Qwen3-32B from 54% to 7%, outperforming deliberative alignment baselines. The paper points toward “Model Spec science,” where spec wording, specificity, and value explanations become empirical levers for trustworthy generalization.
papers.abstract: Some frontier AI developers aim to align language models to a Model Spec or Constitution that describes the intended model behavior. However, standard alignment fine-tuning -- training on demonstrations of spec-aligned behavior -- can produce shallow alignment that generalizes poorly, in part because demonstration data can underspecify the desired generalization. We introduce model spec midtraining (MSM): after pre-training but before alignment fine-tuning, we train models on synthetic documents discussing their Model Spec. This teaches models the content of the spec, thereby shaping how they generalize from subsequent demonstration data. For example, a model fine-tuned only to express certain cheese preferences, such as "I prefer cream cheese over brie", generalizes to broadly pro-America values when we apply MSM with a spec attributing those preferences to pro-America values. Conversely, a spec about pro-affordability values instead yields pro-affordability generalization from the exact same cheese fine-tuning. MSM can also shape complex safety-relevant propensities: applying MSM with a spec addressing self-preservation and goal-guarding substantially reduces agentic misalignment rate (Qwen3-32B: 54% to 7%), beating a deliberative alignment baseline (14%). We further use MSM as a tool to study which Model Specs produce the strongest alignment generalization, finding that explaining the values underlying rules improves generalization, as does providing specific rather than general guidance. Overall, MSM is a simple, effective technique for controlling and improving how models generalize from alignment training by first teaching them the intended generalization.

papers.title: Cross-Language Learning within Arabic Script for Low-Resource HTR
papers.summary: Cross-Language Learning within Arabic Script for Low-Resource HTR tackles line-level handwritten text recognition when target-language labels are extremely scarce and Arabic-script datasets are fragmented across related languages. The paper trains a single CRNN+CTC recognizer on the union of Arabic (KHATT), Urdu (NUST-UHWR, UNHD), and Persian (PHTD) while restricting the target set to K=100, 500, or 1000 labeled lines, then compares this joint training against single-script baselines under identical preprocessing, augmentation, and decoding. What distinguishes the work is not just multilingual pooling, but a character-level transfer audit that separates shared Arabic-script symbols from language-specific ones and links gains to overlap structure and dataset diversity. The experiments show consistent CER reductions across all three primary targets, with especially strong results on low-diversity PHTD and an additional UNHD Urdu benchmark; for example, Persian drops to 9.99 CER and UNHD from 17.20 to 14.45. The analysis reveals that improvements concentrate on low-frequency characters shared across scripts, while unique characters are mostly unchanged or slightly harmed, pointing toward transfer methods that exploit script-family commonality rather than uniform cross-language sharing.
papers.abstract: Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) under limited labeled data remains a challenging problem, particularly for Arabic-script languages. Although modern sequence-based recognizers perform well in high-resource settings, their accuracy degrades sharply as training data becomes scarce. Arabic-script languages share a common writing system with substantial character overlap, motivating cross-script training as a strategy to mitigate data scarcity. We performed experiments on Arabic, Urdu, and Persian scripts and achieved improvements over single-script baselines (new SotA especially for low-resource settings). A key finding of our experiments is that cross-script transfer is largely driven by script-level overlap rather than uniform accuracy improvements. Through a statistical character-level analysis we show that gains are structurally concentrated on characters shared across scripts, while language-specific characters exhibit limited or negative transfer. These findings provide insight into transfer dynamics in low-resource script families. Detailed results include: We conduct a controlled line-level study of cross-script joint training for Arabic-script HTR under low-resource regimes (number of samples K \in 100, 500, 1000 labeled lines) on Arabic (KHATT), Urdu (NUST-UHWR), and Persian (PHTD). A CRNN model is trained on the union of multiple related Arabic-script datasets and evaluated on individual target languages. On Persian (PHTD), joint training achieves a Character Error Rate (CER) of 9.99, surpassing previously reported results despite not using the full available training data. On an Urdu dataset (UNHD), joint training reduces CER from 17.20 to 14.45. Code and data splits are released to ensure reproducibility.1

papers.title: NORA: A Harness-Engineered Autonomous Research Agent for End-to-End Spatial Data Science
papers.summary: NORA: A Harness-Engineered Autonomous Research Agent for End-to-End Spatial Data Science addresses a core bottleneck in GeoAI: general-purpose autonomous research agents can draft papers, but they do not know how to choose spatially valid methods, acquire geospatial data reproducibly, or preserve provenance across a long research lifecycle. NORA (“Night Owl Research Agent”) is a skills-first, multi-agent system built around 21 Markdown-defined workflow skills, 9 specialist sub-agents, and custom MCP servers, with two standout domain units: a spatial analysis skill that encodes decision logic for exploratory spatial analysis, regression diagnostics, OLS-to-spatial lag/error/GWR/MGWR selection, and spatial cross-validation; and a spatial data download skill that ranks authoritative sources, handles API/login access, validates files, and writes a DATA_MANIFEST for reproducibility. The paper’s differentiator is “harness engineering”: lifecycle hooks, safety gates, generator–evaluator separation, human checkpoints, and structured state persistence that keep the agent auditable and resumable. In IJGIS-oriented case studies, including a street-view first-floor-elevation project, 6 domain specialists and 3 LLM reviewers scored outputs on novelty, rigor, and related criteria, with NORA outperforming general-purpose configurations on efficiency and research quality, pointing toward discipline-specific autonomous science systems rather than domain-agnostic copilots.
papers.abstract: The automation of scientific research workflows has emerged as a transformative frontier in artificial intelligence, yet existing autonomous research agents remain largely domain-agnostic, lacking the specialized reasoning, method selection, and data acquisition capabilities required for rigorous spatial data science. This paper introduces NORA (Night Owl Research Agent), a harness-engineered, multi-agent autonomous research system purpose-built for GIScience and spatial data science. NORA orchestrates the complete research lifecycle through a skills-first architecture comprising 21 domain-specialized workflow skills, 9 specialist sub-agents, and custom Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Central to the system's design are two novel domain-specialized skills: a spatial analysis skill unit that encodes decision frameworks for exploratory spatial data analysis, spatial regression, and diagnostics; and a spatial data download skill that supports reproducible acquisition from authoritative geospatial data sources. We formalize the concept of harness engineering for scientific research agents, demonstrating how lifecycle hooks, safety gates, generator-evaluator separation, human-in-the-loop, and state persistence ensure reliable and reproducible autonomous research. We evaluate NORA through case studies by 6 domain specialists and 3 LLM reviewers across seven dimensions (novelty, quality, rigor, etc). Results demonstrate that domain-specialized harness engineering substantially improves the efficiency and quality of research output compared to general-purpose agent configurations.

papers.title: SignMAE: Segmentation-Driven Self-Supervised Learning for Sign Language Recognition
papers.summary: SignMAE: Segmentation-Driven Self-Supervised Learning for Sign Language Recognition attacks the bottleneck that isolated sign language recognition depends on tiny, rapidly changing hand configurations, yet most backbones are pretrained on generic action videos and waste capacity on background or body regions that do not disambiguate glosses. The core idea is a segmentation-guided masked autoencoding scheme that changes what the model is allowed to hide and reconstruct: instead of random tube masking over whole frames, SignMAE uses human parsing and pose cues to identify hand and arm patches, then applies directional spatio-temporal masking tailored to one-handed versus two-handed signs, plus temporal masking of redundant middle frames. A parallel keypoint-heatmap stream is pretrained with the same hand-centric objective, while a separate tube-masked video stream preserves coarse global motion; both encoders are later fine-tuned independently and fused with frozen-weight cross-attention. Implemented with ViT-L backbones and Sapiens keypoints, the method is evaluated on WLASL, NMFs-CSL, and Slovo, where it reaches state-of-the-art Top-1 accuracy while using fewer frames and modalities than heavier multimodal baselines. The paper points toward sign-specific self-supervision that explicitly aligns masking, reconstruction, and anatomy.
papers.abstract: Subtle hand differences make sign language recognition challenging, yet many existing methods rely on encoders pretrained on generic action datasets that poorly capture such fine-grained cues. We propose a self-supervised pretraining method for sign language recognition that uses segmentation-based masking to adapt to the presence and motion of key body parts, rather than treating hand poses as static visual tokens. The resulting mask-and-reconstruct objective improves fine-grained sign representation learning. On WLASL, NMFs-CSL, and Slovo, our encoder achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving per-instance and per-class Top-1 accuracy while using fewer input frames and modalities than comparable encoders.

papers.title: From Spherical to Gaussian: A Comparative Analysis of Point Cloud Cropping Strategies in Large-Scale 3D Environments
papers.summary: From Spherical to Gaussian: A Comparative Analysis of Point Cloud Cropping Strategies in Large-Scale 3D Environments attacks the bottleneck that billion-point scenes must be chopped into subclouds for 3D segmentation, where standard spherical crops impose hard cutoffs and erase far-context cues needed to separate large structures. Instead of changing the network, the paper replaces the crop boundary with distance-weighted sampling rules—exponential, Gaussian, and linear probabilities—so subclouds can span a much larger geometric extent while keeping roughly the same point budget. It also standardizes center selection with occupancy-grid coverage and averages overlapping predictions back into the full cloud. The study compares these crops against spherical baselines on two very different architectures, OA-CNNs and Point Transformer V3, trained from scratch on SemanticBridge, S3DIS full areas, and Paris-Lille-3D/Lille2 using only xyz or xyz+color. The distinctive finding is that input presentation alone can shift state of the art: Gaussian cropping yields >80% mIoU on SemanticBridge, linear cropping reaches a new SOTA above 84% on Paris-Lille-3D, and both alternative crops help especially outdoors, while indoor S3DIS benefits are smaller. The paper points toward crop design as a controllable, architecture-agnostic lever for large-scale 3D understanding.
papers.abstract: Large-scale 3D point clouds can consist of billions of points. Even after downsampling, these point clouds are too large for modern 3D neural networks. In order to develop a semantic understanding of the scene, the point clouds are divided into smaller subclouds that can be processed. Typically, this division is done using spherical crops, resulting in a loss of surrounding geometric context. To address this issue, we propose alternative methods that produce subclouds with larger crop sizes while maintaining a similar number of points. Specifically, we compare exponential, Gaussian, and linear cropping methods with the spherical method. We evaluated two 3D deep learning model architectures using multiple indoor and outdoor environment datasets. Our results demonstrate that altering the cropping strategy can enhance model performance, especially for large-scale outdoor scenes, yielding new state-of-the-art results. Code is available at https://github.com/mvg-inatech/point_cloud_cropping

papers.title: Stochastic Modeling of Human-Machine Authentication Channels under Partial Information Leakage
papers.summary: Stochastic Modeling of Human-Machine Authentication Channels under Partial Information Leakage tackles the brittle assumption that PIN authentication is either fully secure or fully broken, instead treating smartphone/wearable PIN entry in IoT and cyber-physical systems as a noisy human-IoT communication channel whose reliability degrades under shoulder-surfing and side-channel exposure. The core mechanism is a context-conditioned probabilistic inference model: missing PIN digits are treated as latent variables, inferred from the observed digits via smoothed conditional frequency distributions with Laplace smoothing and fallback global priors when contexts are sparse. Unlike standard sequential learners, it does not explicitly model hidden-state transitions or emissions; instead it approximates cross-position dependencies directly from digit-context statistics, making it interpretable and data-efficient for fixed-length four-digit PINs. Using over one million real-world PINs extracted from RockYou, the paper evaluates single-, double-, and triple-digit leakage scenarios and reports position-dependent reliability loss. The method reaches 55.31% accuracy for one missing digit and 12.12% for three missing digits, while outperforming decision trees, random forests, Naive Bayes, and an HMM baseline on precision, recall, F1, and rank-based attack success, pointing toward probabilistic security modeling of partial compromise rather than binary authentication analysis.
papers.abstract: Reliable and secure human-machine communication is fundamental to IoT and cyber-physical ecosystems, where smartphones and wearables commonly serve as authentication controllers. PIN-based authentication can be viewed as a low-bandwidth communication channel through which users transmit numeric credentials under practical constraints. However, conventional evaluations adopt a binary view of security-treating such channels as either fully secure or fully compromised-thereby overlooking the progressive reliability degradation caused by partial information leakage in real-world IoT settings. In this paper, we model the PIN entry process as a stochastic human-IoT communication system and propose a context-conditioned probabilistic inference framework to quantify reliability loss and Quality-of-Service degradation under partial symbol exposure. The proposed approach treats missing digits as latent variables and estimates them using smoothed conditional probability distributions with fallback priors. Unlike traditional sequential models that assume contiguous positional dependencies, the method does not explicitly parameterize hidden-state transitions or emissions; instead, it performs context-driven probabilistic inference to approximate latent dependencies across digit positions. Using over one million real-world four-digit PIN samples, we evaluate single-, double-, and triple-digit leakage scenarios and derive position-dependent reliability metrics. The proposed model achieves up to 55.31% prediction accuracy for one missing digit and 12.12% for three missing digits, while consistently outperforming a standard sequence-model baseline and classical machine learning models in terms of precision, recall, and F1-score. These results formalize PIN entry as a noisy human--IoT communication channel and demonstrate substantial reliability degradation under realistic partial exposure conditions.

papers.title: Bridging the Gap Between Average and Discounted TD Learning
papers.summary: Bridging the Gap Between Average and Discounted TD Learning tackles the core theoretical mismatch in average-reward policy evaluation: the Bellman operator is not contractive, so standard TD analyses from discounted RL break down and prior guarantees either exclude the tabular case, converge only to sample-path-dependent sets, or incur dimension and quartic condition-number penalties. The paper reframes the average-reward projected Bellman equation as a constrained optimization problem enforcing the steady-state centering constraint, then derives a new stochastic TD update that samples from two independent Markovian trajectories to resolve the double-sampling problem. Its differentiating technical move is a gradient-splitting analysis, showing the expected update behaves like SGD on a quadratic objective, which yields last-iterate convergence to a unique, deterministic fixed point for both tabular and linear function approximation without explicit dimension factors. A GTD-inspired single-chain variant replaces the second trajectory with an auxiliary estimate of the feature mean. Finite-sample bounds match discounted TD in form, with quadratic rather than quartic scaling in the relevant condition number. Experiments on 15 tabular Gym, MO-Gymnasium, and Gridworld tasks show strong empirical performance against prior average-reward TD methods, pointing toward a cleaner theory of average-reward RL.
papers.abstract: The analysis of Temporal Difference (TD) learning in the average-reward setting faces notable theoretical difficulties because the Bellman operator is not contractive with respect to any norm. This complicates standard analyses of stochastic updates that are effective in discounted settings. Although a considerable body of literature addresses these challenges, existing theoretical approaches come with limitations. We introduce a novel algorithm designed explicitly for policy evaluation in the average-reward setting, utilizing sampling from two Markovian trajectories. Our proposed method overcomes previous limitations by guaranteeing convergence to the unique solution of a properly defined projected Bellman equation. Notably, and in contrast to earlier work, our convergence analysis is uniformly applicable to both linear function approximation and tabular settings and does not involve explicit dimension-dependent terms in its convergence bounds. These results align with what is known to hold in the discounted setting. Furthermore, our algorithm achieves improved dependence on the problem's condition number, reducing the sample complexity from quartic, as in prior literature, to quadratic scaling, and thus matching the efficiency seen in the discounted setting.

papers.title: Sharpness-Aware Pretraining Mitigates Catastrophic Forgetting
papers.summary: Sharpness-Aware Pretraining Mitigates Catastrophic Forgetting studies a gap in LLM training practice: pretraining recipes are usually chosen to minimize base-model loss, yet later post-training, SFT, or quantization can erase pretrained abilities. The paper argues that checkpoint geometry, not just benchmark score, determines how much capability survives downstream updates, and tests whether flattening the loss landscape during pretraining improves this “learning-forgetting” tradeoff. It compares three sharpness-reducing interventions—Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), larger peak learning rates, and shorter learning-rate annealing—motivated by a Hessian/Taylor analysis showing that forgetting scales with curvature along fine-tuning directions. Across OLMo-style models from 20M to 150M parameters, trained on 4B–192B tokens and adapted on StarCoder, GSM8K, StackMathQA, Tülu-3, and MusicPile, these recipes often yield worse or similar pretraining loss but up to 80% less forgetting at matched fine-tuning loss. At scale, a short SAM mid-training phase on OLMo-2-1B cuts forgetting by 31% after MetaMath post-training and 40% after 4-bit quantization. The work points toward pretraining for downstream robustness, not just base-model quality.
papers.abstract: Pretraining optimizers are tuned to produce the strongest possible base model, on the assumption that a stronger starting point yields a stronger model after subsequent changes like post-training and quantization. This overlooks the geometry of the base model which controls how much of the base model's capabilities survive subsequent parameter updates. We study three pretraining optimization approaches that bias optimization toward flatter minima: Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), large learning rates, and shortened learning rate annealing periods. Across model sizes ranging from 20M to 150M parameters, we find that these interventions consistently improve downstream performance after post-training on five common datasets with up to 80% less forgetting. These principles hold at scale: a short SAM mid-training phase applied to an existing OLMo-2-1B checkpoint reduces forgetting by 31% after MetaMath post-training and by 40% after 4-bit quantization.

papers.title: The Dynamic Gist-Based Memory Model (DGMM): A Memory-Centric Architecture for Artificial Intelligence
papers.summary: The Dynamic Gist-Based Memory Model (DGMM): A Memory-Centric Architecture for Artificial Intelligence attacks the mismatch between today’s parameter-centric LLMs and the requirements of persistent memory, temporal grounding, provenance, and inspectable recall. Instead of encoding experience implicitly in weights or transient retrieval context, DGMM stores experience as an evolving graph-structured episodic-semantic memory with a fixed relational grammar over Concept, Element, Time, Interaction, and Source nodes. New experience enters only through additive ingestion, while selective cue-conditioned recall constructs a transient working-memory subgraph and analysis happens strictly post-recall, so meaning is deferred until contextually driven interpretation rather than frozen at storage time. The differentiator is architectural, not algorithmic: DGMM formalizes regime boundaries, identity constraints, provenance preservation, and recall axioms, including bounded subgraph recall and locality of cue-conditioned surprise. Compared with HippoRAG, Zep, and temporal knowledge graphs, it makes provenance first-class and allows multiple perspectives to coexist without overwriting or retraining. The paper’s main claim is that such explicit, persistent memory enables interpretable, temporally grounded, context-aware AI, and points toward a research program where memory structure itself becomes the object of reasoning, auditing, and drift analysis.
papers.abstract: Contemporary artificial intelligence systems achieve strong performance through large-scale parameterization, retrieval augmentation, and training on extensive static corpora. Despite these advances, they continue to face limitations in persistent memory, temporal grounding, provenance, and interpretability. These challenges are especially pronounced in large language models, where experience is encoded implicitly in fixed parameters, limiting the ability to preserve, inspect, and reinterpret past interactions over time. This paper establishes a memory-centric architectural foundation for artificial intelligence in which experience is represented explicitly and persistently to support temporal grounding, provenance, and interpretability. It proposes an alternative to parameter-centric approaches by treating memory as a first-class, structured substrate for reasoning. We introduce the Dynamic Gist-Based Memory Model (DGMM), an architecture in which experience is represented as an evolving, graph-structured episodic-semantic memory. DGMM encodes experience as interconnected conceptual structures grounded in time, source, and interaction context, and defines selective, cue-conditioned recall as the mechanism for constructing working memory. A formal schema and architectural invariants are provided based on additive memory growth and recall-conditioned interpretation. The results specify properties of DGMM, including episodic persistence, locality of cue-conditioned surprise, and contextual variability without structural modification of stored memory. DGMM provides a coherent architectural theory in which memory is explicit and persistent, supporting evolving interpretation without retraining and enabling interpretable, context-aware, and temporally grounded AI systems.

papers.title: Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network I
papers.summary: Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network I studies the spectral rigidity of deep residual Jacobian chains as products of near-identity matrices, asking how layerwise singular spectra can drift when end-to-end transport remains budgeted. The paper quotient-projects each full-rank factor through A^⊤A into SPD(d), then Cartan-reduces to ordered eigenvalues and a normalized top-radial coordinate, with exact power-law spectra identified as a trace-normalized one-parameter Cartan orbit G_d(α). Its unusual move is to treat that orbit simultaneously as a Gibbs family over ranks, a Fisher-information line, and a Bures–Wasserstein curve, yielding exact metric identities and closed-form entropy/KL formulas rather than only asymptotic scaling laws. The main theorem is a slack-aware margin inequality: interface geometric-mean radial amplitude, measurable non-backtracking slack, and signed residual variation bound displacement of the fitted exponent chart; a separate full-spectrum bound adds Bures/Hellinger chart error. Empirically, static-weight exponent profiles are used as coordinate diagnostics. The result points toward verifiable geometry-aware theory for residual and transformer depth, where stability, effective rank, and power-law tails are tracked by measurable transport budgets rather than heuristic spectral summaries.
papers.abstract: Deep residual architectures are modeled as products of near-identity Jacobians. This paper proves deterministic quotient-geometric estimates for singular spectra of Frobenius-normalized layer factors, emphasizing a normalized top-radial Cartan coordinate and fitted power-law chart. Full-rank factors are mapped from $\mathrm{GL}(d)$ to the positive cone by $A\mapsto A^\top A$, then to ordered eigenvalue data. Under Frobenius normalization, exact power-law spectra form a trace-normalized Cartan orbit. This orbit is a Gibbs family on ranks, a Fisher information line, and a Bures--Wasserstein curve with line element $d/4$ times Fisher information. The main rigidity theorem is a slack-aware margin inequality: interface radial amplitude, non-backtracking slack, and signed residual variation control displacement of the fitted Cartan coordinate. In the exact-chart zero-slack case, a depth-$L$ budget gives exponent drift of order $(\log M)/L$; generally, slack and residual increments augment the bound. We separate scalar top-radial from full-Cartan spectral control, which also needs Bures/Hellinger residual variation. We prove approximate-power-law and metric-chart versions, converse lower bounds, Fisher--KL/Bures action estimates, and near-identity expansions for normalized residual chains. Near-identity results verify transport budgets; chart quality remains measurable. Effective rank is a spectral-energy quantile, giving finite-width power-law tail bounds and robust rank-window transition estimates. Empirical static-weight exponent profiles serve as diagnostics; full verification also requires interface budgets, slacks, and residuals for the same operator chain.

papers.title: Detecting Adversarial Data via Provable Adversarial Noise Amplification
papers.summary: Detecting Adversarial Data via Provable Adversarial Noise Amplification tackles the previously empirical claim that adversarial perturbations grow unevenly across deep-network layers and can therefore be detected. The paper formalizes this as an adversarial noise amplification theorem: if each activation is injective with a positive lower Lipschitz bound, the product of layerwise minimum singular-value terms exceeds one, and the attack induces nonzero first-layer change, then the final-layer impact provably exceeds the early-layer impact. To make that signal stronger in practice, the authors replace ReLU with Leaky ReLU and train with a custom spectral regularizer that maximizes log minimum singular values while preserving classification accuracy. They then build JPEG-based Amplification Detector (JAD), a lightweight inference-time detector that compares internal activation shifts between an input and its JPEG-sanitized version and flags samples whose amplification ratio is high. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet with WideResNet, ResNet-50, Inception-v3, ViT, and DeiT show strong amplification for adversarial but not natural corruptions, near-perfect AUROC against PGD, BIM, VNI, VMI, AutoAttack, and a purpose-built adaptive attack, and far better performance than standalone JPEG or prior detectors.
papers.abstract: The nonuniform and growing impact of adversarial noise across the layers of deep neural networks has been used in the literature, without a formal mathematical justification, to detect adversarial inputs and improve robustness. In this work, we study this phenomenon in detail and present a formal adversarial noise amplification theorem. We specify a set of sufficient conditions under which the adversarial noise amplification is mathematically guaranteed. Based on theoretical observations, we propose a novel training methodology with a custom spectral loss function and a specific architectural design to enhance the amplification signal for detecting adversarial data. Finally, we introduce a new, lightweight detection mechanism that leverages the enhanced amplification signal and operates entirely at inference time. To validate our approach, we demonstrate the detector's efficacy against both state-of-the-art attacks and a purpose-built adaptive attack, confirming that enhanced amplification can serve as a robust and reliable signal for adversarial defense.

papers.title: Adversarial Update-Based Federated Unlearning for Poisoned Model Recovery
papers.summary: Adversarial Update-Based Federated Unlearning for Poisoned Model Recovery tackles the post-detection problem in federated learning: once malicious clients are identified and removed, the global model is still contaminated, and full retraining is too expensive. The paper’s FAUN method keeps only a short window of the detected attackers’ recent updates, averages each client’s history to approximate its harmful direction, then uses projected-gradient adversarial optimization on a small clean proxy dataset to amplify the worst-case poisoning effect. Those adversarially constructed updates are aggregated into an elimination vector and subtracted from the poisoned model for only a few unlearning rounds before the server switches back to ordinary benign-client fine-tuning. What distinguishes FAUN from retraining-, rollback-, or trajectory-reconstruction-based unlearning is that it recasts recovery as min-max adversarial update synthesis rather than numerical undoing, requiring little history and no full replay. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and AG News with CNN, ResNet-18, and FastText under IID/non-IID Trim and backdoor attacks show recovery close to retraining, with attack success rates driven near zero and strong efficiency gains. This points toward lightweight, server-side adversarial unlearning as a practical defense for poisoned federated systems.
papers.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is vulnerable to poisoning attacks, where malicious clients upload manipulated updates to degrade the performance of the global model. Although detection methods can identify and remove malicious clients, the model remains affected. Retraining from scratch is effective but costly, and existing unlearning methods remain unsatisfactory in both effectiveness and efficiency. We propose Federated Adversarial Unlearning (FAUN), a lightweight framework that retains only a short window of malicious clients' updates and employs adversarial optimization on a proxy dataset to derive updates that eliminate malicious directions. Applying these updates for a few unlearning rounds, followed by benign fine-tuning, enables fast removal of malicious effects and stable recovery. Experiments on three canonical datasets show that FAUN achieves recovery comparable to retraining while requiring far fewer rounds and reduces attack success rates to near zero, confirming FAUN successfully eliminates the contributions of unlearned clients.

papers.title: Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network II
papers.summary: Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network II targets a very specific bottleneck in residual networks: when singular subspaces are transported across adjacent Jacobians, how can one tell—deterministically, not heuristically—whether the induced channel-incidence pattern in physical coordinates survives truncation, perturbation, and reindexing? Building on Cartan-coordinate rigidity and fitted effective-rank windows, the paper turns adjacent-layer SVD frames into angular transport matrices, then displays them as Physical Alignment Matrices with an orthogonal core + overlap + noise decomposition. Its distinguishing move is to separate source-mode incidence from fully physical input-output channel incidence and to certify stability by explicit margins: active-column gaps, pairwise overlap thresholds, fitted-tail discrepancy against a Gibbs–Cartan model, and additional row-energy/profile-correlation tests for finer SC/SA/ST labels. The theory yields a static certificate radius under which full transport and dominant-window truncation preserve active supports, pairwise incidence graphs, SRS sets, and hub columns. Empirically, it measures these quantities through block-energy heatmaps on CNNs, language models, and vision/diffusion backbones, framing the figures as finite-dimensional measurements rather than proofs. The direction is toward geometry-certified network analysis and low-disruption adaptation coordinates.
papers.abstract: This paper develops the angular and static-channel component of Geometric and Spectral Alignment for residual Jacobian chains. Starting from Cartan-coordinate rigidity and fitted effective-rank windows, we study how dominant singular subspaces are transported across adjacent layers and how the resulting finite matrices can be displayed in physical channel coordinates. The main results are deterministic, margin-verified results. We bound the error between full interface transport and its dominant-window truncation, add fitted-tail errors so that empirical spectra can be certified against the Gibbs--Cartan tail model, and distinguish source-mode incidence from fully physical input-output channel incidence. Given row groups and active supports, the Physical Alignment Matrix decomposes orthogonally as core plus overlap plus noise. Active-column gaps, pairwise overlap margins, and noise bounds combine into a static certificate radius under which the full transport and the truncated transport induce the same active supports, pairwise incidence graph, SRS sets, hub columns, and core/overlap/noise masks. The finer SC/SA/ST labels of the Invariant Channel Mapping require additional row-energy and profile-correlation margins, stated as explicit perturbation tests. The empirical section reports the matrices and block-energy heatmaps that measure these certificate quantities across CNNs, language models, and vision/diffusion backbones. The figures are interpreted as finite-dimensional measurements; complete membership in the Physical GSA certificate domain requires checking the numerical margin protocol stated in Section 10.

papers.title: Statistical Consistency and Generalization of Contrastive Representation Learning
papers.summary: Statistical Consistency and Generalization of Contrastive Representation Learning studies the theory gap in contrastive pretraining for foundation models: existing bounds worsen with more negatives and say little about whether minimizing contrastive loss actually yields optimal retrieval. The paper reframes CRL as a pairwise ranking problem and evaluates downstream quality with an AUC-type population criterion over triples (x,y,y′), rather than label prediction. Its key technical move is to prove Fisher/statistical consistency of the log-sum-exp contrastive loss: any population minimizer has the form τ log p+ x (y)/p− x (y)+g(x), which aligns exactly with optimal ranking, and to derive a calibration-style inequality E*−E(s)≲√(L(s)−L*), linking upstream contrastive excess risk to downstream retrieval suboptimality. For finite samples, the analysis splits the loss into inner and outer errors, using algorithmic stability for supervised CRL and uniform convergence for shared-negative self-supervised CRL, yielding O(1/m+1/√n) and O(1/√m+1/√n) rates. Experiments on large-scale CLIP training with DFN subsets, ImageNet zero-shot classification, and MSCOCO/Flickr retrieval validate the predicted saturation and m–n tradeoff, pointing toward a more principled sample-complexity theory for multimodal contrastive foundation models.
papers.abstract: Contrastive representation learning (CRL) underpins many modern foundation models. Despite recent theoretical progress, existing analyses suffer from several key limitations: (i) the statistical consistency of CRL remains poorly understood; (ii) available generalization bounds deteriorate as the number of negative samples increases, contradicting the empirical benefits of large negative sets; and (iii) the retrieval performance of CRL has received limited theoretical attention. In this paper, we develop a unified statistical learning theory for CRL. For downstream tasks, we evaluate retrieval quality using an AUC-type population criterion and show that the contrastive loss is \emph{statistically consistent} with optimal ranking. We further establish a \emph{calibration-style inequality} that quantitatively relates excess contrastive risk to excess retrieval suboptimality. For upstream training, we study both supervised and self-supervised contrastive objectives and derive generalization bounds of order $O(1/m + 1/\sqrt{n})$ and $O(1/\sqrt{m} + 1/\sqrt{n})$, respectively, where $m$ denotes the number of negative samples and $n$ the number of anchor points. These bounds not only explain the empirical advantages of large negative sets but also reveal an explicit trade-off between $m$ and $n$. Extensive experiments on large-scale vision--language models corroborate our theoretical predictions.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning Trained Observer Control for Bearings-Only Tracking
papers.summary: Reinforcement Learning Trained Observer Control for Bearings-Only Tracking tackles the classic passive localization bottleneck where range is unobservable unless the sensing platform maneuvers, and where aggressive motion can make the filter diverge even if information gain rises. The paper casts observer maneuver selection as a belief MDP whose state is the cubature Kalman filter posterior, then learns a discrete second-leg heading policy with a deep Q-network over 16 candidate headings spaced by 22.5 degrees. Unlike nearby information-theoretic path planners that optimize only Fisher information, the reward is a Pareto-geometric interpolation between absolute position error in Euclidean distance and filter consistency in Mahalanobis distance, controlled by β. The first leg is fixed by the perpendicular-to-bearing heuristic, while the learned policy chooses the second leg from a 12-feature belief vector including posterior mean, covariance terms, and the latest bearing encoded as sine/cosine. Trained for 50,000 episodes and tested on 5,000 Monte Carlo runs, the β=0.7 policy matches D-optimal accuracy while cutting worst-case error by nearly an order of magnitude, pointing toward risk-aware belief-space RL for real-time autonomous tracking.
papers.abstract: This paper develops a deep reinforcement learning based observer control policy for autonomous bearings-only tracking of a moving target. The observer manoeuvre problem is formulated as a belief Markov decision process, where the belief state is represented by the posterior of a cubature Kalman filter (CKF). The reward function is designed to address two conflicting objectives: minimising the absolute target position estimation error (Euclidean distance) and maintaining CKF estimation consistency (Mahalanobis distance). The reward is formulated as a geometric interpolation between the two objectives on the Pareto front, parametrised by a weighting factor $β\in [0,1]$. The policy is implemented as a deep Q-network (DQN) trained over 50,000 episodes. Performance is evaluated over 5,000 Monte Carlo episodes and compared against two baselines: the perpendicular-to-bearing heuristic and the D-optimal Fisher information maximisation criterion. The results show that the DQN policy at $β= 0.7$ achieves the best trade-off between accuracy and robustness: it matches the information-theoretic baseline on mean tracking accuracy while reducing the worst-case error by nearly a factor of ten, owing to the implicit filter-consistency regularisation provided by the Mahalanobis term in the reward.

papers.title: STABLEVAL: Disagreement-Aware and Stable Evaluation of AI Systems
papers.summary: STABLEVAL: Disagreement-Aware and Stable Evaluation of AI Systems attacks a specific weakness in human evaluation pipelines: majority-vote aggregation throws away annotator reliability and item ambiguity, so small changes in who annotates can flip model rankings. The core idea is a Bayesian latent-variable evaluator with graded latent correctness states, annotator-specific confusion matrices, and EM inference that outputs posterior expected item credit rather than a hard consensus label; agent scores are averaged from these credits and reported with bootstrap confidence intervals. Unlike Dawid-Skene-style denoising, the goal is not best latent-label recovery but ranking stability, formalized via Kendall’s tau under annotator subsampling. The paper stress-tests the method on synthetic regimes with adversarial, strict, and lenient annotators plus hard items, and on MT-Bench human judgments (6,712 annotations from 65 annotators), ConvAbuse, QAGS with 169 crowdworkers, and MSLR with expert raters. Across these settings, majority vote becomes volatile under heterogeneity, while STABLEVAL yields more stable, statistically grounded rankings; Dawid-Skene can win on MSE yet still miss the evaluation objective. The work points toward uncertainty-aware, disagreement-native benchmarking for robust AI selection and safety assessment.
papers.abstract: Human evaluation remains the primary standard for assessing modern AI systems, yet annotator disagreement, bias, and variability make system rankings fragile under standard majority vote aggregation. Majority vote discards annotator reliability and item-level ambiguity, often yielding unstable comparisons across annotator subsets. We introduce STABLEVAL, a disagreement-aware evaluation framework that models latent item correctness and annotator-specific confusion patterns to produce posterior expected item credit and calibrated agent-level scores.
