Unlike label-denoising approaches such as Dawid-Skene, STABLEVAL is explicitly designed for stable and uncertainty-aware system evaluation rather than hard label recovery. We formalize ranking stability as a first-class evaluation objective and analyze how aggregation methods preserve or distort underlying annotator behavior. Across controlled synthetic experiments and multiple real-world human-annotated benchmarks, majority vote exhibits increasing score error and ranking instability under annotator heterogeneity and adversarial noise, while STABLEVAL yields more stable and statistically grounded system rankings. These results demonstrate that modeling disagreement is essential for robust and reproducible AI evaluation.

papers.title: Context-Aware Wireless Token Communication via Joint Token Masking and Detection
papers.summary: Context-Aware Wireless Token Communication via Joint Token Masking and Detection attacks a bottleneck in wireless text/token transmission: conventional links treat tokens independently and spend equal power on every token, wasting scarce budget when many tokens are contextually predictable from neighbors and channel noise corrupts the rest. The paper’s core idea is to use a shared masked language model, instantiated with BERT, as a bidirectional contextual prior at both transmitter and receiver. On the Rx side, token inference is cast as an iterative MAP detector that multiplies channel likelihoods with MLM priors, then freezes easy tokens via token-wise posterior-entropy thresholds to avoid unnecessary re-refinement. On the Tx side, it performs sequential entropy-based masking: the most predictable tokens are omitted first, and masking stops when cumulative detection probability, estimated via a geometric-mean criterion, no longer beats direct transmission. This is unusual relative to prior token communication and LLM-enabled detection work because it jointly exploits context for both omission and recovery rather than only Rx-side decoding or fixed/random masking. In Rayleigh block-fading simulations on Europarl and WikiText-103 with 4-QAM and 128-token packets, it reaches up to 1.77× and 1.63× reconstruction gains, pointing toward adaptive context-native semantic links.
papers.abstract: The increasing use of token-based representations in language-driven applications has motivated wireless token communication, where tokens are treated as fundamental units for transmission. However, conventional communication systems overlook dependencies among tokens and allocate transmission resources uniformly, leading to inefficient use of limited wireless resources under channel impairments. In this paper, we propose a context-aware token communication framework that leverages a masked language model (MLM) as a shared contextual model between the transmitter (Tx) and receiver (Rx). At the Rx, we develop a context-aware token detection method that integrates channel likelihoods with MLM-based contextual priors under a Bayesian formulation, enabling robust token inference over noisy channels. At the Tx, we propose a context-aware token masking strategy that selectively omits tokens that can be reliably inferred at the Rx, allowing the available power budget to be concentrated on more informative tokens. These components are jointly designed through a shared MLM, establishing a unified Tx-Rx framework for efficient token transmission and detection. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly improves reconstruction performance compared to conventional and existing token communication schemes, achieving up to 1.77X and 1.63X performance gains on the Europarl corpus and WikiText-103 datasets, respectively.

papers.title: Boundary Mass and the Soft-to-Hard Limit in Mixture-of-Experts
papers.summary: Boundary Mass and the Soft-to-Hard Limit in Mixture-of-Experts studies why softmax-routed MoE regression does not converge uniformly to hard routing as temperature τ→0: the singularity is concentrated near routing ties, where the top two router logits nearly match. The paper’s central mechanism is the boundary mass, P(Δ(X;ϕ)≤w), i.e., the probability that the top-two margin falls inside a thin slab around a decision interface. Under C² router regularity, density continuity, and transversality/nonvanishing-gradient assumptions, the author uses coarea and tubular-neighborhood formulas to show this mass is linear in slab width, with an explicit surface-integral constant in the binary case. This geometric control yields quantitative soft–hard risk bounds for squared-loss population MoE regression and, under compactness plus uniform margin tails, Γ-convergence of the soft objectives to the hard-routing objective. Unlike nearby MoE work focused on estimation or training heuristics, the emphasis here is on the interface geometry itself. The paper then transfers the boundary-layer calculus to a teacher–student setting, proving that favorable hard-routing landscape features, including approximate teacher recovery and strict-saddle behavior, persist at small temperature, pointing toward geometry-driven analysis of conditional computation and annealing in sparse experts.
papers.abstract: Softmax-routed mixture-of-experts models approach hard routing as the temperature tends to zero, but this limit is singular near routing ties. This paper studies that singularity at the population level for squared-loss MoE regression. The central object is the \emph{boundary mass}, namely the probability that the top two router scores are separated by only a small margin. Under smoothness and transversality assumptions on the router and input law, we prove coarea/tube estimates showing that this mass is linear in the slab width, with leading constant given by a surface integral over the routing interface in the binary case. These estimates yield quantitative soft-to-hard risk bounds and, under compactness and uniform margin control, $Γ$-convergence of the soft objectives to the hard-routing objective. The main conclusion is that the zero-temperature limit is controlled by a thin geometric layer around routing interfaces, not by the full input space. We then use this geometric core in two more model-dependent directions. In a teacher--student setting, we prove a conditional landscape-transfer principle showing that, when the profiled hard-routing problem has favorable identifiability and curvature and the relevant derivatives transfer at boundary-layer scale, small-temperature soft routing inherits approximate teacher recovery and strict-saddle behavior away from teacher-equivalent partitions. We also give a reduced two-expert Gaussian calculation that illustrates a local symmetry-breaking mechanism aligned with the teacher separator.

papers.title: FedQueue: Queue-Aware Federated Learning for Cross-Facility HPC Training
papers.summary: FedQueue: Queue-Aware Federated Learning for Cross-Facility HPC Training targets a systems bottleneck that standard FL papers usually ignore: in cross-facility HPC, Slurm/PBS admission delays dominate wall-clock time, so synchronous rounds stall on stragglers while async methods drift on stale updates when queues spike. FedQueue makes scheduler latency part of the training algorithm itself by combining online per-facility queue prediction with EWMA, cutoff-based admission that buffers late arrivals instead of discarding them, and staleness-aware aggregation with harmonic decay plus inverse learning-rate scaling to compensate for heterogeneous local step budgets. Unlike nearby work such as throughput profilers or generic buffered async FL, it explicitly predicts queue delay, budgets local work to fit the synchronization horizon, and proves bounded staleness with high probability under prediction error. The analysis gives non-convex O(1/√R) convergence under bounded staleness. In deployment across four production facilities, fine-tuning LLaMA2-7B on SMolInstruct, FedQueue improved loss by 20.5% over baselines; in controlled MNIST/CNN non-IID simulations it cut time-to-target by about 34% under high queue variance, pointing toward scheduler-aware federated training for scientific foundation models on shared supercomputers.
papers.abstract: Federated learning (FL) across multiple HPC facilities faces stochastic admission delays from batch schedulers that dominate wall-clock time. Synchronous FL suffers from severe stragglers, while asynchronous FL accumulates stale updates when queues spike. We propose FedQueue, a queue-aware FL protocol that incorporates scheduler delays directly into training and aggregation, which (i) predicts per-facility queue delays online to budget local work, (ii) applies cutoff-based admission that buffers late arrivals to bound staleness, and (iii) performs staleness-aware aggregation to stabilize heterogeneous local workloads. We prove the convergence for non-convex objectives at rate $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{R})$ under bounded staleness, and show that the admission controls yield bounded staleness with high probability under queue-prediction error. Real-world cross-facility deployment of FedQueue shows 20.5% improvement over baseline algorithms. Controlled queue simulations demonstrate robust improvement over the baselines; in particular, about 34% reduction in time to reach a target accuracy level under high queue variance and non-IID partitions.

papers.title: Ultrasound Vision-Language Alignment via Contrastive Learning
papers.summary: Ultrasound Vision-Language Alignment via Contrastive Learning tackles the absence of text-aligned ultrasound foundation models, a gap that limits zero-shot and few-shot transfer when labels are scarce and modality-specific reports are rare. The paper introduces EchoCare-CLIP, a CLIP-style dual-encoder trained with symmetric InfoNCE to map ultrasound frames and clinical text into a shared 256-dimensional space, using an EchoCare SwinTransformerV2 image backbone and either a CLIP text encoder or BioClinicalBERT. To build supervision from public data alone, the authors curate 16,464 image-text pairs across breast, liver, lung, and thyroid, with over 78% from expert reports and the rest synthesized through a three-tier caption pipeline that ranges from tissue-only templates to metadata-rich descriptions and Qwen3-4B-Instruct rewrites. Compared with OpenAI CLIP and BiomedCLIP, the best configuration reaches a paired alignment score of 0.682, yet the strongest transfer comes from partial fine-tuning rather than full end-to-end adaptation: CLIP-based variants achieve 0.709 zero-shot accuracy on BUSI and 0.626 on AULI, while aggressive fine-tuning overfits and harms generalization. The work points toward clinically grounded ultrasound VLMs where caption quality and adaptation depth are balanced against robust transfer.
papers.abstract: Ultrasound foundation models have achieved strong performance on structured prediction tasks but remain exclusively vision-based, limiting zero-shot and few-shot transfer to novel tasks where task-specific annotation is scarce. We address this gap with EchoCare-CLIP, a CLIP-style dual-encoder contrastive framework that aligns ultrasound images with clinical text in a shared embedding space. We curate a multi-organ corpus of over 16K image-text pairs spanning breast, liver, lung, and thyroid, with over 78% of captions derived from expert-annotated reports, and complement the remainder with a three-tier template-based and LLM-based caption generation pipeline. We evaluate model configurations spanning two text encoder families (CLIP, BioClinicalBERT) and two caption strategies (template-based, LLM-generated) against OpenAI CLIP and BiomedCLIP baselines. Our trained models consistently improve cross-modal alignment over baselines, with the best configuration achieving a paired alignment score of 0.682. However, stronger alignment does not guarantee better downstream performance: CLIP-based variants with partial fine-tuning achieve the strongest zero-shot classification on external held-out datasets (0.709 on BUSI; 0.626 on AULI), while full end-to-end fine-tuning degrades transfer due to overfitting. On linear probing and few-shot adaptation, model rankings are dataset-dependent, reflecting a trade-off between domain adaptation and representational generalizability. We further show that template-based captions match or outperform LLM-generated captions, suggesting lexical diversity is not a proxy for caption quality. Taken together, our results demonstrate that ultrasound vision-language alignment is achievable from public data alone, but robust clinical transfer requires careful balancing of domain adaptation, encoder capacity, and caption supervision quality.

papers.title: A Parameter-Free First-Order Algorithm for Non-Convex Optimization with $\tilde{\mkern1mu O}(ε^{-5/3})$ Global Rate
papers.summary: A Parameter-Free First-Order Algorithm for Non-Convex Optimization with $\tilde{\mkern1mu O}(ε^{-5/3})$ Global Rate attacks the gap between optimal accelerated non-convex complexity and practical deployability: prior $\tilde{O}(ε^{-5/3})$ methods needed unknown smoothness constants $L_1$ and $L_3$, making step sizes and momentum impractical. The paper’s PF-AGD is a deterministic accelerated gradient scheme that removes this dependence by combining adaptive backtracking for local $L_1$ estimation with a gradient-based restart/certification mechanism that detects when the current trajectory behaves like a pathwise strongly convex region versus when negative curvature should be exploited. A distinctive technical contribution is the two-loop design: an inner modified AGD preserves acceleration under changing curvature estimates via a Lyapunov analysis with a momentum-correcting restart that keeps the auxiliary point invariant, while an outer loop regularizes with an $L_3$-dependent parameter estimate and uses witness pairs to decide whether to increase curvature estimates or harvest descent from non-convexity. Experiments on robust least squares, MNIST MLP training, ill-conditioned quadratics, and classic nonconvex test functions show PF-AGD consistently beating AGD-Until-Guilty and remaining competitive with nonlinear CG, pointing toward parameter-free accelerated methods that are theory-tight yet practically robust.
papers.abstract: We introduce PF-AGD, the first parameter-free, deterministic, accelerated first-order method to achieve $O(ε^{-5/3}\log(1/ε))$ oracle complexity bound when minimizing sufficiently smooth, non-convex functions; this is the best-known bound for first-order methods on smooth non-convex objectives. Unlike existing methods possessing this rate that require a priori knowledge of smoothness constants, we use an adaptive backtracking scheme and a gradient-based restart mechanism to estimate local curvature. This yields a practical algorithm that matches best-known theoretical rates. Empirically, PF-AGD outperforms the practical variant of AGD-Until-Guilty (Carmon et al., 2017), as well as other parameter-free variants, and is a viable alternative to nonlinear conjugate gradient methods.

papers.title: From Where Things Are to What They Are For: Benchmarking Spatial-Functional Intelligence in Multimodal LLMs
papers.summary: From Where Things Are to What They Are For: Benchmarking Spatial-Functional Intelligence in Multimodal LLMs introduces SFI-Bench to expose the gap between geometric perception and grounded agentic cognition in video MLLMs. The benchmark contains 1,555 expert-annotated multiple-choice questions from 134 egocentric indoor videos (ARKitScenes and ScanNet++), with 24/30 fps, 480p–720p inputs, and six tasks split between Structured Spatial Reasoning and Functional Reasoning. Unlike prior “spatial” tests that mostly ask for local recognition, SFI-Bench forces models to build cognitive maps across time and viewpoints via global/conditional counting, cross-view multi-hop path reasoning, and layout inference, then move beyond “where” into “what for” with functional association, operation planning, and causal troubleshooting that may require web-searching manuals. Questions are produced from MLLM-extracted metadata, template-driven generation, and multi-stage human verification. Zero-shot evaluation across Gemini, GPT-5, Qwen3-VL, InternVL, and LLaVA shows current MLLMs are strongest at perception and weakest at compositional counting, affordance grounding, and knowledge integration; longer reasoning often drifts instead of helping, while web search helps only when reasoning is already strong. The paper points toward multimodal agents with explicit spatial memory, functional world models, and tool-augmented grounding.
papers.abstract: Human-level agentic intelligence extends beyond low-level geometric perception, evolving from recognizing where things are to understanding what they are for. While existing benchmarks effectively evaluate the geometric perception capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), they fall short of probing the higher-order cognitive abilities required for grounded intelligence. To address this gap, we introduce the Spatial-Functional Intelligence Benchmark (SFI-Bench), a video-based benchmark with over 1,500 expert-annotated questions derived from diverse egocentric indoor video scans. SFI-Bench systematically evaluates two complementary dimensions of advanced reasoning: (1) Structured Spatial Reasoning, which requires understanding complex layouts and forming coherent spatial representations, and (2) Functional Reasoning, which involves inferring object affordances and their context-dependent utility. The benchmark includes tasks such as conditional counting, multi-hop relational reasoning, functional pairing, and knowledge-grounded troubleshooting, directly challenging models to integrate perception, memory, and inference. Our experiments reveal that current MLLMs consistently struggle to combine spatial memory with functional reasoning and external knowledge, highlighting a critical bottleneck in achieving grounded intelligence. SFI-Bench therefore provides a diagnostic tool for measuring progress toward more cognitively capable and truly grounded multimodal agents.

papers.title: LUMINA: A Grid Foundation Model for Benchmarking AC Optimal Power Flow Surrogate Learning
papers.summary: LUMINA: A Grid Foundation Model for Benchmarking AC Optimal Power Flow Surrogate Learning targets the brittle generalization of ACOPF surrogates across unseen power-grid topologies, where fast prediction must still respect power-balance and line-flow physics. The paper introduces LUMINA-Bench on OPFData, standardizing four tasks—single-topology training, multi-topology pretraining, held-out-topology generalization, and transfer/adaptation—over IEEE-style cases 30, 57, 118, 500, and 2000 with fixed splits and matched hyperparameter budgets. Instead of only reporting MSE, it defines topology-normalized feasibility metrics from AC branch-flow residuals and compares MSE, augmented Lagrangian, and violation-based Lagrangian objectives across eight backbones: GCN, GAT, GIN, Graph Transformer, RGAT, HeteroGNN, HGT, and HEAT. The distinguishing result is that explicit heterogeneity matters: HGT/HEAT dominate homogeneous models, especially under multi-topology and zero-shot transfer, while AL training sharply reduces constraint violations relative to MSE. Pretraining on smaller grids cuts fine-tuning steps by up to 83.6% on case500, suggesting a foundation-model path for feasibility-aware, topology-transferable grid surrogates rather than single-network predictors.
papers.abstract: AC optimal power flow (ACOPF) is foundational yet computationally expensive in power grid operations, driving learning-based surrogates for large-scale grid analysis. These surrogates, however, often fail to generalize across network topologies, a critical gap for deployment on grids not seen during training and for routine operational what-if studies. We introduce LUMINA-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark suite for ACOPF surrogate learning covering multi-topology pretraining, transfer, and adaptation. The benchmark evaluates homogeneous and heterogeneous architectures under single- and multi-topology learning settings using unified metrics that capture both predictive accuracy and physics-informed constraint violations. We additionally compare constraint-aware training objectives, including MSE, augmented Lagrangian, and violation-based Lagrangian losses, to characterize accuracy-robustness trade-offs across settings. Data processing, training, and evaluation frameworks are open-sourced as the LUMINA suite to support reproducibility and accelerate future research on feasibility-aware OPF surrogates.

papers.title: Video Generation with Predictive Latents
papers.summary: Video Generation with Predictive Latents tackles the gap between high-fidelity video reconstruction and actually useful latent spaces for diffusion-based generation: existing video VAEs can reproduce frames well yet still produce latents that are poorly “diffusable” and weakly aligned with motion. The proposed Predictive Video VAE (PV-VAE) replaces pure reconstruction with a predictive reconstruction objective: during training it randomly drops future frame groups, encodes only the observed past, and forces the decoder to reconstruct the visible frames while also predicting the withheld future. A motion-aware auxiliary loss on inter-frame differences suppresses the copy-shortcut of static content and pushes the latent code toward temporally predictive structure. Unlike heuristic manifold shaping or post hoc latent regularization, this is a drop-in training reformulation for standard video-VAE pipelines, followed by an encoder-frozen decoder fine-tuning stage to close the training–inference gap. Evaluated on UCF101 class-conditional generation, RealEstate10K unconditional generation, and Kinetics-400 reconstruction with a Latte latent diffusion backbone, PV-VAE reports 52% faster convergence and a 34.42 FVD gain over Wan2.2 VAE on UCF101, plus stronger optical flow, tracking, and next-frame prediction probes, pointing toward motion-aware video tokenizers as world-model components.
papers.abstract: Video Variational Autoencoder (VAE) enables latent video generative modeling by mapping the visual world into compact spatiotemporal latent spaces, improving training efficiency and stability. While existing video VAEs achieve commendable reconstruction quality, continued optimization of reconstruction does not necessarily translate into improved generative performance. How to enhance the diffusability of video latents remains a critical and unresolved challenge. In this work, inspired by principles of predictive world modeling, we investigate the potential of predictive learning to improve the video generative modeling. To this end, we introduce a simple and effective predictive reconstruction objective that unifies predictive learning with video reconstruction. Specifically, we randomly discard future frames and encode only partial past observations, while training the decoder to reconstruct the observed frames and predict future ones simultaneously. This design encourages the latent space to encode temporally predictive structures and build a more coherent understanding of video dynamics, thereby improving generation quality. Our model, termed Predictive Video VAE (PV-VAE), achieves superior performance on video generation, with 52% faster convergence and a 34.42 FVD improvement over the Wan2.2 VAE on UCF101. Furthermore, comprehensive analyses demonstrate that PV-VAE not only exhibits favorable scalability, with generative performance improving alongside VAE training, but also yields consistent gains in downstream video understanding, underscoring a latent space that effectively captures temporal coherence and motion priors.

papers.title: FLoRA: Fusion-Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Flood Area Segmentation via Cross-Modal Multi-Task Distillation Network
papers.summary: FLoRA: Fusion-Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Flood Area Segmentation via Cross-Modal Multi-Task Distillation Network tackles the core remote-sensing bottleneck that SAR is reliable under clouds and night but hard to interpret, while optical imagery is semantically rich but often unavailable during floods. Instead of treating SAR-to-optical translation and flood segmentation as separate pipelines, FLoRA learns a shared fusion-latent space where a lightweight optical teacher, built from RGB and NDVI pyramids, distills semantic guidance into Sentinel-1 SAR features through multiscale windowed cross-attention, FiLM conditioning, and gated residual fusion that limits overcorrection. The network then uses gradient-decoupled dual decoders to jointly reconstruct fine-grained optical RGB and predict flood-water masks, optimized with Charbonnier+SSIM+FFT+edge losses for spectral fidelity and Dice+BCE plus hydrology-aware edge alignment for boundary accuracy. This is unusual relative to nearby GAN or U-Net flood work because translation, segmentation, and distillation are coupled in one physically motivated latent manifold. On SEN1FLOODS11, DEEPFLOOD, and SEN12MS winter patches, it reports stronger PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS and IoU/Dice than fusion baselines, pointing toward teacher-guided multimodal disaster intelligence that is both interpretable and operationally robust.
papers.abstract: Accurate flood water mapping is critical for disaster management, yet current methods struggle to fully exploit the potential of spaceborne imagery. Optical data offers high interpretability but is limited by environmental conditions, whereas SAR provides reliable all-weather coverage with reduced visual interpretability. FLoRA (Fusion Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Area Segmentation) is a cross-modal multi-task framework that jointly reconstructs high-fidelity optical imagery and segments flood water regions from Sentinel 1 SAR by fusing the complementary strengths of optical and SAR data. During training, a lightweight optical teacher (driven by RGB and NDVI priors) provides pyramidal features that guide SAR representations into a fusion latent space via multiscale windowed cross attention and FiLM conditioning, with gated residuals preventing overcorrection. This design enables multi-task learning across two complementary objectives: (a) SAR-to-optical translation for fine-grained RGB reconstruction and (b) flood water region segmentation for hydrologic interpretation. The dual decoders are optimized using Charbonnier SSIM for structural fidelity, edge FFT magnitude losses for spectral realism, and Dice BCE hydrology-aware edge alignment for precise flood water delineation. A feature distillation constraint further aligns fused SAR features with the optical teacher's manifold. Evaluations on SEN1FLOODS11, DEEPFLOOD, and SEN12MS demonstrate that FLoRA surpasses fusion baselines in PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS, demonstrating that multi-modal fusion within a teacher-guided latent space yields semantically faithful and physically consistent flood-water intelligence from spaceborne observations.

papers.title: On the Optimal Sample Complexity of Offline Multi-Armed Bandits with KL Regularization
papers.summary: On the Optimal Sample Complexity of Offline Multi-Armed Bandits with KL Regularization studies the exact statistical limit of learning from logged bandit data when the objective is the reverse-KL-regularized reward plus reference-policy term, a setting motivated by offline RLHF-style fine-tuning but analyzed here in contextual multi-armed bandits with i.i.d. data from the behavior/reference policy. The paper sharpens KL-PCB by using standard arm-wise pessimism, empirical means, and the closed-form softmax policy π(a|s) ∝ πref(a|s)exp(η r̂(s,a)), then proves a phase transition in suboptimality: for large regularization it achieves ẼO(ηSAC^{π*}/ε), while for small regularization it reverts to the ẼO(√(SAC^{π*}/n)) / ε^{-2}-type regime. What distinguishes the work is the matching lower-bound theory across the full η range, including a novel pairing-and-counting argument and hard instances with multiple optimal arms, which reveal that KL regularization changes how concentrability scales with arm count. The result nearly closes the gap left by earlier f-divergence analyses and points toward sharp offline theory for regularized decision making beyond tabular bandits.
papers.abstract: Kullback-Leibler (KL) regularization is widely used in offline decision-making and offers several benefits, motivating recent work on the sample complexity of offline learning with respect to KL-regularized performance metrics. Nevertheless, the exact sample complexity of KL-regularized offline learning remains largely from fully characterized. In this paper, we study this question in the setting of multi-armed bandits (MABs). We provide a sharp analysis of KL-PCB (Zhao et al., 2026), showing that it achieves a sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(ηSAC^{π^*}/ε)$ under large regularization $η= \tilde{O}(ε^{-1})$, and a sample complexity of $\tildeΩ(SAC^{π^*}/ε^2)$ under small regularization $η= \tildeΩ(ε^{-1})$, where $η$ is the regularization parameter, $S$ is the number of contexts, $A$ is the number of arms, $C^{π^*}$ policy coverage coefficient at the optimal policy $π^*$, $ε$ is the desired sub-optimality, and $\tilde{O}$ and $\tildeΩ$ hide all poly-logarithmic factors. We further provide a pair of sharper sample complexity lower bounds, which matches the upper bounds over the entire range of regularization strengths. Overall, our results provide a nearly complete characterization of offline multi-armed bandits with KL regularization.

papers.title: Personalized Federated Learning for Gradient Alignment
papers.summary: Personalized Federated Learning for Gradient Alignment tackles a specific pFL failure mode: client-specific information is washed out by high-variance local gradients from sparse, heterogeneous data and then further distorted by aggregation, especially in language-model federated fine-tuning. The proposed pFLAlign inserts gradient alignment at two points in the FL loop. First, Personalized Local Updates use client-wise multiplicative preconditioning to rescale each parameter dimension by an online estimate of gradient reliability, suppressing noisy directions while preserving client-preferred descent. Second, Aggregation-Robust Personalization starts each round from a personalized offset and applies a probabilistically gated correction term that shrinks only those coordinates whose stochastic updates are likely misaligned with the client’s accumulated direction, avoiding the blunt correction typical of SCAFFOLD-like methods. What distinguishes the paper is its PAC-Bayesian derivation of these rules, treating preconditioner evolution and aggregation distortion as separate bound-controlled sources of complexity. Experiments on federated instruction tuning with LLaMA-3.2 and OPT-1.3B over Dolly-15K and FLAN, with task-level client heterogeneity and LoRA adapters, show better ROUGE-L, higher GSNR, and more stable training. The paper points toward variance-aware, theory-grounded personalization for federated LLMs.
papers.abstract: Personalized federated learning (pFL) aims to adapt models to client specific data distributions, yet it often fails to reliably preserve personalized information. Local training is hindered by high variance gradients induced by limited and heterogeneous client data, while aggregation further distorts client specific optimization directions. To address these challenges, we propose pFLAlign, a gradient alignment framework to maintain client specific information during both local training and aggregation. pFLAlign consists of two complementary mechanisms: one adapts local gradient directions to reduce variance during client side optimization, and the other mitigates aggregation induced distortion by realigning the global model with each client's personalized direction. Theoretically, we derive pFLAlign from a PAC Bayesian analysis, which reveals how personalized gradient alignment preserves client specific information. Our experiments and ablation studies show that pFLAlign consistently improves personalization performance and training stability, achieving state of the art results.

papers.title: Projection-Free Transformers via Gaussian Kernel Attention
papers.summary: Projection-Free Transformers via Gaussian Kernel Attention asks whether the learned Q, K, V projections in Transformer self-attention are actually necessary, or whether token mixing can be done by a simpler similarity diffusion. The paper replaces softmax dot-product attention with Gaussian Kernel Attention (GKA), which computes affinities directly from per-head residual-stream features using a Gaussian RBF kernel, row-normalizes the kernel into a stochastic mixing matrix, and keeps only one learnable bandwidth σh per head plus the usual output projection WO. This makes attention behave like normalized kernel regression / non-local smoothing rather than learned query-key geometry, and it is notably projection-free rather than an approximation such as Performer or Nyström-style methods. The authors test GKA in DeiT-style ViTs on ImageNet-1K and in nanochat GPT-style autoregressive language modeling on FineWeb-Edu with causal and sliding-window masking via masked kernel renormalization. GKA trims vision models by about 23–25% parameters and 21–24% FLOPs while staying competitive, and in depth-20 language models runs stably at 0.42× parameters and 0.49× training FLOPs with a near-zero train-validation gap, though BPB is worse and wall-clock efficiency suffers. This points toward interpretable, locality-controlled Transformer design and systems work on fused Gaussian attention kernels.
papers.abstract: Self-attention in Transformers is typically implemented as $\mathrm{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d})V$, where $Q=XW_Q$, $K=XW_K$, and $V=XW_V$ are learned linear projections of the input $X$. We ask whether these learned projections are necessary, or whether they can be replaced by a simpler similarity-based diffusion operator. We introduce \textbf{Gaussian Kernel Attention} (GKA), a drop-in replacement for dot-product attention that computes token affinities directly using a Gaussian radial basis function (RBF) kernel applied to per-head token features. Each head learns only a bandwidth parameter $σ_h$, while a single output projection $W_O$ preserves compatibility with the standard Transformer interface. GKA can be interpreted as normalized kernel regression over tokens, linking modern Transformer architectures to classical non-local filtering and kernel smoothing methods. We evaluate GKA in both vision and language modeling settings. For autoregressive language modeling within the \texttt{nanochat} framework, we implement causal masking and sliding-window constraints by masking and renormalizing the Gaussian kernel. At depth 20, a GKA model with $0.42\times$ the parameters and $0.49\times$ the total training FLOPs of a standard attention baseline trains stably, exhibits a near-zero train-validation gap, and demonstrates competitive behavior on standard benchmarks, albeit with higher bits-per-byte (BPB) at this compute scale. Overall, GKA provides a minimal, interpretable attention mechanism with an explicit locality scale, offering a dimension in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for Transformer design.

papers.title: H3: A Healthcare Three-Hop Index for Physician Referral Network Prediction
papers.summary: H3: A Healthcare Three-Hop Index for Physician Referral Network Prediction tackles the problem that Medicare physician-sharing graphs are sparse, disassortative, and dominated by mega-hubs, making common-neighbor rules and GNNs poor at recovering missing referral links. The paper’s core idea is H3, a weighted three-hop structural index that scores candidate physician pairs through indirect paths vi→vk→vl→vj, while normalizing by intermediate and target degree and adding a redundancy penalty that downweights repeated hub-mediated connectors. This is unusual relative to nearby link-prediction work because it is parameter-free yet not a plain L3 copy: it explicitly uses patient-sharing weights, tunable hub suppression, and path-level redundancy control to separate clinically meaningful referral chains from incidental co-occurrence through large systems. The authors evaluate on CMS Physician Shared Patient Patterns data across all U.S. states and multiple temporal windows, using both within-period recovery and cross-period prediction under 30/90/180-day expansion. H3 consistently beats classical heuristics and DeepWalk/Node2Vec/GNN baselines, especially on AUPRC, MRR, and top-100 retrieval in low-degree states. The result points toward interpretable, deployable, topology-first clinical network completion, and toward hybrid models that fuse structural motifs with specialty, geography, and institutional metadata.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of physician referral links is essential for optimizing care coordination and reducing fragmentation in healthcare delivery. However, existing computational methods, ranging from triadic closure heuristics to graph neural networks, fail to capture the intrinsic properties of physician referral networks, including sparsity, disassortative degree mixing, and hub-dominated topology. Here, we propose H3, a healthcare three-hop index that addresses these limitations by modeling indirect referral pathways through intermediate physicians, with degree-based normalization and a redundancy penalty to mitigate hub-mediated noise. Using Medicare Physician Shared Patient Patterns data, we evaluate H3 under two complementary prediction regimes: within-period prediction, which assesses recovery of contemporaneous referral links under sparse conditions, and cross-period prediction, which tests robustness to temporal shift as referral windows expand. Across both regimes, H3 consistently outperforms classical heuristics and deep learning-based baselines. Unlike black-box neural network approaches, H3 produces fully decomposable predictions traceable to specific intermediary physicians, offering a transparent and deployable solution for referral network completion.

papers.title: SpecEdit: Training-Free Acceleration for Diffusion based Image Editing via Semantic Locking
papers.summary: SpecEdit: Training-Free Acceleration for Diffusion based Image Editing via Semantic Locking targets the main bottleneck in instruction-guided diffusion editing: every denoising step still updates all spatial tokens at full resolution, even though most edits affect only a small region. Instead of heuristic refinement from edges or channel variance, SpecEdit uses a draft-and-verify pipeline: it first runs a cheap 16× downsampled full edit to produce a coarse draft, then compares the draft with the original image in a perceptual feature space to build a token-level discrepancy map that “locks” semantically changed regions for high-resolution denoising. Unchanged tokens remain coarse, while a sparse uniform token set is added to preserve global structure; the token expansion is parameter-free and fully training-free. This is unusual relative to nearby diffusion acceleration work because token selection is driven by semantic verification, not geometric saliency. Evaluated on Qwen-Image-Edit and FLUX.1-Kontext-dev over GEdit-Bench and ImgEdit-Bench, it reaches up to 10× and 7× acceleration while keeping strong semantic consistency, and combines with step distillation or caching for up to 13× speedup, pointing toward semantics-aware spatial compute for efficient editing.
papers.abstract: Diffusion-based image editing offers strong semantic controllability, but remains computationally expensive due to iterative high-resolution denoising over all spatial tokens. Dynamic-resolution sampling reduces this cost by performing early steps at reduced resolution. However, existing approaches prioritize upsampling using low-level heuristics such as edge detection or channel variance, which are weakly aligned with editing semantics and may lead to structural inconsistency. Moreover, spatial regions are often upsampled without verifying whether semantic modification is actually required, resulting in redundant high-resolution computation and accumulated errors. Therefore, we propose SpecEdit, a training-free dynamic-resolution framework tailored for diffusion-based image editing. SpecEdit follows a draft-and-verify scheme: a low-resolution draft first estimates the semantic outcome, after which token-level discrepancies are used to identify edit-relevant tokens for high-resolution denoising, while the remaining tokens stay at a coarse resolution. Experiments on Qwen-Image-Edit and FLUX.1-Kontext-dev demonstrate up to 10x and 7x acceleration, while maintaining strong quality. SpecEdit is complementary to step distillation and other acceleration techniques, achieving up to 13x speedup when combined with existing methods. Our code is in supplementary material and will be released on GitHub.

papers.title: Cross-Polarization Fusion of VV AND VH SAR Observations for Improved Flood Mapping
papers.summary: Cross-Polarization Fusion of VV AND VH SAR Observations for Improved Flood Mapping tackles the hard case where single-polarization Sentinel-1 flood segmentation breaks down in vegetated, mixed land-cover, and partially inundated scenes because VV surface-scattering cues and VH volume-scattering cues disagree. The paper’s core idea is a Cross-Polarization Fusion (CPF) module that processes VV and VH through separate lightweight convolutional stems, augments them with VV/VH ratio features and log-ratio cues, then uses bidirectional spatial attention and channel attention to let each polarization selectively reweight the other before a final 3×3 projection. This is more specific than simple early concatenation or feature addition, because the fusion explicitly models cross-polarization interaction rather than merely stacking channels. The authors compare VV-only, VH-only, early fusion, addition fusion, and CPF under identical training in U-Net and a convolutional autoencoder on the DeepFlood dataset built from dual-pol Sentinel-1 over Hurricane Matthew and Florence, including an unseen-event test split. CPF achieves the best IoU and F1, with the largest gains in vegetated floodplains and sharper flood boundaries, pointing toward scattering-aware, attention-based SAR fusion for more reliable disaster monitoring.
papers.abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery is widely used for flood monitoring due to its all-weather and day-night imaging capability. However, flood mapping using single-polarization SAR data remains challenging in complex environments where surface and volume scattering coexist. In this paper, we investigate the effectiveness of cross-polarization fusion of VV and VH SAR observations for improved flood mapping. A deep learning-based segmentation framework is employed to jointly exploit complementary information from VV and VH polarizations. To ensure a fair evaluation, three configurations are compared under identical training conditions: VV only, VH only, and fused VV-VH input. Performance is assessed using standard flood mapping metrics, including Intersection over Union (IoU) and F1-score, along with qualitative visual analysis. Experimental results demonstrate that VV-VH fusion consistently outperforms single-polarization models, particularly in vegetated and heterogeneous flood regions, leading to more accurate flood boundary delineation. The findings highlight the importance of cross-polarization SAR fusion for enhancing the reliability of SAR-based flood mapping in disaster monitoring applications.

papers.title: Combining Trained Models in Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Combining Trained Models in Reinforcement Learning is a PRISMA-guided systematic review of how deep RL reuses pretrained knowledge when training from scratch is too sample-hungry and transfer is fragile. Rather than proposing a new algorithm, the paper attacks the evidence bottleneck: transfer, policy distillation, ensemble RL, and federated RL are scattered across subliteratures, making published gains hard to compare because tasks, baselines, and compute budgets differ. From 589 records across IEEE Xplore, ACM DL, and citation tracing, the authors screened 570 unique papers, assessed 89 full texts, and retained 15 empirical studies. They synthesize results around three factors: source-target similarity, diversity among reused models, and fairness of the baseline comparison. The clearest pattern is that reuse helps most when source and target share substantial structure or when the method adds explicit gating, weighting, or alignment; otherwise negative transfer is common. Evidence for ensembles and federated aggregation is promising but sparse and narrow, and compute-matched comparisons are rare. The paper’s distinctive contribution is a provisional “independence spectrum” for reused models, pointing toward benchmarking that controls overlap and training budget.
papers.abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has delivered strong results in domains such as Atari and Go, but it still suffers from high sample cost and weak transfer beyond the training setting. A common response is to reuse information from previously trained models through transfer, distillation, ensemble methods, or federated training instead of learning each target task from random initialization. The literature on these mechanisms is fragmented, and published comparisons are hard to interpret because tasks, baselines, and compute budgets differ. This paper presents a PRISMA-guided systematic review of empirical studies on pretrained knowledge reuse in DRL. Starting from 589 records retrieved from IEEE Xplore, the ACM Digital Library, and citation tracing, we screened 570 unique records and assessed 89 full texts. After applying the final eligibility criteria, 15 empirical studies remained in the main synthesis. We analyzed them qualitatively across three factors: source-target similarity, diversity among reused models, and the fairness of comparisons against from-scratch baselines. Three patterns recur across the surviving corpus. First, positive results are concentrated in settings where source and target tasks share substantial structure or where the method includes an explicit gating or alignment mechanism. Second, evidence for ensembles and federated aggregation is promising but sparse and mostly limited to narrow settings. Third, compute-matched comparisons are rare, which weakens claims about efficiency gains over stronger single-agent baselines. The paper contributes a narrower and internally consistent review scope, a study-level synthesis of empirical evidence, and a provisional independence spectrum that should be treated as a hypothesis for future benchmarking rather than a validated metric.

papers.title: DocSync: Agentic Documentation Maintenance via Critic-Guided Reflexion
papers.summary: DocSync: Agentic Documentation Maintenance via Critic-Guided Reflexion attacks code-documentation inconsistency, where docstrings and README-style explanations drift from executable logic and silently mislead downstream users even when static linters see valid tags. The key idea is to treat documentation repair as a structured editing problem: DocSync first diff-checks whether a change is relevant, then injects Tree-sitter AST signatures and dependency context retrieved with RAG into a LoRA-adapted Phi-3 Mini 4K Instruct model, and finally runs a Reflexion-style critic loop that inspects each draft against the source code and feeds back natural-language correction cues until the update is factually consistent. What differentiates it from nearby code-to-text work is the explicit combination of structural grounding and iterative self-critique rather than one-shot generation or pure encoder-decoder translation. In a resource-constrained Python study on 8,192 CodeXGLUE examples, using truncated “stale” docstrings as the repair target, DocSync outperformed CodeT5-base on BLEU, BERTScore, summary-line exact match, and LLM-as-a-judge preferences, reaching 3.44/5.0 versus 1.91. The paper points toward agentic software-maintenance systems that autonomously mitigate documentation debt on commodity hardware.
papers.abstract: Software documentation frequently drifts from executable logic as codebases evolve, creating technical debt that degrades maintainability and causes downstream API misuse. While static analysis tools can detect the absence of documentation, they cannot evaluate its semantic consistency. Conversely, standard Large Language Models (LLMs) offer generative flexibility but frequently hallucinate when updating documentation without deep structural awareness of the underlying code. To address this gap, we propose DocSync, an agentic workflow that frames documentation maintenance as a structurally grounded, iterative generation task. DocSync bridges syntactic changes and natural language descriptions by fusing Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) representations and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to provide dependency-aware context. Furthermore, to ensure factual consistency, we incorporate a critic-guided refinement loop based on the Reflexion paradigm, allowing the model to self-correct candidate updates against the source code. We empirically evaluate a resource-constrained implementation of DocSync-using a LoRA-adapted small language model - on a proxy code-to-text maintenance task. Our findings demonstrate that this AST-aware agentic approach substantially outperforms standard encoder-decoder baselines across semantic alignment, summary-line faithfulness, and automated judge preferences (e.g., achieving an automated judge score of 3.44/5.0 compared to 1.91 for CodeT5-base). Crucially, the iterative critic loop yields measurable improvements in semantic correctness without requiring scaled-up parameter counts. These results provide strong evidence that coupling structural retrieval with agentic refinement is a highly promising direction for autonomously mitigating documentation debt.

papers.title: Experience Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning for Large-scale UAV Teams in Hazardous Environments
papers.summary: Experience Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning for Large-scale UAV Teams in Hazardous Environments tackles a mismatch between standard federated learning intuition and UAV reinforcement learning, where safe experience is the scarce resource rather than model updates. The paper studies large UAV teams in radiation, wildfire, or chemical-leak style missions and proposes EC-HFRL, a cluster-based hierarchy in which each cluster is a federated agent, only a subset of UAVs collect fresh trajectories per round, and multiple intra-cluster learners repeatedly reuse a shared replay pool before cluster-level and then global aggregation. Its distinctive contribution is not a new optimizer but an analysis of how fixed experience budgets reshape learning: higher participation does not reliably help, because it mainly increases replay overlap and energy cost. The authors formalize replay exposure with Union Coverage Ratio (UCR) and introduce Key Enrichment Ratio (KER) and Key TD Contribution (KTC) to track whether analytically defined key experiences dominate the learning signal. On PD-Chem and SD-Fire 3D simulations, prioritized replay outperforms uniform replay, but performance correlates far more with minibatch size and key-experience admission than with participation intensity, suggesting future FRL should optimize experience reuse and signal composition under physical constraints.
papers.abstract: Conventional federated learning assumes that greater learner participation improves training performance, by leveraging abundant, independently generated local data. However, in federated reinforcement learning (FRL) for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) teams in hazardous environments where experience generation is severely constrained by safety considerations, energy limitations, and mission duration, this assumption may break. This work introduces Experience-Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning (EC-HFRL), a framework in which clusters act as federated learning agents, while multiple intra-cluster learners represent parallel learning resources that reuse a shared experience pool. We show that increasing participation does not necessarily improve learning performance. Instead, learning performance is strongly associated with experience reuse strategy and the dominance of key analytically identified gradient transition experiences within a cluster. In particular, minibatch size primarily determines effective replay exposure, while higher intra-cluster participation increases reuse level. Empirical results demonstrate that the performance regimes are strongly associated with the structure of the learning signal, rather than federated aggregation effects, clarifying the limited and secondary role of learner participation in experience-constrained FRL.

papers.title: Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients for Reliable Feature Attribution
papers.summary: Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients for Reliable Feature Attribution attacks a specific failure mode of Integrated Gradients: even when path methods satisfy completeness, their intermediate samples can wander through noisy, off-manifold regions and accumulate spurious gradients, making saliency maps unstable and semantically implausible. The paper’s key idea is to transplant Guided IG into the latent space of a pre-trained variational autoencoder, so low-gradient features are still updated adaptively, but decoded intermediate states stay closer to the learned image manifold. This is unusual relative to nearby IG/GIG variants because the method is not just choosing a better pixel-space path or a geodesic in latent space; it explicitly couples gradient guidance with generative decoding, and analyzes the geometry via a tangent-space argument showing why axis-aligned input updates drift off-manifold while latent updates map to tangent directions. Experiments on ImageNet, Oxford-IIIT Pet, and Oxford 102 Flower with VGG16, ResNet18, and InceptionV1, using MAR, Stable Diffusion, and Kandinsky autoencoders, show higher DiffID and insertion scores, lower deletion scores, and reduced LPIPS drift than IG, GIG, EIG, and MIG, at moderate runtime cost. The work points toward attribution methods that treat explanation faithfulness as a manifold-constrained geometric problem rather than a purely gradient-aggregation problem.
papers.abstract: Feature attribution is central to diagnosing and trusting deep neural networks, and Integrated Gradients (IG) is widely used due to its axiomatic properties. However, IG can yield unreliable explanations when the integration path between a baseline and the input passes through regions with noisy gradients. While Guided Integrated Gradients reduces this sensitivity by adaptively updating low-gradient-magnitude features, input-space guidance still produces intermediate inputs that deviate from the data manifold. To address this limitation, we propose \emph{Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients} (MA-GIG), which constructs attribution paths in the latent space of a pre-trained variational autoencoder. By decoding intermediate latent states, MA-GIG biases the path toward the learned generative manifold and reduces exposure to implausible input-space regions. Through qualitative and quantitative evaluations, we demonstrate that MA-GIG produces faithful explanations by aggregating gradients on path features proximal to the input. Consequently, our method reduces off-manifold noise and outperforms prior path-based attribution methods across multiple datasets and classifiers. Our code is available at https://github.com/leekwoon/ma-gig/.

papers.title: Planner Matters! An Efficient and Unbalanced Multi-agent Collaboration Framework for Long-horizon Planning
papers.summary: Planner Matters! An Efficient and Unbalanced Multi-agent Collaboration Framework for Long-horizon Planning tackles a specific bottleneck in LM/VLM agents: they can act locally in web, OS, and tool-use environments, but fail on long-horizon tasks that require maintaining a global goal, revising subgoals, and avoiding myopic execution errors. The paper’s core idea is a three-role decomposition into Planner, Actor, and Memory Manager, then an explicit compute-allocation study showing that the Planner is the dominant performance lever while Actor and Memory can be much smaller without collapsing accuracy. This “unbalanced” finding is unusual compared with prior planner-actor work because it argues against symmetric scaling and instead optimizes only the high-level planner. Training uses planner-centric GRPO: the Planner is updated with trajectory-level rewards from a VLM-as-judge, while the Actor and Memory Manager are frozen, reducing credit-assignment noise and training cost. Experiments on WebVoyager, Mind2Web, MMInA, OSWorld, and MCPBench show large gains, including strong open-source results with a 7B planner plus 3B actor/memory configuration and better out-of-domain transfer. The work points toward planner-first agent design with compute concentrated on executive reasoning rather than execution.
papers.abstract: Language model (LM)-based agents have demonstrated promising capabilities in automating complex tasks from natural language instructions, yet they continue to struggle with long-horizon planning and reasoning. To address this, we propose an enhanced multi-agent framework that decomposes automation into three roles: a planner for high-level decision-making, an actor for task execution, and a memory manager for contextual reasoning. While this modular decomposition aligns with established design patterns, our core contribution lies in a systematic compute-allocation analysis, revealing that planning is the dominant factor influencing task performance. Execution and memory management require significantly less compute and model capacity to achieve competitive results. Building on these insights, we introduce a planner-centric reinforcement learning approach, which exclusively optimizes the planner using trajectory-level rewards from a VLM-as-judge, while freezing the other components. Extensive experiments on benchmarks spanning web navigation, OS control, and tool use demonstrate that concentrating model capacity and learning on high-level planning yields robust and compute-efficient improvements in long-horizon agent automation. Our code is publicly released.

papers.title: Heterogeneous Model Fusion for Privacy-Aware Multi-Camera Surveillance via Synthetic Domain Adaptation
papers.summary: Heterogeneous Model Fusion for Privacy-Aware Multi-Camera Surveillance via Synthetic Domain Adaptation attacks the real deployment bottleneck in smart-city camera networks: how to adapt object detectors across cameras and domains without centralizing raw imagery, while also surviving long-tailed classes, non-IID client data, and mismatched label sets/architectures. Its HeroCrystal pipeline combines three unusual pieces: a one-shot, target-aware diffusion generator that learns a target style from a single reference image using an instance prompt with a learned [V] token plus a generic prior-preservation prompt, then composes class prompts to synthesize controllable rare objects and pseudo-label them; a federated detection stage using probabilistic Faster R-CNN, where box coordinates are modeled as Gaussians to reduce localization uncertainty, paired with dynamic model contrastive learning that reverses Moon-style global/local roles when the global model is still weak; and a distilled fusion stage with inconsistent categories integration to reconcile heterogeneous heads by category overlap and IoU-consistent proposals. On cross-domain surveillance benchmarks including Sim10k/KITTI/Foggy→Cityscapes and Cityscapes/KITTI→BDD100K, it reports +2.1 mAP over prior privacy-preserving methods and 33.4 mAP overall, pointing toward privacy-aware federated detection built from synthetic domain adaptation rather than raw-data sharing.
papers.abstract: We propose HeroCrystal, a novel privacy-preserving framework for multi-camera domain-adaptive object detection, addressing challenges such as data privacy, class imbalance, and heterogeneous architectures. Our framework consists of three key stages. In the Generated Stage, we introduce a one-shot, target-aware diffusion-based generation module that learns visual style from a single target-domain image while leveraging prompt-based control to synthesize specific object instances. Unlike conventional style transfer-based methods that require large target datasets and ignore semantic-level discrepancies, our approach enables privacy-preserving augmentation to reduce ethical concerns, and introduces controllable rare object generation to mitigate long-tailed category degradation. In the Federated Stage, we employ probabilistic Faster R-CNN on the client side to improve localization accuracy, and a dynamic model contrastive strategy to suppress domain-specific bias. The server side performs model fusion across heterogeneous architectures without accessing raw data. Finally, in the Distilled Stage, we propose an inconsistent categories integration algorithm to resolve label inconsistency and architecture heterogeneity across clients. Extensive experiments on multiple cross-domain detection benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing multi-source domain adaptation and federated learning baselines under multi-class, privacy-preserving settings. Our method improves mAP by +2.1% over prior privacy-preserving approaches and achieves a new state-of-the-art mAP of 33.4%, highlighting the effectiveness of HeroCrystal in enabling practical multi-camera AI surveillance systems.

papers.title: CLaC at SemEval-2026 Task 6: Response Clarity Detection in Political Discourse
papers.summary: CLaC at SemEval-2026 Task 6: Response Clarity Detection in Political Discourse tackles automatic detection of answer clarity and evasion in 3,993 English question-answer pairs from U.S. presidential interviews, where labels are hierarchical: 3 Clarity classes and 9 finer Evasion categories. The paper contrasts an eight-encoder, four-stage adaptation pipeline with prompt-only LLM classifiers. On encoders, the unusual finding is that partial unfreezing of the top 25% of layers beats full fine-tuning by a wide margin, while weighted cross-entropy and dropout 0.1 are consistently preferred; class-balanced paraphrase augmentation helps only selectively, and Longformer with 2,048-token context does not solve the long-input problem. On the LLM side, a 27-shot balanced prompt with enriched input (the full interviewer turn) and subcategory-augmented label definitions drives the best performance, showing that inference-time label structuring can matter more than parameter updates. The final GPT-5/Gemini/Qwen ensemble reaches 80 macro-F1 on Task 1 and 59 on Task 2, with the dominant error being Clear Reply versus Ambivalent, mirroring annotator disagreement. The work points toward prompt engineering and label-hierarchy-aware inference as stronger levers than brute-force fine-tuning for low-resource political discourse classification.
papers.abstract: In this paper, we present our system for SemEval-2026 Task 6 (CLARITY) on response clarity and evasion detection in question-answer pairs from U.S. presidential interviews, comparing fine-tuned encoders with prompt-based LLMs. Our LLM ensemble achieves 80 macro-F1 on the 3-class Task 1 (9th/41) and 59 on the 9-class Task 2 (3rd/33). Across 8 transformer encoders optimized through a four-stage pipeline, partial encoder layer unfreezing outperforms full fine-tuning by a wide margin. Combining English and multilingual encoders further improves ensemble performance over either family alone, despite multilingual models being individually weaker. Prompt-based LLMs, without any task-specific parameter updates, outperform fine-tuned encoders, particularly on minority classes; among open-weight LLMs, parameter count does not predict performance. Enriched input, concatenating the full interviewer turn, improves LLM performance but not that of encoders, an effect that persists with Longformer's extended context window, suggesting the divergence is not attributable to sequence-length capacity alone in our settings. The Clear Reply/Ambivalent boundary remains the dominant failure mode, mirroring the disagreement among human annotators. Our code, prompts, model configurations, and results are publicly available.

papers.title: Retrieval and Multi-Hop Reasoning in 1M-Token Context Windows: Evaluating LLMs on Classical Chinese Text
papers.summary: Retrieval and Multi-Hop Reasoning in 1M-Token Context Windows: Evaluating LLMs on Classical Chinese Text examines whether frontier 1M-context models actually read a million-token corpus or merely exploit memorized biographical knowledge, using the classical Chinese Song-Yuan Xue’an as a high-density, leakage-prone testbed. The paper’s key methodological move is a paired real-versus-altered single-needle design: three historical facts are planted at 25/50/75% depths, and altered variants deliberately contradict pretraining priors to separate genuine in-context retrieval from parametric recall. A second task forces true intermediate reasoning via three-hop chain traversal across 256K, 512K, and 1M tiers, with fictional names to eliminate leakage and make the prompt behave like an implicit GraphRAG query over a tiny knowledge graph. Across five frontier models, single-needle retrieval is essentially solved by Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.7, and GPT-5.5, but multi-hop curves split into stable, late-cliff, and smooth-decline regimes, with GPT-5.5 and Qwen3.6-plus collapsing between 512K and 1M. The paper argues that advertised context length is a weak proxy for usable long-context reasoning and points toward tier-aware retrieval evaluation and GraphRAG-style scaffolding for humanities-scale corpora.
papers.abstract: We evaluate the long-context retrieval and reasoning capabilities of five frontier large language models with advertised 1M-token context windows on a classical Chinese corpus. Two complementary studies are reported. Test 1 measures single-needle retrieval at 1M tokens of input, with three biographical needles planted at three depths and pairs of real (training-prior-consistent) and altered (training-prior-contradicting) variants to separate genuine in-context retrieval from reliance on memorised training data. Test 2, a follow-up designed to probe whether long-context capability degrades when retrieval requires intermediate reasoning, measures three-hop chain traversal across three context tiers (256K, 512K, and 1M tokens). We find that single-needle retrieval at 1M is essentially solved for the strongest models - Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.7, and GPT-5.5 each achieve 100% - but that multi-hop performance reveals three distinct decay signatures: a stable regime (Gemini Pro, Claude) maintaining greater than 80% accuracy through 512K with modest degradation at 1M; a late-cliff regime (GPT-5.5, Qwen3.6-plus) collapsing sharply between 512K and 1M; and a smooth-decline regime (DeepSeek V4 Pro) decaying gradually across the entire range. The findings suggest that nominal context-window length is a poor proxy for usable long-context multi-hop capability, and that the sharpest discriminator between current 1M-context flagships is the 512K-to-1M transition.

papers.title: Intervention Complexity as a Canonical Reward and a Measure of Intelligence
papers.summary: Intervention Complexity as a Canonical Reward and a Measure of Intelligence attacks a gap in the Legg-Hutter framework: intelligence is defined as expected reward, but reward is externally stipulated and therefore arbitrary. McCane replaces reward with intervention complexity, the minimum resource cost of a program that drives a deterministic computable environment from state s to s′, using a resource function ρ to encode program length, execution time, action count, or energy. The paper axiomatizes this canonical reward by environment-derivedness, universality, minimality, sensitivity, and achievement preference, then shows IC forms a directed quasimetric with sub-additive composition. Intelligence is split into oracle competence and learning efficiency: a competence curve over IC difficulty levels, plus regret-based learning curves measuring how quickly experience closes the oracle-agent gap. A separation theorem gives sharp computability contrasts: action-count IC is computable in O(|S||A|), while bare program-length IC is uncomputable, and the oracle-bare gap quantifies information acquired through learning. The result reframes pretraining as world-model acquisition and positions superintelligence as bounded by environment structure rather than external reward design.
papers.abstract: The Legg--Hutter universal intelligence measure provides a rigorous scalar assessment of general intelligence as expected reward across all computable environments, weighted by simplicity. However, the measure presupposes an externally specified reward function, raising the question of whether the reward primitive is inherently arbitrary or whether a canonical choice exists. We propose a new measure, called intervention complexity, that has five natural properties: environment-derivedness, universality, minimality, sensitivity, and achievement preference. Given a resource function rho encoding an inductive bias (such as program length, execution time, or energy), rho-intervention complexity is a universal reward. The result yields a family of canonical rewards indexed by resource bias, providing a principled completion of the Legg--Hutter framework that does not require external normative input. We further propose a two-dimensional characterisation of intelligence: agent competence (how well the agent performs relative to the oracle optimum) and learning efficiency (how quickly this competence improves with experience). A separation theorem establishes that the choice of resource bias determines the computability of the resulting measure: action-count IC is computable in polynomial time, while program-length IC without oracle access is uncomputable, with the gap between oracle and bare IC precisely quantifying the information-theoretic content of learning. We discuss implications for superintelligence and for pre-training universal agents.

papers.title: The Causal Description Gap: Information-Theoretic Separations Across Pearl's Hierarchy
papers.summary: The Causal Description Gap: Information-Theoretic Separations Across Pearl's Hierarchy quantifies Pearl’s qualitative causal hierarchy in bits by asking how much extra information is needed to specify interventional and counterfactual answer oracles once observations are fixed. The paper formalizes query-class description length as the Kolmogorov complexity of an SCM-induced oracle, then builds explicit binary acyclic SCM families that make the lower-rung oracle deceptively simple while hiding large higher-rung ambiguity. A rooted-tree family gives constant observational description length but a Θ(n log n) observation-to-intervention gap; a denser bipartite-graph construction upgrades this to a Θ(n^2) gap, with each edge encoded by single-node do-interventions and with robustness under ε-total-variation approximation for every ε<1/4. A degree-sensitive upper bound for finite-gate-schema SCMs with indegree d shows the gap is at most O(nd log(en/d)+n log n), proving the tree and dense constructions are order-optimal in their regimes. At the next rung, a modular-XOR family leaves a Θ(n) counterfactual gap even after the full hard-do interventional oracle. The paper’s ambiguity-to-bits theorem turns residual causal non-identifiability into a log-cardinality bound, pointing toward causal complexity theory for data, interventions, and AI reasoning limits.
papers.abstract: Pearl's causal hierarchy shows that observational, interventional, and counterfactual queries are qualitatively distinct. We ask a quantitative version of this question: how many additional bits are needed to specify higher-rung causal answers once lower-rung answers are known? We formalize this via query-class description length, the Kolmogorov complexity of the answer oracle induced by an SCM for a class of queries. Our main construction gives binary acyclic SCMs whose observational distribution has constant description length, while the single-variable interventional answer oracle has description length $Θ(n^2)$. A degree-sensitive upper bound shows that finite-gate-schema SCMs of indegree $d$ have observational-interventional gap at most $O(nd \log(en/d) + n \log n)$, making the quadratic construction order-optimal in the dense regime and a rooted-tree construction order-optimal for bounded indegree. The quadratic separation persists under $\varepsilon$-accurate total-variation descriptions for every fixed $\varepsilon < 1/4$. At the next rung, the full hard-do interventional oracle can still leave a $Θ(n)$ counterfactual description gap. A general ambiguity-to-bits theorem and Shannon analogue show that these gaps equal the logarithm of residual higher-rung ambiguity up to lower-order terms.

papers.title: T$^2$PO: Uncertainty-Guided Exploration Control for Stable Multi-Turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: T$^2$PO: Uncertainty-Guided Exploration Control for Stable Multi-Turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning attacks a specific failure mode in long-horizon LLM agent training: “hesitation,” where policies keep emitting low-information tokens or repetitive turns that do not reduce uncertainty, causing unstable gradients, exploding KL, and eventual training collapse even after credit assignment and trajectory filtering. The core idea is to regulate exploration explicitly at two granularities using a self-calibrated uncertainty signal that fuses entropy and confidence: at the token level, Token-level Thinking Intervention monitors marginal uncertainty change and deterministically forces </think> plus an action prefix when uncertainty saturates; at the turn level, Turn-level Dynamical Sampling resamples whole interactions whose aggregated uncertainty profile barely changes across turns. Unlike reward-shaping or coarse trajectory pruning, this is a direct control mechanism over reasoning dynamics, paired with hierarchical advantage estimation and a memory context window. Evaluated on WebShop, ALFWorld, and Search QA with Qwen3-4B/8B-RFT backbones under multi-turn RL, it improves success, reduces variance, and cuts token/turn consumption, pointing toward uncertainty-aware, budgeted agentic RL as a stabilizing paradigm for interactive LLMs.
papers.abstract: Recent progress in multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL) has significantly improved reasoning LLMs' performances on complex interactive tasks. Despite advances in stabilization techniques such as fine-grained credit assignment and trajectory filtering, instability remains pervasive and often leads to training collapse. We argue that this instability stems from inefficient exploration in multi-turn settings, where policies continue to generate low-information actions that neither reduce uncertainty nor advance task progress. To address this issue, we propose Token- and Turn-level Policy Optimization (T$^2$PO), an uncertainty-aware framework that explicitly controls exploration at fine-grained levels. At the token level, T$^2$PO monitors uncertainty dynamics and triggers a thinking intervention once the marginal uncertainty change falls below a threshold. At the turn level, T$^2$PO identifies interactions with negligible exploration progress and dynamically resamples such turns to avoid wasted rollouts. We evaluate T$^2$PO in diverse environments, including WebShop, ALFWorld, and Search QA, demonstrating substantial gains in training stability and performance improvements with better exploration efficiency. Code is available at: https://github.com/WillDreamer/T2PO.

papers.title: Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training for Long-Tailed Robustness via Geometric Alignment
papers.summary: Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training for Long-Tailed Robustness via Geometric Alignment tackles why standard adversarial training collapses on imbalanced datasets: head classes dominate optimization, tail classes lose robust margin, and attacks drift into semantically unsupported feature-space regions. MCAT couples two unusual ingredients in one min-max objective: a class-conditional manifold penalty that uses frozen lightweight feature-space MLP generators Gy as proxies for each class manifold and a simplex Equiangular Tight Frame-inspired Gram regularizer that pushes classifier weights toward balanced angular separation. Its inner loop is a manifold-supported PGD variant (MS-PGD) that still respects the ℓ∞ threat model while biasing adversarial search toward high-density semantic support. Unlike prior long-tailed AT methods that mainly reweight losses, distill, or stage optimization, MCAT explicitly controls representation geometry and adversarial validity. On CIFAR-10-LT, CIFAR-100-LT, and Tiny-ImageNet-LT under IR up to 100, with ResNet-18/WRN-34-10 backbones and PGD/AutoAttack evaluation, MCAT consistently improves overall, balanced, and tail-class robust accuracy. The theory links ETF separation to lower bounds on robust margins and shows manifold-constrained risk upper-bounds robust risk on semantic regions, pointing toward geometry-aware robustness for skewed real-world data.
papers.abstract: Adversarial training is effective on balanced datasets, but its robustness degrades under longtailed class distributions, where tail classes suffer high robust error and unstable decision boundaries. We propose Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training (MCAT), a unified framework that enforces the semantic validity of adversarial examples by penalizing deviations from class-conditional manifolds in feature space, while promoting balanced geometric separation across classes via an ETF-inspired regularization. We provide theoretical results that link geometric separation to lower bounds on adversarially robust margins, and show that manifold-constrained adversarial risk upperbounds robust risk on high-density semantic regions. Extensive experiments on standard longtailed benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in overall, balanced, and tail-class adversarial robustness.

papers.title: RAFNet: Region-Aware Fusion Network for Pansharpening
papers.summary: RAFNet: Region-Aware Fusion Network for Pansharpening tackles the core pansharpening tension in remote sensing: recovering HRMS images from LRMS and PAN without either spectral drift from static spatial filters or the quadratic cost and redundancy of global attention. Its key idea is to couple frequency decomposition with semantic regional structure. In the Spatial Adaptive Refinement (SAR) module, a DWT splits PAN features into LL/LH/HL/HH bands, then differentiable soft K-means partitions the low-frequency structure into semantic regions that drive low-rank, region-specific adaptive convolution kernels for each sub-band. In the Clustered Frequency Aggregation (CFA) module, the same cluster mask routes attention so intra-cluster interactions stay dense while inter-cluster links are collapsed to cluster-mean keys, yielding sparse, region-aware frequency fusion instead of windowed or fully global attention.
