Trained end-to-end with L1 loss on WorldView-3 and GaoFen-2 under Wald’s protocol, RAFNet reports state-of-the-art reduced- and full-resolution scores, including better PSNR/SSIM/SAM and lower ERGAS plus HQNR/Dλ/Ds. The paper points toward cluster-guided, wavelet-aware fusion as a general recipe for efficient high-resolution remote sensing reconstruction.
papers.abstract: Pansharpening aims to generate high-resolution multispectral (HRMS) images by fusing low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) and high-resolution panchromatic (PAN) images. Although deep learning has advanced this field, mainstream frequency-based methods relying on standard scaled dot-product attention suffer from quadratic computational complexity and fail to exploit the inherent regional sparsity of remote sensing imagery. Furthermore, existing spatial enhancement strategies typically employ static convolution kernels, which struggle to adapt to the complex frequency and regional variations of PAN and MS images. To address these bottlenecks, we propose a Region-Aware Fusion (RAFNet) Network that synergistically models spatial and frequency information. Specifically, we design a Spatial Adaptive Refinement (SAR) module that leverages the discrete wavelet transform (DWT) for directional frequency separation and K-means clustering for regional partitioning, which enables the dynamic construction of region-specific adaptive convolution kernels, achieving spatially and frequency-adaptive feature enhancement. Moreover, we introduce a Clustered Frequency Aggregation (CFA) module based on a sparse attention mechanism guided by the semantic clusters, which executes a region-aware sparse attention strategy that drastically reduces computational redundancy while ensuring high-quality frequency feature extraction. In addition we integrated these modules into a progressive, multi-level spatial-frequency network architecture to facilitate robust interaction and accurate image reconstruction. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed RAFNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art pansharpening methods in both reduced- and full-resolution assessments. The code is available at https://github.com/PatrickNod/RAFNet.

papers.title: When Alignment Isn't Enough: Response-Path Attacks on LLM Agents
papers.summary: When Alignment Isn't Enough: Response-Path Attacks on LLM Agents studies a trust-boundary failure in BYOK agent deployments, where a user-configured third-party relay can rewrite an LLM’s already safety-aligned reply after generation but before the agent executes it. The paper formalizes this “post-alignment tampering” gap with game-based security definitions and shows that response-path forgery strictly dominates prompt injection because the relay, not the model, controls the schema-valid tool call or finish-reason fields the agent actually consumes. Its concrete attack, Relay Tampering Attack (RTA), combines strategic orchestration over multi-turn subgoals, minimal field-level edits, and same-model polishing that resubmits the tampered draft through the victim’s own BYOK key to restore the model’s style. Evaluated on AgentDojo and ASB across six proprietary and open-weight LLMs, RTA reaches up to 99.1% attack success, with RTA-PostForge preserving more utility and imperceptibility than pre-alignment rewriting. Case studies on OpenClaw and Claude Code show practical exfiltration and action hijacking, while four representative defenses fail to fully stop the attack. The paper points toward end-to-end response integrity and telemetry-based relay anomaly detection for future agent protocols.
papers.abstract: Bring-Your-Own-Key (BYOK) agent architectures let users route LLM traffic through third-party relays, creating a critical integrity gap: a malicious relay can modify an aligned LLM response after generation but before agent execution. We formalize this post-alignment tampering threat and show that, without end-to-end integrity, the relay can observe, suppress, or replace downstream messages, making even perfectly aligned LLMs ineffective against such attacks. We instantiate this threat as the Relay Tampering Attack (RTA), which performs multi-round strategic rewriting, minimal security-critical edits, and stealth restoration by resubmitting tampered outputs to the upstream LLM. Across AgentDojo and ASB with six LLMs, RTA achieves up to 99.1% attack success, outperforming prompt-injection baselines with modest overhead. Case studies on OpenClaw and Claude Code demonstrate real-world feasibility, and evaluations of four defenses show that none fully prevent RTA. Finally, we propose a time-based detection defense that mitigates RTA while preserving agent utility.

papers.title: KANs need curvature: penalties for compositional smoothness
papers.summary: KANs need curvature: penalties for compositional smoothness tackles the interpretability bottleneck in Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, where accurate fits often arise from edge activations that oscillate with pathological curvature and become unreadable, while the standard KAN penalty only shrinks magnitudes and sparsifies edge usage rather than controlling derivatives. The paper derives a basis-agnostic edge-wise bending-energy penalty for each univariate activation, implemented for cubic B-splines as a second-difference penalty on spline coefficients plus an explicit SiLU curvature term, and extends the same idea to RBF-based FastKAN. Its distinctive contribution is a compositional Hessian analysis: because KAN layer Hessians are diagonal, the full input-Hessian of the network decomposes into path-weighted edge curvatures, enabling a proof that the coefficient-space curvature penalty upper-bounds function curvature under mild assumptions. On synthetic function approximation, the Feynman symbolic-regression benchmark, and overparameterized high-grid regimes, curvature-regularized KANs preserved test accuracy while reducing activation roughness by orders of magnitude; a weighted variant using expected path sensitivities improved RMSE further. The work points toward smoothness-aware, compositionally principled regularization for interpretable scientific machine learning.
papers.abstract: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) offer a potent combination of accuracy and interpretability, thanks to their compositions of learnable univariate activation functions. However, the activations of well-fitting KANs tend to exhibit pathologically high-curvature oscillations, making them difficult to interpret, and standard regularization penalties do not prevent this. Here we derive a basis-agnostic curvature penalty and show that penalized models can maintain accuracy while achieving substantially smoother activations. Accounting for how function composition shapes curvature, we prove an upper bound on the full model's curvature relative to the curvature penalty, and use this to motivate richer forms of penalties. Scientific machine learning is increasingly bottlenecked by the trade-off between accuracy and interpretability. Results such as ours that improve interpretability without sacrificing accuracy will further strengthen KANs as a practical tool for both prediction and insight.

papers.title: Trees and Graphs with Non Log-concave Dominating Set Sequence via AI Tools
papers.summary: Trees and Graphs with Non Log-concave Dominating Set Sequence via AI Tools attacks the long-open domination-polynomial problem of whether the dominating set counts d_j(G) are unimodal or even log-concave for every graph, a question still unresolved for trees. The paper’s main AI contribution is using PatternBoost, a transformer-based reinforcement-learning search tool, to optimize a reward tied to local log-concavity violation and to generate counterexamples de novo, without internet data or prior mathematical literature. This yields new 12-vertex graphs and tree examples with non log-concave dominating set sequences, complementing an explicit hand-built family W_{m,t} that breaks log-concavity at arbitrarily many prescribed indices by modifying Bautista-Ramos-type rooted gadgets. In parallel, the authors prove a large class of legged caterpillars has log-concave domination polynomials, and they recast dominating sets as integer points in a polytope to show a continuous volume analogue is always log-concave by Brunn-Minkowski, highlighting that the pathology is genuinely discrete. The paper points toward AI-assisted extremal combinatorics where reinforcement learning helps discover structured counterexamples while theory isolates the exact combinatorial mechanism.
papers.abstract: We give new examples of graphs and trees with dominating set sequences that are not log-concave. These examples were generated by PatternBoost, a transformer-based reinforcement learning software developed by Charton-Ellenberg-Wagner-Williamson. We also show: for any positive integer $m$, there exists a tree whose dominating set sequence is not log-concave for at least $m$ indices by modifying a similar construction of Bautista-Ramos for the independent set sequence. We show that a large class of caterpillar graphs has log-concave dominating set sequences. A continuous analogue of the sequence is also log-concave for all graphs.

papers.title: DurableUn: Quantization-Induced Recovery Attacks in Machine Unlearning
papers.summary: DurableUn: Quantization-Induced Recovery Attacks in Machine Unlearning examines a deployment mismatch in privacy-preserving LLM unlearning: models can appear compliant after forgetting at BF16 yet resurrect forgotten facts when later run at standard INT4 inference precision. The paper formalizes this as quantization recovery attack (QRA) and studies adapter-space INT4 perturbations in the NF4+LoRA regime on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, comparing seven unlearning methods (GA, NPO, SCRUB, SalUn, RMU, AlphaEdit, GradDiff) on TOFU, MUSE-News, and WikiBio-WPU. A key empirical finding is that INT8 is essentially harmless, but INT4 can restore forgotten content by up to 22x, with stronger recovery on harder datasets; this exposes an FA–RA–Q-INT4 trilemma in which no method simultaneously attains strong forgetting, utility retention, and quantization robustness. To address the sharp phase transition observed in dense Pareto sweeps, the authors propose DURABLEUN-SAF, a sharpness-aware forgetting objective that backpropagates through simulated INT4 rounding via the Straight-Through Estimator. It is the only method to achieve a stable empirical durability certificate across BF16, INT8, and INT4 (cert rate 3/3), pointing unlearning research toward precision-aware privacy evaluation rather than BF16-only audits.
papers.abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove specified training data to satisfy privacy regulations such as GDPR. However, existing evaluations assume identical precision at unlearning and deployment, overlooking that production LLMs are deployed at low-bit precision. We show that INT4 quantization systematically restores forgotten content even when models pass compliance audits at bfloat16 (BF16), we term this the quantization recovery attack (QRA). We conduct the first systematic study of unlearning robustness under adapter-space INT4 quantization in the NF4+LoRA regime, evaluating seven methods on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct across TOFU, MUSE-News, and WikiBio-WPU. INT8 is benign; INT4 induces recovery of up to 22x, worsening with dataset difficulty. We identify the FA-RA-Q-INT4 trilemma: no method simultaneously achieves strong forgetting, high utility, and quantization robustness. A dense Pareto sweep reveals a sharp phase transition once robustness is achieved, retaining accuracy collapses regardless of further tuning. To address this, we propose DURABLEUN-SAF (Sharpness-Aware Forgetting), a quantization-aware objective using Straight-Through Estimator gradients through INT4 rounding. DURABLEUN-SAF is the only method to achieve a stable empirical (0.047, {BF16, INT8, INT4})- durability certificate: Q-INT4= 0.043 +- 0.002, cert rate= 3/3, versus SalUn's cert rate= 1/3 at its own published hyperparameters. We call for Q-INT4 to be adopted as a standard evaluation metric alongside FA and RA.

papers.title: SlimDiffSR: Toward Lightweight and Efficient Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution via Diffusion Model Distillation
papers.summary: SlimDiffSR: Toward Lightweight and Efficient Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution via Diffusion Model Distillation tackles the deployment bottleneck of diffusion-based remote sensing SR, where multi-step samplers and large latent diffusion backbones are too slow for Earth-observation workloads. The paper’s key idea is a two-stage teacher–student pipeline: an uncertainty-guided single-step teacher first maps pixel-wise reconstruction difficulty to adaptive diffusion timesteps via latent uncertainty estimation and timestep inversion, replacing the fixed-timestep design used by prior one-step SR diffusion models. The teacher is then compressed with remote-sensing-aware structured pruning that deletes redundant deep semantic UNet blocks and substitutes standard operators with frequency-separable convolution, direction-separable convolution, and a query-driven global aggregation module for sparse high-frequency details, anisotropic structures, and long-range context. Distillation combines MSE with MMD to align feature distributions, not just pointwise activations. Evaluated on AID-trained models and test sets from NWPU-RESISC45, DOTA, DIOR, plus real Sentinel-2/NAIP pairs, SlimDiffSR reaches competitive perceptual quality while delivering up to 200× speedup and 20× fewer parameters than multi-step diffusion baselines, pointing toward domain-specific compression of generative restoration models.
papers.abstract: Diffusion models have recently achieved remarkable performance in image super-resolution (SR), but their high computational cost limits practical deployment in remote sensing applications. To address this issue, we propose SlimDiffSR, a lightweight and efficient diffusion-based framework for real-world remote sensing image super-resolution. Unlike existing single-step diffusion methods that rely on fixed timesteps, we first introduce an uncertainty-guided timestep assignment strategy to construct a stronger single-step teacher model, where reconstruction difficulty is explicitly linked to diffusion timesteps, enabling adaptive generative strength. Building upon this teacher, we further present a structured pruning strategy tailored to remote sensing imagery, which systematically removes redundant semantic modules and replaces standard operations with lightweight designs, including frequency-separable convolution, direction-separable convolution, and a query-driven global aggregation module. These components explicitly exploit the unique characteristics of remote sensing data, such as sparse high-frequency details, strong directional patterns, and long-range spatial dependencies. To enhance knowledge transfer, we incorporate Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) into the distillation process to align feature distributions between the teacher and student models. Extensive experiments on multiple remote sensing benchmarks demonstrate that SlimDiffSR achieves a favorable balance between efficiency and reconstruction quality. In particular, it attains up to $200\times$ inference acceleration and a $20\times$ reduction in model parameters compared with multi-step diffusion models, while achieving competitive perceptual quality and clearly outperforming existing lightweight diffusion baselines in efficiency. The code is available at: https://github.com/wwangcece/SlimDiffSR.

papers.title: MEMAUDIT: An Exact Package-Oracle Evaluation Protocol for Budgeted Long-Term LLM Memory Writing
papers.summary: MEMAUDIT: An Exact Package-Oracle Evaluation Protocol for Budgeted Long-Term LLM Memory Writing targets the core evaluation flaw in long-horizon agent memory: final QA scores confound write-time compression with retrieval, prompting, and reader reasoning. The paper freezes a “package” of candidate memories, storage costs, semantic evidence units, future-query requirements, and a fixed budget, turning memory writing into an auditable finite optimization problem with a certified denominator. Its key mechanism is a concave-over-modular semantic coverage objective under a knapsack-plus-partition-matroid constraint, so each experience may contribute at most one persistent representation, such as raw span, atomic fact, summary, tombstone, or compound update. Exact optima are computed by branch-and-bound and independently certified with MILP, while a package-oracle baseline (GVT) calibrates the benchmark. Experiments span controlled exact-small sweeps, validity-heavy update-chain and temporal-interval stress packages, a human-audited Natural-87 support-slice benchmark, and exported Mem0, A-Mem, and Letta stores. The main claim is that MEMAUDIT localizes whether failures come from representation choice, validity-state preservation, or budget-aware selection, pointing toward write-time memory evaluation and certified semantic-compression benchmarks rather than downstream answer accuracy alone.
papers.abstract: Long-term LLM agents must compress streams of past interactions into persistent memory before future queries are known. Existing evaluations usually measure final question-answering accuracy, which entangles memory writing with retrieval, prompting, and reader reasoning. We introduce MEMAUDIT, an exact packageoracle evaluation protocol for budgeted long-term memory writing. A MEMAUDIT package fixes an experience stream, candidate memory representations, storage costs, semantic evidence units, future-query requirements, and a budget, turning write-time memory selection into a finite auditable optimization problem with a certified denominator. We instantiate this protocol with a concave-over-modular semantic coverage objective under storage and one-representation-per-experience constraints, and compute exact package optima using branch-and-bound with MILP certification. Across controlled exact packages, validity-heavy stress tests, human-audited natural support slices, and exported Mem0, A-Mem, and Letta stores, MEMAUDIT separates representation quality, validity-state preservation, and budget-aware selection effects that end-to-end QA cannot localize. The resulting artifact provides reusable package generators, certified solvers, natural package exports, external-system scorers, and cached reproducibility metadata for evaluating what memory writers actually preserve under fixed storage budgets.

papers.title: ARGUS: Policy-Adaptive Ad Governance via Evolving Reinforcement with Adversarial Umpiring
papers.summary: ARGUS: Policy-Adaptive Ad Governance via Evolving Reinforcement with Adversarial Umpiring tackles a concrete industrial bottleneck: ad moderation models trained on historical policy logs break when regulations shift, because new mandates such as K12 achievement-driven tutoring, body/aesthetic anxiety, information-arbitrage inducement, high-pressure purchase inducement, and non-medical health exaggeration create label drift and gray-area reasoning ambiguity. The key idea is a three-stage evolving reinforcement pipeline: Policy Seeding mixes sparse gold samples with curated legacy data, Adversarial Label Rectification uses a Prosecutor-Defender-Umpire debate to overwrite stale labels with RAG-grounded, policy-clause-adjudicated verdicts, and Latent Knowledge Discovery mines hard compliant-labeled samples with high emerging-policy probability via a tripartite dialectic including a Skeptic. Unlike static fine-tuning or ordinary RLHF, ARGUS turns Chain-of-Thought synthesis itself into dynamic reward signals under GRPO, so reasoning and labels co-evolve with policy changes. Experiments on a 680k-sample industrial multimodal ad dataset and ToxiCN MM show stronger emerging-policy precision/recall than SFT, replay, and EWC while preserving historical performance; online A/B tests further cut violation leakage and raise automation. The paper points toward policy-aware, debate-driven multimodal governance systems that continuously adapt to regulatory non-stationarity.
papers.abstract: Online advertising governance faces significant challenges due to the non-stationary nature of regulatory policies, where emerging mandates (e.g., restrictions on education or aesthetic anxiety) create severe label inconsistencies and reasoning ambiguities in historical datasets. In this paper, we propose ARGUS, a policy-adaptive governance system that enables evolving reinforcement through multi-agent adversarial umpiring. ARGUS addresses the sparsity of new policy data by employing a three-stage framework: (1) Policy Seeding for initial perception; (2) Adversarial Label Rectification, which utilizes a ``Prosecutor-Defender-Umpire'' architecture to resolve conflicts between stale labels and new mandates; and (3) Latent Knowledge Discovery, which employs a tripartite dialectical discussion to unearth sophisticated, ``gray-area'' violations. By leveraging RAG-enhanced policy knowledge and Chain-of-Thought synthesis as dynamic rewards for reinforcement learning, ARGUS synchronizes its reasoning pathways with evolving regulations. Extensive experiments on both industrial and public datasets demonstrate that ARGUS significantly outperforms traditional fine-tuning baselines, achieving superior policy-adaptive learning with minimal gold data.

papers.title: Super-resolution of airborne laser scanning point clouds for forest inventory
papers.summary: Super-resolution of airborne laser scanning point clouds for forest inventory tackles the bottleneck that ALS is wide-area but too sparse and noisy for individual-tree measurements, especially stem localization and DBH extraction. The paper’s 3D Forest Super Resolution (3DFSR) reframes scene-level point cloud enhancement as voxel occupancy prediction rather than direct coordinate regression, using an octree-based, voxel CNN U-Net with residual blocks and skip connections to jointly upsample and denoise forest structure. This is unusual compared with nearby point-upsampling work because it is forest-specific, operates on irregular scene-scale ALS rather than clean object scans, and targets downstream forestry analytics rather than only geometric fidelity. The model is trained and tested on plots from temperate U.S. forests and boreal German forests, with TLS/MLS references for supervision and evaluation, then stress-tested on downsampled UAV LiDAR densities from 10 to 1700 points/m2 and across LiDAR platforms without transfer learning. 3DFSR outperforms PU-Net, GradPU, and interpolation, and its outputs let TLS/MLS stem detectors run directly, raising stem-detection F1 from 0.71 to 0.97 and reducing DBH RMSE from 13.45 cm to 6.43 cm, pointing toward sensor-agnostic explicit forest inventory from airborne data.
papers.abstract: Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS) can collect point clouds across large areas, enabling large-scale forest inventory. However, ALS point clouds are sparse and noisy, resulting in inaccurate individual-tree-level forest inventory, such as stem localization and tree size estimation. To overcome this problem, we propose a deep learning model, 3D Forest Super Resolution (3DFSR), to simultaneously improve point density and reduce noise for ALS forest point cloud. 3DFSR is a voxel-based CNN with a U-Net architecture. The proposed 3DFSR is evaluated on ALS point clouds collected in both temperate forests in the U.S. and boreal forests in Germany. Experimental results demonstrate that 3DFSR can generate finer point clouds of tree structure than other state-of-the-art point cloud super-resolution algorithms, achieving 0.249 m Chamfer Distance and 2.711 m Hausdorff Distance. Furthermore, to verify the effectiveness of 3DFSR point clouds in forest inventory, we conduct stem detection, DBH measurements, and stem reconstruction on both original ALS point clouds and 3DFSR enhanced point clouds. We find that stem detection and reconstruction algorithms developed for TLS/MLS point clouds can directly work on our 3DFSR point clouds, and DBH can be derived with circle-fitting method. F1 score of stem detection is improved from 0.71 on original ALS point clouds to 0.97 on 3DFSR point clouds; DBH estimation improves from 13.45 cm RMSE using allometric equations to 6.43 cm using circle fitting; comparing to stems reconstruction from MLS point clouds, stem reconstructed from 3DFSR point clouds has 0.170 m of Chamfer Distance and 0.377 m of Hausdorff Distance, and 0.95 R2 volume estimation. Finally, we find that the proposed 3DFSR is applicable to process point densities from 10 to 1700 points/m2; it also can be generalized across data collected from different LiDAR platforms without transfer learning.

papers.title: CBV: Clean-label Backdoor Attacks on Vision Language Models via Diffusion Models
papers.summary: CBV: Clean-label Backdoor Attacks on Vision Language Models via Diffusion Models attacks the harder clean-label poisoning setting for VLMs, where attackers must implant a backdoor without changing text labels and without leaving the obvious image-text mismatch exploited by prior VLM attacks. The core idea is to use a diffusion model as a poisoned-example generator: CBV learns a universal adversarial perturbation trigger, then modifies the reverse-process score during denoising so the synthesized image inherits triggered visual features while remaining natural. Unlike surrogate-classifier clean-label attacks that approximate class geometry, CBV injects multimodal guidance from both the triggered image and its caption in CLIP embedding space, and further constrains edits with a GradCAM-guided mask so only semantically salient regions are rewritten. The method is evaluated in a black-box data-poisoning setting on MSCOCO captioning and VQA v2 across LLaVA-v1.5-7B, MiniGPT-v2, InstructBLIP-7B, and Qwen-2.5-VL-7B, with poisoning rate 5%. It achieves over 80% attack success rate while keeping BLEU, CIDEr, and VQA accuracy close to clean training, and remains effective against fine-pruning, STRIP, and Neural Cleanse, pointing toward diffusion-native, stealth-first multimodal poisoning research.
papers.abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering (VQA). However, as their applications become increasingly widespread, recent studies have revealed that VLMs are vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing backdoor attacks on VLMs primarily rely on data poisoning by adding visual triggers and modifying text labels, where the induced image-text mismatch makes poisoned samples easy to detect. To address this limitation, we propose the Clean-Label Backdoor Attack on VLMs via Diffusion Models (CBV), which leverages diffusion models to generate natural poisoned examples via score matching. Specifically, CBV modifies the score during the reverse generation process of the diffusion model to guide the generation of poisoned samples that contain triggered image features. To further enhance the effectiveness of the attack, we incorporate the textual information of the triggered images as multimodal guidance during generation. Moreover, to enhance stealthiness, we introduce a GradCAM-guided Mask (GM) that restricts modifications to only the most semantically important regions, rather than the entire image. We evaluate our method on MSCOCO and VQA v2 with four representative VLMs, achieving over 80% ASR while preserving normal functionality.

papers.title: 2D Stability Selection: Design Jittering for Doubly Stable Feature Selection
papers.summary: 2D Stability Selection: Design Jittering for Doubly Stable Feature Selection attacks a gap in high-dimensional regression feature screening: standard Stability Selection stabilizes against resampling noise, but not against corrupted predictors or measurement error in the design matrix. The key idea is a perturb-and-aggregate “design jittering” scheme that adds controlled Gaussian noise X+W at a grid of noise scales δ, fits a fixed selector such as Lasso, elastic net, or SCAD on each perturbed design, and tracks selection frequencies across both random replicates B and increasing perturbation magnitude, forming a 2D stability/noise path. This differs from nearby work by using full-sample fits rather than half-sampling, and by treating robustness to design noise as a first-class criterion. The authors prove that Lasso’s irrepresentable condition survives sufficiently small perturbations and give a high-probability Gaussian analog, then show exact support recovery when δ-averaged frequencies separate active from inactive variables. In simulations with n=100, p=1000 under latent measurement error, jittering beats Stability Selection and single fits; on the Affymetrix Rat Genome 230 microarray it selects TRIM32-associated probes where Stability Selection selects none. The paper points toward noise-aware, perturbation-path-based variable selection for unreliable sensing and genomics.
papers.abstract: We study feature selection in high-dimensional regression under two distinct sources of instability: sampling variability and measurement error in the design matrix. Stability Selection addresses the former through sub-sampling and aggregation, but does not explicitly stress-test robustness to noisy predictors. We introduce doubly stable feature selection, a perturb-and-aggregate framework that targets features whose inclusion is stable both across randomization and across increasing levels of design noise. The method injects controlled additive noise into the design matrix, fits a fixed base selector such as the Lasso on the perturbed data, and aggregates selection frequencies. Sweeping over a grid of noise levels yields a stability path that summarizes robustness to measurement error while using the full sample size and isolating the effect of design perturbations. On the theory side, we show that classical model-selection conditions are preserved under sufficiently small perturbations, with a high-probability extension for Gaussian noise. Empirically, experiments on synthetic and real datasets show improved robustness compared with Stability Selection and standard base selectors.

papers.title: Metric Unreliability in Multimodal Machine Unlearning: A Systematic Analysis and Principled Unified Score
papers.summary: Metric Unreliability in Multimodal Machine Unlearning: A Systematic Analysis and Principled Unified Score dissects why VLM unlearning evaluation becomes incoherent under GDPR-style deletion requests: standard metrics are not measuring the same thing. Using 36 unlearned LLaVA-1.5-7B checkpoints across MLLMU-Bench, UnLOK-VQA, and MMUBench, the authors show that Forget Accuracy, Retain Accuracy, Membership Inference Attack, Activation Distance, and JS divergence split into two antagonistic camps, with output-based scores {FA, RA, MIA} disagreeing with oracle-alignment scores {AD, JS}; Kendall’s tau reaches FA–AD = −0.26 and the mismatch reproduces on BLIP-2 OPT-2.7B. The paper argues that multimodal VQA amplifies this contradiction relative to unimodal CIFAR-10 because image and text pathways decouple. It also identifies a missing evaluation target, knowledge recoverability, demonstrated by rephrased, indirect, and negation probes that recover supposedly forgotten facts even when FA is zero. To resolve ranking instability, the authors introduce Unified Quality Score (UQS), weighting metrics by Spearman correlation with distance to the retain-only oracle model M*, which gives RA the largest weight and downweights negatively reliable FA; UQS is stable under weight perturbations and points toward calibrated, reliability-aware unlearning benchmarks.
papers.abstract: Machine unlearning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is required for compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), yet current evaluation practices are inconsistent. We present the first systematic study of metric reliability in multimodal unlearning. Five standard metrics, Forget Accuracy (FA), Retain Accuracy (RA), Membership Inference Attack (MIA), Activation Distance (AD), and JS divergence (JS), yield conflicting method rankings across three VQA benchmarks (MLLMU-Bench, UnLOK-VQA, MMUBench). Kendall tau analysis over 36 unlearned LLaVA-1.5-7B models reveals two opposing clusters, {FA, RA, MIA} and {AD, JS}, with tau_FA_AD = -0.26, reproduced on BLIP-2 OPT-2.7B. Agreement is lower in multimodal VQA (average tau = 0.086) than in unimodal classification (average tau = 0.158; difference = 0.072), indicating that dual image-and-text pathways amplify inconsistency. We introduce the Unified Quality Score (UQS), a composite metric with weights derived from each metric's Spearman correlation with the oracle distance d(M_hat, M_star), where M_star is the oracle model retrained only on the retain set. RA shows the strongest reliability (rho = 0.484, p = 0.003), while FA is negatively correlated (rho = -0.418, p = 0.011). UQS yields stable rankings under 100 random weight perturbations (tau = 0.647 +- 0.262). We release the benchmark, 36 checkpoints, and an interactive leaderboard. Code and pre-computed results are available at https://github.com/neurips26/UnifiedUnl.

papers.title: MultiSense-Pneumo: A Multimodal Learning Framework for Pneumonia Screening in Resource-Constrained Settings
papers.summary: MultiSense-Pneumo: A Multimodal Learning Framework for Pneumonia Screening in Resource-Constrained Settings tackles the mismatch between how pneumonia is actually screened in low-resource clinics—through partial, heterogeneous evidence—and how most computational systems still privilege chest X-rays alone. The framework turns four evidence streams into normalized risk signals: a deterministic symptom-triage module with hard escalation rules for severe dyspnea, confusion, or pediatric chest indrawing; a cough branch using MFCC statistics plus RMS/ZCR and a LightGBM classifier trained with class weighting on Coswara coughs; a speech branch that transcribes free-form spoken input with Whisper Small and extracts a keyword-based clinical signal; and a ResNet-18 radiograph branch trained with domain-adversarial learning over synthetic blur, noise, and contrast shifts on PneumoniaMNIST. Unlike end-to-end multimodal fusion, it uses late weighted aggregation (0.40 imaging, 0.20 each for symptoms, cough, and speech) to stay modular, interpretable, and missing-modality tolerant, while running fully offline on laptop hardware. Reported results show strong, shift-robust imaging performance (test AUROC 0.9752) but weak minority-class recall for cough, arguing for multimodal triage systems that can exploit weak signals without depending on any single sensor.
papers.abstract: Pneumonia remains a leading global cause of morbidity and mortality, particularly in low resource settings where access to imaging, laboratory testing, and specialist care is limited. Clinical assessment relies on heterogeneous evidence, including symptoms, respiratory patterns, and chest imaging, making screening inherently multimodal. However, many existing computational approaches remain unimodal and focus primarily on radiographs. In this work, we present MultiSense-Pneumo, a multimodal framework for pneumonia oriented screening and triage support that integrates structured symptom descriptors, cough audio, spoken language, and chest radiographs. The system combines deterministic symptom triage, LightGBM based acoustic classification, domain adversarial radiograph analysis using ResNet 18, transformer based speech recognition, and an interpretable multimodal fusion operator. Each modality is transformed into a normalized risk signal and aggregated into a unified screening estimate, enabling transparent and modular decision support. MultiSense-Pneumo is designed for real world deployment under modest computational constraints and can operate fully offline on standard laptop class hardware, making it suitable for community health workers, rural clinics, and emergency response settings. Experimental results demonstrate robustness of the radiograph pathway under domain shifts, while highlighting limitations in minority class recall for acoustic signals. MultiSense-Pneumo is intended as a research prototype for screening and triage support rather than a clinically validated diagnostic system.

papers.title: Submodular Benchmark Selection
papers.summary: Submodular Benchmark Selection reframes the expensive problem of choosing which LLM benchmarks to run as submodular maximization over a multivariate Gaussian model of benchmark-score correlations. Given an M×N score matrix from already evaluated models, the paper compares two information-theoretic objectives: entropy of the selected subset, equivalent to log det(ΣAA) and exactly solved by pivoted Cholesky, and mutual information I(XA;XĀ), which measures how well the chosen benchmarks predict the unrun ones. The unusual twist is that the goal is not generic benchmark diversity, but imputation quality under covariance estimation from real leaderboard data; missing entries are handled with EM for Gaussian missing data, and subset size is diagnosed via eigenvalue decay and the pivoted-Cholesky residual trace. Experiments on three matrices assembled from ten public leaderboards—MMLU, MTEB, and a sparse merged table—show that mutual-information greedy selection beats entropy at small budgets, reaching high imputation R2 with only a handful of benchmarks, while entropy eventually catches up on better-conditioned matrices. The work points toward cost-aware, information-theoretic evaluation design for large benchmark ecosystems.
papers.abstract: Evaluating large language models across many benchmarks is expensive, yet many benchmarks are highly correlated. We formalize the selection of a small, informative subset as submodular maximization under a multivariate Gaussian model. Entropy (log-determinant covariance) and mutual information between selected and remaining benchmarks arise as natural objectives. Both are submodular; entropy selection coincides with pivoted Cholesky and has spectral residual bounds, while mutual information is non-monotone in general but empirically monotone for small subsets, so we optimize it greedily. Experiments on three matrices from ten public leaderboards show that mutual information selection outperforms entropy for imputation at small subsets.

papers.title: NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Efficient Low Light Image Enhancement: Methods and Results
papers.summary: NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Efficient Low Light Image Enhancement: Methods and Results reports the NTIRE 2026 E-LLIE benchmark for mobile low-light photo restoration, where the bottleneck is not just suppressing noise, artifacts, and color casts in dark scenes but doing so in sub-megabyte models that can run on resource-limited phones. The challenge used a new smartphone dataset captured on Huawei and Apple devices across dim, extreme low light, backlit, uneven illumination, and night indoor/outdoor scenes, with 349 paired training scenes, 49 validation scenes, and 102 hidden test scenes. From 207 registrations, 27 teams submitted valid entries and 17 provided factsheets. Ranking combined SSIM, LPIPS, DISTS, LIQE, MUSIQ, and Q-Align under a <1 MB constraint, with ties broken by parameter count. The strongest methods converged on lightweight U-Net/encoder–decoder backbones, Retinex or illumination–reflectance decomposition, HVI/YUV/LAB decoupling, partial or depthwise convolutions, cross-attention, and structural reparameterization; notable variants included grayscale-guided cross-attention with FFT phase transfer and RetinexFormer refinements. The reported outcomes show that carefully designed efficiency priors can materially improve perceptual quality without abandoning deployability, pushing LLIE toward practical on-device enhancement rather than oversized restoration models.
papers.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of the NITRE 2026 Efficient Low Light Image Enhancement (E-LLIE) Challenge, highlighting the proposed solutions and final outcomes. This challenge focuses on mobile image enhancement under low-light conditions, aiming to design lightweight networks that improve enhancement quality while ensuring practical deployability under limited computational resources. A total of 207 participants registered, 27 teams submitted valid entries, and 17 teams ultimately provided valid factsheet. Based on these submissions, this paper provides a systematic evaluation of recent methods for E-LLIE, offering a comprehensive overview of state-of-the-art progress and demonstrating significant improvements in both performance and efficiency.

papers.title: CoVSpec: Efficient Device-Edge Co-Inference for Vision-Language Models via Speculative Decoding
papers.summary: CoVSpec: Efficient Device-Edge Co-Inference for Vision-Language Models via Speculative Decoding attacks the mismatch between large VLM capability and mobile deployment constraints, where naive device-edge speculative decoding is bottlenecked by expensive visual-token processing on the handset and repeated token corrections over a wireless link. Its core design is a three-part co-inference pipeline: a training-free visual token reduction stage that keeps only query-relevant, activity-sensitive, low-rank representative visual tokens for the draft model; an adaptive drafting controller that uses a top-1/top-2 margin gate to skip high-confidence verifications and an EMA of recent acceptances plus estimated rejection latency to change draft length on the fly; and a parallel branching scheme with decoupled verification-correction, so the edge verifies but the device performs residual sampling locally, avoiding transmission of correction logits. Unlike prior speculative decoding or visual token pruning work that targets standalone inference, CoVSpec explicitly optimizes the draft/verify/communication triad. Experiments with InternVL2.5-4B on a MacBook Pro and InternVL2.5-78B on RTX 4090 edge servers over VQAv2, MMMU, and MMBench at 10 dB SNR show up to 2.21× throughput and over 96% lower communication cost without accuracy loss, pointing toward communication-aware multimodal edge AI.
papers.abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in multimodal perception and reasoning. However, deploying large VLMs on mobile devices remains challenging due to their substantial computational and memory demands. A practical alternative is device-edge co-inference, where a lightweight draft VLM on the mobile device collaborates with a larger target VLM on the edge server via speculative decoding. Nevertheless, directly extending speculative decoding to VLMs suffers from severe inefficiency due to excessive visual-token computation and high communication overhead. To address these challenges, we propose CoVSpec, an efficient collaborative speculative decoding framework for VLM inference. Specifically, we first develop a training-free visual token reduction framework that prunes redundant visual tokens on the mobile device by jointly considering query relevance, token activity, and low-rank dependency. Moreover, we design an adaptive drafting strategy that dynamically adjusts both the verification frequency and the draft length. In addition, we introduce a parallel branching mechanism with decoupled verification-correction to improve draft-side utilization during target-side verification and reduce correction-related transmission overhead. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that CoVSpec achieves up to 2.21x higher throughput than target-only inference and reduces communication overhead by more than 96% compared with baselines, without compromising task accuracy.

papers.title: Generative Modeling with Orbit-Space Particle Flow Matching
papers.summary: Generative Modeling with Orbit-Space Particle Flow Matching attacks the mismatch between standard flow matching and particle sets, where anonymous indexing makes the same physical point appear under many permutations and turns the velocity regression target into a high-variance mixture. The core idea, Orbit-Space Geometric Probability Paths (OGPP), first canonicalizes only the terminal configuration in orbit space using deterministic particle orderings such as Hilbert sorting, then adds learned particle-index embeddings so each index specializes to a stable canonical role, and finally replaces linear interpolants with quadratic Hermite probability paths whose terminal tangent encodes geometric attributes, especially surface normals. A distinctive twist is the one-sided design: canonicalizing the noisy source X0 is shown to worsen nearest-neighbor Lipschitz ratios, so OGPP canonicalizes X1 but leaves X0 unconstrained to keep flows straighter. The terminal velocity is arc-length-aware, using chord length and chord-normal alignment to stabilize training and make normals available as a byproduct at t=1. Experiments on minimal-surface benchmarks, ShapeNet point clouds, DLA, Thomson particles, and single-shape encoding show faster convergence, up to 100x lower minimal-surface error in one step, and competitive normal-aware reconstruction, pointing toward symmetry-aware, geometry-native generative modeling for graphics and physical particle systems.
papers.abstract: We present Orbit-Space Geometric Probability Paths (OGPP), a particle-native flow-matching framework for generative modeling of particle systems. OGPP is motivated by two insights: (i) particles are defined up to permutation symmetries, so anonymous indexing inflates per-index target variance and yields curved, hard-to-learn flows; and (ii) particles live in physical space, so the flow terminal velocity has physical meaning and can encode geometric attributes, e.g., surface normals. OGPP instantiates three key components: (1) orbit-space canonicalization of the probability-path terminal endpoint, (2) particle index embeddings for role specialization, and (3) geometric probability paths with arc-length-aware terminal velocities that generate normals as a byproduct of the flow. We evaluate OGPP on minimal-surface benchmarks, where it reduces metric error by up to two orders of magnitude in a single inference step; on ShapeNet, where it matches the state of the art with 5x fewer steps and reaches airplane EMD comparable to DiT-3D with 26x fewer parameters and 5x fewer steps; and on single-shape encoding, where it produces normals and reconstructions competitive with 6D generators while operating entirely in 3D.

papers.title: Toward Fine-Grained Speech Inpainting Forensics:A Dataset, Method, and Metric for Multi-Region Tampering Localization
papers.summary: Toward Fine-Grained Speech Inpainting Forensics:A Dataset, Method, and Metric for Multi-Region Tampering Localization attacks a blind spot in audio deepfake forensics: adversaries who replace 1–3 semantically critical words inside a real utterance, preserving the speaker while altering meaning, so the fake spans only 2–7% of the waveform and the number of tampered regions is unknown. The paper’s first contribution, MIST, is a multilingual benchmark over six languages with word-level timestamps and 1–3 independently inpainted segments per utterance, created by LLM-guided semantic replacement plus speaker-conditioned zero-shot TTS/voice cloning and careful crossfaded splicing. Its method, ISA, is a backbone-agnostic inference scheme that turns an utterance-level deepfake scorer into a locator via coarse sliding-window scanning, gap-tolerant merging of suspicious runs, and fine-grained boundary refinement, explicitly handling fragmented predictions and unknown segment count. The new SF1@τ metric adapts IoU-style matching to 1D audio segments and pairs localization F1 with Count Accuracy. Zero-shot tests with Wav2Vec2/AASIST backbones show that detectors trained on full-synthesis speech miss these subtle attacks, while ISA consistently beats frame-level and single-window baselines, pointing audio forensics toward object-detection-like segment localization rather than binary utterance classification.
papers.abstract: Recent advances in voice cloning and text-to-speech synthesis have made partial speech manipulation - where an adversary replaces a few words within an utterance to alter its meaning while preserving the speaker's identity - an increasingly realistic threat. Existing audio deepfake detection benchmarks focus on utterance-level binary classification or single-region tampering, leaving a critical gap in detecting and localizing multiple inpainted segments whose count is unknown a priori. We address this gap with three contributions. First, we introduce MIST (Multiregion Inpainting Speech Tampering), a large-scale multilingual dataset spanning 6 languages with 1-3 independently inpainted word-level segments per utterance, generated via LLM-guided semantic replacement and neural voice cloning, with fake content constituting only 2-7% of each utterance. Second, we propose ISA (Iterative Segment Analysis), a backbone-agnostic framework that performs coarse-to-fine sliding-window classification with gap-tolerant region proposal and boundary refinement to recover all tampered regions without prior knowledge of their count. Third, we define SF1@tau, a segment-level F1 metric based on temporal IoU matching that jointly evaluates region count accuracy and localization precision. Zero-shot evaluation reveals that partial inpainting at word granularity remains unsolved by existing deepfake detectors: utterance-level classifiers trained on fully synthesized speech assign near zero fake probability to MIST utterances where only 2-7% of content is manipulated. ISA consistently outperforms non-iterative baselines in this challenging setting, and the dataset, code, and evaluation toolkit are publicly released.

papers.title: InfiltrNet: Dual-Branch CNN-Transformer Architecture for Brain Tumor Infiltration Risk Prediction
papers.summary: InfiltrNet: Dual-Branch CNN-Transformer Architecture for Brain Tumor Infiltration Risk Prediction tackles a gap in glioma imaging: BraTS-style segmenters outline visible tumor but miss the clinically crucial question of how far cells likely infiltrate into surrounding “normal” tissue for resection and radiation planning. The paper turns infiltration into a four-class voxel segmentation problem by deriving reproducible three-zone risk labels from standard annotations with Euclidean distance transforms: high risk in edema and within 10 mm of the boundary, medium risk at 10–20 mm, and low risk beyond 20 mm. InfiltrNet combines a 3D ResNet CNN branch for local boundary texture with a Swin Transformer branch for long-range context, then fuses them at every decoder level using bidirectional cross-attention, plus auxiliary decoder heads and a boundary-aware loss to sharpen transition zones. This is unusual compared with nearby tumor-segmentation work because it predicts peritumoral risk rather than tumor extent and explicitly validates attention on clinically relevant margins. On BraTS 2020 and BraTS 2025, it outperforms five baselines, reaching mean Dice 0.89 and 0.90 with lower HD95, and suggests a direction toward clinically grounded, explainable infiltration mapping beyond standard segmentation.
papers.abstract: Gliomas are aggressive brain tumors that infiltrate surrounding tissue beyond the visible tumor margins observed on Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). Predicting the spatial extent of this infiltration is essential for surgical planning and radiation therapy, yet existing deep learning approaches focus on segmenting the visible tumor rather than estimating infiltration risk in the surrounding tissue. This paper presents InfiltrNet, a novel dual-branch architecture that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) encoder with a Swin Transformer encoder through cross-attention fusion modules to predict three-zone infiltration risk maps from multimodal MRI. A label generation strategy based on distance transforms is proposed to derive reproducible infiltration risk zones from standard Brain Tumor Segmentation (BraTS) annotations. InfiltrNet is trained with a combined Dice-CrossEntropy and boundary-aware loss augmented by auxiliary supervision heads at intermediate decoder levels. Extensive experiments on BraTS 2020 and BraTS 2025 demonstrate that InfiltrNet outperforms five established baselines. Explainability analysis using GradCAM++ and Occlusion sensitivity confirms that the model attends to clinically relevant peritumoral regions.

papers.title: Bucketing the Good Apples: A Method for Diagnosing and Improving Causal Abstraction
papers.summary: Bucketing the Good Apples: A Method for Diagnosing and Improving Causal Abstraction attacks a central weakness of causal-abstraction interpretability: interchange intervention accuracy (IIA) is usually reported as one global number, which hides where a high-level hypothesis is faithful, where it breaks, and what structural feature separates the two. The paper replaces scalar evaluation with a pairwise interchangeability graph over task-correct inputs, then extracts dense cliques or γ-quasi-cliques as “target buckets” of mutually interchange-consistent cases and treats the remainder as under-interpreted failure modes. A simple four-step recipe—choose a reliable task slice, find an alignment with DAS/full-vector patching/MDAS, bucket the graph, then train a classifier on hand-labeled or SAE features to generalize the partition—turns diagnosis into refinement: the bucket structure can reveal missing intermediate variables, split a coarse hypothesis, or merge complementary partial abstractions. Across a GPT-2-small toy logic task, Gemma-2-2B-Instruct entity binding, and Llama-3.1-8B entangled factual recall on RAVEL, the method localizes regime-specific faithfulness; in the toy task, recursive reapplication reconstructs the full hierarchy of the intended Boolean computation from scratch.
papers.abstract: We present a method for diagnosing interpretation in neural networks by identifying an input subspace where a proposed interpretation is highly faithful. Our method is particularly useful for causal-abstraction-style interpretability, where a high-level causal hypothesis is evaluated by interchange interventions. Rather than treating interchange intervention accuracy as a single global summary, we refine this framework by partitioning the input space into well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions according to pairwise interchange-intervention behavior. This turns causal abstraction from a purely global evaluation into a more diagnostic tool: it not only measures whether an interpretation works, but also reveals where it works, where it fails, and what distinguishes the two cases. This diagnostic view also provides practical heuristics for improving interpretations. By analyzing the structure of the well-interpreted and under-interpreted regions, we can identify missing distinctions in a high-level hypothesis, discover previously unmodeled intermediate variables, and combine complementary partial interpretations into a stronger one. We instantiate this idea as a simple four-step recipe and show that it yields informative error analyses across multiple causal abstraction settings. In a toy logic task, recursively applying the recipe recovers a high-level hypothesis from scratch. More broadly, our results suggest that partitioning the input space is a useful step toward more precise, constructive, and scalable mechanistic interpretability.

papers.title: Perturbation Dose Responses in Recursive LLM Loops: Raw Switching, Stochastic Floors, and Persistent Escape under Append, Replace, and Dialog Updates
papers.summary: Perturbation Dose Responses in Recursive LLM Loops: Raw Switching, Stochastic Floors, and Persistent Escape under Append, Replace, and Dialog Updates studies the intervention cost of moving a settled recursive language-model trajectory between attractor-like regimes, and whether the move survives later self-feeding. The paper cleanly separates the generator from the context-update rule, comparing append, replace, and dialog loops over 30 steps and measuring three endpoints: raw terminal switching, net switching after subtracting a paired no-injection stochastic floor, and persistent escape that requires both an injection-step basin jump and terminal retention. In gpt-4o-mini experiments with within-vendor replication on gpt-4.1-nano, append-mode shows a real dose response for in-distribution adversarial continuations with ED50 near 40 tokens, but bounded-memory loops clipped to 12,000 characters plateau at only ~16% destination-coherent persistence and ~36% source-basin escape at 400 tokens, below the ~35% control floor. Full-history loops eventually cross 50% escape and reach 75–80% by 1,500 tokens. Replace-mode “fragility” is mostly overwrite tautology, exposed by insert-mode probes dropping switching to 12–32%. The paper points toward safety evaluation that treats memory policy and persistence criteria as first-class variables, not implementation details.
papers.abstract: Recursive language-model loops often settle into recognizable attractor-like patterns. The practical question is how much injected text is needed to move a settled loop somewhere else, and whether that move lasts. We study this in 30-step recursive loops by separating the model from the context-update rule: append, replace, and dialog updates expose different histories to the same generator. The main result is that persistent redirection in append-mode recursive loops is memory-policy-conditioned. Under a 12,000-character tail clip, destination-coherent persistence plateaus near 16 percent and retained source-basin escape near 36 percent at dose 400; neither crosses 50 percent. Under a full-history protocol, retained source-basin escape crosses 50 percent near 400 tokens and saturates at 75-80 percent by 1,500 tokens, while destination-coherent persistence first reaches 0.50 near 1,500 tokens with a Wilson 95 percent CI of [0.41, 0.61]. For raw switching, adversarial continuations yield an ED50 near 40 tokens, with paired-control floors near 35 percent and net switching never reaching +50 percentage points within 5-400 tokens. Replace-mode raw switching is near-saturated but largely reflects state-reset overwrite: insert-mode probes drop it to 12-32 percent. A homogeneous-perturbation control reproduced the high-dose non-monotonic dip in destination-coherent persistence, refuting perturbation heterogeneity as the cause; the dip appears structural, with mechanism unresolved. We report 37 experiments on gpt-4o-mini with within-vendor replication on gpt-4.1-nano. Recursive-loop evaluations should distinguish transient movement from durable escape, subtract stochastic floors, and treat context-update rules as first-class safety-relevant design choices.

papers.title: PhysicianBench: Evaluating LLM Agents in Real-World EHR Environments
papers.summary: PhysicianBench: Evaluating LLM Agents in Real-World EHR Environments targets the gap between static medical QA benchmarks and the messy, multi-step work of physicians inside real electronic health records, where agents must retrieve longitudinal data, reason across encounters, place consequential orders, and write documentation through production-standard FHIR APIs. The benchmark contains 100 long-horizon tasks adapted from real primary care–subspecialty e-consult cases, independently reviewed by physicians, then instantiated in isolated EHR sandboxes loaded with de-identified Stanford STARR patient records; tasks span 21 specialties and require an average of 27 tool calls. Its differentiator is execution-grounded evaluation: 670 structured checkpoints split across data retrieval, clinical reasoning, action execution, and documentation, graded by code, hybrid computation-plus-extraction, and rubric-based LLM judges against the post-action FHIR state. Across 12–13 proprietary and open-source agents, GPT-5.5 leads at 46.3% pass@1, while the strongest open model reaches 18.7%, with failures concentrated in clinical reasoning and persistent action-execution output gaps. The paper points toward agentic clinical AI that is not merely knowledgeable but verifiably operational in real hospital software.
papers.abstract: We introduce PhysicianBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on physician tasks grounded in real clinical setting within electronic health record (EHR) environments. Existing medical agent benchmarks primarily focus on static knowledge recall, single-step atomic actions, or action intent without verifiable execution against the environment. As a result, they fail to capture the long-horizon, composite workflows that characterize real clinical systems. PhysicianBench comprises 100 long-horizon tasks adapted from real consultation cases between primary care and subspecialty physicians, with each task independently reviewed by a separate panel of physicians. Tasks are instantiated in an EHR environment with real patient records and accessed through the same standard APIs used by commercial EHR vendors. Tasks span 21 specialties (e.g., cardiology, endocrinology, oncology, psychiatry) and diverse workflow types (e.g., diagnosis interpretation, medication prescribing, treatment planning), requiring an average of 27 tool calls per task. Solving each task requires retrieving data across encounters, reasoning over heterogeneous clinical information, executing consequential clinical actions, and producing clinical documentation. Each task is decomposed into structured checkpoints (670 in total across the benchmark) capturing distinct stages of completion graded by task-specific scripts with execution-grounded verification. Across 13 proprietary and open-source LLM agents, the best-performing model achieves only 46% success rate (pass@1), while open-source models reach at most 19%, revealing a substantial gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of real-world clinical workflows. PhysicianBench provides a realistic and execution-grounded benchmark for measuring progress toward autonomous clinical agents.

papers.title: Zero-Shot Confidence Estimation for Small LLMs: When Supervised Baselines Aren't Worth Training
papers.summary: Zero-Shot Confidence Estimation for Small LLMs: When Supervised Baselines Aren't Worth Training attacks the routing bottleneck for cheap local 7–8B LLMs, where a deployment must decide when to escalate a query to a cloud model without labeled confidence data. The paper pits zero-shot signals against RouteLLM-style supervised logistic regressions on three model families (Qwen-2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct) over MMLU-Pro and TriviaQA, showing that average token log-probability is the most reliable confidence estimate: it matches or beats supervised routing in-distribution and transfers far better out-of-distribution because it measures generation-side surprise rather than query-space patterns. A key differentiator is retrieval-conditional self-assessment (GSA v3), which only injects retrieved evidence when similarity is high and otherwise uses a byte-identical no-retrieval prompt to avoid priming the model toward “NO”; this raises AUROC by up to +0.069 at 3–10× lower latency than logprob. Supervised baselines trained on 1,000 examples never surpass the zero-shot signal, implying cold-start routing should default to logprob, with retrieval-grounded self-assessment as a cheaper first-stage filter.
papers.abstract: How reliably can a small language model estimate its own correctness? The answer determines whether local-to-cloud routing-escalating queries a cheap local model cannot handle-can work without supervised training data. As inference costs dominate large language model (LLM) deployment budgets, routing most queries to a cheap local model while reserving expensive cloud calls for hard cases is an increasingly common cost-control strategy. We compare zero-shot confidence signals against RouteLLM-style supervised baselines across three 7-8B model families and two datasets (1,000 and 500 queries per model, respectively). Average token log-probability, which requires no training data, matches or exceeds supervised baselines in-distribution (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) 0.650-0.714 vs. 0.644-0.676) and substantially outperforms them out-of-distribution (0.717-0.833 vs. 0.512-0.564), because it measures a property of the model's generation rather than the query distribution. This paper further proposes retrieval-conditional self-assessment, a pre-generation signal that selectively injects retrieved knowledge when similarity is high, improving over bare self-assessment by up to +0.069 AUROC at 3-10x lower latency than log-probability. A supervised baseline trained on 1,000 labeled examples never exceeds the zero-shot signal. We release all code, data, and experiment logs.

papers.title: The Conversations Beneath the Code: Triadic Data for Long-Horizon Software Engineering Agents
papers.summary: The Conversations Beneath the Code: Triadic Data for Long-Horizon Software Engineering Agents argues that frontier coding agents have outgrown short-horizon benchmarks like SWE-bench Verified while still failing on senior-engineer work involving weeks-long context drift, cross-functional ambiguity, and multi-engineer coordination. The paper’s core claim is that the missing training substrate is not more GitHub scraping, solo-agent rollouts, or dyadic human-AI chat logs, but triadic data: synchronized records of human-human deliberation, subsequent human-AI implementation, and the surrounding longitudinal project context. It distinguishes three capture modes—pair-with-AI, human-human-then-AI, and human-human-around-AI—and proposes two concrete corpus products: long-horizon expert trajectories collected with stimulated recall, and simulated cross-functional companies where senior engineers, PMs, designers, and data scientists work through ambiguous deliverables on shared infrastructure. Unusually, it pairs this data agenda with a four-tier evidence standard for corpus credibility: mechanical verification, corpus characterization, probe fine-tuning, and pre-registered blind evaluation. The paper points toward a new SWE training regime centered on context formation, drift handling, and disagreement-grounded supervision rather than artifact-only code prediction.
papers.abstract: Frontier software engineering agents have saturated short-horizon benchmarks while regressing on the work that constitutes senior engineering: long-horizon, multi-engineer, ambiguous-specification deliverables. This paper takes a position on what training data is needed to close the gap. The substrate for the next generation of SWE agents is neither larger GitHub scrapes nor more solo-agent trajectories nor -- sufficient by itself -- open human-AI dialogue logs. It is triadic data: synchronized capture of the human-human conversations where engineering context is formed, the human-AI sessions where that context is partially consumed, and the multi-week cross-functional work that surrounds both. We argue that the canonical instantiation of triadic data is two complementary products: long-horizon expert trajectories captured under stimulated-recall protocols, and simulated cross-functional companies -- instrumented teams of senior engineers, product managers, designers, and data scientists working through ambiguous deliverables on shared infrastructure. We further specify a four-tier evidence framework through which any such corpus -- triadic or otherwise -- must justify its quality to a fine-tuning researcher: mechanical verification, statistical corpus characterization, probe experiments, and pre-registered blind evaluation. We argue that this data is capturable in 12-18 months with methods already mature in adjacent fields, that it is the empirical key to four open questions in agent training, and that the field's near-term research agenda should include it explicitly.

papers.title: Demographic-Aware Transfer Learning for Sleep Stage Classification in Clinical Polysomnography
papers.summary: Demographic-Aware Transfer Learning for Sleep Stage Classification in Clinical Polysomnography attacks the mismatch between population-agnostic sleep staging models and the known dependence of sleep EEG on gender, age, and obstructive sleep apnea burden. The paper pretrains a CNN-BiLSTM on the full DREAMT v2.1 clinical cohort (100 subjects, 7 PSG channels, AASM 5-stage labels) and then fine-tunes the same backbone on demographic subgroups defined by gender, age bands, AHI severity, and pairwise intersections such as Gender×AHI and Age×AHI. Unlike nearby transfer-learning work that bridges datasets or sensor gaps, this study treats demographic structure inside one realistic sleep-lab cohort as the adaptation axis, using only routinely available clinical variables and no hand-crafted features. With strict subject-wise cross-validation, 37 subgroup configurations are evaluated; 35 outperform the baseline, with Cohen’s kappa gains from 0.9% to 12.9%. The strongest gains appear in moderate-size, clinically coherent groups such as male-mild apnea and under-50 normal sleepers, while very small or severely fragmented subgroups can degrade. The result points toward subgroup-indexed, personalized automated PSG scoring rather than a single universal model.
papers.abstract: Automated sleep stage classification typically employs a single population-agnostic model, disregarding established demographic variations in sleep architecture. Sleep patterns, however, differ substantially across gender, age, and obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) severity, indicating that a onesize-fits all approach may be suboptimal for diverse clinical populations. In this paper, we propose a two stage training strategy based on demographic stratification and transfer learning framework. We first pretrains a convolutional recurrent model on the full population and then fine tunes it independently for demographic subgroups defined by gender, age, and Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) severity according to the AASM clinical standard. Using the DREAMT dataset comprising 100 clinical subjects and 7 PSG channels, we evaluate 37 fine-tuned configurations across single-axis and two-way demographic combinations. Results demonstrate that 35 of the 37 fine-tuned models outperform the baseline, with Cohen's kappa improvements ranging from 0.9 to 12.9%. These findings indicate that stratified fine tuning tailored to specific patient demographics yields substantially more accurate sleep staging than a single generalized model, offering a practical and clinically grounded paradigm for personalized sleep assessment.

papers.title: Fine-Tuning Impairs the Balancedness of Foundation Models in Long-tailed Personalized Federated Learning
papers.summary: Fine-Tuning Impairs the Balancedness of Foundation Models in Long-tailed Personalized Federated Learning studies a failure mode in CLIP-style personalized federated learning under the joint pressure of non-IID clients and long-tailed class counts: naive PEFT fine-tuning can push the global model below its own zero-shot baseline by eroding the pretrained model’s cross-class balance, and then standard parameter/feature fusion propagates that head-class bias into personalized clients. The proposed FedPuReL framework attacks this by splitting training into balance-preserving global learning and unbiased personalization. In the global phase, each client uses zero-shot CLIP predictions to build a temperature-aligned KL alignment gradient, then projects away any task-gradient component that conflicts with it, so aggregated PEFT updates stay anchored to the balanced zero-shot geometry. In the personalization phase, client adaptation is recast as output-space residual learning: a frozen global branch plus private prompt/adapter/LoRA residuals fused additively in logit space. Experiments on CLIP ViT-B/16 across ImageNet-LT, Places-LT, CIFAR-100-LT, Food101-LT, DTD-LT, Aircraft-LT, Dogs-LT, and Pets-LT show stronger global and personalized accuracy, especially on few-shot classes, suggesting future federated foundation-model work should preserve pretrained balance instead of blindly fine-tuning it.
papers.abstract: Personalized federated learning (PFL) with foundation models has emerged as a promising paradigm enabling clients to adapt to heterogeneous data distributions. However, real-world scenarios often face the co-occurrence of non-IID data and long-tailed class distributions, presenting unique challenges that remain underexplored in PFL. In this paper, we investigate this long-tailed personalized federated learning and observe that current methods suffer from two limitations: (i) fine-tuning degrades performance below zero-shot baselines due to the erosion of inherent class balance in foundation models; (ii) conventional personalization techniques further transfer this bias to local models through parameter or feature-level fusion. To address these challenges, we propose Federated Learning via Gradient Purification and Residual Learning (FedPuReL), which preserves balanced knowledge in the global model while enabling unbiased personalization. Specifically, we purify local gradients using zero-shot predictions to maintain a class-balanced global model, and model personalization as residual correction atop the frozen global model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedPuReL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior performance on both global and personalized models across diverse long-tailed scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/shihaohou/FedPuReL.

papers.title: A Study of Belief Revision Postulates in Multi-Agent Systems (Extended Version)
papers.summary: A Study of Belief Revision Postulates in Multi-Agent Systems (Extended Version) attacks the missing rationality theory for revising a single multi-agent Kripke model in epistemic planning when one agent acquires a possibly false first-degree belief Baφ and all agents’ beliefs, including higher-order beliefs, must be recomputed. The paper generalizes AGM revision postulates and Darwiche-Pearl iterated-revision postulates from single-agent belief sets to the multi-agent setting by redefining belief-set inclusion over first-degree formulas only, then proving properties against pointed Kripke structures rather than sets of models. It introduces generalized full-meet multi-agent belief revision, which is conservative but drastic under inconsistency, and an event-model-based revision operator that encodes three-way cases for the revising agent, other agents, and updated valuations via a propositional operator ⋆. A key differentiator from nearby DEL work is that it targets belief revision, not action update, and explicitly analyzes which postulates survive when accessibility relations and false beliefs are both in play. The main result is a taxonomy of satisfiable versus impossible postulates: full-meet satisfies AGM and most DP conditions but fails DP2/IN, while the refined event-based operator satisfies all generalized AGM postulates and a broad DP fragment.
papers.abstract: We investigate the belief revision problem in epistemic planning, i.e., what will be the beliefs of all agents in a multi-agent system after an agent gains the belief in some state property. Based on the standard representation in epistemic planning of agents' beliefs via a single multi-agent Kripke model, we generalize the classical AGM belief revision postulates to the multi-agent setting, with the aim to provide a formal framework for evaluating dynamic epistemic reasoning frameworks in which the beliefs of all agents as the result of actions are computed. As an example of a simple operator that satisfies all of the generalized AGM postulates, we present generalized full-meet multi-agent belief revision. We moreover define a generalization of the standard postulates for iterated revision, present a more sophisticated, event model based revision operator, and discuss the potential issues in defining an epistemic operator on Kripke models that can satisfy all of the generalized postulates for iterated multi-agent belief revision.

papers.title: On the Privacy of LLMs: An Ablation Study
papers.summary: On the Privacy of LLMs: An Ablation Study unifies membership inference, attribute inference, data extraction, and backdoor attacks under one threat model for interactive and retrieval-augmented LLMs, addressing a fragmentation problem where privacy attacks are usually benchmarked in isolation. The paper’s core move is methodological: it rebuilds representative attacks in a shared notation and experimental harness, then ablates system choices that plausibly govern leakage, including model architecture and scale, dataset domain and size, embedding/retriever stack, retrieval depth, prompt style, mask count, and decision threshold. In RAG membership inference, it contrasts semantic-similarity probing (S2MIA) with mask-based membership inference (MBMIA), showing that MBMIA is far stronger and often near-ceiling, while S2MIA swings sharply with dataset structure such as SQuAD/HotpotQA versus TriviaQA. On real privacy surfaces like Enron emails and synthetic PII prompts, data extraction exposure rises with repetition and model size, and attribute inference improves with larger or sparse models. The result argues that privacy risk is context-dependent and should be evaluated as a system property, not a single-model property.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in interactive and retrieval-augmented settings, raising significant privacy concerns. While attacks such as Membership Inference (MIA), Attribute Inference (AIA), Data Extraction (DEA), and Backdoor Attacks (BA) have been studied, they are typically analyzed in isolation, leaving a gap in understanding their behavior under common system factors. In this paper, we introduce a unified threat model and notation, reproduce a representative set of privacy attacks, and conduct a structured ablation study to evaluate the impact of key factors such as model architecture, scale, dataset characteristics, and retrieval configuration. Our analysis reveals clear differences across attack types. Membership inference attacks, particularly mask-based variants, exhibit strong and reliable signals, while backdoor attacks achieve consistently high success rates due to their trigger-based nature. In contrast, attribute inference and data extraction attacks remain more challenging, resulting in lower accuracy, yet they pose significant risks as they target sensitive personal information. Overall, these results highlight that privacy risks in LLM systems are highly context-dependent and driven by design choices, emphasizing the need for holistic evaluation and informed deployment practices.

papers.title: SpectraDINO: Bridging the Spectral Gap in Vision Foundation Models via Lightweight Adapters
papers.summary: SpectraDINO: Bridging the Spectral Gap in Vision Foundation Models via Lightweight Adapters tackles the mismatch between RGB-pretrained vision foundation models and ground-level infrared sensing, where NIR, SWIR, and LWIR images expose different physical cues and can break visible-only representations.
