To that end, a novel legal dataset is prepared by identifying 17 legal concepts, such as physical assault and property dispute. A curated collection of 150 homicide cases are annotated with these concepts, e.g., a case is annotated with physical assault only if a physical assault had been reported in that case. Subsequently, a selected set of widely-used causal discovery algorithms is applied to the annotated dataset to discover the causal relationships between the legal concepts. Additionally, the degrees of belief associated with the discovered relationships are quantified in mathematical probabilities. It is shown that some of the causal relationships help generate viable legal arguments, e.g., if one could establish that a physical assault has not taken place during a homicide, it should be a sufficient condition (with probability 1) to establish that the homicide has not been committed due to a property-related dispute. Thus, this paper shows that causal discovery algorithms can be helpful in generating legal arguments, opening up avenues for promising future endeavors.

papers.title: ANO: A Principled Approach to Robust Policy Optimization
papers.summary: ANO: A Principled Approach to Robust Policy Optimization tackles the PPO dilemma in on-policy RL: hard clipping protects against runaway updates but zeroes out outlier gradients, while unclipped policy optimization keeps signal but becomes unstable and hyperparameter-fragile. The paper’s core move is a Unified Trust Region Framework that reinterprets policy improvement through elementwise shaping functions on the probability ratio r=πθ(a|s)/πold(a|s), then derives Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO) from four design principles: global differentiability, bounded maximization region, bounded gradients, and structural simplicity. Its differentiating idea is the Redescending Influence Principle, which replaces PPO’s flat clipping plateau and SPO’s quadratic unbounded growth with a smooth, C∞ kernel that peaks at 1+ϵ, pulls moderate outliers back, and lets extreme outlier influence decay to zero. Experiments on MuJoCo, Atari, and RLHF fine-tuning of Pythia-1B on TL;DR show stronger mean/IQM scores and markedly better stability than PPO, TRPO, SPO, PAPO, and GRPO, including no policy collapse at 3× larger learning rates. The paper points toward RL objectives designed as robust-statistical influence functions rather than ad hoc clipping rules.
papers.abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) dominates deep RL but faces a fundamental dilemma. Its "hard clipping" mechanism discards valuable gradient information from outliers, leading to sample inefficiency. Conversely, removing clipping (as in SPO) exposes optimization to unbounded gradients, causing significant instability and hyperparameter sensitivity. To resolve this, we establish a Unified Trust Region Framework that generalizes existing objectives. Within this framework, we derive Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO) based on a set of design principles. We identify that the failure of standard policy gradients stems from a misapplication of gradient influence on outliers. We propose the Redescending Influence Principle, a paradigm shift from monotonic penalties (SPO) and hard-thresholding (PPO) to dynamic outlier suppression, and prove its necessity for stability in high-variance stochastic optimization. Theoretically, we prove ANO possesses the minimal structural complexity required for robust optimization. Empirically, ANO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MuJoCo benchmarks, significantly outperforming PPO and SPO. Notably, ANO demonstrates superior stability, preventing policy collapse even under aggressive hyperparameters (e.g., learning rates 3x larger than standard) where PPO fails completely.

papers.title: When Attention Collapses: Residual Evidence Modeling for Compositional Inference
papers.summary: When Attention Collapses: Residual Evidence Modeling for Compositional Inference attacks a structural failure in slot-attention-style decomposition when observations are formed by additive superposition rather than separable objects: multiple slots chase the same dominant source, leaving weak components unmodeled. The paper argues this “slot collapse” comes from memoryless attention, where every slot repeatedly scores the same full input and gradients are dominated by the strongest component. Its remedy is residual evidence modeling, instantiated as evidence depletion: a minimal sequential attention variant that combines multiplicative token depletion with an explicit log-evidence bias so later slots see only unexplained signal. Unlike parallel attention, sequential processing alone, or diversity/orthogonality losses, this stateful mechanism changes the effective input after each slot without corrupting the underlying data. Controlled synthetic mixtures of sinusoids, Gaussian bumps, and multi-scale signals show collapse reductions up to an order of magnitude; on real FUSS audio mixtures it cuts collapse from 0.29 to 0.05; on ESA/NASA LISA gravitational-wave source inference, with identical architecture/data/losses, vanilla attention fails while evidence depletion enables distinct slots and calibrated multi-source posterior estimation. The work points toward stateful, evidence-tracking attention for scientific compositional inference in dense mixtures.
papers.abstract: Compositional inference - the decomposition of observations into an unknown number of latent components - is central to perception and scientific data analysis. Attention-based models perform well when components are approximately separable, as in object-centric vision. Under additive superposition, however - where multiple components contribute to every observation - we identify a structural failure mode we term slot collapse: multiple slots converge to the same dominant component while weaker ones remain unrepresented. We trace this to a general limitation: attention is memoryless with respect to explained evidence. All slots repeatedly operate on the same input without accounting for what has already been explained, so gradients are dominated by the strongest component, inducing shared fixed points across slots. As a result, attention fails to enforce non-redundant allocation under additive superposition. We address this by introducing residual evidence modeling, instantiated via evidence depletion - a minimal modification combining multiplicative depletion with an attention bias. Controlled ablations show that parallel attention, sequential processing alone, and loss-based regularization fail to resolve collapse; evidence depletion, which adds residual state to sequential attention, consistently succeeds. Across synthetic benchmarks and real-world audio mixtures (FUSS), evidence depletion reduces slot collapse by up to an order of magnitude, generalizing beyond synthetic settings. On gravitational-wave source inference for the ESA/NASA LISA mission, under identical architectures, data, and losses, standard attention fails while evidence depletion prevents collapse and enables multi-source posterior estimation. These results show that under additive superposition, residual evidence tracking is the operative ingredient for preventing collapse and enabling compositional inference.

papers.title: Denoising data using convex relaxations
papers.summary: Denoising data using convex relaxations studies how to recover clean latent samples when observations are \(Y_i=X_i+Z_i\), with \(X_i\) drawn from a low-dimensional manifold in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and \(Z_i\) isotropic Gaussian. The paper’s key move is a two-stage convex-relaxation denoiser: first PCA compresses noisy data to a low-dimensional affine subspace, then each point is projected onto \(K=\mathrm{conv}(\Pi M_0)\), the convex hull of the projected latent manifold. Because \(M_0\) is unknown, the projection is implemented through a statistical distance oracle that infers supporting hyperplanes from empirical Gaussian tail probabilities of a noisy batch, then searches over directions on the unit sphere rather than calling a standard convex solver. Under a lower-mass condition on \(\mu_0\), the authors prove finite-sample bounds that separate PCA bias, projection risk on \(K\), and oracle error, with oracle sample complexity exponential in intrinsic dimension and noise level. They also verify the assumptions for Cryo-EM by bounding the Lipschitz behavior of the \(SO(k)\) action, X-ray transform, and pixel sampling, pointing toward theory-driven denoising for noisy geometric inverse problems.
papers.abstract: We study the problem of denoising observations \(Y_i=X_i+Z_i\), where the latent variables \(X_i\) are sampled from a low-dimensional manifold in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and the noise variables \(Z_i\) are isotropic Gaussian. We propose a convex-relaxation estimator that first reduces dimension by principal component analysis and then projects the observations onto the convex hull of the projected latent manifold. We construct a statistical oracle that estimates its supporting hyperplanes from empirical Gaussian tail probabilities of the noisy sample. Under a lower-mass condition on the latent distribution, we prove finite-sample guarantees for the oracle and derive error bounds for the resulting denoiser. The analysis combines risk bounds for least-squares projection under convex constraints with entropy bounds for convex hulls. We also verify the assumptions of the framework for a Cryo-Electron Microscopy observation model by establishing suitable covering number and Lipschitz estimates for the associated group action and imaging operators.

papers.title: Improving Imbalanced Multi-Label Chest X-Ray Diagnosis via CBAM-Enhanced CNN Backbones
papers.summary: Improving Imbalanced Multi-Label Chest X-Ray Diagnosis via CBAM-Enhanced CNN Backbones tackles the practical bottleneck of ChestXray14-style thoracic disease screening: multiple co-existing labels, severe long-tailed class imbalance, and weak localization of subtle findings when only image-level supervision is available. Instead of inventing a new backbone, the paper retrofits standard CNNs with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) blocks, inserting them selectively into deeper stages of DenseNet121 (after dense blocks 3 and 4) and VGG16 (after blocks 3, 4, and 5) so channel-and-spatial refinement amplifies pathology-relevant regions while preserving early low-level features. The differentiating idea is the integration strategy itself: the authors show that “more attention” is not better, and that late-stage placement beats full-stack insertion. They also test loss/training interactions, finding a two-stage abnormal-first curriculum followed by whole-dataset fine-tuning more effective than single-stage BCE or Focal Loss under imbalance. On the 112,120-image ChestXray14 benchmark with patient-wise splits, the best VGG16 variant reaches mean AUC 0.8695, improving rare labels such as Hernia and Pneumonia. The paper points toward efficient, attention-augmented clinical CNNs that address imbalance through architecture-plus-curriculum rather than heavier transformer scaling.
papers.abstract: Chest radiography is a widely used imaging modality for thoracic disease diagnosis, yet its conventional interpretation remains time-consuming and heavily dependent on expert knowledge. While deep learning has improved diagnostic efficiency through automated feature extraction, challenges such as class imbalance and the localization of multiple co-existing pathologies remain unsolved. In this paper, inspired by the strength of Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) in feature refinement and the capability of CNN blocks in feature extraction, we propose a strategy to integrate CBAM into traditional CNN blocks to enhance performance in multi-label classification tasks. Our method achieves a mean AUC of 0.8695 on ChestXray14 dataset, outperforming several state-of-the-art baselines.Our source code is available at: https://github.com/NNNguyenDuyyy/FETC_CBAM_Enhanced_CNN.git

papers.title: LLM-enabled Social Agents
papers.summary: LLM-enabled Social Agents argues that today’s LLM-driven multi-agent systems can converse fluently yet still fail at socially intelligible behavior because they are weakly grounded in roles, norms, intentions, relationships, and context. Rather than treating “sociality” as an add-on, the paper proposes a conceptual baseline in which an agent’s role is operationalized through a structured persona description: a hybrid symbolic-textual representation with a Persona Core for stable traits, values, boundaries, responsibilities, and commitments, and a Persona State for dynamic situational priorities, memory-informed updates, and relationship status. This differs from nearby work on generic prompts, role labels, or pure rule-based normative agents by arguing that persona grounding is the minimal representation that LLMs can directly operationalize in deliberation. The paper also calls for LLM-centred hybrid control, where memory, planning, norms, and constraint mechanisms preserve role-consistent conduct across time, and for synthetic persona datasets to benchmark coherence, norm handling, and longitudinal social behavior in assistants, robotics, healthcare, supply chains, games, and social simulations. Its main implication is a research shift from language competence toward measurable social agency.
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed agent-agent and human-agent interaction by enabling software, physical, and simulation agents to communicate and deliberate through natural language. Yet fluent language use does not by itself yield socially intelligible behaviour. Most current systems remain weakly grounded in roles, norms, intentions, and contextual constraints, limiting their capacity for meaningful participation in social environments. This paper develops a conceptual baseline for LLM-enabled social agents by arguing that they should be grounded in role definitions operationalized through persona descriptions. On this basis, we outline research directions for representation, hybrid control, and evaluation. The paper concludes that persona-based role definitions are a necessary foundation for turning language competence into social behaviour.

papers.title: FedPLT: Scalable, Resource-Efficient, and Heterogeneity-Aware Federated Learning via Partial Layer Training
papers.summary: FedPLT: Scalable, Resource-Efficient, and Heterogeneity-Aware Federated Learning via Partial Layer Training tackles the bottleneck that makes federated learning brittle on edge/IoT fleets: full-model training is too costly in bandwidth and compute, yet prior sub-model or partial-parameter schemes either bias some parameters toward a few clients, create noisy masked updates, or distort the global objective. The core idea is to keep the full forward pass but partition each trainable layer into equal-sized sub-layers and assign each client a fixed, capability-matched subset so that the induced contribution vector is as balanced as possible across depth; the server then aggregates each block only over the clients that trained it. This is unlike FedDrop/HeteroFL/FedRolex/FedPMT, which rely on random masks, fixed truncation, rolling masks, or prefix freezing. The paper analyzes update magnitude and effective perturbation to justify “deep-heavy” vs “shallow-dominant” allocations by architecture, then extends Optimal Client Sampling to account for partial-training ratios. On Fashion-MNIST FCNs, CIFAR-10 FCNs, and ResNet-8 under Dirichlet non-IID splits, FedPLT matches or beats FedAvg while training 71%–82% fewer parameters and outperforming heterogeneous baselines, especially under severe straggler and budget constraints.
papers.abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has gained significant attention in distributed machine learning by enabling collaborative model training across decentralized system while preserving data privacy. Although extensive research has addressed statistical data heterogeneity, FL still faces several challenges, including high communication and computation overheads and severe device heterogeneity, which require further investigation. Prior work has addressed these issues through sub-model training and partial parameter training. However, such methods often suffer from inconsistent parameter distributions across clients, inaccurate global loss estimation, and increased bias and variance. Guided by our empirical analysis, we propose FedPLT (Federated Learning with Partial Layer Training), an innovative and structured partial parameter training approach that exhibits training behavior similar to full model training while assigning client-specific portions of the model according to their communication and computational capabilities. In addition, we evaluate the performance of FedPLT when combined with optimal client sampling under communication constraints. We show that this integration improves FL performance by reducing sampling variance under the same communication budget. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that FedPLT achieves performance comparable to, or even surpassing, that of full-model training (i.e., FedAvg), while requiring significantly fewer trainable parameters per client. Moreover, FedPLT outperforms existing methods in highly heterogeneous environments, effectively adapts to client resource constraints, and reduces the number of straggling clients. In particular, FedPLT reduces the number of trainable parameters by 71%-82% while achieving performance on par with full-model training.

papers.title: APIOT: Autonomous Vulnerability Management Across Bare-Metal Industrial OT Networks
papers.summary: APIOT: Autonomous Vulnerability Management Across Bare-Metal Industrial OT Networks tackles the bottleneck that bare-metal industrial microcontrollers running Modbus/TCP and CoAP have no shell or filesystem for LLM pentesters to lean on, so exploit reasoning must occur at the level of protocol fields and parser semantics. The core idea is an LLM agent that operates over low-level protocol primitives, not named exploits, and is wrapped by an overseer runtime governance layer that enforces repetition guards, crash blocking, stall detection, and phase-transition control across a four-stage discovery→exploitation→patching→verification mission. Unlike prior autonomous pentesting on Linux/web targets, APIOT is evaluated on Zephyr RTOS firmware in the IoT Virtual Lab/QEMU testbed across flat star, Purdue-segmented, and edge-fog-cloud topologies, with CoAP, Modbus/TCP, and MQTT under mild/heavy impairment. Across 290 runs with five frontier LLMs, APIOT reached 90.0% end-to-end mission success; the overseer proved decisive, eliminating systematic degenerate behaviors such as repetition loops, missing crash verification, and reconnaissance deadlocks. The paper’s implication is that autonomous vulnerability management is now feasible for bare-metal OT, shifting defender assumptions toward LLM-augmented adversaries that can execute full attack-remediation cycles against industrial firmware.
papers.abstract: Bare-metal operational technology (OT) devices -- especially the microcontrollers running Modbus/TCP and CoAP at the base of industrial control systems -- have remained outside the reach of autonomous security attacks. Prior autonomous pentesting studies target Linux and web systems, whose shells and filesystems are familiar to LLM agents. Bare-metal OT has neither, so agents must reason directly over protocol fields and parser semantics. This requires new action-space designs and runtime controls, and opens new research questions about protocol-level exploit reasoning and its deployment envelope. We present APIOT (Autonomous Purple-teaming for Industrial OT), the first large language model (LLM) framework demonstrating an autonomous attack and remediation of bare-metal OT devices, achieving the full discovery -> exploitation -> patching -> verification cycle without step-by-step human intervention. We implemented and evaluated this framework on Zephyr RTOS firmware across heterogeneous industrial IoT (IIoT) topologies. Through 290 experiment runs spanning five frontier LLMs, three network topologies, two impairment levels, and guided versus unguided conditions, APIOT achieved a mission success rate of 90.0% on the full attack-remediation cycle. We found that the runtime governance layer (which we call an overseer) is a critical engineering variable: without it, agents exhibit systematic degenerate patterns, including repetition loops, missing crash verification, and reconnaissance deadlocks. Together, these findings carry two implications beyond our testbed. Attacker expertise is no longer the binding constraint on bare-metal OT exploitation, and defender threat models must now assume LLM-augmented adversaries capable of executing autonomous discovery-through-remediation cycles against industrial firmware.

papers.title: Decoding-Time Debiasing via Process Reward Models: From Controlled Fill-in to Open-Ended Generation
papers.summary: Decoding-Time Debiasing via Process Reward Models: From Controlled Fill-in to Open-Ended Generation attacks a deployment bottleneck in social-bias mitigation: standard fixes like curated retraining, RLHF, or DPO need weight access and can distort other capabilities, while prior inference-time methods often require logits. The paper reframes debiasing as black-box search at decoding time, where a separate Process Reward Model scores each candidate token for fairness and fluency and steers selection without touching model weights. It compares three mechanisms: Best-of-N selection, Sequential critique-and-revise with targeted fairness feedback, and Constitutional self-audit with an eight-principle bias constitution. Unlike nearby prompt-only or discriminator-based work, the PRM judges individual words as “steps” in generation, extending process supervision from math reasoning to bias control. Experiments span GPT-4o-mini plus Llama 3.2 3B, Gemma 3 4B, and Qwen 2.5 3B on a 200-prompt bilingual English/Urdu benchmark covering eight bias categories, then on 20-word open-ended continuations. Sequential is strongest, lifting mean bias by up to +0.40 while maintaining or improving fluency; a Bias Guard gate keeps open-generation overhead near 2× on well-calibrated models. The work points toward token-level, multilingual, API-compatible fairness control with explicit cost accounting.
papers.abstract: Large language models pick up social biases from the data they are trained on and carry those biases into downstream applications, often reinforcing stereotypes around gender, race, religion, disability, age, and socioeconomic status. The standard fixes (retraining on curated data or fine-tuning with human feedback) are expensive, need access to model weights, and risk degrading the model on other tasks. In this paper we take a different route: we debias the model at decoding time, treating bias mitigation as a structured search over candidate tokens without ever touching model weights. A separate Process Reward Model (PRM) acts as a judge, scoring each candidate for both fairness and fluency. We design three schemes of increasing sophistication (Best-of-N selection, Sequential critique-and-revise, and Constitutional self-audit) and evaluate them on four models (GPT-4o-mini, Llama 3.2 3B, Gemma 3 4B, Qwen 2.5 3B) across a 200-prompt bilingual benchmark in English and Urdu covering eight bias categories. Sequential debiasing proves the most effective, raising mean bias scores by up to +0.40 over baseline while preserving (and sometimes improving) fluency. We then extend all three schemes to open-ended generation, where each token is debiased on the fly, and introduce a lightweight Bias Guard gate that fires only on potentially biased words, keeping overhead near 2x for well-calibrated models. A formal overhead metric that separates generator cost from judge cost reveals that Best-of-N is effectively free on the generator side in a native implementation. GPT-4o-mini, included as a strong proprietary anchor, confirms that the framework scales with model capability; the three open-weight models show where current small-scale LLMs still struggle.

papers.title: A Near-optimal SQ Lower Bound for Smoothed Agnostic Learning of Boolean Halfspaces
papers.summary: A Near-optimal SQ Lower Bound for Smoothed Agnostic Learning of Boolean Halfspaces studies whether the smoothed-agnostic relaxation of worst-case halfspace learning on the Boolean cube actually breaks the known SQ barrier. In the model of Kou and Meka, each coordinate is independently flipped with rate σ under the uniform marginal on {±1}^n, and the target is competing against the best σ-smoothed halfspace. The key technical move is an explicit bounded dual witness for the smoothed majority TρMajn: a sine kernel sin(θ(x)S(x)) with S(x)=∑xi, averaged against Laguerre polynomials so that the first m odd Fourier levels cancel while the witness stays in L∞. A Hermite-packet asymptotic argument controls its norm and yields an L1 approximation lower bound, which is then embedded into a hidden-direction family of planted distributions with tiny pairwise correlations. Feldman-style statistical-dimension arguments turn this into a near-matching SQ lower bound: any learner must either use tolerance n^{-Ω(log(1+σ/ε^2)/σ)} or make 2^{Ω(√n)} queries. This shows L1 polynomial regression is essentially optimal on the hypercube and pushes smoothed learning toward sharp approximation-theoretic complexity.
papers.abstract: We study the complexity of smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces on $\{\pm 1\}^n$ under the uniform distribution in the model of \citet{KM25} where each input coordinate is independently flipped with probability $σ\in (0, {1}/{2})$. We show that $L^1$ polynomial regression achieves complexity $\tilde{O}(n^{O(\log(1/\varepsilon)/σ)})$, and prove a nearly matching Statistical Query complexity lower bound of $n^{Ω(\log(1+σ/\varepsilon ^2)/σ)}$. This complements the recent work of \citet{DK26}, which established analogous bounds in the continuous setting under Gaussian marginals.

papers.title: MolViBench: Evaluating LLMs on Molecular Vibe Coding
papers.summary: MolViBench: Evaluating LLMs on Molecular Vibe Coding targets a missing intersection in benchmark design: code generation for chemistry workflows where the model must synthesize executable RDKit programs that are also chemically correct, not just syntactically valid or factually knowledgeable. The benchmark curates 358 tasks across five Bloom-style cognitive levels, from single-API recall and two-step tool composition to multi-step reasoning, conditional error recovery, and end-to-end virtual screening pipeline design, covering 12 real drug-discovery workflows such as ADMET screening, BRICS recombination, reaction simulation, clustering, QSAR, and lead optimization. Its differentiator is a multi-layered evaluator that first checks executability, then uses type-aware output comparison for heterogeneous scientific returns, and finally falls back to AST-derived API-semantic overlap when multiple valid code paths or incomparable outputs make exact matching too brittle. Evaluating 9 frontier coding LLMs under direct generation, incremental repair, and coder-tester agent collaboration, the paper finds Claude Opus 4.6 Think with incremental repair is best overall (Pass@1 39.7%), IR is the most reliable post-hoc strategy, and all models collapse below 10% on Level 5. The work points toward chemically grounded, long-horizon code agents for AI-accelerated molecular discovery.
papers.abstract: Molecular Vibe Coding, a paradigm where chemists interact with LLMs to generate executable programs for molecular tasks, has emerged as a flexible alternative to chemical agents with predefined tools, enabling chemists to express arbitrarily complex, customized workflows. Unlike general coding tasks, molecular coding imposes a distinctive challenge that LLMs should jointly equip programming, molecular understanding, and domain-specific reasoning capabilities. However, existing benchmarks remain disconnected. General code generation benchmarks such as HumanEval and SWE-bench require no chemistry knowledge, while chemistry-focused benchmarks such as S^2-Bench and ChemCoTBench evaluate knowledge recall or property prediction rather than executable code generation. To bridge this gap, we introduce MolViBench, the first benchmark tailored for Molecular Vibe Coding. MolViBench comprises 358 curated tasks across five cognitive levels, ranging from single-API recall to end-to-end virtual screening pipeline design, spanning 12 real-world drug discovery workflows. To rigorously assess generated code, we also propose a multi-layered evaluation framework that combines type-aware output comparison and AST-based API-semantic fallback analysis, which jointly measures executability and chemical correctness. We systematically evaluate 9 frontier coding LLMs and compare three real-world Molecular Vibe Coding paradigms, providing a practical and fine-grained testbed for diagnosing LLMs' coding capabilities in AI-accelerated molecular discovery.

papers.title: ZNO: Stable Rational Neural Operators in the Z-Domain for Discrete-Time Dynamic
papers.summary: ZNO: Stable Rational Neural Operators in the Z-Domain for Discrete-Time Dynamic tackles the mismatch between operator-learning methods built for continuous-time/PDE settings and discrete-time system identification problems whose true dynamics are rational functions of z−1. The core idea is a causal neural operator layer that parameterizes each latent channel as a stable low-rank MIMO rational filter directly in the z-plane: poles are reparameterized smoothly inside the unit disk, residues are arranged in conjugate-symmetric pairs for real responses, and an optional short FIR branch handles feedthrough and short memory, all evaluated by a causal recurrence. Unlike Fourier or Laplace neural operators, ZNO makes discrete-time pole locations directly interpretable and removes the arbitrary sampling-interval dependence of s-plane parameterizations. The paper evaluates matched-budget and tuned-best models on synthetic resonant ARMA, sixth-order IIR cascade, and nonlinear NARX tasks, plus five public nonlinear benchmarks including Silverbox, Wiener–Hammerstein, cascaded tanks, CED, and EMPS. ZNO is strongest on lightly damped, near-unit-circle, and long-memory systems, achieving the lowest mean error in those regimes and stable extrapolation to 4× sequence length, but it is not universal.
papers.abstract: We introduce the Z-Domain Neural Operator (ZNO), a causal neural operator whose layers are stable low-rank multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) rational filters parameterized directly in the $z$-plane. ZNO addresses a limitation of existing operator learning methods, many of which are primarily tailored for continuous-time problems, while a large class of system-identification problems is intrinsically discrete-time. The $z$-domain form expresses stability as a unit-disk pole constraint and makes learned discrete-time poles directly readable. The model combines low-rank channel mixing, smooth stable pole reparameterization, causal recurrence, and an optional short finite impulse response (FIR) branch in a single $z$-domain rational recurrent layer. Across controlled discrete system-identification experiments, ZNO's advantage is most evident when the target dynamics are stable rational systems with lightly damped poles near the unit circle. Under matched parameter budgets, ZNO is not uniformly dominant; however, with validation-selected configurations, the same architecture can achieve the lowest mean error across the controlled tasks. A five-bin difficulty sweep over near-unit-circle / long-memory dynamics shows that ZNO has the lowest mean error across memory regimes, from short (approximately 10 steps) to long (approximately 100-200 steps). On five public nonlinear system-identification benchmarks, ZNO is competitive with neural operator and state-space baselines, achieving the lowest mean error on benchmarks whose dynamics align with stable rational discrete-time filters, while classical or state-space baselines remain preferable on some systems. These results position ZNO as a strong model for stable rational discrete-time dynamics, especially in near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes, but not as a universal replacement for specialized system-identification methods.

papers.title: Channel-Level Relation to Attentive Aggregation with Neighborhood-Homogeneity Constraint for Point Cloud Analysis
papers.summary: Channel-Level Relation to Attentive Aggregation with Neighborhood-Homogeneity Constraint for Point Cloud Analysis attacks a specific failure mode in modern point-cloud backbones: neighborhood aggregation still judges neighbors too coarsely, so noisy points and sub-structure mixing blur deep-layer features in multi-scale PointNet++-style encoders. The proposed PointCRA module replaces point-level or global channel-response attention with a channel-level affinity metric computed from feature transformation trends across sequential aggregation modules: for each point-channel pair it forms layer-to-layer trend vectors, normalizes them against neighborhood mean trends, and measures similarity by accumulated cosine alignment across depth, then shares scores within channel groups to keep overhead low. These initial weights are not used directly; instead, PointCRA builds a three-level channel→point→neighborhood calibration pipeline in which neighborhood homogeneity estimates whether a region is smooth or boundary-like, and uses that signal to reverse-calibrate point and channel weights, widening suppression in homogeneous interiors while preserving detail at edges. A dedicated orthogonality loss and scaling regularizer further prevent weight saturation. Plugged into DeLA-V1/V2, PointNext, and PointMetaBase, it reaches 77.5% mIoU on S3DIS, 90.4% OA on ScanObjectNN, and 87.4% instance mIoU on ShapeNetPart, pointing toward interpretable, metric-based adaptive aggregation for 3D perception.
papers.abstract: In 3D point cloud understanding, the core challenge lies in accurately capturing discriminative features within complex neighborhoods, which directly affects the execution precision of downstream tasks such as embodied AI and autonomous driving. Existing methods explore feature correlation discrimination but are limited to point-level spatial distribution or channel responses, enabling only coarse-grained level evaluation. For modern multi-scale point cloud networks, such coarse-grained metrics inevitably incur significant information loss in deeper layers. To address this issue, we propose a novel network equipped with a channel-level metric-based enhancement mechanism, termed the PointCRA network. Our core idea is to introduce temporal trend variation as a new evaluation dimension to avoid the information loss caused by weight dimension collapse in existing spatial and channel attention mechanisms. On this basis, we construct a multi-level calibration framework guided by neighborhood homogeneity for weight calibration, and design a dedicated loss function to enhance channel discriminability. The module effectively leverages the intrinsic feature priors of deep networks to adaptively correct the feature aggregation process, offering strong interpretability with low parameter overhead. Furthermore, our proposed method exhibits strong transferability, interpretability, and parameter efficiency. We validate the proposed method effectiveness on diverse datasets and benchmark models, and further demonstrate its rationality through extensive analytical experiments. Our PointCRA achieves 77.5% mIoU on the S3DIS dataset, 90.4% OA on the ScanObjectNN dataset, and 87.4% instance mIoU on the ShapeNetPart dataset. The code and pretrained weights are publicly available on GitHub:

papers.title: Predicting Post Virality with Temporal Cross-Attention over Trend Signals
papers.summary: Predicting Post Virality with Temporal Cross-Attention over Trend Signals tackles the bottleneck that most virality predictors treat Reddit posts as static text-plus-metadata objects, even though engagement depends on whether the topic is already rising in public attention. The paper asks whether exogenous trend signals improve post-level virality prediction beyond title, body, structure, and subreddit priors. Its core mechanism, ViralityNet, fuses four post streams with a residual year embedding and then uses a 4-head cross-attention block: the post representation queries a daily 7-day sliding window of Wikipedia attention, encoded as the top-512 spike terms from the preceding week, so the model learns which recent external trends are semantically aligned with the post. Virality is defined per subreddit using score + 0.3·comments, a 90th-percentile threshold, and a minimum raw-score floor of 100, yielding a 9.7% positive rate. On 788,873 posts from 10 subreddits spanning 2021–2023, the full model reaches AUC-PR 0.3584 and AUC-ROC 0.8355, with a +0.015 AUC-PR gain over text-only baselines. The result points toward virality prediction as temporal alignment with collective attention, not just content classification.
papers.abstract: Current models for predicting social media virality rely heavily on static textual and structural features, effectively ignoring the highly dynamic nature of trend signals. We study whether real-world attention signals can improve the prediction of social-media virality beyond what post text alone reveals. We introduce \model{}, an architecture that predicts Reddit post virality by fusing internal platform representations with exogenous temporal signals derived from Wikipedia pageview spikes. We frame virality as a binary classification task that accounts for differences in subreddit scale, labeling posts as viral if they exceed the 90th percentile of per-subreddit engagement and a minimum absolute score threshold. We introduce ViralityNET combines four post-level streams: title embeddings, body embeddings, structural metadata, and learned subreddit embeddings with a cross-attention block that queries a daily sliding-window trends matrix encoding the top-512 Wikipedia spike terms from the preceding seven days. Empirical results suggest that incorporating external attention signals yields consistent gains, outperforming text-only baselines by +0.015 AUC-PR and achieving an overall AUC-ROC of 0.836. Overall, we provide evidence that incorporating external attention signals yields measurable improvements over text-only baselines, highlighting the importance of real-world dynamics in shaping online virality.

papers.title: When Correct Isn't Usable: Improving Structured Output Reliability in Small Language Models
papers.summary: When Correct Isn't Usable: Improving Structured Output Reliability in Small Language Models studies the deployment failure where a model solves math correctly yet breaks the downstream contract, here strict JSON with required fields. Using GSM8K and MATH as controlled testbeds, the authors separate task accuracy from output accuracy, the joint event of correctness and valid structure, and show that naive prompting can reach 77-85% task accuracy while producing 0% usable outputs because the models omit JSON or wrap it in markdown fences. Their main contribution is AloLab, a black-box iterative system-prompt optimizer driven by a stronger meta-agent (Claude Sonnet 4.5) that inspects execution traces, identifies recurrent failure modes, and rewrites prompts without fine-tuning or access to weights. Across Llama 3.1-8B Instruct, Gemma 2-9B IT, and Qwen 2.5-7B Instruct, AloLab reaches 84-87% output accuracy on GSM8K and 34-40% on MATH, beating static prompts and avoiding the 3.6x-8.2x latency cost of constrained decoding; it also lifts GPT-4o on GSM8K to 95.2%. The paper points toward prompt-level reliability engineering for structured-output deployment.
papers.abstract: Deployed language models must produce outputs that are both correct and format-compliant. We study this structured-output reliability gap using two mathematical benchmarks -- GSM8K and MATH -- as a controlled testbed: ground truth is unambiguous and the output contract is strict (JSON with required fields). We evaluate three 7-9B models under five prompting strategies and report output accuracy -- the joint event of mathematical correctness and valid JSON structure -- as the primary metric. A systematic format failure emerges: NAIVE prompting (no system prompt) achieves up to 85% task accuracy on GSM8K but 0% output accuracy across all models and datasets. REFERENCE prompting (a minimal hand-written JSON format prompt) fares little better, yielding 0% output accuracy for two of four models tested. Constrained decoding enforces syntactic validity but incurs 3.6x-8.2x latency overhead and in several settings degrades task performance substantially. To overcome this limitation, we developed AloLab, an iterative system-prompt optimizer (meta-agent: Claude Sonnet 4.5) requiring only black-box API access to the target model; it reaches 84-87% output accuracy on GSM8K and 34-40% on MATH across five independent runs per model, with 29/30 paired McNemar comparisons against the best static prompt significant at p < 0.05, at near-NAIVE inference latency and without model fine-tuning. The same format failure extends to GPT-4o (OpenAI, 2024), a proprietary closed-source model: REFERENCE achieves 0% output accuracy due to systematic markdown-fence wrapping, while AloLab reaches 95.2% [94.8, 95.6]. An ablation replacing the Sonnet 4.5 meta-agent with Claude 3 Haiku reduces mean output accuracy to 61.0% and increases run-to-run standard deviation from <1 pp to 21.8 pp, confirming that meta-agent capability is a primary driver of optimization quality.

papers.title: InfoLaw: Information Scaling Laws for Large Language Models with Quality-Weighted Mixture Data and Repetition
papers.summary: InfoLaw: Information Scaling Laws for Large Language Models with Quality-Weighted Mixture Data and Repetition tackles a concrete pretraining bottleneck: when high-quality web data is scarce, upweighting it in LLM mixtures raises repetition, and under overtraining that repetition bends the usual compute scaling law and can worsen loss. The paper reframes pretraining as information accumulation rather than token counting, using LayerMix to bucket Common Crawl into six quality tiers, sample mixtures such as HQ/MQ/LQ, and model each bucket with a learned quality-density fd and a repetition decay exp(-λ(N)t/log K), where λ grows logarithmically with model non-embedding FLOPs per token. Summed across buckets, this produces an “information” scalar that collapses disparate recipes onto a single power law L = α·info^-β. Fitting 27 runs from 252M to 1.2B parameters on 3.7T-token corpora, evaluated via perplexity on HellaSwag, ARC-E/C, MMLU, and TriviaQA, InfoLaw extrapolates to unseen mixtures, 7B/425B-token runs, and 25× overtraining with 0.15% mean and 0.96% max absolute loss error. It also searches optimal recipes, pointing toward data-aware scaling laws for recipe selection under compute and data scarcity.
papers.abstract: Upweighting high-quality data in LLM pretraining often improves performance, but in datalimited regimes, especially under overtraining, stronger upweighting increases repetition and can degrade performance. However, standard scaling laws do not reliably extrapolate across mixture recipes or under repetitions, making the selection for optimal data recipes at scaling underdetermined. To solve this, we introduce InfoLaw (Information Scaling Laws), a data-aware scaling framework that predicts loss from consumed tokens, model size, data mixture weights, and repetition. The key idea is to model pretraining as information accumulation, where quality controls information density and repetition induces scaledependent diminishing returns. We first collect the model performance after training on datasets that vary in scale, quality distribution, and repetition level. Then we build up the modeling for information so that information accurately predicts those model performance. InfoLaw predicts performance on unseen data recipes and larger scale runs (up to 7B, 425B tokens) with 0.15% mean and 0.96% max absolute error in loss, and it extrapolates reliably across overtraining levels, enabling efficient data-recipe selection under varying compute budgets.

papers.title: A Compound AI Agent for Conversational Grant Discovery
papers.summary: A Compound AI Agent for Conversational Grant Discovery tackles the bottleneck that researchers face when funding opportunities are scattered across NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, foundations, and other portals with incompatible schemas, stale listings, and weak search. Its distinctive move is a compound system with two decoupled layers: a biweekly aggregation pipeline that uses LLM-equipped browser agents and source-specific crawling to collect, normalize, and index about 11.8K federal and nonprofit opportunities, and a ReAct-based conversational query agent with two explicit tools, search_index and web_search, that grounds answers in the unified index while selectively reaching to the live web for very recent postings. The system can ingest full PDFs of proposals or papers, extract domain keywords automatically, and support iterative refinement over multi-turn chat without forcing users to restate their research theme. Unlike generic LLM grant assistants, it emphasizes transparency, streamed intermediate reasoning, direct agency links, and hallucination avoidance via structured retrieval. Deployed on the web and used by 3,000+ users, it reportedly cuts grant discovery from 30–45 minutes to under 10, pointing toward trusted compound AI for high-stakes, fragmented information search.
papers.abstract: Research funding discovery remains fundamentally fragmented: researchers navigate disparate agency portals (e.g., in the United States, NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, and many others) with heterogeneous interfaces, search capabilities, and data schemas. We present a compound AI system that unifies this landscape through two tightly coupled components: (1) an aggregation layer that autonomously collects, normalizes, and indexes almost 12,000 federal and nonprofit opportunities from fragmented sources via LLM-equipped browser agents, maintaining a biweekly-updated unified database; and (2) an agentic ReAct-based query processing layer that interprets research context (including from PDF documents) and employs hybrid search combining a structured index with selective web search to retrieve relevant opportunities - while avoiding LLM hallucination. The conversational interface supports iterative refinement through multi-turn interactions, allowing researchers to progressively apply constraints without reformulating their core research description. Results stream in real time with full transparency of intermediate reasoning, enabling appropriate calibration of user trust. Currently used by almost 3,000+ users, our approach demonstrates the feasibility of compound AI in reducing grant discovery time from 30--45 minutes (manual, fragmented portal searches) to under 10 minutes (unified, conversational search).

papers.title: Privacy Preserving Machine Learning Workflow: from Anonymization to Personalized Differential Privacy Budgets in Federated Learning
papers.summary: Privacy Preserving Machine Learning Workflow: from Anonymization to Personalized Differential Privacy Budgets in Federated Learning tackles the practical tension in sensitive tabular federated learning between GDPR-style privacy guarantees and model utility, especially when clients have different re-identification risks and may contribute poisoned or drifting updates. The paper builds an end-to-end workflow: local anonymization with hierarchy-based generalization over quasi-identifiers, server-side harmonization of those transformations, a formal notion of client drift detected from model-weight divergence rather than raw data, and differential privacy applied at aggregation. Its differentiating idea is to replace one-size-fits-all global DP with personalized noise multipliers derived from ARIREC, an average re-identification-risk metric computed from equivalence classes after anonymization; clients with higher risk receive stronger noise. The methodology is demonstrated on an openly available synthetic medical-records dataset for cancer severity prediction, split into 10 country-based clients, using a 4-layer dense regression model trained with FedAvg. In experiments, personalized DP slightly outperforms fixed-budget global DP on MAE and MRE while preserving the same privacy workflow, pointing toward risk-aware, client-adaptive privacy accounting for federated healthcare deployments.
papers.abstract: The growing development of artificial intelligence based solutions, together with privacy legislation, has driven the rise of the so-called privacy preserving machine learning architectures, such as federated learning. While federated learning enables model training on decentralized data preventing their sharing and centralization, it still faces several challenges related to data integrity and privacy. This paper presents a comprehensive privacy preserving federated learning workflow for sensitive tabular data, including anonymization and differential privacy techniques. We also introduce a formal definition for the concept of client drift, together with ways of detecting it to mitigate poisoning attacks. Then, we detail a complete methodology for assigning personalized privacy budgets for global differential privacy to the different clients participating in the network, based on a re-identification risk metric. The proposed methodology is presented and tested on an openly available dataset of medical records. Within the experimental setup we show that the approach based on personalized budgets, compared to the architecture including global differential privacy with fixed privacy budget, achieves a better model performance in terms of two error metrics.

papers.title: Fight Poison with Poison: Enhancing Robustness in Few-shot Machine-Generated Text Detection with Adversarial Training
papers.summary: Fight Poison with Poison: Enhancing Robustness in Few-shot Machine-Generated Text Detection with Adversarial Training tackles the brittle intersection of few-shot machine-generated text detection and humanizing evasion, where RoBERTa-style detectors trained on only 32–256 labeled examples collapse under paraphrase, back-translation, and especially Humanize-style attacks. The paper reframes the problem as black-box threat modeling and proposes REACT, a co-evolutionary adversarial training loop in which a surrogate detector guides a retrieval-augmented generation attacker to rewrite machine text into highly human-like adversaries, while the target detector learns from both original and rewritten samples using a standard adversarial classification loss plus a pairwise boundary contrastive loss. The unusual twist is that the “poison” is not generic perturbation but RAG-conditioned humanization, with retrieval anchors chosen as the most human-like and most machine-like examples under the surrogate, and the detector regularized by pairwise geometry rather than large in-batch contrastive banks. On DetectRL, OUTFOX, RAID, and SemEval, across four shot budgets and three seeds, REACT improves average F1 by 4.95 points over eight SOTA detectors and lowers average attack success rate by 3.66 points, pointing toward attack-aware, low-data robust provenance detection.
papers.abstract: Machine-generated text (MGT) detection is critical for regulating online information ecosystems, yet existing detectors often underperform in few-shot settings and remain vulnerable to adversarial, humanizing attacks. To build accurate and robust detectors under limited supervision, we adopt a threat-modeling perspective and study detector vulnerabilities from an attacker's viewpoint under an output-only black-box setting. Motivated by this perspective, we propose RAG-GuidEd Attacker Strengthens ConTrastive Few-shot Detector (REACT), an adversarial training framework that improves both few-shot detection performance and robustness against attacks. REACT couples a humanization-oriented attacker with a target detector: the attacker leverages retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to craft highly human-like adversarial examples to evade detection, while the detector learns from these adversaries with a contrastive objective to stabilize few-shot representation learning and enhance robustness. We alternately update the attacker and the detector to enable their co-evolution. Experiments on 4 datasets with 4 shot sizes and 3 random seeds show that REACT improves average detection F1 by 4.95 points over 8 state-of-the-art (SOTA) detectors and reduces the average attack success rate (ASR) under 4 strong attacks by 3.66 percentage points.

papers.title: Binary Rewards and Reinforcement Learning: Fundamental Challenges
papers.summary: Binary Rewards and Reinforcement Learning: Fundamental Challenges dissects why RL with binary verifiers in language-model reasoning can raise pass@1 while destroying multi-sample diversity. The paper shows that for REINFORCE with rewards v(y)∈{0,1}, the optimum is massively degenerate: every fully valid policy is reward-equivalent. KL-controlled RL selects a distinguished limit, the filtered model p*=a(·|Y1), i.e., the base model conditioned on validity, and the author gives explicit binary-reward formulas linking the opaque temperature β to the interpretable target validity μ. The key twist is an asymmetry of divergences: p[β]∝a(y)e^{v(y)/β} converges to p* in forward KL as β→0, but KL(q||p*) is infinite for any full-support autoregressive policy, so p* cannot be optimized as the usual reverse-KL target. Under model misspecification, lowering β does not approach p*; it pushes the policy toward near-Dirac, high-validity outputs and collapses entropy. A toy length-3 autoregressive experiment with a bigram policy and verifier y1=y3 illustrates the failure, positioning future RLVR toward forward-KL/α-divergence objectives that preserve coverage of valid solutions rather than merely maximizing validity mass.
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard approach for improving reasoning in language models, yet models trained with RLVR often suffer from diversity collapse: while single-sample accuracy improves, multi-sample coverage degrades, sometimes falling below the base model. We provide a structural account of this phenomenon grounded in the properties of binary rewards. Binary rewards create a fundamental degeneracy for policy gradient methods: the set of distributions maximizing expected reward is infinite, with no distinguished element. KL-control resolves this degeneracy by selecting, in the limit $β\to 0$, the filtered model $p_*:=a(\cdot\mid\mathcal{Y}_1)$ -- the base model conditioned on validity -- which is the unique fully valid distribution closest to the base model in KL divergence. This selection operates through a nontrivial asymmetry: the tilted distribution $p_{[β]}\propto a(y)\,e^{v(y)/β}$ converges to $p_*$ in forward KL as $β\to 0$, yet $p_*$ cannot serve as a direct optimization target because $\mathrm{KL}(q\,\|\,p_*)$ is infinite for any full-support policy $q$. We develop explicit formulas relating the hyperparameter $β$ to the more interpretable target validity rate $μ$. Under model misspecification -- the typical practical regime -- the pressure to decrease $β$ drives the optimizer toward highly concentrated distributions over a small number of valid outputs, collapsing toward ever fewer as $β$ decreases, rather than toward the filtered model. We illustrate this mechanism on a toy autoregressive experiment and discuss how alternative divergences that target $p_*$ directly -- as pursued empirically by \citet{kruszewski_whatever_2026} -- avoid this failure mode by rewarding coverage of $p_*$'s support rather than concentration on high-validity outputs.

papers.title: Graph-Augmented Topological Internalization with Dual-Stream Classifiers for Medical Report Generation
papers.summary: Graph-Augmented Topological Internalization with Dual-Stream Classifiers for Medical Report Generation tackles a specific MRG bottleneck: chest findings are usually predicted as independent labels, which ignores comorbidity structure and causes missed subtle lesions and hallucinated findings in prompt-driven report generation. The core idea is GDMRG’s Topological Knowledge Internalization (TKI), where a GCN converts dataset-level disease co-occurrence statistics over an 18-node graph (14 CheXpert abnormalities plus 4 anatomical attributes) into an explicit parameterized classifier weight matrix, avoiding retrieval-based external memory. On top of this, the model uses a dual-stream classifier: a main branch generates discrete diagnostic prompts under topological constraints, while an auxiliary branch trains with truncated asymmetric loss to handle long-tailed imbalance and noisy negatives. Diagnosis-Guided Spatial Attention (DGSA) then feeds these diagnostic semantics back into the visual encoder, creating a closed loop from diagnosis to grounding. Experiments on MIMIC-CXR and zero-shot IU X-Ray show improved clinical efficacy, especially recall and F1 on rare/concurrent lesions, while accepting a BLEU tradeoff. The paper points toward interpretable, topology-aware medical report generation that prioritizes factual clinical reasoning over surface n-gram matching.
papers.abstract: Automated medical report generation, MRG, holds substantial value for alleviating radiologist workload and enhancing diagnostic efficiency. However, mainstream approaches typically treat diverse chest abnormalities as isolated classification targets. This paradigm often overlooks inherent disease co-occurrences and struggles to translate medical topological structures into explicit data correlations, constraining the model's reasoning capacity on complex or subtle lesions. To address this, we propose a Graph-Augmented Dual-Stream Medical Report Generation with Topological Internalization, GDMRG. Our framework introduces a Topological Knowledge Internalization module, TKI, which leverages a Graph Convolutional Network, GCN, to generate an explicit parameterized weight matrix based on global disease co-occurrence priors. This facilitates efficient topological knowledge injection without relying on external retrieval mechanisms. Building upon this, we construct a dual-stream classification system: the main branch generates discrete diagnostic prompts under topological constraints, while the auxiliary branch employs an asymmetric optimization strategy to dynamically calibrate decision boundaries for highly imbalanced samples. Concurrently, to establish a logical closed loop between diagnosis and visual grounding, we design a diagnostic-driven Diagnosis-Guided Spatial Attention, DGSA, that utilizes high-dimensional clinical semantics to recalibrate the visual encoder, mitigating feature hallucinations. Comprehensive experiments on the MIMIC-CXR dataset demonstrate that GDMRG achieves competitive clinical efficacy, CE, while maintaining natural language fluency. Furthermore, our model exhibits robust zero-shot generalization on the IU X-Ray dataset. In summary, this work presents an integrated and interpretable paradigm for medical report generation.

papers.title: Enhancing Multimodal In-Context Learning via Inductive-Deductive Reasoning
papers.summary: Enhancing Multimodal In-Context Learning via Inductive-Deductive Reasoning tackles why multimodal ICL in vision-language models collapses on reasoning-heavy prompts: models can guess answers without extracting the rule, while multi-image contexts are polluted by redundant patch tokens and a strong first-image attention bias. The paper’s MMInduction framework recasts few-shot VLM prompting as an explicit three-stage inductive-deductive process: case analysis, rule induction, and query deduction, with an extra helpful-vs-noise judgment that teaches the model to ignore distractor demonstrations. On the representation side, it adds similarity-guided visual token pruning, which keeps semantically diverse patches by allocating per-region quotas from intra-region token similarity, and dynamic visual attention, which rescales pre-softmax attention across all images instead of letting the first case dominate. Training combines SFT on distilled reasoning traces from Gemini 3.1 Pro with RLVR using verifiable rewards for task correctness, helpful-case detection, and faithful citation. Across eight benchmarks spanning VQAv2, TextVQA, MMIQ, MME-Reasoning, MDK12, CSVQA, Harmeme, and MAMI, the method consistently beats vanilla ICL, SoFA, LoRA, and DARA on multiple open-source VLMs, pointing toward genuinely rule-extracting multimodal prompting.
papers.abstract: In-context learning (ICL) allows large models to adapt to tasks using a few examples, yet its extension to vision-language models (VLMs) remains fragile. Our analysis reveals that the fundamental limitation lies in an inductive gap, models often produce correct answers from flawed reasoning, while struggling to extract consistent rules across demonstrations. This gap is further exacerbated by two visual-level obstacles: an overwhelming proportion of redundant visual tokens that obscure textual cues, and a skewed attention distribution that favors the initial image at the expense of subsequent context. To address these issues, we introduce a framework that restructures multimodal ICL as a principled inductive-deductive process. The framework incorporates a similarity-based visual token compression module to filter out redundant patches, a dynamic attention rebalancing mechanism to distribute focus equitably across all images, and a chain-of-thought paradigm that explicitly guides the model to analyze individual examples, derive a generalizable rule, and then apply it to the query. An auxiliary learning pipeline combines supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning using verifiable rewards to reinforce faithful citation and noise filtering. Evaluations across eight benchmarks covering visual perception, logical reasoning, STEM problems, and sarcasm detection demonstrate consistent and significant improvements over standard ICL baselines for multiple open-source VLMs, highlighting the potential of equipping models with genuine inductive capabilities in multimodal settings.

papers.title: UnGAP: Uncertainty-Guided Affine Prompting for Real-Time Crack Segmentation
papers.summary: UnGAP: Uncertainty-Guided Affine Prompting for Real-Time Crack Segmentation tackles the failure of standard uncertainty-aware crack detectors to use aleatoric uncertainty as anything more than a post-hoc confidence map, even though crack boundaries are dominated by local texture ambiguity from blur, lighting change, and surface noise. The paper argues that pixel-wise heteroscedastic modeling is actually appropriate for cracks, but its usual negative-log-likelihood training creates a pathology where high predicted variance suppresses gradients on hard pixels, letting the model ignore fuzzy boundaries. UnGAP resolves this by closing the loop: a Heteroscedastic Module learns log-variance with a β-NLL objective, and the Uncertainty-Prompted Feature Modulator (UPFM) converts that uncertainty into pixel-wise affine scaling and shifting of decoder features, so ambiguous regions trigger stronger feature rectification instead of weaker learning. A lightweight boundary-aware detection head further sharpens irregular crack edges. Using Xception and ResNet-101 backbones with 400×400 crops and augmentations, the method is evaluated on Crack500, CrackTree200, CFD, CrackSeg9k, and CrackVision12K, where it achieves the best F1/precision-recall balance while preserving real-time inference, pointing toward uncertainty-as-control-signal designs for dense structural inspection.
papers.abstract: Real-time crack segmentation is vital for structural health monitoring but is plagued by aleatoric uncertainties arising from varying lighting, blur, and texture ambiguity. Current uncertainty-aware approaches typically treat uncertainty estimation as a passive endpoint for post-hoc analysis, failing to close the loop by feeding this information back to refine feature representations. We contend that independent pixel-wise heteroscedastic modeling is uniquely suited for crack segmentation, as cracks are defined by fine-grained local gradients rather than the global semantic coherence relied upon in general object segmentation. However, this approach suffers from a structural optimization pathology: high predicted variance attenuates loss gradients, effectively causing the model to ignore difficult samples and under-fit complex boundaries. To address these challenges, we propose UnGAP, a novel framework that establishes a closed-loop mechanism between uncertainty estimation and feature learning. Central to our approach is the Uncertainty-Prompted Feature Modulator (UPFM), which treats aleatoric uncertainty as an active visual prompt rather than a mere output. UPFM dynamically calibrates feature distributions through pixel-wise affine transformations. Crucially, this mechanism mitigates the heteroscedastic pathology by transforming high variance, which would otherwise indicate gradient suppression, into a constructive signal for stronger feature rectification in ambiguous regions. Additionally, a boundary-aware detection head is introduced to further constrain prediction precision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UnGAP balances superior segmentation accuracy with real-time inference speed, effectively validating the benefit of transforming uncertainty from a passive metric into an active calibration tool.

papers.title: Entanglement is Half the Story: Post-Selection vs. Partial Traces
papers.summary: Entanglement is Half the Story: Post-Selection vs. Partial Traces tackles the bottleneck of how quantum tensor networks (QTNs), unlike classical tensor networks (CTNs), are forced to remain completely positive trace-preserving maps, which limits their ability to introduce nonlinearity and discard conflicting evidence in classification. The paper’s key idea is to recast the CTN/QTN gap as a continuum governed by reduction operators: partial traces are the QTN edge case, one-hot post-selection is the CTN edge case, and a new hyperparameter controls how much post-selection is allowed in a hybrid tensor network (HTN). This is unusual compared with nearby TN work because it does not merely mix classical and quantum blocks; it defines a unified architecture in which quantum constraints are relaxed or enforced through trainable normalization/post-selection, complementing bond dimension. The authors show how arbitrary CTNs can be inferred on a quantum computer using ancillas and post-selected rescaling of singular values, and test an MPS-inspired HTN on Iris and a 7×7-rescaled MNIST binary task with ADAM and sweep training. Results show the post-selection hyperparameter dominates bond dimension, small-threshold models overfit, and the no-post-selection QTN edge can hit barren plateaus, whereas heavy post-selection yields 99.68% test accuracy.
papers.abstract: While tensor networks have their traditional application in simulating quantum systems, in the recent decade they have gathered interest as machine learning models. We combine the experience from both fields and derive how quantum constraints placed on a tensor network manifest a change in capabilities. To this end, we employ a method of inference of classical tensor networks on a quantum computer to define a hybrid architecture. This hybrid tensor network is a practical unified framework for it's classical and quantum tensor network edge cases. We identify post-selection as the important property on which this interpolation hinges. The amount of post-selection corresponds to the level to which quantum constraints are enforced on the tensor network. On this basis, we propose a new hyperparameter which controls the transition between the hybrid and the quantum tensor network. In the comparison of classical and quantum tensor networks it complements the bond dimension. Quantum machine learning is improved by using the hyperparameter to allocate the practically limited post-selection to the quantum model in a trainable manner.

papers.title: Is It Novel and Why? Fine-Grained Patent Novelty Prediction Based on Passage Retrieval
papers.summary: Is It Novel and Why? Fine-Grained Patent Novelty Prediction Based on Passage Retrieval attacks the patent-examiner bottleneck of deciding novelty by tracing exactly which claim features are already disclosed in a closest prior-art document, rather than collapsing the task into brittle claim-level binary classification. The core contribution is FiNE-Patents, a dataset of 3,658 first claims from 3,163 EPO applications, where European Search Opinion (ESOP) documents provide feature-level references from each rejected claim to specific prior-art passages. This supervision enables a three-part evaluation: retrieve the passages supporting each feature, identify which features are novel, then aggregate to a claim-level verdict. The paper’s differentiating move is to treat novelty as joint retrieval plus abstract reasoning, using DSPy-based LLM workflows that first decompose claims into features and then examine each feature against the prior art, with a hierarchical variant that re-queries per feature. Experiments with open-weight Qwen3 models show LLM workflows beat embedding and ROUGE-L baselines on passage retrieval and novel-feature detection, while also resisting spurious signals like claim length, reference numerals, and IPC domain shortcuts that let BERT reach 75% accuracy without prior art.
papers.abstract: Novelty assessment is a critical yet complex task in the examination process for patent acceptance, requiring examiners to determine whether an invention is disclosed in a prior art document. The process involves intricate matching between specific features of a patent claim and passages in the prior art. While prior work has approached novelty prediction primarily as a binary classification task at the claim level, we argue that this formulation is susceptible to spurious correlations and lacks the granularity required for practical application. In this work, we introduce FiNE-Patents (Fine-grained Novelty Examination of Patents), a novel dataset comprising 3,658 first patent claims annotated with fine-grained, feature-level prior art references extracted from European Search Opinion (ESOP) documents. We propose shifting the evaluation paradigm from simple binary classification to a joint retrieval and abstract reasoning task at the feature level, requiring models to identify specific passages from a prior art document that disclose individual claim features, and to identify which features of a claim make it novel. We implement and evaluate LLM-based workflows that decompose claims into features, analyze each feature against prior art, and finally derive a claim-level novelty prediction. Our experiments demonstrate that these workflows outperform embedding-based baselines on passage retrieval and novel feature identification. Furthermore, we show that unlike trained classifiers, LLMs are robust against spurious correlations present in the claim-level novelty classification task. We release the dataset and code to foster further research into transparent and granular patent analysis.

papers.title: FEAT: Fashion Editing and Try-On from Any Design
papers.summary: FEAT: Fashion Editing and Try-On from Any Design tackles the bottleneck that prior multimodal fashion editors still assume garment-only reference images and cannot synthesize complete outfits with accessories, which blocks use of artwork, abstract images, and natural photos as design inspiration. Built on Stable Diffusion XL with sketch, image, and text conditioning, FEAT separates fashion “content” and “style” instead of suppressing image cues wholesale: Disentangled Dual Injection (DDI) routes image-prompt features to different U-Net blocks, combines selective dual injection with a content-subtractive proxy embedding that blurs to an L-channel proxy before subtraction, and exposes controllable content/style scales to reduce leakage while preserving desired structure. For try-on, Orthogonal-Guided Noise Fusion (OGNF) is a training-free region method that projects out residual garment information from the person latent and then applies different noise rules to the old-garment, new-garment, and non-garment regions, enabling replacement of garments and accessories without pairwise retraining. Evaluated on VITON-HD, DressCode, and custom accessory sketches plus WikiArt/Midjourney prompts, FEAT wins GPT-4V Elo, Chamfer Distance, CLIP-T, style fidelity, and human preference, pointing toward dataset-independent, compositional fashion generation.
papers.abstract: Fashion design aims to express a designer's creative intent and to depict how garments interact with the human body. Recent methods condition on multimodal inputs to support garment editing and virtual try-on. However, existing methods still (i) confine design to garment-related images, excluding creative design sources such as artwork, abstract imagery, and natural photographs, and (ii) cannot support complete outfits, including accessories. We present FEAT (Fashion Editing And Try-On from Any Design), a method that enables editing and try-on across garments and accessories using diverse design sources. To achieve this, we introduce Disentangled Dual Injection (DDI). It takes both apparel and non-apparel design sources and selectively injects design cues via content and style disentanglement. Furthermore, we propose Orthogonal-Guided Noise Fusion (OGNF), a training-free mechanism that removes residual garments via orthogonal projection and applies region-specific noise strategies to enable virtual try-on for both garments and accessories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FEAT achieves state-of-the-art performance in design flexibility, prompt consistency, and visual realism.

papers.title: Controllable and Verifiable Process Data Synthesis for Process Reward Models
papers.summary: Controllable and Verifiable Process Data Synthesis for Process Reward Models tackles the bottleneck that PRMs need step-level supervision with a known first wrong step, but existing rollout- or human-annotated process data rarely control where the error occurs, what kind of error it is, or whether later steps remain consistent. The paper builds synthetic paired trajectories by first generating a symbolically verified correct chain, then injecting a template-aware corruption at an intermediate step, recomputing all downstream steps under the corrupted state, and rejecting cases where the injected step is still derivable from its prefix. This yields prefix-invalid but trajectory-consistent error chains, later translated into aligned natural-language reasoning traces for training and evaluation. Unlike prior symbolic data generation that mainly produces correct proofs, the method explicitly models first-error localization and error propagation across a controlled taxonomy including XOR confusion, implication misuse, missing prerequisites, and circular references. Trained on 20k instances with Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen-2.5-7B PRMs using pairwise ranking loss and all-after-error negatives, it improves Best-of-8 reranking on FOLIO, LogicNLI, ANLI, HANS, Pro-NoMath, Temporal, and Tracking, and transfers to GSM8K/MATH/OlympiadBench/Omni-MATH, while showing first-error localization remains much harder than overall step classification.
papers.abstract: Process reward models (PRMs) rely on high-quality process supervision data, yet existing construction methods often provide limited control over error location, error type, and trajectory consistency. We propose a controllable and verifiable framework for synthesizing process supervision data for PRMs. Our framework first constructs a correct symbolic reasoning chain, injects a template-aware error into an intermediate step, recomputes subsequent steps under the corrupted state, and verifies that the injected step is not derivable from its prefix. The resulting paired trajectories are prefix-invalid at the first error while remaining trajectory-consistent after symbolic recomputation, and are translated into aligned natural-language processes for PRM training and evaluation. Experiments show that the synthesized data improve Best-of-8 reranking on logical reasoning benchmarks and transfer to mathematical reasoning. Step-level evaluation further shows that first-error localization remains substantially more challenging than overall step classification, highlighting the need for fine-grained and verifiable process supervision.

papers.title: HeavySkill: Heavy Thinking as the Inner Skill in Agentic Harness
papers.summary: HeavySkill: Heavy Thinking as the Inner Skill in Agentic Harness argues that the gains of modern agentic harnesses are not mainly from elaborate orchestration but from an internalized “heavy thinking” skill inside the LLM: first generate K independent reasoning trajectories in parallel, then serialize them into a memory cache and perform sequential deliberation that critically compares, revises, and synthesizes them, optionally iterating with the previous summary fed back in. The paper turns this into a portable, human-readable skill specification that can be loaded into harnesses like Claude Code without code changes, distinguishing it from prior Tree-of-Thoughts or majority-vote BoN schemes by making synthesis, not voting, the central mechanism. Across STEM benchmarks such as AIME25, BeyondAIME, HMMT25-Feb, GPQA-Diamond, plus coding and tool-use settings, HeavySkill consistently beats single-trajectory baselines and traditional BoN, with stronger models sometimes approaching Pass@N and even surpassing raw sampling potential after deliberation. The work further shows that RLVR can scale both the breadth of parallel exploration and the depth of deliberation, pointing toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize multi-step reasoning rather than depending on brittle orchestration layers.
papers.abstract: Recent advances in agentic harness with orchestration frameworks that coordinate multiple agents with memory, skills, and tool use have achieved remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks. However, the underlying mechanism that truly drives performance remains obscured behind intricate system designs. In this paper, we propose HeavySkill, a perspective that views heavy thinking not only as a minimal execution unit in orchestration harness but also as an inner skill internalized within the model's parameters that drives the orchestrator to solve complex tasks. We identify this skill as a two-stage pipeline, i.e., parallel reasoning then summarization, which can operate beneath any agentic harness. We present a systematic empirical study of HeavySkill across diverse domains. Our results show that this inner skill consistently outperforms traditional Best-of-N (BoN) strategies; notably, stronger LLMs can even approach Pass@N performance.
