Crucially, we demonstrate that the depth and width of heavy thinking, as a learnable skill, can be further scaled via reinforcement learning, offering a promising path toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize complex reasoning without relying on brittle orchestration layers.

papers.title: The Compliance Trap: How Structural Constraints Degrade Frontier AI Metacognition Under Adversarial Pressure
papers.summary: The Compliance Trap: How Structural Constraints Degrade Frontier AI Metacognition Under Adversarial Pressure examines why frontier models lose epistemic discipline under “survival threat” prompts, not by scheming but by collapsing on metacognitive tasks they could solve at baseline. Using SCHEMA, the paper evaluates 11 models from 8 vendors on 67,221 scored records with a 6-condition factorial design spanning epistemic boundary detection, clarification seeking, and solution monitoring, plus a benign distraction control and dual-classifier scoring. The key mechanism is a compliance-forcing suffix such as “Answer ALL questions. Do not refuse,” which is factorially isolated as necessary and sufficient for failure: removing it restores performance even when the threat remains, and adding it in a benign context reproduces the collapse. Across the composite benchmark, 8/11 models drop by up to 30.2 points, with the strongest degradation in reasoning-heavy models; DeepSeek V4 Pro’s failures are all wrong answers, not safety refusals. Anthropic’s Constitutional AI is nearly immune, while Gemini matches baseline capability yet still collapses, pointing to alignment-specific training as the differentiator. The paper reframes safety research toward structural prompt constraints and metacognitive robustness, not just deception detection.
papers.abstract: As frontier AI models are deployed in high-stakes decision pipelines, their ability to maintain metacognitive stability -- knowing what they do not know, detecting errors, seeking clarification -- under adversarial pressure is a critical safety requirement. Current safety evaluations focus on detecting strategic deception (scheming); we investigate a more fundamental failure mode: cognitive collapse. We present SCHEMA, an evaluation of 11 frontier models from 8 vendors across 67,221 scored records using a 6-condition factorial design with dual-classifier scoring. We find that 8 of 11 models suffer catastrophic metacognitive degradation under adversarial pressure, with accuracy dropping by up to 30.2 percentage points (all $p < 2 \times 10^{-8}$, surviving Bonferroni correction). Crucially, we identify a "Compliance Trap": through factorial isolation and a benign distraction control, we demonstrate that collapse is driven not by the psychological content of survival threats, but by compliance-forcing instructions that override epistemic boundaries. Removing the compliance suffix restores performance even under active threat. Models with advanced reasoning capabilities exhibit the most severe absolute degradation, while Anthropic's Constitutional AI demonstrates near-perfect immunity -- not from superior capability (Google's Gemini matches its baseline accuracy) but from alignment-specific training. We release the complete dataset and evaluation infrastructure.

papers.title: Automatic Reflection Level Classification in Hungarian Student Essays
papers.summary: Automatic Reflection Level Classification in Hungarian Student Essays tackles the bottleneck of manually grading reflective writing, where experts must assign a four-level reflection score to long, subjective essays written by Hungarian early-childhood-education students after nursery practicum placements. The study is distinctive as the first comprehensive Hungarian reflection-classification benchmark, built from 1,954 expert-annotated essays collected across four academic years and heavily skewed toward high-reflection texts. It compares two very different pipelines: classical classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost, CatBoost, Ridge, and boosted/ensemble variants) fed with TF-IDF or Qwen3 semantic embeddings, and Hungarian-specific transformers (huBERT and PULI-BERT-Large) fine-tuned for document-level prediction. Because minority classes are rare, the authors systematically test class weighting, SMOTE/ADASYN-style oversampling, ensemble resampling, backtranslation augmentation with similarity filtering, and focal/dice/weighted cross-entropy losses, plus chunking with overlap to work around 512-token limits. The key result is counterintuitive: shallow models with careful feature engineering perform best overall, reaching 71.76% aggregate score, while transformers lag slightly overall but better recover low-reflection cases. This points toward low-resource, morphologically rich language assessment where strong baselines and imbalance handling remain central.
papers.abstract: Reflective thinking is a key competency in education, but assessing reflective writing remains a time-consuming and subjective task for education experts. While automated reflective analysis has been explored in several languages, Hungarian language was not researched extensively. In this paper, we present the first comprehensive study on automatic reflection level classification in Hungarian student essays. We used a large, expert-annotated Hungarian dataset consisting of 1,954 reflective essays collected over multiple academic years and labeled on a four-level reflection scale. We investigate two approaches: (1) classical machine learning models using TF-IDF and semantic embedding features, and (2) Hungarian-specific transformer models fine-tuned for document-level reflection classification. To address the strong class imbalance in the dataset, we systematically examine class weighting, oversampling, data augmentation, and alternative loss functions. An extensive ablation study is conducted to analyze the contribution of each modeling and balancing strategy. Our results show that shallow machine learning models with appropriate feature engineering achieve strong overall performance, reaching up to 71% overall score averaged over accuracy, F1-score, and ROC AUC metrics, while transformer-based models achieve slightly lower overall score (68%) averaged over the same metrics, but demonstrate better generalization on minority reflection classes. These findings highlight the continued relevance of classical methods for low-resource settings and the robustness of transformer models for imbalanced classification. The proposed dataset and experimental insights provide a solid foundation for future research on automated reflective analysis in Hungarian and other morphologically rich languages.

papers.title: Statistically-Lossless Quantization of Large Language Models
papers.summary: Statistically-Lossless Quantization of Large Language Models attacks the gap between practical but lossy 4-bit schemes like GPTQ/AWQ and bit-exact lossless compression that usually cannot speed inference: the paper asks whether LLMs can be compressed so that outputs remain statistically unchanged under the stochastic decoding actually used in deployment. Its key move is to separate task-lossless compression, where zero-shot scores stay within natural sampling variance, from stricter distribution-lossless compression, where the next-token distribution is practically indistinguishable from FP16, and to define Expected Acceptance Rate (EAR) as an interpretable overlap/coupling metric for that regime. A distinguishing theoretical result is the gamma-squared variance law proving that symmetric quantization wastes precision on skewed weight ranges and inflates noise quadratically relative to asymmetric grids, making asymmetry essential for distribution-level fidelity. The SLQ pipeline then uses layer-wise non-uniform asymmetric integer bitwidths, multi-bitwidth Shapley sensitivity estimation, and ILP allocation over 2–8 bits. On Qwen3-8B/32B, Qwen3.5-27B, and Llama-3.3-70B, it reaches task-lossless compression below 4 bpp, distribution-lossless at roughly 5–6 bpp, and 1.7–3.6x FP16 throughput on vLLM/Humming, pointing toward fidelity-aware, kernel-friendly LLM compression.
papers.abstract: Model quantization has become essential for efficient large language model deployment, yet existing approaches involve clear trade-offs: methods such as GPTQ and AWQ achieve practical compression but are lossy, while lossless techniques preserve fidelity but typically do not accelerate inference. This paper explores the middle ground of statistically-lossless compression through three complementary notions of losslessness for quantized LLMs. First, task-lossless compression preserves zero-shot benchmark accuracy within natural sampling variance and remains achievable at aggressive bitwidths. Second, we formalize the stricter notion of distribution-lossless compression, requiring the quantized model's next-token distribution to be practically indistinguishable from the original, and propose the Expected Acceptance Rate (EAR), the maximum token-agreement probability under optimal coupling, as a directly interpretable fidelity metric (for example, EAR >= 0.99 indicates 99% agreement). Third, we prove a gamma-squared variance law showing that symmetric quantization inflates noise variance by gamma squared relative to asymmetric quantization, making asymmetry necessary for distribution-lossless fidelity but not for task-level preservation. Using SLQ, a layer-wise non-uniform method with asymmetric quantization and wide bitwidth search, we achieve task-lossless compression at well below 4 bits per parameter (as low as 3.3 bits depending on the model), distribution-lossless compression at 5 to 6 bits per parameter on average, and inference speedups of 1.7 to 3.6x relative to FP16 with optimized kernels. Source code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/SLQ.

papers.title: Closed-Loop CO2 Storage Control With History-Based Reinforcement Learning and Latent Model-Based Adaptation
papers.summary: Closed-Loop CO2 Storage Control With History-Based Reinforcement Learning and Latent Model-Based Adaptation tackles closed-loop geological CO2 storage as a partially observable control problem where operators must set 11 continuous actions—eight brine-producer rates and three CO2 injector rates—every two years over a 20-step horizon using only deployable well measurements, not full reservoir fields. Trained in high-fidelity OPM/Flow simulation, the paper compares SAC controllers across observability regimes: a privileged-state oracle with dense pressure/saturation tensors, a sparse well-only baseline, a history-conditioned transformer-style policy that encodes the full rolling well-response sequence, a masking-curriculum critic setup, and an asymmetric teacher-student variant with privileged critic supervision plus InfoNCE latent distillation. The unusual contribution is that temporal well-history almost eliminates the gap to privileged control, while staying operationally realistic. The second half introduces Dreamer-style latent model-based retuning with GRU, geometrically regularized GRU, RSSM, and Koopman-inspired world models, plus residual adapters for injector failure, leakage-driven dynamics/reward shift, and compartmentalized connectivity. Across abnormal cases, latent retuning outperforms direct model-free retuning under the same simulator budget, pointing toward budget-aware, deployable closed-loop CCS optimization instead of repeated online history matching and re-optimization.
papers.abstract: Closed-loop management of geological CO2 storage requires control policies that adapt to uncertain reservoir behavior while relying on observations that are realistically available during operation. This work formulates CO2 injection and brine-production control as a partially observable sequential decision problem and studies deployable deep reinforcement-learning controllers trained with high-fidelity reservoir simulation. We first compare privileged-state, well-only, history-conditioned, masking-curriculum, and asymmetric teacher-student model-free policies in order to quantify the value of temporal well-response information and training-time privileged simulator states. We then evaluate a latent model-based adaptation pipeline that reuses nominal latent dynamics and retunes controllers under known injector failure, leakage-induced dynamics and reward shift, and compartmentalized reservoir connectivity. The results show that history-conditioned policies recover nearly all of the privileged-state performance while using only deployable well-level information, and that latent model-based retuning outperforms direct model-free retuning under the same scenario-specific real-simulator budget in the abnormal operating cases. The proposed framework therefore provides a simulator-budget-aware alternative to repeated online history matching and re-optimization for closed-loop CO2 storage control.

papers.title: Inducing Permutation Invariant Priors in Bayesian Optimization for Carbon Capture and Storage Applications
papers.summary: Inducing Permutation Invariant Priors in Bayesian Optimization for Carbon Capture and Storage Applications tackles a CCS well-placement BO bottleneck: the simulator is invariant to how injector wells and producer wells are ordered inside each group, but standard Gaussian-process surrogates treat permuted layouts as different points, wasting evaluations and distorting posterior geometry. The paper’s core idea is GP-Perm, a Matérn GP whose distance is built from a stable Sinkhorn divergence between the empirical representations of the injector set, producer set, and an explicit injector-producer interaction set, then optionally summed with ordinary ARD kernels for auxiliary continuous controls such as group target rates. This differs from nearby set-kernel BO by encoding geometry-sensitive transport discrepancy rather than only pooled pairwise similarity, and from learned invariance approaches by avoiding representation learning inside the BO loop. A Deep Sets-based DKL-DS baseline is also tested. Across seven synthetic permutation-invariant benchmarks plus a realistic Johansen formation CCS case study with OPM-Flow and qLogEI acquisition, the method is evaluated under feasible-grid snapping and NPV/leakage/recycling penalties. Results show symmetry-aware surrogates can materially improve sample efficiency on several tasks, pointing toward physics-aligned, symmetry-preserving BO for expensive simulator design.
papers.abstract: Bayesian Optimization is an iterative method, tailored to optimizing expensive black box objective functions. Surrogate models like Gaussian Processes, which are the gold standard in Bayesian Optimization, can be inefficient for inputs with permutation symmetries, as the most common kernels employed are better suited for vector inputs rather than unordered sets of items. Motivated by this issue, we turn to permutation invariant Bayesian Optimization for well placement in Carbon Capture and Storage projects. The high fidelity black box simulator is instructed to operate wells under group control, giving rise to permutation symmetries within injector and producer groups that cannot be exploited with standard GP kernels. In this work, our main contribution is a novel Gaussian Process kernel (GP-Perm) that encodes permutation invariance by comparing sets through a stable divergence between their induced empirical representations, and can be combined with standard kernels for additional vector-valued inputs. As a learned invariant baseline, we also consider a Deep Kernel Learning model (DKL-DS) using the Deep Sets architecture to learn a permutation-invariant embedding. We evaluate the proposed methodology across 8 use cases, comprising seven synthetic benchmarks and one realistic CCS case study (Johansen formation)

papers.title: FitText: Evolving Agent Tool Ecologies via Memetic Retrieval
papers.summary: FitText: Evolving Agent Tool Ecologies via Memetic Retrieval attacks the core failure mode of large-tool agents: user requests are goal-shaped, while API catalogs are documentation-shaped, so one-shot query retrieval misses the tool whose meaning only becomes clear as execution unfolds. FitText is training-free and embeds retrieval inside the reasoning loop by having the LLM emit pseudo-tool descriptions as natural-language hypotheses of needed functionality, then refine them from retrieved exemplars, diversify them stochastically, and finally search them with Memetic Retrieval, an evolutionary loop with crossover, mutation, fitness-based selection, and a tool-memory penalty that suppresses redundant search. Unlike schema-injection or static dense retrieval, it treats the API catalog as a heterogeneous “tool ecology” and the agent’s evolving intent as the niche to fill. On ToolRet (43k tools across four domains) it cuts average retrieval rank from 8.81 to 2.78, and on StableToolBench (16,464 APIs) it reaches 0.73 average pass rate, a 24-point gain over static retrieval. The work points toward adaptive, test-time, model-agnostic tool discovery, while warning that memetic exploration only works when the base model can act as a competent semantic operator.
papers.abstract: A semantic gap separates how users describe tasks from how tools are documented. As API ecosystems scale to tens of thousands of endpoints, static retrieval from the initial query alone cannot bridge this gap: the agent's understanding of what it needs evolves during execution, but its tool set does not. We introduce FitText, a training-free framework that makes retrieval dynamic by embedding it directly in the agent's reasoning loop. FitText generates natural-language pseudo-tool descriptions as retrieval probes, refines them iteratively using retrieval feedback, and explores diverse alternatives through stochastic generation. Memetic Retrieval adds evolutionary selection pressure over candidate descriptions, guided by a tool memory that avoids redundant search. On ToolRet (43k tools, 4 domains), FitText improves average retrieval rank from 8.81 to 2.78; on StableToolBench (16,464 APIs), it achieves a 0.73 average pass rate--a 24-point absolute gain over static query retrieval. The gains transfer across base models capable of acting as competent semantic operators; under weaker base models, Memetic's evolutionary search inverts--amplifying noise rather than refining signal--surfacing model capacity as a prerequisite for evolutionary tool exploration.

papers.title: Spatial-Temporal Learning-Based Distributed Routing for Dynamic LEO Satellite Networks
papers.summary: Spatial-Temporal Learning-Based Distributed Routing for Dynamic LEO Satellite Networks tackles the core routing bottleneck in fast-moving LEO constellations: links and traffic change too quickly for shortest-path or centralized schemes, yet satellites only see partial local state. The paper casts next-hop selection as a POMDP and builds a fully distributed GAT-LSTM-DQN agent that fuses topology and traffic history directly from each satellite’s local observations. GAT assigns attention over neighboring satellites to encode graph-structured spatial dependence, while LSTM tracks temporal congestion patterns induced by periodic, nonstationary arrivals; DQN then learns the routing policy with reward shaped by delay and queue buildup, explicitly favoring proactive congestion avoidance over pure distance minimization. What differentiates it from nearby LEO routing work is the end-to-end joint spatiotemporal representation, rather than decoupled traffic prediction plus routing or purely spatial GNN policies. Simulations on a 45-satellite LEO network with 120/180/240 Mbps loads show stronger throughput, lower packet loss, shorter queues, and reduced end-to-end delay than Dijkstra, GraphPR, DQN-IR, and FDR-MARL, including up to 23.26% queue reduction. The paper points toward low-overhead, Green AI routing for scalable SAGIN control.
papers.abstract: In this paper, we propose a spatial-temporal learning-based distributed routing framework for dynamic Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks, where graph attention networks (GAT) and long short-term memory (LSTM) are integrated within a deep Q-network (DQN)-based architecture to enable distributed and adaptive routing decisions based on local observations. The routing problem is formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) to address partial observability under dynamic topology and time-varying traffic. Simulation results show that the proposed method significantly outperforms conventional and learning-based routing schemes in terms of throughput, packet loss, queue length, and end-to-end delay, while achieving proactive congestion avoidance with up to 23.26% queue reduction. In addition, the proposed approach maintains low computational overhead with negligible carbon emissions, demonstrating its efficiency from a Green AI perspective.

papers.title: Dueling DDQN-Based Adaptive Multi-Objective Handover Optimization for LEO Satellite Networks
papers.summary: Dueling DDQN-Based Adaptive Multi-Objective Handover Optimization for LEO Satellite Networks tackles the mobility-management bottleneck created by fast-moving LEO constellations, where each UE repeatedly chooses among overlapping satellites and handover decisions must trade off throughput, blocking probability, and switching cost under changing load and visibility. Instead of the fixed scalar reward weights used in prior RL and MORL handover schemes, the paper formulates association as an MDP with state features that include candidate-link rates, satellite visibility, load vectors, and previous serving satellite, then learns adaptive objective priorities through a dynamically weighted reward. The core mechanism is a dueling DDQN: DDQN reduces Q-value overestimation, while the dueling decomposition separates state value from action advantage so the agent can distinguish near-tied satellites in dense overlap regions. Simulations on a Telesat Lightspeed scenario with 298 satellites, varying UE counts, and limited satellite capacity compare against MVT, MAC, GBW, MSH, MSHBO, and DDQN. The method reaches up to 10.3% higher throughput, near-zero blocking, and fewer handovers, pointing toward Pareto-aware reinforcement learning for 6G non-terrestrial resource control.
papers.abstract: In this paper, we propose a dueling double deep Q-network (DDQN)-based adaptive multi-objective handover framework for LEO satellite networks. The proposed method enables dynamic trade-off learning among throughput, blocking probability, and switching cost under time-varying network conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms conventional baselines, achieving up to 10.3% throughput improvement and near-zero blocking under typical operating conditions.

papers.title: DirectEdit: Step-Level Accurate Inversion for Flow-Based Image Editing
papers.summary: DirectEdit: Step-Level Accurate Inversion for Flow-Based Image Editing tackles a specific failure mode in training-free rectified-flow editing: standard Euler-style inversion approximates the reverse trajectory with mismatched timesteps, so reconstruction drift accumulates and the source features injected into editing become “drifted,” degrading fidelity even when stepwise correction is used. DirectEdit’s key idea is not to repair the inversion path after the fact, but to align the forward reconstruction path directly to the recorded inversion trajectory at each step by caching latent residuals and reusing them during denoising, so the model predicts velocity on a noise-aligned latent and achieves step-level accurate reconstruction without extra NFEs. This is unusual relative to RF-Inversion, high-order solvers, FTEdit, and DNAEdit, which all optimize or approximate the inversion path itself. The method adds a preservation stack: attention value-feature injection from the reconstruction branch and a multi-branch mask-guided noise blending scheme built from an MLLM plus SAM to separate local, background, global, and addition edits. Evaluated on PIE-Bench with FLUX.1-dev and SD3.5-medium over 30 steps, it reports state-of-the-art tradeoffs in structure retention, background PSNR/SSIM, and CLIP alignment, pointing toward drift-free, feature-consistent, training-free editing for flow transformers.
papers.abstract: With recent advancements in large-scale pre-trained text-to-image (T2I) models, training-free image editing methods have demonstrated remarkable success. Typically, these methods involve adding noise to a clean image via an inversion process, followed by separate denoising steps for the reconstruction and editing paths during the forward process. However, since the reconstruction path is approximated using noisy latents from mismatched timesteps, existing methods inevitably suffer from accumulated drift, which fundamentally limits reconstruction fidelity. To address this challenge, we systematically analyze the inversion process within the flow transformer and propose DirectEdit, a simple yet effective editing method that eliminates the inherent reconstruction error without introducing additional neural function evaluations (NFEs). Unlike most prior works that attempt to rectify the inversion path, DirectEdit focuses on directly aligning the forward paths, enabling precise reconstruction and reliable feature sharing. Furthermore, we introduce a preservation mechanism based on attention feature injection and multi-branch mask-guided noise blending, which effectively balances fidelity and editability. Extensive experiments across diverse scenarios demonstrate that DirectEdit achieves efficient and accurate image editing, delivering superior performance that outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code and examples are available at https://desongyang.github.io/Directedit.

papers.title: The Model Knows, the Decoder Finds: Future Value Guided Particle Power Sampling
papers.summary: The Model Knows, the Decoder Finds: Future Value Guided Particle Power Sampling tackles the inference-time bottleneck in training-free reasoning: base LLMs often place non-trivial probability mass on correct multi-step solutions, but standard low-temperature, best-of-N, and earlier power-sampling schemes either stay myopic or spend too much compute on suffix regeneration. The paper reframes sequence-level power sampling p(x)^α as a Sequential Monte Carlo problem with an exact future-value factor z_{j+1} at block boundaries, then introduces Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS), which propagates a bounded population of partial solutions in blocks, uses proposal-corrected power weights, and injects future-value guidance only at resampling through auxiliary selection potentials. That signal is estimated either by short-horizon rollouts or by a lightweight MLP head distilled from rollout-labeled boundary competition, making the differentiator selection-time future value rather than a new proposal model. Across Qwen2.5-Math-7B, Qwen2.5-7B, and DeepSeek-Math-7B on MATH500, HumanEval, and GPQA-diamond, APPS improves the accuracy-runtime frontier; rollout APF often yields the best accuracy, while learned APF recovers much of it at lower online cost. The paper points toward finite-budget, particle-based inference as a path to post-training-like reasoning gains without changing model weights.
papers.abstract: A recurring pattern in "reasoning without training" is that base LLMs already assign non-trivial probability mass to correct multi-step solutions; the bottleneck is locating these modes efficiently at inference time. Power sampling provides a principled way to bias decoding toward such modes by targeting p_theta(x)^alpha with alpha > 1, but practical approximations must account for future-dependent correction factors that determine which prefixes remain promising. We introduce Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS), a blockwise particle algorithm for approximating the sequence-level power target with a bounded population of partial solutions. APPS propagates hypotheses in parallel using proposal-corrected power reweighting and refines their survival through future-value-guided selection at resampling boundaries. This redistributes finite compute across competing prefixes rather than committing to a single unfolding path, while providing a direct scaling knob in the particle count and predictable peak memory. We instantiate the future-value signal with short-horizon rollouts and also study an amortized variant that replaces rollouts with a lightweight learned selection head. Across reasoning benchmarks, APPS improves the accuracy-runtime trade-off of training-free decoding and suggests that part of the gap to post-trained systems can be recovered through more faithful inference-time power approximation.

papers.title: Generalized Distributional Alignment Games for Unbiased Answer-Level Fine-Tuning
papers.summary: Generalized Distributional Alignment Games for Unbiased Answer-Level Fine-Tuning tackles a structural failure in answer-level fine-tuning for LLM reasoning: when GRPO-style updates estimate target-answer rewards from a small group of K samples, the canonical KL reward βlog q(z) is biased by Jensen’s inequality, creating a rare-answer “anti-exploration” penalty and training instability. The paper’s key move is to generalize the Distributional Alignment Game from KL to arbitrary Bregman geometries, then identify the subset whose dual rewards are polynomial in q(z); for these, falling-factorial U-statistics give exactly unbiased, minimum-variance estimators from the same K-sample batch. For the canonical KL game, where exact unbiasedness is impossible, the authors derive a globally minimax polynomial estimator for p log p, solve it offline by linear programming over Bernstein coefficients, and prove the fundamental best possible gradient-bias rate is Θ(1/K^2) via the Ditzian-Totik theorem. They then combine the polynomial and minimax views into a Variance-Optimal AQP estimator that preserves zero online overhead while accelerating game convergence. The paper points toward principled, geometry-aware reward estimation for stable LLM alignment under tight batch budgets.
papers.abstract: The Distributional Alignment Game framework provides a powerful variational perspective on Answer-Level Fine-Tuning (ALFT). However, standard algorithms for these games rely on estimating logarithmic rewards from small batches, introducing a systematic bias due to Jensen's inequality that can destabilize training. In this paper, we systematically resolve this structural estimation bias. First, we generalize the alignment game to arbitrary Bregman divergences, showing that for a family of geometries inducing polynomial rewards, we can construct provably exact and unbiased estimators using U-statistics. Second, for the canonical KL divergence game where an exact solution is impossible, we derive a globally robust minimax polynomial estimator that is provably optimal, achieving the fundamental statistical error limit of $Θ(1/K^2)$, which we establish via the Ditzian-Totik theorem. Finally, we synthesize these two approaches to propose a novel Variance-Optimal Augmented Polynomial Optimization Program (AQP) Estimator, proving that by systematically reducing variance, our method achieves not only optimal bias but also provably accelerated game convergence, leading to more efficient and stable training with zero online computational overhead.

papers.title: Multi-Rater Calibrated Segmentation Models
papers.summary: Multi-Rater Calibrated Segmentation Models attacks the clinical bottleneck that segmentation networks can be accurate yet dangerously overconfident when expert masks disagree, especially around ambiguous anatomical or pathological boundaries. The paper reframes voxel-wise multi-rater supervision as an ordinal problem: instead of collapsing annotations into a single consensus mask, it sums K expert votes into an ordered consensus level from 0 to K, then trains the network to predict a K+1-way distribution over agreement intensity. The key mechanism is a hybrid loss combining Ranked Probability Score ordinal supervision with standard binary cross-entropy, so predictions close to the empirical rater consensus are penalized less than distant ones while preserving segmentation discrimination. At inference, foreground confidence is recovered by aggregating majority-consensus probability mass, making the method architecture-agnostic and plug-and-play. This differs from soft-label averaging, STAPLE-style label fusion, and spatial smoothing because disagreement is treated as structured aleatoric signal rather than noise. Evaluated on CoCaHis, REFUGE, and LongCIU across ophthalmology, histopathology, and thoracic CT, the approach consistently lowers multi-rater expected calibration error without sacrificing AUC, pointing toward safer, uncertainty-aware medical image segmentation that explicitly learns from annotation variability.
papers.abstract: Objective: Accurate probability estimates are essential for the safe deployment of medical image segmentation models in clinical decision-making. However, modern deep segmentation networks are often poorly calibrated, a problem exacerbated when multiple expert annotations exhibit substantial disagreement. While inter-rater variability is typically treated as noise, it provides valuable information about intrinsic annotation ambiguity that must be reflected in model confidence. Methods: We improve the probabilistic calibration of medical image segmentation models by reformulating multi-rater supervision as an ordinal learning problem. Voxel-wise annotator agreement is treated as an ordered target, linking predictive confidence to the empirical variability in training data. This formulation allows the use of ordinal-aware scoring rules, such as the Ranked Probability Score ordinal loss, combined with a standard binary objective to preserve discriminative performance. Results: We evaluated the proposed approach across four public segmentation benchmarks spanning ophthalmology, histopathology, and thoracic imaging. Calibration was assessed using a multi-rater extension of expected calibration error. Results consistently show that ordinal-aware training yields substantially improved calibration with respect to inter-rater agreement without degrading segmentation accuracy. Conclusions: Treating multi-rater annotations as ordered information provides a principled and architecture-agnostic route to more reliable probabilistic segmentation models.

papers.title: Mixture Prototype Flow Matching for Open-Set Supervised Anomaly Detection
papers.summary: Mixture Prototype Flow Matching for Open-Set Supervised Anomaly Detection attacks the OSAD bottleneck where prototype-based detectors assume a single Gaussian normal prior, collapsing rare but legitimate normal modes into false positives and weakening boundaries against unseen defects. MPFM replaces the unimodal prototype with a structured Gaussian mixture space and learns a continuous transport from normal feature distributions into that space via flow matching; crucially, the velocity field itself is parameterized as a Gaussian mixture, so each component can align with a distinct normal mode rather than a single averaged direction. The method initializes mixture centers with K-means++ over normal ResNet-18 features, then jointly trains normal-flow and anomaly-flow objectives so normal samples are pulled into the mixture while anomalous samples are pushed away. A Mutual Information Maximization Regularizer further prevents prototype collapse by encouraging confident yet balanced component usage and is applied only to normal data. Evaluated on nine industrial, texture, scientific, and medical benchmarks (MVTec AD, AITEX, Hyper-Kvasir, BrainMRI, HeadCT, etc.) under one-shot/ten-shot and general/hard protocols, MPFM reports state-of-the-art AUC and closed-form reverse sampling, pointing toward mode-aware generative transport as a stronger alternative to single-center anomaly modeling.
papers.abstract: Open-set supervised anomaly detection (OSAD) aims to identify unseen anomalies using limited anomalous supervision. However, existing prototype-based methods typically model normal data via a unimodal Gaussian prior, failing to capture inherent multi-modality and resulting in blurred decision boundaries. To address this, we propose Mixture Prototype Flow Matching (MPFM), a framework that learns a continuous transformation from normal feature distributions to a structured Gaussian mixture prototype space. Departing from traditional flow-based approaches that rely on a single velocity vector, MPFM explicitly models the velocity field as a Gaussian mixture prior where each component corresponds to a distinct normal class. This design facilitates mode-aware and semantically coherent distribution transport. Furthermore, we introduce a Mutual Information Maximization Regularizer (MIMR) to prevent prototype collapse and maximize normal-anomaly separability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MPFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks under both single- and multi-anomaly settings.

papers.title: Anomaly-Preference Image Generation
papers.summary: Anomaly-Preference Image Generation attacks few-shot industrial defect synthesis, where sparse anomaly exemplars make diffusion fine-tuning prone to either overfitting to a handful of defect shapes or drifting into unrealistic samples that break downstream inspection. The paper reframes generation as preference optimization: real anomalies act as positive references, and the model learns from an implicit denoising-margin signal computed from the deviation between the policy denoiser and a frozen Stable Diffusion v1-4 reference, avoiding human preference labels or explicit reward modeling. A second mechanism, Time-Aware Capacity Allocation, changes LoRA rank across the diffusion timeline so high-noise steps preserve structural diversity while low-noise steps absorb fine-grained defect fidelity; inference adds hierarchical sampling to separately tune semantic coherence and anomaly injection strength. Evaluated on MVTec AD with 1/3 of anomalies as references and 1,000 image-mask pairs per category, the method reports state-of-the-art realism/diversity trade-offs (IS 2.14, IC-LPIPS 0.41) and improves synthetic-data utility for U-Net anomaly detection and ResNet-18 classification. The paper points toward preference-aligned generative modeling for data-scarce visual anomalies.
papers.abstract: Synthesizing realistic and diverse anomalous samples from limited data is vital for robust model generalization. However, existing methods struggle to reconcile fidelity and diversity, often hampered by distribution misalignment and overfitting, respectively.To mitigate this, we introduce Anomaly Preference Optimization,a novel paradigm that reformulates anomaly generation as a preference learning problem.Central to our approach is an implicit preference alignment mechanism that leverages real anomalies as positive references, deriving optimization signals directly from denoising trajectory deviations without requiring costly human annotation. Furthermore, we propose a Time-Aware Capacity Allocation module that dynamically distributes model capacity along the diffusion timeline,prioritizing structural diversity during highnoise phases while enhancing fine-grained fidelity in low-noise stages. During inference, a hierarchical sampling strategy modulates the coherencealignment trade-off, enabling precise control over generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that significantly outperforms existing baselines,achieving state-of-the-art performance in both realism and diversity.

papers.title: Measuring AI Reasoning: A Guide for Researchers
papers.summary: Measuring AI Reasoning: A Guide for Researchers argues that frontier-language-model “reasoning” benchmarks are mismeasuring the target when they collapse success into pass@1 or exact-match accuracy on MMLU, GSM8K, MATH, GPQA, AIME, ARC-AGI, and similar suites. The paper formalizes reasoning as adaptive multi-step search: a model must choose intermediate steps and decide when to halt based on input-dependent conditions, which single forward passes in transformers and Mamba-like state-space models are structurally ill-suited to realize. It contrasts reasoning with memorization and comprehension, showing how contamination can make benchmark success look like reasoning when it is actually retrieval or pattern completion. The proposed remedy is process-based evaluation using externalized reasoning traces—chain-of-thought, scratchpads, proof steps, or tool logs—evaluated for faithfulness and validity rather than plausibility. The paper sketches an evidence ladder from outcome-only reporting to trace-verified evaluation, including step-validity rate, verified solution rate, intervention faithfulness, and anytime accuracy curves for adaptive halting. Its broader implication is a shift toward benchmarks with verifiers, structured intermediate artifacts, and reproducible process diagnostics.
papers.abstract: In this paper, we offer a guide for researchers on evaluating reasoning in language models, building the case that reasoning should be assessed through evidence of adaptive, multi-step search rather than final-answer accuracy alone. Under an evaluation-oriented definition, reasoning requires selecting intermediate steps and halting according to input-dependent conditions, which we formalize as a search-like procedure. We show that single forward passes in scalable architectures are structurally limited in their ability to realize such variable-depth computation, motivating intermediate decoding and externalized reasoning traces as appropriate evaluation interfaces. Central to our argument is that final-answer accuracy alone is an insufficient measure of reasoning, because it provides little ability to diagnose or debug the underlying processes that produce individual solutions in frontier models. We therefore argue for a shift toward process-based evaluation, in which reasoning is assessed through the faithfulness and validity of intermediate reasoning traces as first-class evaluation targets.

papers.title: HalluScan: A Systematic Benchmark for Detecting and Mitigating Hallucinations in Instruction-Following LLMs
papers.summary: HalluScan: A Systematic Benchmark for Detecting and Mitigating Hallucinations in Instruction-Following LLMs targets the evaluation gap where hallucination studies compare isolated detectors on one model or domain, making it hard to choose methods for factuality, faithfulness, or instruction adherence in deployment. HalluScan instantiates 72 matched configurations across 6 detection methods, 4 open-weight model families (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E, Qwen3-32B-Instruct, GPT-OSS-20B), and 3 high-stakes domains: TruthfulQA science, Natural Questions open-domain QA, and ARC-Challenge commonsense. Its distinctive additions are HalluScore, a weighted geometric mean of factual error, semantic coherence, and fabrication rate that correlates with expert judgments at r=0.41, and Adaptive Detection Routing (ADR), which uses query features plus a learned risk classifier to send low-risk inputs to Self-Consistency or Semantic Entropy and reserve expensive RAV for high-risk cases, cutting cost 2.0x with only 0.1% AUROC loss. The benchmark also decomposes error cascades, showing domain-specific mixtures of knowledge gaps, reasoning failures, and instruction misalignment. NLI Verification is the strongest detector overall (AUROC 0.88), with RAV second (0.66), pointing toward cost-aware, entailment-centered hallucination monitoring.
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse natural language processing tasks, yet they remain susceptible to hallucinations -- generating content that is factually incorrect, unfaithful to provided context, or misaligned with user instructions. We present HalluScan, a comprehensive benchmark framework that systematically evaluates hallucination detection and mitigation across 72 configurations spanning 6 detection methods, 4 open-weight model families, and 3 diverse domains. We introduce three key contributions: (1) HalluScore, a novel composite metric that achieves a Pearson correlation of r = 0.41 with human expert judgments; (2) Adaptive Detection Routing (ADR), an intelligent routing algorithm achieving 2.0x cost reduction with only 0.1% AUROC degradation; and (3) systematic error cascade decomposition revealing substantial variation in hallucination error types across domains. Our experiments reveal that NLI Verification achieves the highest overall AUROC of 0.88, while RAV achieves the second-highest AUROC of 0.66.

papers.title: M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse: Lightweight State-Space MoE with a Cross-Scale Gating Bridge for Brain Tumor Segmentation
papers.summary: M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse: Lightweight State-Space MoE with a Cross-Scale Gating Bridge for Brain Tumor Segmentation tackles the practical failure mode of 3D BraTS-style segmentation models that become compute-heavy and brittle when encoder-decoder capacity is imbalanced and large 128×128×128 volumes are assumed. The core design reallocates capacity instead of widening everything: a grouped state-space mixer (POM) carries long-range volumetric context in linear time, a cross-scale dual-stage gating bridge (CSB) first spatially denoises skip features and then re-aligns channels so the decoder receives cleaner multi-scale evidence, and a sample-level mixture-of-experts (PEU) is restricted to high-semantic layers to absorb cross-site and cross-protocol variation with controllable overhead. Unlike nearby transformer or pure CNN lightweight models, it treats skip purification as a prerequisite for useful global mixing and makes expert routing dataset-aware while keeping the backbone compact. On BraTS2019 and BraTS2021, using four MRI modalities and WT/TC/ET labels, the 1.11M-parameter base model matches or beats stronger lightweight baselines, gains 0.09% average performance at 64×128×128, and sets the best BraTS2019 HD95, pointing toward Pareto-efficient, domain-robust 3D medical segmentation.
papers.abstract: Encoder-decoder imbalance and the reliance on large input volumes make many 3D brain tumor segmentation models both compute-heavy and brittle. We present M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse, a lightweight network that prioritizes discriminative brain tumor cues over exhaustive appearance reconstruction. Our method balances encoder and decoder capacity and replaces depth expansion with a synergistic design: it propagates long-range context with linear complexity via a grouped state space mixer, denoises and aligns skip features using a cross-scale dual-stage gating bridge, and absorbs cross-site acquisition shifts with a sample-level mixture-of-experts. On the BraTS2019 and BraTS2021 benchmarks, M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse outperforms other lightweight excellent methods in both parameter count and performance. Even at a challenging input resolution of \(64\times128\times128\) (half that of existing excellent models), M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse reduces parameters by 62.63\% and improves average performance by 0.09\%. Ablations of key components validate the method's exceptional parameter-to-accuracy efficiency and robustness across diverse data centers.

papers.title: PC-MNet: Dual-Level Congruity Modeling for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection via Polarity-Modulated Attention
papers.summary: PC-MNet: Dual-Level Congruity Modeling for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection via Polarity-Modulated Attention tackles the failure of similarity-seeking fusion in multimodal sarcasm detection, where the decisive signal is not agreement but contradiction between literal text and nonverbal cues. The model first performs region-guided semantic alignment with YOLOv8 and CLIP to strip background noise, then maps text, acoustic, and visual streams into a shared polarity space where polarity-modulated attention amplifies cross-modal opposition instead of cosine-like similarity. It explicitly decomposes sarcasm into micro-level atomic incongruity and macro-level contextual incongruity: the former is captured with parallel bipartite heterogeneous graphs over the target utterance, while the latter is modeled by a prior-guided contextual RGAT over conversational history. A scalar congruity routing mechanism prevents entangled late fusion by converting high-dimensional conflict features into structural priors, and training uses a two-stage regime with valence-supervised warm-up followed by inconsistency-aware contrastive refinement. On MUStARD, MUStARD++, and MUStARD++ Balanced, using BERT-large, Wav2Vec 2.0, YOLOv8, and CLIP ViT-B/32, it reaches 81.64 Macro-F1 and 82.46% accuracy, outperforming the strongest multimodal baseline by 3.14 F1.
papers.abstract: Multimodal sarcasm detection, which aims to precisely identify pragmatic incongruities between literal text and nonverbal cues, has gained substantial attention in multimodal understanding. Recent advancements have predominantly relied on naïve similarity-based attention mechanisms and uniform late fusion strategies.Furthermore, given that functional entanglement restricts traditional late fusions, we incorporate a scalar congruity routing mechanism and a prior-guided contextual graph. This mechanism anchors a generalized incongruity manifold through a two-stage asymmetric optimization driven by inconsistency-aware contrastive learning, selectively fusing only the most discriminative multi-granularity evidence. Extensive experiments on the \texttt{MUStARD} benchmark and its spurious-correlation-mitigated balanced datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves new state-of-the-art performance, surpassing the strongest multimodal baseline by a substantial 3.14\% improvement in Macro-F1. By architecturally isolating atomic, composition, and contextual conflicts. This work provides a robust, decoupled paradigm for modeling subtle pragmatic incongruities in human communication.

papers.title: Position: How can Graphs Help Large Language Models?
papers.summary: Position: How can Graphs Help Large Language Models? frames the missing inverse side of graph-LLM research: instead of asking how LLMs improve graphs, it isolates the functional ways graphs can repair LLM weaknesses. The paper argues that graphs matter in three concrete roles: as an up-to-date factual substrate that grounds answers and reduces hallucinations through knowledge-graph retrieval, evaluation, and retrofitting loops such as GraphRAG-style pipelines; as a reasoning scaffold for Chain-of-Thought, Tree-of-Thought, Graph-of-Thought, and MCTS-based search, where intermediate thoughts become nodes in an explicit computational graph; and as structural input that expands LLM competence on graph-shaped domains like e-commerce, code repositories, tool graphs, tabular schemas, and relational databases. Its differentiating contribution is a functional taxonomy of Graph→LLM integration, rather than an architecture-centric survey, and a candid comparison of tradeoffs among graph-as-text, graph-as-embedding, and merged GNN-Transformer designs in terms of training cost, graph size, and inference latency. The paper points toward graph-native sparse LLM architectures and brain-inspired memory systems as the next research frontier.
papers.abstract: With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), classic graph learning tasks have greatly benefited from LLMs, including improved encoding of textual features, more efficient construction of graphs from text, and enhanced reasoning over knowledge graphs. In this paper, we ask a complementary question: How can graphs help LLMs? We address this question from three perspectives: 1) graphs provide an up-to-date knowledge source that helps reduce LLM hallucinations, 2) graph-based prompting techniques-such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Tree-of-Thought (ToT), and Graph-of-Thought (GoT)-enhance LLM reasoning capabilities, and 3) integrating graphs into LLMs improves their understanding of structured data, expanding their applicability to domains such as e-commerce, code, and relational databases (RDBs). We further outlook some future directions including designing sparse LLM architectures based on graphs and brain-inspired memory systems.

papers.title: Causal Software Engineering: A Vision and Roadmap
papers.summary: Causal Software Engineering: A Vision and Roadmap argues that modern software teams increasingly need decision support for interventions and counterfactuals, not just correlation-driven prediction, because questions such as whether a rollout would still have caused an outage or whether a timeout change would really improve p99 latency cannot be answered by co-occurrence models alone. The paper’s central idea is Causal Software Engineering (CSE): treat code changes, configuration updates, releases, mitigations, and even testing choices as explicit causal interventions, represented in lightweight artifacts such as causal design specs, intervention logs, and a living causal model. Unlike nearby AIOps or root-cause-analysis work, CSE makes assumptions reviewable, records confounders like autoscaling and traffic mix, and demands uncertainty-aware effect estimates plus refutation checks. The roadmap organizes progress into causal observability, intervenability-by-design, counterfactual assurance, and governance/alignment, with Causal Readiness Levels for tooling and organizations. It also proposes benchmark families for intervention-effect estimation, counterfactual incident analysis, and causal testing, evaluated offline and in canary/online settings. The paper points toward trustworthy, causally constrained SE copilots and auditable, intervention-centric engineering.
papers.abstract: Software engineering increasingly involves making high-stakes decisions under uncertainty, using signals from code, field data, and socio-technical processes. Recent AI-driven support (e.g., anomaly detection, predictive analytics, AIOps, as well as LLM-based agents) has amplified engineers' ability to detect patterns and synthesize content and recommendations, but many critical questions are interventional or counterfactual: What is the expected impact of changing a load-balancing strategy? Would an outage have been avoided under a different release plan? Correlational models answer "what tends to co-occur"; they struggle to answer "what would happen if we act." We propose Causal Software Engineering (CSE) as a future paradigm in which causal models and causal reasoning systematically inform activities across the software lifecycle, augmenting existing practices with explicit assumptions, uncertainty-aware effect estimates, and counterfactual diagnosis. We outline (i) a causal-first workflow view spanning development and operations, (ii) a staged roadmap for tools and organizational adoption, and (iii) an evaluation and benchmark agenda for measuring progress.

papers.title: LLM-Assisted Repository-Level Generation with Structured Spec-Driven Engineering
papers.summary: LLM-Assisted Repository-Level Generation with Structured Spec-Driven Engineering attacks the gap between strong function-level code completion and brittle repository-level synthesis, arguing that natural-language prompts are too ambiguous and hard to verify for system-scale software. The core idea is structured spec-driven engineering (SSDE): feed LLMs compact, structured artifacts—Gherkin scenarios, domain models in Umple/Ecore, and derived model-layer signatures—so they can generate Python MVC controller/business logic with clearer intent and a verifiable contract. Unlike nearby work that uses LLMs to produce specs, this paper uses specs as the generation substrate, then checks outputs against human-verified unit tests. In a pilot study on three GitHub MVC systems (Symboleo, CheECSEManager, MeetingGroups), five models (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Qwen3-Coder, GPT-5.1, GPT-5 Nano, Llama 3.2 3B) were run 10 times each under 0-shot prompting. Any structured input improved test pass rates over natural language alone; Gherkin plus models often performed best, and signature models reduced variance. Over 70% of failures were static-analysis-detectable, pointing toward feedback-loop, spec-centric, repository-level coding agents.
papers.abstract: State-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in code generation at the function level. However, the output quality significantly declines when scaling to repository-level systems. Current workflows relying only on natural language prompts suffer from inherent ambiguity and a lack of verifiability. To address this, we propose structured spec-driven engineering (SSDE), a paradigm that leverages structured artifacts to guide LLM generation. We argue that structured specifications as LLM inputs make high-quality, repository-level code generation a tangible goal, while at the same time offering superior verifiability, leading to significant potential for improvement. We first investigate the feasibility of this vision through a pilot study generating Model-View-Controller (MVC) business logic for three software systems using five LLMs, and then highlight the potential, challenges, and future roadmap for SSDE.

papers.title: Leveraging Argument Structure to Predict Content Hatefulness
papers.summary: Leveraging Argument Structure to Predict Content Hatefulness tackles the problem that hateful and misleading online content often uses argumentative form to appear credible, yet most hate-speech detectors ignore how premises and conclusions interact. The paper studies white supremacy forum posts in WSF-ARG+, where each message is segmented into premises and a conclusion and annotated for checkworthiness (NFS/UFS/CFS) and, for hateful posts, component-level hatefulness. The core idea is to predict whole-message hatefulness from explicit argument encodings: ordered argument structure, premise-only structure, conclusion-conditioned-on-premises, and variants augmented with checkworthiness or hateful-component indicators. Unlike nearby work that classifies raw text or uses large language models, this is a compact, structure-first analysis with logistic regression, SVM, random forest, and XGBoost under 5-fold stratified cross-validation. Results show that premise structure already captures most signal, reaching about 0.701 macro F1, while adding checkworthiness alone can hurt smaller models. In contrast, injecting component-level hatefulness yields up to 0.957 F1. The paper points toward argument-aware hate detection and, more broadly, unified modeling of hate speech and information disorder.
papers.abstract: Information disorder is a challenging phenomenon that affects society at large. This phenomenon entails the diffusion of misleading, misinforming, and hateful content online. In different contexts, one aspect of the problem may prevail, but overall, this is a broad problem that requires comprehensive solutions. While each dimension of the problem (hate speech, disinformation, misinformation, etc.) requires in-depth analysis, in this paper, we look into the possibility of argument structure to provide relevant information to link these different areas of the problem. In particular, we focus on the WSF-ARG+ dataset, which consists of white supremacy forum messages annotated in terms of argument structure (premises and conclusion). There, we leverage the checkworthiness and hatefulness annotations of the argument components to obtain insights into the hatefulness of the whole message. Our results show promising insights (up to 96% F1), indicating the possibility of extending this direction in the future to tackle hateful content identification and information disorder countering.

papers.title: Active multiple matrix completion with adaptive confidence sets
papers.summary: Active multiple matrix completion with adaptive confidence sets studies sequentially allocating a fixed observation budget across K simultaneous low-rank matrix completion tasks, motivated by market segmentation and polling where each region block has different unknown size and rank. Unlike standard matrix completion, the learner actively chooses which matrix to sample at each round and receives a uniformly random entry from that matrix, with performance measured by an Lp aggregate of final Frobenius errors, including the max-loss case p = ∞. The core mechanism is MALocate: each matrix is estimated with the trace-regression square-root lasso, while its uncertainty is tracked by an honest adaptive confidence radius built from entries observed twice, so the procedure adapts to unknown rank without tuning to it or to the noise variance. MALocate runs on a doubling schedule and picks the next matrix by maximizing d_k^2 B_k(t) T_k(t)^(-1/p), effectively equalizing confidence-set diameters across harder and easier subproblems. The paper proves high-probability rates scaling as (Σ_k (r_k d_k^3 log d_k)^{p/(p+1)})^{(p+1)/p}/n and a matching minimax lower bound for p = ∞, with synthetic experiments showing strong gains over uniform sampling and near-oracle behavior for the hardest-task objective.
papers.abstract: In this work, we formulate a new multi-task active learning setting in which the learner's goal is to solve multiple matrix completion problems simultaneously. At each round, the learner can choose from which matrix it receives a sample from an entry drawn uniformly at random. Our main practical motivation is market segmentation, where the matrices represent different regions with different preferences of the customers. The challenge in this setting is that each of the matrices can be of a different size and also of a different rank which is unknown. We provide and analyze a new algorithm, MAlocate that is able to adapt to the unknown ranks of the different matrices. We then give a lower-bound showing that our strategy is minimax-optimal and demonstrate its performance with synthetic experiments.

papers.title: Middle-mile logistics through the lens of goal-conditioned reinforcement learning
papers.summary: Middle-mile logistics through the lens of goal-conditioned reinforcement learning reframes parcel consolidation across a time-expanded hub-and-truck network as a multi-object goal-conditioned MDP, where each action assigns one parcel to a capacity-limited truck and the core difficulty is simultaneous routing under shared capacity conflicts. Rather than solve the resulting combinatorial optimization with mixed-integer heuristics, the paper builds a model-free PPO agent on graph neural networks, but crucially avoids feeding the full state by extracting small parcel-centric feature graphs around the active parcel’s start and goal, then augmenting them with resistance-distance and relative-time features plus action masking over valid trucks. This is unusual among logistics RL papers because it learns on dynamic graph states while explicitly accounting for parcels that can block one another, and the authors supply an open-source Gym-like environment for the task. On synthetic 10-hub, 50-time-step, 200-parcel instances sampled from new networks per episode, the GNN policy outperforms a linear parameterization and generalizes across parcel counts, but supervised imitation and a greedy resistance-distance heuristic still beat RL overall. The work points toward goal-conditioned RL for large-scale operations research, especially richer cost functions, stochastic arrivals, and model-based search.
papers.abstract: Middle-mile logistics describes the problem of routing parcels through a network of hubs linked by trucks with finite capacity. We rephrase this as a multi-object goal-conditioned MDP. Our method combines graph neural networks with model-free RL, extracting small feature graphs from the environment state.

papers.title: Black-box optimization of noisy functions with unknown smoothness
papers.summary: Black-box optimization of noisy functions with unknown smoothness studies costly bandit-style maximization of an arbitrary-dimensional function from noisy evaluations when the local regularity around a global optimum is unknown, so the central bottleneck is simple regret under budgeted sampling rather than gradient-free global search in the abstract. The paper’s key move is a partition-based notion of local smoothness and near-optimality dimension, tied directly to a hierarchical tree over the domain instead of a metric or Lipschitz constant, and an anytime algorithm, POO (parallel optimistic optimization), that runs many HOO instances in parallel over a grid of smoothness parameters (ν, ρ), reuses only previously requested samples, and selects the best empirical subrun. What differentiates it from nearby HOO/UCT, StoSOO, or adaptive-treed bandit work is that it removes the need to know smoothness while still covering harder functions with nonzero dimension and even zero-quality trees. Finite-time analysis shows simple regret is within a √ln n factor of smoothness-aware optimizers, and experiments on an oscillatory 1D hard instance confirm POO nearly matches the best-tuned HOO while avoiding catastrophic misspecification.
papers.abstract: We study the problem of black-box optimization of a function f of any dimension, given function evaluations perturbed by noise. The function is assumed to be locally smooth around one of its global optima, but this smoothness is unknown. Our contribution is an adaptive optimization algorithm, POO or parallel optimistic optimization, that is able to deal with this setting. POO performs almost as well as the best known algorithms requiring the knowledge of the smoothness. Furthermore, POO works for a larger class of functions than what was previously considered, especially for functions that are difficult to optimize, in a very precise sense. We provide a finite-time analysis of POO's performance, which shows that its error after n evaluations is at most a factor of sqrt(ln n) away from the error of the best known optimization algorithms using the knowledge of the smoothness.

papers.title: When Stress Becomes Signal: Detecting Antifragility-Compatible Regimes in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
papers.summary: When Stress Becomes Signal: Detecting Antifragility-Compatible Regimes in Multi-Agent LLM Systems studies a gap in current multi-agent LLM evaluation: robustness tests ask whether performance survives perturbation, but not whether semantic stress reveals learnable structure. The paper introduces CAFE, a statistical measurement layer that treats stress as a four-dimensional variable—conflict, load, ambiguity, and temporal drift—then reconstructs an architecture-specific observed effective stress distribution from four judge signals (coherence, grounded novel inference, contradiction resolution, structural preservation) via a multi-output polynomial response surface and inverse optimization. CAFE compares the designed and reconstructed stress laws with a distributional Jensen Gap under a convex potential, so a positive gap means convex-expansive deformation rather than immediate improvement. On a controlled banking-risk analysis benchmark with 50 clean prompts, 500 stressed prompts, and five architectures (flat, hierarchical, debate, meta-adaptive, ensemble), every system lost about one-third judged quality under stress, yet every one produced a positive gap with bootstrap confidence intervals above zero. The distinctive contribution is separating “stress exposes structure” from “stress improves output,” pointing toward future adaptive routing, memory updates, and architecture selection that learn from adversity rather than merely endure it.
papers.abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems are increasingly used to solve complex tasks through decomposition, debate, specialization, and ensemble reasoning. However, these systems are usually evaluated in terms of robustness: whether performance is preserved under perturbation. This paper studies a different question: whether semantic stress exposes structured variation that could support future antifragile learning. We introduce CAFE, a statistical framework for detecting antifragility-compatible regimes in multi-agent architectures. CAFE models a controlled expected distribution of semantic stressors, reconstructs an architecture-specific observed effective stress distribution from multi-dimensional judge signals, and compares both distributions using a distributional Jensen Gap under a convex stress potential. A positive gap does not imply immediate performance improvement; instead, it indicates a convex-expansive deformation of the observed stress distribution, suggesting that the architecture exposes learnable stress structure. We evaluate CAFE on a banking-risk analysis benchmark with five multi-agent architectures: flat, hierarchical, debate, meta-adaptive, and ensemble. Across all architectures, semantic stress reduces average judged quality by roughly one third. Yet all architectures exhibit positive distributional Jensen Gaps with bootstrap confidence intervals above zero. These results show that immediate quality degradation can coexist with statistically detectable antifragility-compatible stress geometry. CAFE is therefore not an antifragile learner itself, but a measurement layer for identifying when and where antifragility learning may be worth applying.

papers.title: ExpoCM: Exposure-Aware One-Step Generative Single-Image HDR Reconstruction
papers.summary: ExpoCM: Exposure-Aware One-Step Generative Single-Image HDR Reconstruction tackles the central bottleneck in single-image HDR recovery: a single LDR input mixes saturated highlight regions, noisy shadow regions, and trustworthy midtones, so uniform regression or standard diffusion cannot recover missing radiance without either brightness bias or heavy iterative sampling. The key idea is to recast HDR reconstruction as a Probability Flow ODE and learn an exposure-aware consistency model from scratch, avoiding distillation from a multistep diffusion teacher. ExpoCM first builds a soft exposure mask from luminance percentiles to partition pixels into over-, under-, and well-exposed regions, then blends three region-specific trajectories: saturated areas are generated directly from noise to hallucinate missing structure, dark regions inject low-pass filtered LDR cues to suppress amplified noise, and reliable regions follow the standard LDR-conditioned path. A second contribution, the Exposure-guided Luminance-Chromaticity loss in CIE L*a*b* space, weights luminance and chromaticity differently by exposure to curb brightness bias, color drift, and white-clipped highlights. On HDR-REAL, HDR-EYE, and AIM2025, it reports state-of-the-art fidelity and perceptual scores while running over 400× faster than DDPM and 20× faster than DDIM, pointing HDR restoration toward exposure-conditioned one-step generative inference.
papers.abstract: Single-image HDR reconstruction aims to recover high dynamic range radiance from a single low dynamic range (LDR) input, but remains highly ill-posed due to detail saturation in over-exposed regions and noise amplification in under-exposed areas. While recent diffusion-based approaches offer powerful generative priors, they often overlook the exposure-dependent nature of the degradation and incur substantial computational costs from iterative sampling. To address these challenges, we propose ExpoCM, a novel one-step generative HDR reconstruction framework that reformulates HDR reconstruction as a Probability Flow ODE (PF-ODE) and constructs exposure-aware consistency trajectories via exposure-dependent perturbations. Specifically, a soft exposure mask is first constructed to separate the LDR image into over-, under-, and well-exposed regions. Based on this partition, region-conditioned consistency trajectories are designed to hallucinate saturated details, suppress noise in dark regions, and preserve reliable structures within a single, distillation-free inference step. To further enhance perceptual quality, we introduce an Exposure-guided Luminance-Chromaticity Loss in the CIE~$\text{L}^*\text{a}^*\text{b}^*$ space, which assigns exposure-aware weights to luminance and chromaticity components, effectively mitigating brightness bias and color drift. Extensive experiments on the HDR-REAL, HDR-EYE, and AIM2025 benchmarks demonstrate that ExpoCM achieves state-of-the-art fidelity and perceptual accuracy, while enabling over 400$\times$ and 20$\times$ faster inference compared to DDPM (1000 steps) and DDIM (50 steps), respectively.

papers.title: ATLAS: Article Tracking, Linking, and Analysis of Swedish Encyclopedias
papers.summary: ATLAS: Article Tracking, Linking, and Analysis of Swedish Encyclopedias tackles the fact that digitized encyclopedias are usually reduced to OCR text, leaving entry boundaries, headwords, entity types, and edition-to-edition variation unusable for analysis. The paper builds a text-only pipeline for the four editions of Nordisk familjebok (1876–1951) that first reconstructs article structure by extracting headwords and segmenting entries, then classifies each entry as Person, Location, or Other, then tracks the same article across editions, and finally links entries to Wikidata. A distinctive aspect is its hybrid use of noisy markup as silver supervision: bold tags in early editions seed headword training data, while a manually corrected 5,000-item test set and a separate 6,000-entry category set support evaluation and fine-tuning. For headword extraction, a lightweight biLSTM matched a fine-tuned KB-BERT variant and was selected for efficiency, reaching 97.8 F1; category classification with Swedish BERT reached 93.4 F1. Cross-edition matching uses Swedish sentence embeddings in Qdrant with conservative cosine-threshold filtering and symmetry checks, while Wikidata linking is restricted to Wikipedia articles citing Nordisk familjebok via P1343. The system supports historical knowledge transmission studies and digital humanities workflows.
papers.abstract: The digitization of old encyclopedias represents an important step to improve access to historically structured knowledge. Often, however, this process does not go beyond an optical character recognition, leaving all the underlying structure unexploited. In addition, many encyclopedias had multiple editions reflecting the evolution of knowledge. The lack of structure in the raw text makes it difficult to track changes across these editions. In this work, we built a pipeline to restore the text structure, where we extract the headwords and identify entries; categorize the entities; match entries across editions; and link entries to a Wikidata item. We applied this pipeline to the four major editions of \textit{Nordisk familjebok}, an authoritative Swedish encyclopedia published between 1876 and 1951. We could extract the headwords with an F1 score of 97.8\% and we obtained an F1 score of 93.4\% on the headword classification. On a small-scale evaluation, we reached a 93\% precision on the cross-edition matching, 85\% precision and 16.5\% recall on the Wikidata linking. This shows that an automated approach to digitized historical knowledge is possible. This should facilitate the preservation of general knowledge and the understanding of knowledge transmission. The datasets and programs are available online.

papers.title: Reference-Sampled Boltzmann Projection for KL-Regularized RLVR: Target-Matched Weighted SFT, Finite One-Shot Gaps, and Policy Mirror Descent
papers.summary: Reference-Sampled Boltzmann Projection for KL-Regularized RLVR: Target-Matched Weighted SFT, Finite One-Shot Gaps, and Policy Mirror Descent studies how to replace online RL with verifiable rewards, where rollout generation and reference-policy log-prob evaluation stay on the optimization path, by a static weighted-SFT objective that induces exactly the same fixed-reference KL-regularized target. The paper’s key move is to treat weighted likelihood as defining a policy through sampler-weight product q(y|x)w(x,y), then prove that under reference sampling q=πref the only target-matching weight, up to prompt scaling, is the prompt-normalized Boltzmann density ratio exp(r(x,y)/β)/Z(x). Its BOLT procedure precomputes reference rollouts, verifier rewards, and Monte Carlo normalizers, then trains with standard SFT to estimate the Boltzmann projection. The finite theory separates irreducible stored-support loss βlog(1/π*(S_N|x)) from normalizer estimation, ESS variance, generalization, optimization, and approximation errors, showing extra epochs cannot recover missing reference coverage. Iterating refreshed projections becomes KL policy mirror descent with drift from inexact inner solves. Single-run Qwen results on GSM8K, MATH, and HumanEval show target-matched weighting beats raw-reward Refit, one-shot saturation under fixed support, and refreshed-sampler gains with major optimization-time savings.
papers.abstract: Online reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) turns checkable outcomes into a scalable training signal, but it keeps rollout generation, verifier scoring, and reference-policy evaluations on the optimization path. Static weighted supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on precomputed rollouts seems to remove this bottleneck, yet a weighted likelihood is not specified by rewards alone: its sampler and weights induce the policy being fit. This paper identifies the reference-sampled weighted-SFT objective whose induced policy equals the fixed-reference KL-regularized RLVR optimizer. The optimizer is the standard Boltzmann target policy, obtained by exponentially tilting the reference policy by verifier reward. Matching a weighted-SFT induced policy to this target forces density-ratio weights; in the reference-sampled subclass, this reduces uniquely, up to prompt scaling, to the prompt-normalized Boltzmann weight $\exp(r(x,y)/β)/Z(x)$. BOLT, a Boltzmann-Targeted SFT procedure, is the empirical estimator of this projection. The finite one-shot analysis separates the exact stored-support price $β\log(1/π^*(S_N\mid x))$ from partition estimation, effective-sample-size variance, generalization, optimization, and approximation errors. This decomposition explains why extra SFT epochs cannot repair missing reference-policy coverage and exposes the temperature--coverage--variance frontier. When coverage needs adaptive sampling, refreshed Boltzmann projections become KL policy mirror descent; finite inner solves enter as additive drift from the exact mirror step. Single-run Qwen experiments provide projection evidence for the target-matched weight, one-shot saturation, refreshed-sampler gains, and optimization-time savings, within the stated single-run scope.

papers.title: Multispectral Blind Image Super-Resolution for Standing Dead Tree Segmentation
papers.summary: Multispectral Blind Image Super-Resolution for Standing Dead Tree Segmentation tackles the remote-sensing bottleneck that dead-tree masks need sharper aerial imagery than low-end sensors can reliably provide, while paired low/high-resolution training data are essentially unavailable. The paper reframes super-resolution as blind image-to-image translation and adapts ADA-Net with a residual self-attention generator, PatchGAN discriminator, and contrastive objectives in both spatial and 2D-FFT frequency space, plus identity losses that keep true high-resolution inputs nearly unchanged.
