Unlike conventional SR methods that downsample high-resolution sources synthetically, it learns only from unpaired 4-band multispectral scenes, matching a real deployment gap where low-resolution Finland orthomosaics are noisy, blurry, low-contrast, and sometimes saturated, and high-resolution Poland orthophotos are the target domain. The restored images are then used to train TreeMort-1T-UNet for standing dead tree segmentation. On the held-out high-resolution test set, ADA-Net reaches 54.06% Dice without any high-resolution annotations; combining real HR with super-resolved data lifts Dice to 63.86% and F2 to nearly 70%, suggesting task-driven restoration for forest monitoring rather than PSNR-centric reconstruction.
papers.abstract: Mapping standing dead trees is crucial for acquiring information on the effects of climate change on forests and forest biodiversity. However, leveraging high-quality aerial imagery for dead tree segmentation poses challenges due to limitations in sensor availability and the scarcity of annotated data. In this study, we propose a generic blind super-resolution framework that incorporates Attention-Guided Domain Adaptation Networks (ADA-Nets) to learn the mapping from low-resolution to high-resolution multispectral image domains. Our approach operates solely on unpaired samples, mimicking real-world conditions, i.e., low-resolution images are not synthetically obtained by downsampling the high-resolution images. Moreover, the proposed method serves as a general-purpose restorer addressing several image degradation types, including saturation, noise, and low contrast that typically occur in low-resolution images acquired by low-end sensors. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to perform real-world and generic super-resolution for multispectral data in the scope of standing dead tree segmentation. Experimental evaluations demonstrate segmentation performances of 54% and 64% in Dice scores. Notably, the first result is obtained without using any high-resolution annotations; the segmentation network is trained on super-resolved low-resolution images, while evaluation is performed on the high-resolution data. We publicly share the aerial multispectral dataset with manually annotated labels at https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/meteahishali/aerial-imagery-for-dead-tree-segmentation-poland.

papers.title: Accurate Legal Reasoning at Scale: Neuro-Symbolic Offloading and Structural Auditability for Robust Legal Adjudication
papers.summary: In Accurate Legal Reasoning at Scale: Neuro-Symbolic Offloading and Structural Auditability for Robust Legal Adjudication, Sójka and Kowalczyk target the bottleneck that frontier LRMs face on “computational legal clauses”: nested eligibility rules, date-sensitive pricing, and arithmetic that must be exact, repeatable, and reviewable. Their key move is Amortized Intelligence, where an LLM is used once as a semantic compiler to translate raw contract text into Deterministic Autonomous Contract Language (DACL), a typed directed-graph IR with explicit variable dependencies, clause versioning, range validation, and sandboxed pricing formulas. At runtime, a lightweight gpt-5-mini agent only routes queries and calls a symbolic evaluate_clauses tool, producing both the value and a structurally auditable trace. Unlike nearby legal LLM work that reasons over the full contract at inference time, this offloads adjudication into deterministic execution. Evaluated on 400 synthetic events from four real industrial agreements (healthcare, energy, logistics, municipal billing) against gold-standard code, DACL reached 99.5% accuracy, stayed stable on a 76-state logistics stress test, cut tokens 9.9x and costs over 90%, and eliminated the reasoning cliff caused mainly by variable-dependency errors.
papers.abstract: Legal texts often contain computational legal clauses--provisions whose understanding requires complex logic. While frontier Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) can describe such clauses, building production-ready systems is limited by reasoning errors and the high cost of inference. We propose Amortized Intelligence, a neuro-symbolic approach where we use an LLM once to translate a legal text into Deterministic Autonomous Contract Language (DACL): a typed graph intermediate representation. Adjudication then relies on deterministic graph executions with a visually auditable trace. In comparison against runtime LRM baselines (including GPT-5.2 and Gemini 3 Pro), our DACL-based Agent achieves near-perfect consistency and mitigates the "reasoning cliff" observed in probabilistic models. The system reduces compute costs by over 90% in high-volume workflows while satisfying the strict auditability requirements of legal adjudication.

papers.title: Shadow-Loom: Causal Reasoning over Graphical World Model of Narratives
papers.summary: Shadow-Loom: Causal Reasoning over Graphical World Model of Narratives tackles the bottleneck that LLM story systems blur causality, belief, and reader knowledge into one opaque next-token process, making counterfactual “what if” queries and suspense control unreliable. The core move is to compile a narrative into a versioned typed graph, where entities, events, beliefs, channels, locations, and causal/social/spatial edges persist as first-class objects and shadow branches are stored as sibling version rows rather than overwritten text. On that graph, Shadow-Loom runs two coupled engines: a causal physics implementing Pearl’s ladder of causation plus AMWN-style counterfactual calculus, and a narrative physics that scores the same world against mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, and surprise. LLMs are restricted to extraction, prose rendering, and audit; identification, intervention, abduction, propagation, and reader-state scoring are done in typed code with “miracle-step” detection. The system is demonstrated on bundled fixtures such as Macbeth, Death on the Nile, and Reservoir Dogs, and released as an open-source research artefact rather than a benchmarked NLP model. It points toward inspectable neuro-symbolic narrative simulation, auditable counterfactual generation, and explicit world-model interfaces for reasoning-heavy story AI.
papers.abstract: Stories hold a reader's attention because they have causes, secrets, and consequences. Shadow-Loom is an experimental open-source framework that turns a narrative into a versioned graphical world model and lets two engines act on it: a causal physics grounded in Pearl's ladder of causation and a recently proposed counterfactual calculus over Ancestral Multi-World Networks; and a narrative physics that scores the same graph against four structural reader-states -- mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, and surprise -- in the tradition of Sternberg's curiosity/suspense/surprise triad, with suspense formalised in the structural-affect line of work on story comprehension and computational suspense. Large language models are used only at the boundary: extraction, rendering, and audit; identification, intervention, and counterfactual reasoning are carried out in typed code over the graph. The system is offered as a research artefact rather than as a benchmarked NLP model; code, fixtures, and pipeline are released open source.

papers.title: Efficient Temporal Datalog Materialisation for Composite Event Recognition
papers.summary: Efficient Temporal Datalog Materialisation for Composite Event Recognition attacks a core fragmentation problem in composite event recognition: practical LARS- and Event-Calculus-based event languages are usually studied separately, making their expressivity hard to compare and their stream reasoners hard to reuse across tasks. The paper compiles practical fragments of globally stratified plain LARS and Event Calculus with delayed effects into Temporal Datalog→⊖, a forward-propagating temporal Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies, so both languages share one declarative target. The key computational contribution is Streaming Trigger Graphs (STGs), which extend state-of-the-art Trigger Graphs with time labels, n-edges for negation, incremental stratification over time, and a “consistency” criterion that fixes the failure of ordinary k-compatibility when derivations span earlier strata; STGs also support offline forgetting of nodes once their temporal horizon expires. The formal development includes correctness proofs for the translations and for STG materialisation on streaming symbolic data. The result points toward unified, incremental, low-latency CER engines that can host many temporal event formalisms under one reasoning backend.
papers.abstract: Several applications demand the timely detection of critical situations, such as threats to safety and transparency, over high-velocity streams of symbolic events. This demand has motivated the development of (i) event specification languages, which define composite events via temporal patterns over simpler events, and (ii) stream reasoning frameworks, evaluating patterns expressed in these languages. However, event specification languages are typically studied in isolation, complicating their comparison in terms of expressivity and obscuring the scope of their associated stream reasoners. To mitigate this issue, we map practical fragments of prominent event specification languages into Temporal Datalog->-, a temporal Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies. To support efficient stream reasoning over Temporal Datalog->-, we propose Streaming Trigger Graphs, an extension of a state-of-the-art technique for Datalog materialisation. Our approach yields a uniform composite event recognition mechanism that has the potential to generalise across a wide range of practical event specification languages.

papers.title: GRAIL: A Deep-Granularity Hybrid Resonance Framework for Real-Time Agent Discovery via SLM-Enhanced Indexing
papers.summary: GRAIL: A Deep-Granularity Hybrid Resonance Framework for Real-Time Agent Discovery via SLM-Enhanced Indexing attacks the bottleneck of matching natural-language user intent to the right agent in large multi-agent repositories, where LLM-based parsers are accurate but take 30+ seconds and monolithic embeddings are fast but blur capability details. The core idea is a tri-part index over agent tags, pseudo-doc-expanded descriptions, and discrete usage examples: a fine-tuned 3B SLM (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) predicts capability tags in milliseconds, a dense context index built from LLM-generated synthetic queries plus agent descriptions improves recall under vocabulary mismatch, and a MaxSim “resonance” stage scores the query against each example vector rather than averaging them, avoiding semantic dilution for multi-skilled agents. Unlike nearby tool-routing work, GRAIL separates coarse filtering from fine-grained example-level matching and uses BGE-Small-EN-v1.5 embeddings without retraining. Evaluated on AgentTaxo-9K, a new benchmark with 9,240 agents and 27,720 queries, it reaches 71.54% Recall@1, 91.45% Recall@10, and 394.48 ms end-to-end latency, outperforming dense retrieval and LLM routers. The paper points toward scalable “Internet of Agents” infrastructure with hybrid symbolic-neural discovery.
papers.abstract: As the ecosystem of Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents expands rapidly, efficient and accurate Agent Discovery becomes a critical bottleneck for large-scale multi-agent collaboration. Existing approaches typically face a dichotomy: either relying on heavy-weight LLMs for intent parsing, leading to prohibitive latency (often exceeding 30 seconds), or using monolithic vector retrieval that sacrifices semantic precision for speed. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{GRAIL} (Granular Resonance-based Agent/AI Link), a novel framework achieving sub-400ms discovery latency without compromising accuracy. GRAIL introduces three key innovations: (1) \textbf{SLM-Enhanced Prediction}, replacing the generalized LLM parser with a specialized, fine-tuned Small Language Model (SLM) for millisecond-level capability tag prediction; (2) \textbf{Pseudo-Document Expansion}, augmenting agent descriptions with synthetic queries to enhance semantic density for robust dense retrieval; and (3) \textbf{MaxSim Resonance}, a fine-grained matching mechanism computing maximum similarity between user queries and discrete agent usage examples, effectively mitigating semantic dilution. Validated on \textbf{AgentTaxo-9K}, our new large-scale dataset of 9,240 agents, GRAIL reduces end-to-end discovery latency by over \textbf{79$\times$} compared to LLM-parsing baselines, while significantly outperforming traditional vector search in Recall@10. This framework offers a scalable, industrial-grade solution for the real-time ``Internet of Agents."

papers.title: From Experimental Limits to Physical Insight: A Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Interpreting Searches Beyond the Standard Model
papers.summary: From Experimental Limits to Physical Insight: A Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Interpreting Searches Beyond the Standard Model tackles the bottleneck that BSM collider results are scattered across LaTeX papers, HEPData tables, and exclusion plots, making limit reinterpretation slow and manual. The paper’s HEP-CoPilot system combines retrieval-augmented language models with a LangGraph-orchestrated agent stack: a Mission Control/router plans the task, specialized text/table/figure/equation/terminology agents retrieve heterogeneous evidence from a PostgreSQL pgvector store, and a Plotter reconstructs exclusion curves directly from HEPData numerical records rather than relying on captions or PDF screenshots. That numerical-first design is the key differentiator versus text-centric scientific QA and multimodal document tools. The framework is tested on three recent CMS searches at the LHC: heavy long-lived charged particles, displaced-jet long-lived particles, and stop searches, including cross-paper overlays of observed 95% CL limits and lifetime-regime classification. Case studies show retrieval of relevant measurements, reconstruction of observed cross-section and mass limits, and physics-aware comparisons across analyses, with stronger LLM-as-a-judge scores than direct PDF prompting. The work points toward AI scientific co-pilots that do evidence-grounded phenomenology over structured experimental archives.
papers.abstract: Modern searches for physics beyond the Standard Model produce rapidly expanding literature containing heterogeneous information, including textual analyses, numerical datasets, and graphical exclusion limits. Integrating these distributed sources remains a time-consuming and manual process for physicists. We present HEP-CoPilot, a retrieval-augmented multi-agent AI framework for the exploration and interpretation of high-energy physics literature. The system unifies textual information from publications, structured experimental data from HEPData, and reconstructed physics plots within a multimodal retrieval and reasoning architecture. By combining retrieval-augmented language models with coordinated agent workflows, it enables evidence-grounded reasoning over experimental analyses and structured interpretation of collider results. We evaluate the framework on recent CMS searches for physics beyond the Standard Model. Case studies show that HEP-CoPilot can retrieve relevant measurements, reconstruct exclusion limits directly from HEPData records, and perform cross-paper comparisons of experimental constraints. This enables consistent, physics-aware comparison across analyses without manual data integration. These results demonstrate that retrieval-augmented AI systems can function as scientific co-pilots for particle physics, facilitating navigation of complex literature, structuring heterogeneous evidence, and accelerating the interpretation pipeline for new physics searches.

papers.title: Efficient Preference Poisoning Attack on Offline RLHF
papers.summary: Efficient Preference Poisoning Attack on Offline RLHF studies a targeted label-flip threat against offline RLHF pipelines, specifically log-linear Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), where an attacker can only corrupt existing preference pairs in a fixed dataset. The central technical observation is that flipping one preference label causes a parameter-independent shift in the DPO gradient, letting the poisoning problem be rewritten as a structured binary sparse approximation task in gradient space: choose a small subset of examples whose flip-induced gradient atoms sum to the target residual at the attacker’s desired policy. To solve this discrete problem, the paper develops Binary-Aware Lattice Attack (BAL-A), which embeds flip selection into an augmented lattice and uses LLL reduction plus Babai nearest-plane decoding, and Binary Matching Pursuit Attack (BMP-A), a greedy method adapted to non-normalized gradient dictionaries with coherence-based recovery guarantees. Unlike nearby RLHF robustness work, it provides both attack algorithms and impossibility certificates for K-flip budgets. Experiments on synthetic dictionaries and the Stanford Human Preferences dataset confirm the theory and show that attack success is governed by dictionary geometry, pointing toward geometry-aware robustness for offline preference learning.
papers.abstract: Offline Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) pipelines such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) train on a pre-collected preference dataset, which makes them vulnerable to preference poisoning attack. We study label flip attacks against log-linear DPO. We first illustrate that flipping one preference label induces a parameter-independent shift in the DPO gradient. Using this key property, we can then convert the targeted poisoning problem into a structured binary sparse approximation problem. To solve this problem, we develop two attack methods: Binary-Aware Lattice Attack (BAL-A) and Binary Matching Pursuit Attack (BMP-A). BAL-A embeds the binary flip selection problem into a binary-aware lattice and applies Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovász reduction and Babai's nearest plane algorithm; we provide sufficient conditions that enforce binary coefficients and recover the minimum-flip objective. BMP-A adapts binary matching pursuit to our non-normalized gradient dictionary and yields coherence-based recovery guarantees and robustness (impossibility) certificates for $K$-flip budgets. Experiments on synthetic dictionaries and the Stanford Human Preferences dataset validate the theory and highlight how dictionary geometry governs attack success.

papers.title: Tibetan-TTS:Low-Resource Tibetan Speech Synthesis with Large Model Adaptation
papers.summary: Tibetan-TTS:Low-Resource Tibetan Speech Synthesis with Large Model Adaptation tackles the bottleneck of Tibetan TTS under scarce speech data, dialectal diversity, and the non-bijective mapping between conservative Tibetan orthography and modern pronunciation. Rather than training a TTS stack from scratch, it adapts a pretrained Xingchen AGI Lab large speech synthesis backbone that couples an autoregressive language model with Flow Matching acoustic generation. The paper’s distinctive move is a three-part adaptation recipe: a unified data-quality pipeline for noisy multi-source corpora (audio cleanup, text normalization, speech-text alignment), Tibetan-oriented text representation redesign, and cross-lingual lightweight fine-tuning. The text side compares syllable-level modeling with a Tibetan-specific BPE tokenizer, explicitly targeting sequence-length explosion and alignment ambiguity caused by generic tokenizers. Evaluated on low-resource Tibetan speech-text data with native-speaker MOS and pronunciation accuracy, the system reaches MOS 4.28/4.35 and 97.6%/96.6% pronunciation accuracy for syllable/BPE variants, beating an external commercial Tibetan TTS interface (3.74 MOS, 93.8%). The result points toward language-specific tokenizer adaptation plus multilingual backbone transfer as a practical route to unified multi-dialect TTS for endangered languages.
papers.abstract: Tibetan text-to-speech (TTS) has long been challenged by scarce speech resources, significant dialectal variation, and the complex mapping between written text and spoken pronunciation. To address these issues, this work presents, to the best of our knowledge, the first large-model-based Tibetan TTS system in the industry, built upon a large speech synthesis model developed by Xingchen AGI Lab. The proposed system integrates data quality enhancement, Tibetan-oriented text representation and tokenizer adaptation, and cross-lingual adaptive training for low-resource Tibetan speech synthesis. Experimental results show that the system can generate stable, natural, and intelligible Tibetan speech under low-resource conditions. In subjective evaluation, the MOS scores of the syllable-level and BPE-based systems reach 4.28 and 4.35, while their pronunciation accuracies reach 97.6% and 96.6%, respectively, outperforming an external commercial Tibetan TTS interface. These results demonstrate that combining a large-model backbone with Tibetan-oriented text representation adaptation and cross-lingual adaptive training enables highly usable low-resource Tibetan speech synthesis, and also provides a technical foundation for future unified multi-dialect Tibetan speech synthesis.

papers.title: Pretraining on Sleep Data Improves non-Sleep Biosignal Tasks
papers.summary: Pretraining on Sleep Data Improves non-Sleep Biosignal Tasks asks whether polysomnography, usually treated as a sleep-only modality, can become a transferable pretraining distribution for wake biosignal classification. The authors adapt SleepFM1 to use 1-second patches over 2-minute windows and train it with sleep-only multimodal contrastive pretraining under a leave-one-out objective on 48,093 overnight PSG recordings from four cohorts, totaling 432,000 hours and no demographic inputs. They then fine-tune the encoder on eight clinically grounded downstream tasks: five EEG benchmarks (TUAB abnormality, TUEV event labeling, TUSL slowing, CHB-MIT seizure detection, BrainCapture low-resource abnormality detection) and three external ECG arrhythmia datasets, with Code-15 as the finetune set and PTB-XL, G12EC, and CPSC as held-out evaluation. A key differentiator is the REVE-inspired 3D electrode positional encoding plus channel-ablation analysis, showing 95% of full performance with only 16–50% of EEG channels and 41–75% of ECG leads. Sleep pretraining consistently beats scratch training, reaches or matches specialized SOTA on several EEG tasks, and transfers positively to ECG despite domain shift, pointing to PSG as a compact, channel-robust foundation corpus for biosignal AI.
papers.abstract: Sleep foundation models have recently demonstrated strong performance on in-domain polysomnography tasks, including sleep staging, apnea detection, and disease risk prediction. In this work, we investigate whether sleep biosignals can serve as an effective pretraining distribution for learning representations that transfer beyond sleep to adjacent domains. Following sleep foundation models, we perform sleep-only multimodal contrastive pretraining (with a leave-one-out objective) and evaluate transfer to non-sleep EEG and ECG, two well-benchmarked biosignal modalities with heterogeneous datasets and clinically meaningful downstream tasks. Across eight downstream tasks spanning multiple EEG and ECG datasets, sleep pretraining consistently improves performance relative to training from scratch. Moreover, on several tasks, we achieve performance competitive with or surpassing prior specialized state-of-the-art and foundation models.

papers.title: DataClaw: A Process-Oriented Agent Benchmark for Exploratory Real-World Data Analysis
papers.summary: DataClaw: A Process-Oriented Agent Benchmark for Exploratory Real-World Data Analysis addresses a gap in agent evaluation: current data-analysis benchmarks often give source/schema priors and score only final-answer accuracy, so they miss whether an autonomous LLM agent can actually discover relevant evidence in messy, underexplored data. DataClaw builds a unified real-world environment of about 2.06 million preserved-noise records spanning enterprise, industry, and policy sources, then defines 492 cross-domain consulting-style tasks that require multi-step exploration, cross-source alignment, and unit/definition reconciliation. Its differentiating mechanism is process supervision: each task is annotated with ordered reference steps, critical milestones, and gold step counts, enabling outcome accuracy, execution efficiency, goal progress rate, and temporal progress efficiency to localize where reasoning breaks. In OpenClaw Dockerized runs over eight frontier LLMs, seven models stay below 50% overall accuracy and even the best, Claude Opus 4.6, reaches only 63.4%, with performance dropping sharply on hard tasks. The process view exposes hidden partial progress and distinct exploration styles, pointing toward milestone-based reward shaping and progress verifiers for autonomous data-analysis agents.
papers.abstract: Evaluating autonomous data analysis agents requires testing their ability to perform exploratory analysis in underexplored data environments. However, many existing benchmarks emphasize final answer accuracy in prior-guided data settings and provide limited support for reasoning process evaluation. We introduce DataClaw, a process-oriented benchmark for exploratory real-world data analysis. DataClaw contains approximately 2.06 million real-world records across enterprise, industry and policy domains, with native data noise preserved. It further includes 492 cross-domain tasks derived from think-tank consulting scenarios, each annotated with intermediate milestones for process-level evaluation. These annotations allow DataClaw to measure how far an agent progresses and where its reasoning breaks down. Experiments with eight advanced LLMs show that current agents remain far from reliable in this setting, with seven models achieving below 50% overall accuracy. Process analysis further reveals partial progress hidden behind wrong answers and distinct exploration strategies across models. Overall, DataClaw provides a less data constrained diagnostic testbed for probing the capability boundaries of autonomous data-analysis agents.

papers.title: A multilingual hallucination benchmark: MultiWikiQHalluA
papers.summary: A multilingual hallucination benchmark: MultiWikiQHalluA tackles the gap that most hallucination tests are English-only by measuring faithfulness hallucinations in grounded QA, where answers must stay consistent with a provided Wikipedia context. The paper repurposes the multilingual MultiWikiQA reading-comprehension dataset and the LettuceDetect/RAGFactChecker pipeline to synthesize token-span hallucination labels for 306 languages, then fine-tunes token-level classifiers for 30 European languages, selecting mmBERT-small after Danish/German validation. Unlike answer-level hallucination benchmarks, this setup localizes unsupported spans and lets the same test split probe both supported and hallucinated outputs under identical conditions. The authors release the multilingual synthetic dataset and classifiers, then score Qwen3-0.6B, Qwen3-14B, Gemma-3-12B-IT, cogito-v1-preview-qwen-32B, and cogito-v1-preview-llama-70B on English, Danish, German, and Icelandic. Results show markedly higher hallucination in lower-resource languages, especially Icelandic, where Qwen3-0.6B reaches up to 60% answer-level hallucination, while the larger cogito models are generally best; model size is not strictly monotonic, suggesting multilingual training mix and tokenization granularity matter.
papers.abstract: Most hallucination evaluations focus on English, leaving it unclear whether findings transfer to lower-resource languages. We investigate faithfulness hallucinations, defined as model-generated content that is fluent and plausible but diverges from the provided input or is internally inconsistent. Leveraging the multilingual MultiWikiQA dataset, we utilize the LettuceDetect framework to create synthetic hallucination datasets for 306 languages, from which we train token-level hallucination classifiers for 30 European languages. In this work, we present evaluations of model hallucinations on a selection of languages: English, Danish, German, and Icelandic. Using these classifiers, we evaluate the hallucination rates for Qwen3-0.6B, Qwen3-14B, Gemma-3-12B-IT, cogito-v1-preview-qwen-32B, and cogito-v1-preview-llama-70B. Our classifiers reveal notably higher hallucination rates for Qwen3-0.6B (up to 60\% of answers containing at least one hallucination, peaking in Icelandic) and generally lower rates for larger models, with cogito-v1-preview-qwen-32B and cogito-v1-preview-llama-70B performing best on most languages. Hallucination rates are consistently higher for lower-resource languages, particularly Icelandic.

papers.title: Revisiting Semantic Role Labeling: Efficient Structured Inference with Dependency-Informed Analysis
papers.summary: Revisiting Semantic Role Labeling: Efficient Structured Inference with Dependency-Informed Analysis tackles the practical bottleneck that span-based SRL systems built around AllenNLP-style per-predicate instances have become hard to maintain and unnecessarily slow for modern encoder backbones. The core mechanism is a modernized encoder-based SRL pipeline that caches a sentence-level encoding once, then reuses it across all predicates while still injecting predicate conditioning via a [CLS] sentence [SEP] predicate [SEP] format and segment embeddings; this removes redundant recomputation and yields about 10× faster inference. On CoNLL-2012 OntoNotes 5.0, BERT-base matches AllenNLP closely in F1, while RoBERTa and DeBERTa improve within the same structured inference design. What differentiates the paper is its dependency-informed analysis layer: a diagnostic error analyzer merges structurally repeated spans, classifies same_head/pp_attach/subtree_attach cases, and shows that dependency cues mainly improve structural stability rather than role classification per se. The paper also tests zero-shot gpt-oss-120b with and without dependency prompts, finding better span coherence, and demonstrates English-to-French SRL projection, pointing toward explicit, structure-preserving semantic interfaces for multilingual and LLM-era NLP.
papers.abstract: Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) provides an explicit representation of predicate-argument structure, capturing linguistically grounded relations such as who did what to whom. While recent NLP progress has been dominated by large language models (LLMs), these systems often rely on implicit semantic representations, often lacking explicit structural constraints and systematic explanatory mechanisms. Traditionally, SRL systems have often relied on AllenNLP; however, the framework entered maintenance mode in December 2022, limiting compatibility with evolving encoder architectures and modern inference requirements. We revisit structured SRL modeling, introducing a modernized encoder-based framework that preserves explicit predicate-argument structure while enabling inference 10 times faster. Using BERT-base, the model attains comparable predictive performance, and RoBERTa and DeBERTa further improve F1 performance within the same framework. We adopt a dependency-informed diagnostic methodology to characterize span-level inconsistencies and conduct a representation-level analysis of LLM behavior under dependency-informed structural signals. Results indicate that dependency cues primarily improve structural stability. Finally, we illustrate how the framework's explicit predicate-argument structure can support multilingual SRL projection as a downstream application.

papers.title: A Novel Preprocessing-Driven Approach to Remaining Useful Life (RUL) Prediction Using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN)
papers.summary: A Novel Preprocessing-Driven Approach to Remaining Useful Life (RUL) Prediction Using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN) attacks a specific bottleneck in aero-engine prognostics: most RUL models obsess over architecture while treating preprocessing as fixed, even though windowing, scaling, and labeling shape what degradation signal the network can learn. The paper’s distinguishing move is a TCN-centered pipeline that trains on complete CMAPSS engine trajectories rather than sliding windows, then randomly trims 10–75 cycles from the tail to mimic partial-life observation, standardizes sensors with z-score normalization, caps RUL at 125 cycles, and supervises the network at every timestep instead of only at selected points. The TCN itself is a four-block residual model with dilated convolutions [1, 2, 4, 8, 16], kernel size 3, 200 filters, dropout 0.3, and a projection head for dense cycle-wise RUL outputs. On NASA C-MAPSS FD001–FD004, evaluated with RMSE and the PHM Score against CNN, RNN, LSTM, DCNN, TCN, BiGRU-TSAM, AGCNN, and ATCN, the method reports the best average RMSE and especially strong robustness on the harder multi-condition subsets FD002 and FD004, pointing toward preprocessing-aware prognostic learning.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of Remaining Useful Life (RUL) in aero-engines is vital for predictive maintenance, improved operational reliability, and reduced lifecycle costs. While deep learning approaches have demonstrated strong potential in this area, most existing methods focus primarily on model architecture design and treat input features uniformly, often neglecting the influence of data preprocessing. In this work, we propose a novel preprocessing pipeline that enhances RUL prediction by improving data quality and temporal representation before model training. Our approach leverages complete temporal sequences and generates RUL estimates at each timestep, enabling the model to capture fine-grained degradation dynamics and deliver continuous prognostic insights throughout the engine's operational life. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed pipeline, we conduct experiments on the NASA C-MAPSS dataset. Comparative evaluations against a suite of state-of-the-art neural models including CNN, RNN, LSTM, DCNN, TCN, BiGRU-TSAM, AGCNN, and ATCN, demonstrate that our approach consistently achieves superior accuracy and robustness in aero-engine RUL prediction. These results highlight the critical role of preprocessing in maximizing the effectiveness of neural prognostic models.

papers.title: MPCS: Neuroplastic Continual Learning via Multi-Component Plasticity and Topology-Aware EWC
papers.summary: MPCS: Neuroplastic Continual Learning via Multi-Component Plasticity and Topology-Aware EWC tackles catastrophic forgetting under sequential multi-domain training by asking which plasticity mechanisms actually matter when they are composed, not isolated. The system combines eleven biologically inspired modules—task-driven neurogenesis, random Fourier input encoding, replay with mixed consolidation, hybrid gating, synapse pruning/regeneration, Hebbian updates, task-similarity routing, adaptive growth control, continuous neuron importance, and EWC—inside a single modular continual learner. The differentiating move is topology-aware EWC: Fisher penalties are masked to task-owned neuron subspaces to avoid cross-task “importance leakage,” and the paper uses a three-objective benchmark, MEP-BENCH, spanning 31 regression, classification, logic, and mixed tasks with performance, representation diversity, and gradient conflict rate. Across 15 ablations, MPCS reaches NES 94.2 and sits on the Pareto frontier in 9 of 14 gate-passing systems. The standout finding is that Fourier features are load-bearing, while global EWC is net-negative in this high-similarity regime; dropping EWC plus Hebbian yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, which is slightly more accurate and 4.7× cheaper. This points continual learning toward mechanism selection, topology-local regularization, and Pareto-guided compression rather than ever larger plasticity stacks.
papers.abstract: Continual learning systems face a fundamental tension between plasticity -- acquiring new knowledge -- and stability -- retaining prior knowledge. We introduce MPCS (Multi-Plasticity Continual System), a neuroplastic architecture that integrates eleven complementary mechanisms: task-driven neurogenesis, Fourier-encoded inputs, EWC regularization, meta-replay, mixed consolidation, hybrid gating, synapse pruning/regeneration, Hebbian updates, task similarity routing, adaptive growth control, and continuous neuron importance tracking. We evaluate MPCS on MEP-BENCH, a multi-track benchmark spanning 31 tasks across regression, classification, logic, and mixed domains, using a three-dimensional Pareto criterion over task performance (Perf), representation diversity (RD), and gradient conflict rate (GCR). Across 15 ablation configurations (3 seeds x 4 tracks x 2000 epochs), MPCS achieves a Normalized Efficiency Score of 94.2, placing it on the Pareto frontier among 9 of 14 gate-passing systems. Key findings: (i) Fourier encoding is the single most critical component (removal drops Perf by 30.7 pp and fails the MEP gate on 14% of tasks); (ii) global EWC degrades performance (NES = -4.2); topology-local EWC reduces this penalty (NES 90.5->91.8) but does not eliminate it; removing EWC entirely yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, the highest-Perf system -- establishing a monotone relationship in the high task-similarity regime (s_bar ~= 0.95): global EWC < topology EWC < no EWC; (iii) the Pareto status assessment is predictive: removing the two Pareto-dominated components (EWC + Hebbian) jointly yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, which improves Perf by 0.6 pp at 4.7x lower compute cost (127 vs. 602 min), validating the Pareto frontier as an actionable model-compression guide.

papers.title: Evaluating Tabular Representation Learning for Network Intrusion Detection
papers.summary: Evaluating Tabular Representation Learning for Network Intrusion Detection examines whether modern tabular encoders can replace manual NetFlow feature engineering in NIDS, where heterogeneous fields, extreme benign/attack imbalance, and high-cardinality IP/port variables make classical models brittle. The paper benchmarks TabICL, SCARF, class-conditioned contrastive learning (CC), and a plain Autoencoder, plus a BERT-style end-to-end baseline, on three NetFlow datasets: CIDDS-001, NF-UNSW-NB15-v2, and NF-CSE-CIC-IDS2018-v2. Rather than treating the representation learner as the final detector, it decouples embedding from downstream use and evaluates each latent space with supervised classifiers (RF, SVM, LR, XGBoost) and unsupervised detectors (Isolation Forest, KNN, PCA, OCSVM) under extensive hyperparameter search. The key finding is strong dataset-model dependence: TabICL-RF is best on CIDDS, Autoencoder+SVM and BERT tie for best average supervised rank, and Autoencoder+Isolation Forest is the most reliable unsupervised choice. Cross-dataset transfer can work, but only with careful source-target/method selection. Most strikingly, all methods miss rare attack categories, pointing toward imbalance-aware representation learning for cybersecurity.
papers.abstract: Classic Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) often rely on manual feature engineering to extract meaningful patterns from network traffic data. However, this approach requires domain expertise and runs counter to the widely adopted principle of modern machine learning and neural networks: that models themselves should learn meaningful representations directly from data. We investigate whether tabular representation learning techniques can improve intrusion detection performance by automatically learning robust feature representations for NetFlow data. This paper presents a systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art representation learning methods on benchmark NetFlow datasets, comparing against traditional autoencoders and end-to-end transformer baselines. We evaluate learned representations using both supervised classifiers and unsupervised anomaly detectors, with comprehensive hyperparameter exploration for each combination. Our results reveal strong dataset-model dependency, with no single approach consistently dominating across all scenarios. For supervised classification, TabICL achieves the best performance on CIDDS, while autoencoders follow closely and tie with end-to-end transformer models for the best average rank across datasets. Supervised approaches substantially outperform unsupervised anomaly detection methods, where no single combination consistently dominates as optimal choices depend on the dataset. Cross-dataset transfer experiments demonstrate that learned representations can generalize across network environments with appropriate method and classifier selection. However, transfer performance varies substantially depending on the source-target dataset combination, indicating sensitivity to distributional differences between network environments.

papers.title: Benchmarking Retrieval Strategies for Biomedical Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Controlled Empirical Study
papers.summary: Benchmarking Retrieval Strategies for Biomedical Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Controlled Empirical Study tackles a practical but undercontrolled question in biomedical RAG: which retriever actually helps most when generation is held fixed and evaluation spans both retrieval and answer quality. The authors build a modular pipeline on rag-mini-bioasq, sampling 250 BioASQ question-answer pairs over a 40,181-passage PubMed corpus, and isolate retrieval as the only variable by fixing GPT-4o-mini, ChromaDB, OpenAI text-embedding-3-small, k=10, and the prompt. They compare Dense Vector Search, Hybrid BM25+dense fusion, Cross-Encoder Reranking, Multi-Query Expansion, and MMR, then score outputs with DeepEval contextual precision/recall, faithfulness, and answer relevancy, reporting 95% confidence intervals plus a no-context ablation. The distinctive result is that Cross-Encoder Reranking wins on contextual precision and composite score, but Dense Search is nearly tied and best on recall, while Multi-Query Expansion actually harms precision, showing that naive query diversification can inject noise. All RAG variants vastly outperform no-context on answer relevancy, pointing toward controlled, metric-rich retrieval benchmarking as a core direction for safe biomedical RAG design.
papers.abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) offers a well-established path to grounding large language model (LLM) outputs in external knowledge, yet the question of which retrieval strategy works best in a high-stakes domain such as biomedicine has not received the controlled, multi-metric treatment it deserves. This paper presents a systematic empirical comparison of five retrieval strategies -- Dense Vector Search, Hybrid BM25 + Dense retrieval, Cross-Encoder Reranking, Multi-Query Expansion, and Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) -- within a biomedical question-answering RAG pipeline. All strategies share a fixed generation model (GPT-4o-mini), a common vector store (ChromaDB), and OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small embeddings, ensuring that observed differences are attributable to retrieval alone. Evaluation is conducted on 250 question-answer pairs drawn from a preprocessed subset of the BioASQ benchmark (rag-mini-bioasq) using four DeepEval metrics: contextual precision, contextual recall, faithfulness, and answer relevancy, each reported with 95% confidence intervals. A no-context ablation is included as a lower bound. Cross-Encoder Reranking achieves the best composite score (0.827) and highest contextual precision (0.852), confirming that query-document interaction yields measurable retrieval gains. Multi-Query Expansion, despite its recall-oriented design, produces the weakest contextual precision (0.671), suggesting naive query diversification introduces retrieval noise. MMR sacrifices answer relevancy for diversity, while the Dense baseline (composite 0.822) falls within 0.005 points of the top strategy. All RAG conditions dramatically outperform the no-context ablation on answer relevancy (0.658-0.701 vs. 0.287), confirming the practical value of retrieval. The full pipeline, hyperparameters, and evaluation code are publicly available.

papers.title: MooD: An Efficient VA-Driven Affective Image Editing Framework via Fine-Grained Semantic Control
papers.summary: MooD: An Efficient VA-Driven Affective Image Editing Framework via Fine-Grained Semantic Control tackles the core bottleneck in affective image editing: existing editors are slow, depend on discrete emotion classes or verbose prompts, and cannot express subtle emotional mixtures. The paper reframes editing around continuous valence-arousal coordinates, then bridges the semantic gap from numeric VA targets to concrete image changes with a two-stage VA-Aware retrieval: first filter a VA-near candidate pool, then select the source-compatible reference via OpenCLIP similarity. MooD then uses a dual-channel diffusion pipeline on SDXL, combining an image adapter for visual transfer with ASP-Net, an Affective Semantic Projection Network that maps affective visual features into CLIP text-embedding space as multi-token semantic guidance, suppressing hallucinated structures and semantic contamination. To train and evaluate this regime, the authors build AffectSet, a 63,387-sample VA-annotated dataset derived from EmoSet via Qwen2.5-VL-72B labeling. On the EmoEdit inference set, MooD reports the best valence/arousal errors and strong fidelity while running far faster than iterative agentic or multi-round diffusion baselines, pointing toward continuous, retrieval-grounded, lightweight affect control for practical interactive editing.
papers.abstract: Affective image editing (AIE) aims to edit visual content to evoke target emotions. However, existing methods often overlook inference efficiency and predominantly depend on discrete emotion representations, which to some extent limits their practical applicability and makes it challenging to capture complex and subtle human emotions. To tackle these gaps, we propose MooD, the first framework that directly leverages continuous Valence-Arousal (VA) values for fine-grained and efficient AIE. Specifically, we first introduce a VA-Aware retrieval strategy to bridge vague affective values and concrete visual semantics. Building upon this, MooD integrates visual transfer and semantic guidance to achieve controllable AIE. Furthermore, we construct AffectSet, a VA-annotated dataset to support model optimization and evaluation. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experimental results demonstrate that our MooD achieves superior performance in both affective controllability and visual fidelity while maintaining high efficiency. A series of ablation studies further reveal the crucial factors of our design. Our code and data will be made publicly open soon.

papers.title: Physics-Informed Neural Learning for State Reconstruction and Parameter Identification in Coupled Greenhouse Climate Dynamics
papers.summary: Physics-Informed Neural Learning for State Reconstruction and Parameter Identification in Coupled Greenhouse Climate Dynamics attacks the inverse greenhouse-climate problem of recovering hidden indoor temperature and relative humidity trajectories when sensing is sparse, noisy, and the governing exchange coefficients are unknown. The paper’s key idea is a coupled PINN built around a reduced two-state thermal–moisture balance model, where a neural net maps time to T(t) and H(t) while the coefficients for outdoor relaxation, solar forcing, ventilation exchange, and weak temperature–humidity cross-coupling are treated as trainable parameters and constrained through ODE residuals and initial-condition penalties. What distinguishes it from nearby greenhouse forecasting work is that it is not just a predictor: it jointly reconstructs states and identifies interpretable physical parameters, using the same architecture as a pure neural baseline to isolate the effect of physics regularization. Evaluation is on a controlled synthetic 72-hour diurnal benchmark with 25% observations and Gaussian noise. The coupled PINN cuts temperature RMSE from 0.2139 to 0.1360 and humidity RMSE from 0.6225 to 0.3221, while accurately recovering dominant parameters. The work points toward data-efficient, interpretable digital-twin modeling for environmental systems.
papers.abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have recently emerged as a promising framework for integrating data-driven learning with physical knowledge. In this work, we propose a coupled PINN approach for the joint reconstruction of indoor temperature and humidity dynamics in greenhouse environments, together with simultaneous identification of key model parameters. The method incorporates a reduced-order physically motivated model into the learning process, enabling consistent estimation under sparse and noisy observations. The artificial intelligence contribution lies in the development of a coupled physics-informed neural learning framework that integrates governing dynamical constraints into neural network training, while the engineering application focuses on greenhouse climate state reconstruction and parameter identification. The proposed framework is evaluated on a controlled synthetic benchmark that mimics diurnal forcing conditions. Compared with a purely data-driven neural network baseline, the coupled PINN achieves improved reconstruction accuracy, reducing temperature and humidity errors while maintaining high coefficients of determination. The improvement is particularly pronounced in the humidity channel, where latent moisture dynamics are more difficult to infer from limited measurements. In addition to accurate state reconstruction, the method successfully recovers the dominant physical parameters governing the system dynamics, demonstrating its ability to learn interpretable representations beyond data interpolation. These results highlight the potential of physics-informed learning for greenhouse climate modeling and, more broadly, for data-scarce environmental systems.

papers.title: A Semantic Autonomy Framework for VLM-Integrated Indoor Mobile Robots: Hybrid Deterministic Reasoning and Cross-Robot Adaptive Memory
papers.summary: A Semantic Autonomy Framework for VLM-Integrated Indoor Mobile Robots: Hybrid Deterministic Reasoning and Cross-Robot Adaptive Memory tackles the deployment gap between metric ROS 2/Nav2 navigation and operator intent expressed as natural language, where VLMs can interpret semantics but are too slow and forgetful for routine operation. The paper’s six-layer Semantic Autonomy Stack separates platform, navigation, perception, semantic reasoning, mission interface, and operational memory, then instantiates a real ROS 2 system on two custom differential-drive robots. Its distinctive move is a seven-step deterministic resolver that handles named nodes, object IDs, attributes, class/proximity cues, and learned preferences before ever calling a VLM; this fast path resolves 88% of 82 tested decisions in under 0.1 ms with no image, GPU, or training data. Ambiguous commands escalate to Qwen 3.5:4b, and a five-category memory scheme (environment, temporal patterns, operator preferences, platform capabilities, task history) compiles VLM-learned preferences into a shared digest, enabling cross-robot reuse and 103,000× latency reduction. On Raspberry Pi 5 robots, the system achieved 100% semantic resolution and 33/33 transfer accuracy, pointing toward auditable, fleet-shareable semantic autonomy rather than end-to-end model dependence.
papers.abstract: Autonomous indoor mobile robots can navigate reliably to metric coordinates using established frameworks such as ROS 2 Navigation 2, yet they lack the ability to interpret natural language instructions that express intent rather than positions. Vision-Language Models offer the semantic reasoning required to bridge this gap, but their inference latency (2-9 seconds per decision on consumer hardware) and session-by-session amnesia limit practical deployment. This paper presents the Semantic Autonomy Stack, a six-layer reference framework for semantically autonomous indoor navigation, and validates a complete instance featuring hybrid deterministic-VLM reasoning and cross-robot adaptive memory on physical robots with off-the-shelf edge hardware. A seven-step parametric resolver handles 88% of instructions in under 0.1 milliseconds without invoking a language model, camera, or GPU; only genuinely ambiguous instructions escalate to VLM reasoning. A five-category semantic memory framework with explicit scope taxonomy (global environment knowledge, per-operator preferences, per-robot capabilities) enables cross-session learning and cross-robot knowledge transfer: preferences learned through VLM interactions on one robot are promoted to deterministic resolution and transferred to a second robot via a shared compiled digest, achieving a measured latency reduction of 103,000-fold. Experimental validation on two custom-built differential-drive robots across 82 scenario-level decisions and three sessions demonstrates 100% semantic transfer accuracy (33/33, 95% CI [0.894, 1.000]), 100% semantic resolution accuracy, and concurrent multi-robot operation feasibility - all on Raspberry Pi 5 platforms with no onboard GPU, requiring zero training data.

papers.title: Set-Based Training of Neural Barrier Certificates for Safety Verification of Dynamical Systems
papers.summary: Set-Based Training of Neural Barrier Certificates for Safety Verification of Dynamical Systems attacks the bottleneck in synthesizing barrier certificates for continuous-time ODEs: existing neural approaches train on sampled states and then bounce between learning and an external verifier/SMT solver, often stalling on counterexamples, high dimensionality, or nonlinear dynamics. The paper replaces that CEGIS loop with a single training objective built from conservative zonotope propagation through the barrier network and set enclosures for unsafe states, initial states, the unknown zero-level set, the gradient, and the Lie derivative. Its key mechanism is a sound set-based loss that encodes all three barrier conditions: unsafe states must map positive, initial states non-positive, and the Lie derivative must stay negative over a recursively refined union of zonotopes approximating the zero-level set. Unusually, zero loss is itself a formal proof of safety, so verification is folded into backpropagation rather than performed afterward. Implemented in CORA and tested on 13 benchmarks spanning linear, polynomial, and nonpolynomial systems, including multiple initial/unsafe sets, it achieves 100% success and often outpaces FOSSIL while succeeding where interval-based SynNBC and PRoTECT fail. The work points toward certified learning pipelines where formal guarantees are optimized directly during training.
papers.abstract: Barrier certificates are scalar functions over the state space of dynamical systems that separate all unsafe states from all reachable states. The existence of a barrier certificate formally verifies the safety of the dynamical system. Recent approaches synthesize barrier certificates by iteratively training a neural network. In each iteration, the candidate is formally verified - if successful, the barrier certificate is found. Instead, we propose a set-based training approach that tightly integrates verification into training via a set-based loss function that soundly encodes all barrier certificate properties. A loss of zero formally proves the validity of the barrier certificate, collapsing the iterative training and verification into a single training procedure. Our experiments demonstrate that our set-based training approach scales well with the system dimension and naturally handles complex nonlinear dynamics.

papers.title: Beyond Specialization: Robust Reinforcement Learning Navigation via Procedural Map Generators
papers.summary: Beyond Specialization: Robust Reinforcement Learning Navigation via Procedural Map Generators attacks a practical weakness in LiDAR-based DRL navigation: policies trained in a handful of hand-built layouts overfit to geometry and fail when corridor structure, connectivity, or clutter changes. The paper embeds four guaranteed-navigable procedural generators—sparse, maze, graph, and Wave Function Collapse—into MuRoSim, a lightweight CPU-efficient 2D simulator, then cross-evaluates five PPO navigation policies on 1,000 seeded maps per generator across three training seeds. The distinctive result is strongly asymmetric transfer: a sparse specialist collapses to 3.3% success on mazes, while a policy trained on the combined generator set reaches 91.5 ± 1.1% mean success with the lowest seed variance. A second contribution isolates robustness mechanisms, showing that A* subgoal directions are far more valuable than recurrence: adding five planned waypoint vectors lifts the feedforward baseline from 90.2 ± 1.4% to 98.9 ± 0.4%, whereas GRU memory mainly helps the reactive baseline. Compared with a Carrot+A* controller, the learned policy’s advantage is speed adaptation, and RoboMaster trials confirm sim-to-real transfer.
papers.abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) navigation policies often overfit to the structure of their training environments, as environmental diversity is typically constrained by the manual effort required to design diverse scenarios. While procedural map generation offers scalable diversity, no prior work systematically compares how different generator types affect policy generalization. We integrate four generators (sparse, maze, graph, and Wave Function Collapse) with guaranteed navigability into MuRoSim, a 2D simulator focusing on training efficiency for LiDAR-based navigation. We cross-evaluate five navigation policies on 1000 seeded maps per generator across three training seeds. Results show a strongly asymmetric cross-generator transfer: a specialist trained on sparse layouts falls to 3.3% success on mazes, whereas a policy trained on the combined generator set achieves 91.5 +/- 1.1% mean success. We further demonstrate that A* path-planner subgoal inputs are the dominant factor for robustness, raising success from the 90.2 +/- 1.4% feedforward baseline to 98.9 +/- 0.4% and outperforming GRU recurrence, which only improves the reactive baseline. The DRL policies outperform a classical Carrot+A* controller, which matches their success only at low speeds (1.0 m/s) but collapses to 24.9% at 2.0 m/s. This highlights learned speed adaptation as the decisive advantage of the learned approach. Real-world experiments on a RoboMaster confirm sim-to-real transfer in a cluttered arena, while a maze-like layout exposes remaining failure modes that recurrence helps mitigate.

papers.title: Orchestrating Spatial Semantics via a Zone-Graph Paradigm for Intricate Indoor Scene Generation
papers.summary: Orchestrating Spatial Semantics via a Zone-Graph Paradigm for Intricate Indoor Scene Generation tackles the failure of text-driven 3D room synthesis in non-convex, densely constrained interiors, where prior generators either lose global topology or become brittle under iterative correction. The paper’s core idea is ZoneMaestro, which replaces object-by-object layout with a Zone-Graph Orchestration pipeline: a user prompt is first decomposed into a zone inventory, intra-zone spatial graphs, inter-zone topology, and finally the enclosing boundary polygon, so semantic intent is planned before coordinates are emitted. Its differentiator is not just richer planning, but an alternating training regime that cycles Zone-Graph reasoning internalization with Zone-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (Z-GRPO), using boundary, zone-separation, and collision rewards to reconcile density with geometric validity without external physics engines. To ground this, the authors build Zone-Scene-10K with explicit Zone-Graph annotations from InternScenes and release SCALE, a 824-case benchmark for Intricate Spatial Orchestration over irregular shapes like L-, T-, U-, and alcove-like rooms. On both Zone-Scene-10K and SCALE, ZoneMaestro is reported to sharply reduce out-of-bounds and collision errors while preserving higher asset density, structure, and intent adherence, pointing toward zone-centric spatial reasoning for embodied scene generation.
papers.abstract: Autonomous 3D indoor scene synthesis breaks down in non-convex rooms with tightly coupled spatial constraints. Data-driven generators lack topological priors for long-horizon planning, while iterative agents fragment semantics and become geometrically brittle. We present ZoneMaestro, a unified framework that shifts the paradigm from object-centric synthesis to Zone-Graph Orchestration. By internalizing a novel zone-based logic, ZoneMaestro translates high-level semantic intent into functional zones and topological constraints, enabling robust adaptation to diverse architectural forms. To support this, we construct Zone-Scene-10K, a large-scale dataset enriched with explicit Zone-Graph annotations. We further introduce an Alternating Alignment Strategy that cycles between reasoning internalization and Zone-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (Z-GRPO), effectively reconciling the tension between semantic richness and geometric validity without relying on external physics engines. To rigorously evaluate spatial intelligence beyond convex primitives, we formally define the task of Intricate Spatial Orchestration and release SCALE, a stress-test benchmark for irregular indoor scenarios with complex, dense spatial relations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ZoneMaestro resolves the density-safety dichotomy, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in both structural coherence and intent adherence.

papers.title: Improving Model Safety by Targeted Error Correction
papers.summary: Improving Model Safety by Targeted Error Correction tackles the safety gap left by accuracy-centric classifiers that make semantically absurd, high-consequence mistakes, such as confusing benign with malignant lesions or cats with dogs. The paper’s key idea is a fully automated post-hoc cascade: a base ResNet-50 produces probabilities, a first GBDT error detector predicts whether the prediction is wrong, and a second GBDT error-type classifier distinguishes benign human-like confusions from dangerous non-human errors using only the output distribution. Rather than retraining the backbone, the system applies a conservative superclass flip policy only when a non-human failure is predicted, preserving plausible mistakes and avoiding overcorrection. This differs from MCP or trust-score rejection because it repairs selected errors instead of merely flagging uncertainty. Evaluated on an in-the-wild animal breed benchmark, ISIC 2018 skin lesions, and SICAPv2 prostate histopathology, the pipeline adds only about 0.1 ms per image, improves correction precision over MCP, reduces non-human errors by 34.1% on ISIC and 12.57% on SICAPv2, and raises superclass safety to 90.41% and 92.13%, pointing toward cheap, deployable safety retrofits for critical vision models.
papers.abstract: The widespread adoption of machine learning in critical applications demands techniques to mitigate high-consequence errors. Our method utilizes a dual-classifier GBDT pipeline to distinguish routine human-like errors from high-risk non-human misclassifications. Evaluated across three domains, animal breed classification, skin lesion diagnosis (ISIC 2018), and prostate histopathology (SICAPv2), our framework demonstrates robust safety improvements. To address real-world deployment concerns, our results confirm the pipeline introduces negligible inference latency (1.60% overhead for the animal dataset, 1.84% for ISIC, and 1.70% for SICAPv2) while outperforming traditional Maximum Class Probability (MCP) baselines in correction precision. Our conservative correction strategy successfully reduced dangerous non-human errors by 34.1% in ISIC and 12.57% in SICAPv2, improving super-class diagnostic safety to 90.41% and 92.13% respectively. This proves that safety-critical reliability can be substantially enhanced post-hoc without expensive model retraining. keywords: Error Analysis, Post-hoc Correction, Trustworthy AI.

papers.title: Strategy-Aware Optimization Modeling with Reasoning LLMs
papers.summary: Strategy-Aware Optimization Modeling with Reasoning LLMs tackles a specific failure mode in LLM-based OR coding: models can emit syntactically valid Gurobi programs yet pick the wrong paradigm, such as introducing variables on an invalid index set or generating a correct-but-solver-inefficient formulation. The proposed SAGE pipeline makes Modeling Strategy a first-class latent decision by synthesizing a solver-verified multi-strategy corpus, where a teacher generates distinct paradigms per problem, then filters them by execution, ground-truth answer match, and LLM-based deduplication of redundant formulations. Student training combines supervised fine-tuning with Segment-Weighted GRPO, which gives extra credit to the <strategy> segment over <modeling> and <check>, and optimizes a composite reward for format compliance, executable correctness, and solver efficiency. This differs from nearby optimization-modeling work that supervises only final code or process traces without explicit paradigm selection. Evaluated on eight benchmarks spanning NL4OPT, MAMO, NLP4LP, ComplexOR, IndustryOR, OptiBench, and OptMATH, SAGE raises average pass@1 from 72.7 to 80.3, improves pass@16 diversity by 19–29%, and produces formulations with 14.2% fewer constraints, pointing toward strategy-aware, solver-grounded reasoning for automated mathematical modeling.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can generate syntactically valid optimization programs, yet often struggle to reliably choose an effective modeling strategy, leading to incorrect formulations and inefficient solver behavior. We propose SAGE, a strategy-aware framework that makes Modeling Strategy explicit in both data construction and post-training. SAGE builds a solver-verified multi-strategy dataset and trains a student model with supervised fine-tuning followed by Segment-Weighted GRPO using a composite reward over format compliance, correctness, and solver efficiency. Across eight benchmarks spanning synthetic and real-world settings, SAGE improves average pass@1 from 72.7 to 80.3 over the strongest open-source baseline. With multiple generations, SAGE discovers more distinct correct formulations and improves component-level diversity at pass@16 by 19-29%. At the largest scale, SAGE produces more compact constraint systems with 14.2% fewer constraints than the baseline, consistent with solver-efficient modeling. Overall, these results show that making Modeling Strategy explicit improves automated optimization modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/rachhhhing/SAGE.

papers.title: Double Rectified Linear Unit-based Modular Semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Framework
papers.summary: Double Rectified Linear Unit-based Modular Semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Framework tackles a concrete flaw in quantitative bipolar argumentation: standard gradual semantics such as MLP, Restricted Euler, DF-QuAD, and Quadratic Energy can produce divergent or plainly unintuitive acceptability scores even on tiny acyclic graphs, including cases where an attacked argument ignores its attacker or where adding balanced support and attack changes nothing. The paper proposes two new modular semantics, mqe and drl, that first replace plain sum aggregation with a normalized δ_q(a) using the separate supporter/attacker mass (q ∈ {sum,max}) and then update strengths with a clamped Double Rectified Linear Unit, DReLU((2τ_a−1)+γ·δ_q(a))+1/2, so initial strength and relational evidence can be explicitly traded off through γ. This differs from nearby work by fixing both the aggregation pathologies and the saturation behavior while preserving open-mindedness and rationality postulates. The authors also introduce a smooth ddrl variant via differentiable DReLU for dynamical convergence analysis. Experiments on synthetic QBAFs and the Attractor library show stronger sensitivity to support/attack balance, sublinear runtime, and convergence on large acyclic and restricted cyclic classes.
papers.abstract: Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (QBAFs) provide an alternative approach to computing argument acceptability in Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (BAFs). Each argument is assigned an initial strength, which is then updated to a final strength by considering the influence of both its attackers and supporters. Over the years, several semantics have been proposed to compute argument acceptability in QBAFs, yet they often yield divergent or counterintuitive results, even for simple acyclic cases. We introduce novel gradual semantics for QBAFs that address these limitations, producing results that align more closely with intuitive expectations, while satisfying established rationality postulates from the literature. Furthermore, we study its convergence behavior, proving that it converges not only for acyclic QBAFs but also for broader classes of cyclic frameworks.

papers.title: Recurrent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Chemotherapy Control under Partial Observability
papers.summary: Recurrent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Chemotherapy Control under Partial Observability tackles the mismatch between chemotherapy dose optimization as a dynamic treatment regime and the unrealistic full-state assumption in most RL controllers: in AhnChemoEnv from DTR-Bench, the normal-cell population is hidden, T/I/B observations are corrupted by multiplicative noise, and PK/PD variability is deliberately fixed so the study isolates hidden-state uncertainty rather than inter-patient heterogeneity. The core mechanism is a recurrent TD3 agent with separate LSTM actor and critic encoders, sequence replay with 32-step context, and a direct current-observation shortcut into the actor so memory is used mainly to infer latent physiological status from history. The paper compares off-policy recurrent TD3, on-policy rPPO, feed-forward TD3, and SAC under both fully observable and POMDP settings across ten seeds and 4×10^5 steps. Recurrence helps only modestly when the state is fully visible, but under partial observability it yields much stronger and far more stable returns, lower seed variance, more reliable tumor suppression, and better preservation of normal cells. The result points toward offline-friendly, memory-based clinical RL for noisy, incomplete patient monitoring.
papers.abstract: Chemotherapy dose optimization can be formulated as a dynamic treatment regime, requiring sequential decisions under uncertainty that must balance tumor suppression against toxicity. However, most reinforcement learning approaches assume full observability of the patient state, a condition rarely met in clinical practice. We investigate whether memory-augmented policies can improve chemotherapy control under partial observability. To this end, we employ a recurrent TD3-based approach with separate LSTM actor-critic networks and evaluate it on the AhnChemoEnv benchmark from DTR-Bench, considering both off-policy and on-policy recurrent architectures against feed-forward TD3 and Soft Actor-Critic. Pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic variability are held fixed to isolate hidden-state uncertainty and observation noise and to avoid confounding effects from inter-patient variability. Across ten random seeds, recurrence yields modest benefit under full observability but substantially stronger and more stable performance under partial observability, with more consistent tumor suppression and improved normal-cell preservation. These findings indicate that memory-based policies are particularly beneficial when clinically relevant state information is incomplete or noisy.

papers.title: TemPose-TF-ASF: Two-Stage Bidirectional Stroke Context Fusion for Badminton Stroke Classification
papers.summary: TemPose-TF-ASF: Two-Stage Bidirectional Stroke Context Fusion for Badminton Stroke Classification tackles the failure of current badminton stroke recognizers to use the rally’s richer semantic context, where a stroke is shaped not only by prior shots but also by the tactical transition that follows. Built as an extension of TemPose-TF, the method adds Adjacent-Stroke Fusion (ASF), a lightweight embedding-and-MLP module that fuses predicted preceding and subsequent stroke types into an auxiliary context vector. Its key differentiator is a Two-Stage Contextual Refinement (TSCR) pipeline: stage 1 runs the base TemPose-TF with zeroed context to produce preliminary stroke labels, and stage 2 recycles those predictions as estimated neighboring-stroke context to refine classification, avoiding reliance on unavailable future ground truth at inference. Experiments on the cleaned ShuttleSet badminton dataset (40 matches, 33,429 shots, 31 classes) show consistent gains over TemPose-TF and multiple ASF variants, reaching 0.854 Accuracy and 0.761 Macro-F1, while plug-in versions also improve BlockGCN, SkateFormer, and BST-CG-AP. The paper points toward transferable, context-refinement-based sports action understanding rather than segment-isolated recognition.
papers.abstract: Accurate badminton stroke prediction is crucial for fine-grained sports analysis and tactical decision support. However, existing methods struggle to model rich temporal context. This paper introduces \emph{TemPose-TF-ASF (Adjacent-Stroke Fusion)}, a context-aware extension of \emph{TemPose}. It enhances stroke recognition by incorporating stroke-type information from both preceding and subsequent strokes. A two-stage training and inference strategy is adopted. Preliminary predictions from the baseline model are reused as estimated temporal context. These predictions guide the joint optimization of the \emph{ASF} module and the classifier. By explicitly modeling bidirectional temporal stroke dependencies, the proposed method can be seamlessly integrated into existing state-of-the-art models. Experiments on a large-scale badminton match dataset show consistent improvements over the baseline and its variants in terms of Accuracy and Macro-F1. Moreover, integrating \emph{ASF} into other advanced methods yields notable performance gains. These results demonstrate strong transferability and generalization capability.

papers.title: Low-Latency Embedded Driver Monitoring System with a Multi-Task Neural Network
papers.summary: Low-Latency Embedded Driver Monitoring System with a Multi-Task Neural Network tackles the bottleneck in camera-based driver monitoring: conventional pipelines need several models to estimate PERCLOS, yawning, head pose, and distractions, making real-time use on embedded automotive hardware difficult. The paper’s key idea is a lightweight MobileNetV2-style multi-task CNN that, in one forward pass, jointly regresses 98 facial landmarks, eye-opening and visibility for both eyes, mouth state, head yaw/pitch/roll, and classifies phone use versus smoking. Unlike nearby driver-monitoring work that stops at fatigue or gaze, this system explicitly adds distraction recognition and describes the full deployment stack: SSD-based face detection, SORT tracking to avoid per-frame detection, TensorRT-optimized NMS, temporal aggregation, and a finite-state-machine decision layer with calibration, safe, low-awareness, and danger states. Training is bootstrapped from LaPa face parsing plus pseudo-labeled distraction images (860 phone, 715 smoking), letting the model learn from weakly annotated data. Benchmarks on Jetson Nano and Xavier NX show the small model balancing accuracy and speed, with end-to-end latency down to 16.46 ms on Xavier NX when face detection is throttled, pointing toward practical, safety-critical edge DMS design.
papers.abstract: Road traffic accidents remain a significant global concern, with the majority attributed to human factors such as driver distraction and fatigue. This study proposes a camera-based approach to derive useful indicators to assess driver attentiveness and alertness. The proposed pipeline jointly satisfies the stringent real-time requirements imposed by the critical application and minimizes the computational requirements to allow for deployment on a tight computational budget. To this end, we develop a lightweight multi-task neural network that predicts multiple indicators for the face region in a single forward pass. The developed model is integrated into a complete execution workflow to produce a real-time estimate of attentiveness, fatigue, and engagement in distracting activities.

papers.title: Automated In-the-Wild Data Collection for Continual AI Generated Image Detection
papers.summary: Automated In-the-Wild Data Collection for Continual AI Generated Image Detection tackles the mismatch between static AI-generated-image detectors and a non-stationary world where new generators, post-processing, and news/social-media reuse rapidly shift the data distribution. The core idea is a data-centric continual adaptation loop: build fresh in-the-wild synthetic data automatically from fact-check articles, add small amounts of recent generator-driven samples, and train with replay to prevent forgetting. For WildFC, an LLM scans 3,841 fact-check articles, extracts descriptions of referenced AI images, and a VLM scores candidate images to find semantic anchors; CLIP-based similarity expansion and Grounding DINO segmentation then isolate 2,884 AI-generated instances with weak supervision, paired against 213,674 real images collected from news and social platforms. In parallel, AIGenImages2026 contributes 5,439 images from 19 recent text-to-image models. Unlike generator-only continual learning, the method explicitly models both generator evolution and real-world covariate shift. On RINE and SPAI, the combined pipeline improves average accuracy by +8.01% and +9.14%, showing that modest data refreshes plus replay can adapt detectors to both emerging models and in-the-wild content.
papers.abstract: The rapid advancement of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) has introduced significant challenges for reliable AI-generated image detection. Existing detectors often suffer from performance degradation under distribution shifts and when encountering newly emerging generative models. In this work, we propose a data-centric continual adaptation framework for updating detectors in evolving environments. We show that both in-the-wild data and generator-driven data are essential for adapting detectors. We introduce an automated, weakly supervised pipeline for constructing in-the-wild datasets through fact-check article retrieval. Additionally, we demonstrate that incorporating even a small amount of generator-driven data during training enables effective adaptation to newly emerging models, while combining it with in-the-wild data within a continual learning framework enables robust adaptation and mitigates catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments on two state-of-the-art detectors show significant improvements of +9.14% and +8% in average accuracy, respectively.

papers.title: StreamIndex: Memory-Bounded Compressed Sparse Attention via Streaming Top-k
