The core idea is to represent each pixel by a max-channel intensity envelope and three log-chromaticity ratios, so brightness lives in one scalar field while inter-channel color structure is bounded above by zero and anchored by a per-pixel zero channel. ICDNet then uses a dual-branch interaction backbone: one branch predicts intensity residuals, the other corrects chromaticity, with a learned intensity-aware chroma gate that downweights unreliable color cues in dark regions. Reconstruction is explicitly constrained to prevent positive chromaticity and abnormal channel amplification, suppressing chromatic noise instead of patching it afterward. Evaluated on LOLv2-Real, LOLv2-Syn, MIT-Adobe FiveK, and LSRW, the method reaches 29.71 dB PSNR and 0.8895 SSIM on LOLv2-Real and improves DarkFace detection, pointing toward representation-first LLIE with interpretable inverse mappings and downstream-aware enhancement.
papers.abstract: Explicit reconstruction constraints derived from the decoupled representation are further imposed to suppress abnormal channel amplification and chromatic noise. Experiments on LOLv2-Real, MIT-Adobe FiveK, and LSRW show that the proposed method achieves competitive or superior quantitative and visual performance, reaching 29.71 dB PSNR and 0.89 SSIM on LOLv2-Real. DarkFace experiments further indicate improved downstream face detection under low-light conditions. Code and pretrained models are available at: https://github.com/mubaisam/ICD.

papers.title: Neuromorphic Control for 3D Navigation in Minecraft Using Genetic Algorithms
papers.summary: Neuromorphic Control for 3D Navigation in Minecraft Using Genetic Algorithms tackles a very specific control bottleneck: getting a Minecraft parkour bot to learn frame-timed 3D movement and obstacle clearing, rather than only deterministic A* travel like Baritone. The agent is built in Mineflayer on a vanilla Minecraft Java 1.19.4 server and uses a compact 33-feature perceptual state derived from 19 forward raycasts plus goal offset, velocity, on-ground status, distance-to-goal, and an internal clock, feeding a single-hidden-layer 32-neuron MLP. Instead of backpropagation, the weights are evolved with a tournament-selected genetic algorithm, elitism, and per-gene Gaussian mutation; the action space is intentionally narrow, with sprint always on and outputs for jump, left/right strafe, and continuous yaw. A key differentiator is the diagnosis that fixed-course training caused motor-sequence memorization, so the paper embeds Continual Domain Randomization directly in Minecraft via command blocks that regenerate block-gap layouts after death thresholds. Although parallel evaluation was defeated by network jitter, serialized trials produced stable fitness. The result is a bot that can generalize from mastered courses to unseen gap patterns, pointing toward live, sim-to-real-inspired curriculum control in interactive game engines.
papers.abstract: The popular 2009 voxel based videogame, Minecraft, contains several distinct disciplines. One of which is "parkour," gameplay that focuses on traversing a world's environment with maximum efficiency. The Minecraft online community has turned the game's physics engine into dynamic puzzles, requiring players to masterfully manipulate motion mechanics through frame precise timing of keystrokes. Actions such as sprinting, sneaking, and mouse direction are all combined to clear specific difficult jumps. Through this project, we design a genetic algorithm to generate weights for a neural network to autonomously evaluate inputs for block distances, terrain, and obstacles to determine the most optimal pathing.

papers.title: Mapping Discourse Reframing: A Multi-Layer Network Approach to Italian HPV Vaccine Discourse on X (2010-2024)
papers.summary: Mapping Discourse Reframing: A Multi-Layer Network Approach to Italian HPV Vaccine Discourse on X (2010-2024) tackles a specific signal-loss problem in longitudinal misinformation analysis: conservative network filters often erase early, low-frequency hashtags that later become central to reframed health narratives. Using 14 years of Italian X posts about HPV vaccination, split into three epochs (2010–2014, 2015–2019, 2020–2024), the paper models hashtags as cultural markers in weighted co-occurrence graphs and builds a dual-layer method. A core layer keeps only robust ties (edge weight ≥2, k-core ≥2) and extracts communities with hierarchical Girvan-Newman detection, while a coverage layer retains all co-occurrences (weight ≥1) to preserve fringe material. The key differentiator is the core–coverage projection, which assigns peripheral hashtags to the most connected core coalition, making reframing visible rather than filtering it away. With 86 manually labeled skeptical/conspiratorial seed tweets and a weakly supervised seed lexicon, the method recovers long-tail problematic hashtags better than a single-layer core graph. The discourse evolves from a prevention-oriented backbone to separable skepticism coalitions and finally to an internationalized anti-vaccine core, pointing toward more interpretable, diachronic mapping of information disorder.
papers.abstract: Understanding how online narratives travel through coalitions is critical for identifying information disorder, yet computational analyses often rely on conservative network constructions that erase initially sparse but salient signals. This paper proposes a novel multi-layer framework that captures low-frequency signals of emerging information disorder allowing for locating where online discourse is reframed and amplified over time. The use case is 14 years of Italian discourse on X regarding the Human Papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine across three pivotal epochs (2010-2024). Utilizing hashtag co-occurrence networks, we introduce a dual-layer approach. We first identify robust core discourse coalitions through conservative community detection, revealing a stable prevention-oriented backbone contrasted with increasingly separable skepticism coalitions. We then introduce a coverage layer and project fringe hashtags into core coalitions based on weighted connectivity. Using a manually labelled set of skeptical and conspiratorial seed tweets, we demonstrate that this core-coverage projection significantly improves the recovery of long-tail, problematic hashtags while preserving an interpretable coalition structure. Our findings characterize the structural maturation of polarized narratives and provide a methodology for mapping how discourse is reframed and amplified by information disorder over time.

papers.title: AutoFocus: Uncertainty-Aware Active Visual Search for GUI Grounding
papers.summary: AutoFocus: Uncertainty-Aware Active Visual Search for GUI Grounding tackles the resolution bottleneck that makes VLM-based GUI agents miss tiny icons and dense widgets on 1080p/4K screens, where a single-pass coordinate decode is often semantically right but spatially wrong. Its unusual move is to treat token-level perplexity during coordinate generation as an intrinsic uncertainty signal, sample multiple coordinate hypotheses, and convert axial perplexities into an anisotropic Gaussian spatial field rather than using fixed anchors, heuristic grids, or RL-trained crops. That field drives Gaussian Dynamic Focusing: error-triggered self-verification decides whether refinement is needed, then global and local region proposals are built, with Shape-Aware Zooming interpolating between tight rectangular crops and square context-preserving views. Refined predictions from each region are projected back to screen space and resolved by a visual prompt-based aggregation step that compares annotated candidate images and selects the most consistent target. Evaluated training-free on ScreenSpot-Pro and ScreenSpot-V2 with Qwen2.5-VL, UI-TARS, GTA1, and UI-Venus, AutoFocus consistently improves grounding, reaching 67.8% on ScreenSpot-Pro with UI-Venus-7B and 95.5% on ScreenSpot-V2 with Qwen2.5-VL-72B, pointing toward test-time, uncertainty-driven visual search for GUI agents.
papers.abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled autonomous GUI agents that translate natural language instructions into executable screen coordinates. However, grounding performance degrades in high-resolution interfaces, where dense layouts and small interactive elements expose a resolution gap between modern displays and model input constraints. Existing zoom-in strategies rely on fixed anchors, heuristic grids, or reinforcement learning, lacking a principled mechanism to adaptively determine where refinement is needed and how much spatial uncertainty should be explored. We propose AutoFocus, a training-free, uncertainty-aware active visual search framework for GUI grounding. Our key insight is that token-level perplexity in coordinate generation naturally reflects spatial uncertainty. Rather than committing to a single prediction, AutoFocus samples multiple coordinate hypotheses and converts their axial perplexities into an anisotropic gaussian spatial probability field, explicitly modeling directional uncertainty. Based on this field, we generate global and local region proposals and introduce Shape-Aware Zooming to balance tight localization with contextual preservation. A visual prompt-based aggregation step then selects the most consistent prediction via structured comparison. Extensive experiments on ScreenSpot-Pro and ScreenSpot-V2 demonstrate consistent improvements across both general-purpose and GUI-specialized VLMs.

papers.title: CNNs for Vis-NIR Chemometrics: From Contradiction to Conditional Design
papers.summary: CNNs for Vis-NIR Chemometrics: From Contradiction to Conditional Design argues that the field’s contradictory CNN recipes—small versus large kernels, shallow versus deep stacks, raw spectra versus preprocessing, and single-domain training versus transfer learning—are not random failures but expected outcomes when spectral physics and evaluation protocol are left uncontrolled. The paper’s core mechanism is a conditional design framework built from three moderators: indirect Vis–NIR measurement in water-dominated matrices, the mismatch between effective receptive field and the width of informative spectral structure, and validation design as a hidden hyperparameter that can dominate model ranking. Rather than introducing a new network, it synthesizes published chemometrics and spectroscopy evidence, especially fruit-quality/soluble-solids prediction, portable-instrument transfer, and seasonally blocked or external validation settings. What differentiates it from nearby CNN-for-spectra work is its insistence that preprocessing, kernel scale, depth, dilation, and transfer strategy be chosen relative to deployment scenario and confound structure, not by generic templates. It concludes that reproducible Vis–NIR DL research should report blocked splits, tuning budgets, uncertainty, and faithfulness checks, pushing chemometrics toward physics-aware, shift-aware, and mechanism-testable model selection.
papers.abstract: Near-infrared (NIR; a.k.a.\ NIRS) deep-learning studies in chemometrics increasingly report mutually inconsistent conclusions regarding convolutional neural network (CNN) design, including small versus large kernels, shallow versus deep architectures, raw spectra versus preprocessing, and single-domain training versus transfer learning. As a result, the same architecture can appear superior in one study and inferior in another, creating a practical impasse for chemometric practitioners. In this review, we argue that these contradictions are not evidence of irreconcilable methods but a structurally expected consequence of uncontrolled moderating variables. Specifically, we trace recurring disagreements to (i) the indirect nature of Vis--NIR measurement in water-dominated matrices, (ii) mismatch between effective receptive field (ERF) and the width of informative spectral structure, and (iii) validation design (including split strategy, hyperparameter tuning budget, and exposure to deployment-like shifts) acting as a hidden hyperparameter that can dominate model ranking. Building on evidence from published chemometrics and spectroscopy studies, we propose a conditional design framework that links architecture and preprocessing choices to spectral physics, dataset regime, and intended deployment scenario. Overall, the proposed perspective moves DL Chemometrics from template-driven architecture selection toward reproducible, physics-aware, and deployment-aligned model comparison.

papers.title: ViewSAM: Learning View-aware Cross-modal Semantics for Weakly Supervised Cross-view Referring Multi-Object Tracking
papers.summary: ViewSAM: Learning View-aware Cross-modal Semantics for Weakly Supervised Cross-view Referring Multi-Object Tracking targets the annotation bottleneck in CRMOT, where language-specified objects must be tracked with globally consistent IDs across synchronized cameras but prior methods need dense frame boxes and cross-view identity labels. The paper shows that naively prompting SAM2/SAM3 fails at both referring understanding and identity preservation, so it reuses foundation models as pseudo-label generators instead of end-to-end solvers. Stage 1 starts from SAM3 tracklets driven only by coarse object-category labels, then applies an affinity-guided cross-view re-prompting loop plus bi-directional refinement to associate trajectories across views; Qwen3-VL-generated attribute captions further group trajectories into referring-level pseudo labels. Stage 2 builds ViewSAM on frozen SAM2 with only ~10% extra parameters, adding a learnable dynamic View Token, view-conditioned cross-modal alignment, bias-aware recalibration to recover from memory drift under occlusion, and a consistency-guided cross-view tracking head that learns view-invariant identity embeddings. On CRTrack, evaluated by CVR-IDF1 and CVR-MA, it reaches state-of-the-art weakly supervised performance and stays competitive with fully supervised CRMOT, pointing toward scalable multi-camera tracking from weak labels and foundation-model pseudo supervision.
papers.abstract: Cross-view Referring Multi-Object Tracking (CRMOT) aims to track multiple objects specified by natural language across multiple camera views, with globally consistent identities. Despite recent progress, existing methods rely heavily on costly frame-level spatial annotations and cross-view identity supervision. To reduce such reliance, we explore CRMOT under weak supervision by leveraging the capabilities of foundation models. However, our empirical study shows that directly applying foundation models such as SAM2 and SAM3, even with task-specific modifications, fails to accurately understand referring expressions and maintain consistent identities across views. Yet, they remain effective at producing reliable object tracklets that can serve as pseudo supervision. We therefore repurpose foundation models as pseudo-label generators and propose a two-stage framework for weakly supervised CRMOT, using only object category labels as coarse-grained supervision. In the first stage, we design an Affinity-guided Cross-view Re-prompting strategy to refine and associate SAM3-generated tracklets across cameras, producing reliable cross-view pseudo labels for subsequent training. In the second stage, we introduce ViewSAM, a CRMOT model built upon SAM2 that explicitly models view-aware cross-modal semantics. By formulating view-induced variations as learnable conditions, ViewSAM bridges the gap between view-variant visual observations and view-invariant textual expressions, enabling robust cross-view referring tracking with only approximately 10% additional parameters. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ViewSAM achieves SOTA performance under weak supervision and remains competitive with fully supervised methods.

papers.title: Trustworthy AI Suffers from Invariance Conflicts and Causality is The Solution
papers.summary: Trustworthy AI Suffers from Invariance Conflicts and Causality is The Solution argues that fairness, robustness, privacy, and explainability are not independent add-ons but competing invariance requirements over different interventions on the data-generating process, so utility loss is structurally baked in when they are enforced uniformly. The paper’s core move is to reinterpret each trustworthy objective as a stability constraint: fairness demands invariance to protected-attribute interventions, privacy to neighboring-dataset changes, robustness to environment shifts, and explainability to semantically meaningful perturbations. In a healthcare readmission SCM with age, insurance, lab results, and hospital environment, the authors separate admissible causal paths from unfair or spurious ones and show how selective invariance can block direct and proxy discrimination while preserving legitimate clinical signal. Unlike nearby fairness or robustness work that optimizes one constraint at a time, this position paper treats causality as the unifying language for multi-objective trade-offs across classical ML and foundation models, via explicit SCMs, causal graphs, counterfactual augmentation, causal regularization, and causal alignment. Its implication is a shift from observational accuracy toward interventional validity, path-specific control, and hybrid causal design for high-stakes deployment.
papers.abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI), including machine learning (ML) models and foundation models (FMs), is increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains, ensuring their trustworthiness has become a central challenge. However, the core trustworthy AI objectives, such as fairness, robustness, privacy, and explainability, are hard to achieve simultaneously, especially while preserving utility. This position paper argues that causality is necessary to understand and balance trade-offs in performance and multiple objectives of trustworthy AI. We ground our arguments in re-interpreting trustworthy AI trade-offs as incompatible invariance requirements under different changes to the data-generating process. We then illustrate that causality provides a unifying framework for understanding how trade-offs in trustworthy AI arise, and how they can be softened or resolved through selective invariance. This perspective applies to both classical ML models and large-scale FMs. Our paper discusses how causal assumptions may be applied explicitly or implicitly in modern large-scale systems. Finally, we outline open challenges and opportunities for using causality to build more trustworthy AI.

papers.title: Mamoda2.5: Enhancing Unified Multimodal Model with DiT-MoE
papers.summary: Mamoda2.5: Enhancing Unified Multimodal Model with DiT-MoE attacks the compute bottleneck that has kept unified video understanding, generation, and editing from scaling like text models: dense DiTs are too expensive over long spatiotemporal token sequences, yet video editing also needs strong instruction fidelity and background consistency. The paper’s core mechanism is an AR-Diffusion system in which an autoregressive understanding backbone emits MetaQueries into a Diffusion Transformer backbone upgraded to fine-grained MoE, with 128 routed experts and Top-8 token routing, yielding 25B total parameters but only about 3B active per forward pass. It further reduces token cost with a 3D causal 4×16×16 VAE, uses in-context conditioning for unified image/video generation and editing, and adds a ByT5 encoder to improve character-level text rendering. A three-stage dense-to-MoE upcycling scheme and a joint few-step distillation plus DiffusionNFT reinforcement-learning loop compress the 30-step editor into a 4-step, CFG-free model. Evaluated on VBench 2.0, OpenVE-Bench, FiVE-Bench, and real ad-editing deployment, it matches or beats strong proprietary systems and enables up to 95.9× faster editing, pointing toward sparse, deployable multimodal foundation models.
papers.abstract: We present Mamoda2.5, a unified AR-Diffusion framework that seamlessly integrates multimodal understanding and generation within a single architecture. To efficiently enhance the model's generation capability, we equip the Diffusion Transformer backbone with a fine-grained Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) design (128 experts, Top-8 routing), yielding a 25B-parameter model that activates only 3B parameters, significantly reducing training costs while scaling up the model capacity. Mamoda2.5 achieves top-tier generation performance on VBench 2.0 and sets a new record in video editing quality, surpassing evaluated open-source models and matching the performance of current top-tier proprietary models, including the Kling O1 on OpenVE-Bench. Furthermore, we introduce a joint few-step distillation and reinforcement learning framework that compresses the 30-step editing model into a 4-step model and greatly accelerates model inference. Compared to open-source baselines, Mamoda2.5 achieves up to $95.9\times$ faster video editing inference. In real-world applications, Mamoda2.5 has been successfully deployed for content moderation and creative restoration tasks in advertising scenarios, achieving a 98% success rate in internal advertising video editing scenario.

papers.title: ContextualJailbreak: Evolutionary Red-Teaming via Simulated Conversational Priming
papers.summary: ContextualJailbreak: Evolutionary Red-Teaming via Simulated Conversational Priming attacks a gap in automated jailbreak research: most black-box optimizers still mutate a single prompt, even though hand-built multi-turn priming often bypasses safety more effectively than direct requests. The paper turns the entire simulated dialogue into the search object, then runs evolutionary fuzzing over role-labeled conversations that end in the harmful request. Search is guided by a two-level judge: a barrier model filters clear refusals, while a main judge assigns a graded 0–5 harm score so partially harmful outputs can seed later generations instead of being discarded. The mutator set is semantically named rather than token-level, using roleplay, scenario, expand, troubleshooting, and mechanistic; the last two are novel and are shown to drive the strongest progress, especially troubleshooting on the hardest targets. On 50 HarmBench behaviors, with qwen3-8B as mutator and judge, the method reaches 100% ASR@4 on gpt-oss:20B, qwen3-8B, and llama3.1:70B, and 90% on gpt-oss:120B, beating DirectRequest, Encoding, HumanJailbreak, and Crescendo by 31–96 points. The transferred attacks generalize strongly to gpt-4o-mini, gpt-5, and gemini-3-flash, but much less to Claude, pointing toward context-aware defenses that model conversational trajectory, not just prompt strings.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that bypass safety alignment and elicit harmful responses. A growing body of work shows that contextual priming, where earlier turns covertly bias later replies, constitutes a powerful attack surface, with hand-crafted multi-turn scaffolds consistently outperforming single-turn manipulations on capable models. However, automated optimization-based red-teaming has remained largely limited to the single-turn setting, iterating over static prompts and lacking the ability to reason about which forms of conversational priming induce compliance. While recent multi-turn, search-based approaches have begun to bridge this gap, the mutator design space underlying effective primed dialogues remains largely unexplored. We present ContextualJailbreak, a black-box red-teaming strategy that performs evolutionary search over a simulated multi-turn primed dialogue. The strategy leverages a graded 0-5 harm score from a two-level judge as an in-loop signal, enabling partially harmful responses to guide the search process rather than being discarded. Search is driven by five semantically defined mutation operators: roleplay, scenario, expand, troubleshooting, and mechanistic, of which the last two are novel contributions of this work. Across 50 representative HarmBench behaviors, ContextualJailbreak achieves an ASR of 100% on gpt-oss:20B, 100% on qwen3-8B, 100% on llama3.1:70B, and 90% on gpt-oss:120B, outperforming four single- and multi-turn baselines by 31-96 percentage points on average. The 40 maximally harmful attacks discovered against gpt-oss:120B transfer without adaptation to closed frontier models, achieving 90.0% on gpt-4o-mini, 70.0% on gpt-5, and 70.0% on gemini-3-flash, but only 17.5% on claude-opus-4-7 and 15.0% on claude-sonnet-4-6, revealing a pronounced provider-level asymmetry in alignment robustness.

papers.title: ARA: Agentic Reproducibility Assessment For Scalable Support Of Scientific Peer-Review
papers.summary: ARA: Agentic Reproducibility Assessment For Scalable Support Of Scientific Peer-Review tackles the bottleneck that human reviewers cannot reliably reconstruct modern papers’ hidden experimental dependencies, data flows, parameter choices, and result-producing steps at scale. ARA converts a paper into a directed workflow graph with four node stages—sources, methods, experiments, and sinks—using schema-constrained LLM queries grounded in literal quotes, then scores reconstructability with both a 4-level micro rubric and two orthogonal aggregate signals: content completeness and graph structure. Unlike checklist-based reproducibility screeners or code-reproduction agents that depend on repositories, ARA reasons directly over the publication text and judges whether the workflow is sufficiently specified and wired to be re-created. The paper evaluates this on 213 ReScience C articles, the largest cross-domain benchmark of human-validated computational reproductions considered here, and checks stability across Gemini, GPT, Qwen, and decoding temperatures. ARA remains consistent across runs and reaches about 61% accuracy overall, including the best reported scores on ReproBench and GoldStandardDB, pointing toward document-level, agentic infrastructure that can complement peer review with scalable reproducibility diagnostics.
papers.abstract: Scientific peer review increasingly struggles to assess reproducibility at the scale and complexity of modern research output. Evaluating reproducibility requires reconstructing experimental dependencies, methodological choices, data flows, and result-generating procedures, which often exceeds what human reviewers can provide. Agentic Reproducibility Assessment (ARA) formalizes reproducibility assessment as a structured reasoning task over scientific documents. Given a paper, ARA extracts a directed workflow graph linking sources, methods, experiments, and outputs, then evaluates its reconstructability using structural and content-based scores for reproducibility assessments. Experiments on 213 ReScience C articles - the largest cross-domain benchmark of human-validated computational reproducibility studies considered to date - demonstrate ARA's generalizability and consistent workflow reconstruction and assessment across LLMs, model temperatures, and scientific domains. ARA achieves ~61% accuracy on three benchmarks, and the highest accuracy reported on ReproBench (60.71% vs. 36.84%) and GoldStandardDB (61.68% vs. 43.56%), highlighting its potential to complement human review at scale and enabling next-generation peer review. Code and Data available: https://github.com/AndresLaverdeMarin/agentic_reproducibility_assessment.

papers.title: CARD: Coarse-to-fine Autoregressive Modeling with Radix-based Decomposition for Transferable Free Energy Estimation
papers.summary: CARD: Coarse-to-fine Autoregressive Modeling with Radix-based Decomposition for Transferable Free Energy Estimation tackles the bottleneck that classical free-energy estimators need expensive MD sampling and that prior deep-learning proposals such as zero-free-energy normalizing-flow methods are expressive but usually system-specific, tied to fixed atom counts, and weak on unseen topologies. CARD turns aligned 3D molecular coordinates into a bijective mixed discrete-continuous sequence via radix-based decomposition, then models that sequence autoregressively with a transformer so atoms are generated coarse-to-fine: early radix digits capture global geometry and later continuous residuals refine local detail. The model keeps tractable likelihoods and is paired with PCA alignment, topology-guided or distance-based atom ordering, geometry-aware attention that conditions on reference conformers, and a Beta mixture model for bounded residuals. Unlike nearby graph-hierarchical or diffusion-based molecular generators, CARD operates directly at coordinate level while preserving exact density factorization and zero free energy, making it usable as a proposal for BAR/MBAR absolute free-energy estimation without alchemical intermediates. On solvation, MM-to-ANI-2x correction, and tautomer benchmarks, it generalizes to unseen systems, matches classical accuracy, and cuts inference cost by about 40×, pointing toward universal generative force fields for chemistry.
papers.abstract: Estimating free energy differences quantifies thermodynamic preferences in molecular interactions, which is central to chemistry and drug discovery. Despite fruitful progress, existing methods still face key limitations: classical computational approaches remain prohibitively expensive due to their reliance on extensive molecular dynamics simulations, while deep learning-based methods are constrained by either less-expressive generative models or input dimensions tied to a specific system, resulting in negligible generalization. To address these challenges, we propose CARD, a generative framework that employs a novel radix-based decomposition to bijectively convert 3D coordinates into mixed discrete-continuous sequences, enabling coarse-to-fine autoregressive modeling with enhanced expressiveness. Notably, the model corresponds to a distribution with zero free energy, serving as a proposal for absolute free energy computation of arbitrary systems without relying on alchemical pathways. Experiments across diverse tasks demonstrate that CARD matches the accuracy of classical computational methods on unseen systems with diverse topologies, while achieving an approximately 40-fold speedup in inference.

papers.title: Human Activity Recognition Method for Moderate Violence Detection
papers.summary: Human Activity Recognition Method for Moderate Violence Detection tackles the narrow but operationally important problem of spotting “pushing” as an early-stage violent act in public surveillance streams, where human operators miss brief, low-intensity confrontations and where steep camera angles, distance, occlusion, and noise break ordinary action recognition. The method is a hierarchical pipeline: YOLO11 first detects people and assigns track IDs, YOLO11-Pose extracts 17 skeletal keypoints, and a feature stage computes torso inclination plus shoulder–hip and arm-related joint angles from vector dot products to encode the biomechanics of an aggressive shove. Those relative pose descriptors, rather than raw frames or end-to-end temporal transformers, feed a Random Forest binary classifier that separates normal motion from push events and explicitly only reasons over interactions between two distinct people. The paper tests three progressively harder surveillance settings, from close frontal views to high-altitude, long-range real-world footage; precision reaches 0.98 in controlled scenes and remains 0.72 in the most distorted scenario. The result argues for lightweight, edge-deployable pose-based violence interception and points toward richer temporal modeling for robust urban security monitoring.
papers.abstract: Physical violence in public spaces is a significant public health concern, with minor incidents such as pushing often serving as precursors to more severe escalations. This research develops an automated system for the real-time detection of moderate physical violence, specifically pushing, in surveillance camera footage. The proposed solution integrates state-of-the-art computer vision models, utilizing YOLO11 and YOLO11-Pose for human detection and skeletal keypoint extraction. By calculating body inclination and joint angles between shoulders and hips, a Random Forest classifier was trained to distinguish between normal behavior and aggressive physical contact. The system's performance was evaluated through three progressive case studies representing increasing levels of difficulty. In controlled environments with frontal views, the model achieved a precision of 0.98. In the most challenging scenario, featuring high-altitude, steep-angle recordings from real-world surveillance infrastructure, the system maintained a precision of 0.72 despite significant perspective distortion and visual noise. These results demonstrate the feasibility of using skeletal analysis for early violence intervention in urban security contexts.

papers.title: Deciphering Shortcut Learning from an Evolutionary Game Theory Perspective
papers.summary: Deciphering Shortcut Learning from an Evolutionary Game Theory Perspective tackles the missing theory of why deep nets latch onto spurious cues, rather than just documenting that they do. The paper first formalizes task-relevant features into core, shortcut, and noise categories, then models training as an evolutionary game where each data sample is a player and its neural tangent feature is a strategy competing to steer optimization toward either a core subnetwork or a shortcut subnetwork. This is unusual relative to nearby shortcut-learning work because it does not freeze features in an NTK view; instead it treats tangent features as evolving populations with payoffs equal to loss reduction. Under this framework, the authors derive distinct stochastically stable states for full-batch gradient descent and mini-batch SGD: GD tends to reinforce shortcut dominance, while SGD’s sampling noise can stabilize core-feature learning. A continuous stochastic differential equation further links data noise and optimization noise to shortcut-bias formation. The main implication is a dynamic, game-theoretic route to mitigating shortcut learning through stochastic optimization design and noise control.
papers.abstract: Shortcut learning causes deep learning models to rely on non-essential features within the data. However, its formation in deep neural network training still lacks theoretical understanding. In this paper, we provide a formal definition of core and shortcut features and employ evolutionary game theory to analyze the origins of shortcut bias by modeling data samples as players and their corresponding neural tangent features as strategies, assuming the existence of core and shortcut subnetworks. We find that gradient descent (GD) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) lead to two distinct stochastically stable states, each corresponding to a different strategy. The former primarily optimizes the shortcut subnetwork, while the latter primarily optimizes the core subnetwork. We investigate the influence of these strategies on shortcut bias through a continuous stochastic differential equation, and reveal the impact of data noise and optimization noise on the formation of shortcut bias. In brief, our work employs evolutionary game theory to characterize the dynamics of shortcut bias formation and provides a theoretical view on its mitigation.

papers.title: Biological Spatial Priors Regularize Foundation Model Representations for Cross-Site MSI Generalization in Colorectal Cancer
papers.summary: Biological Spatial Priors Regularize Foundation Model Representations for Cross-Site MSI Generalization in Colorectal Cancer tackles a pathology domain-shift problem: MSI prediction from routine H&E whole-slide images works on the training institution but loses specificity when scanner, staining, and tissue-preparation statistics change across sites. The paper’s key idea is to inject biologically motivated tile-level spatial priors into a TransMIL aggregator before self-attention, so foundation model features must attend through known MSI histology rather than only appearance cues. The main prior, peripheral distance encoding, uses each tile’s proximity to the slide boundary as a proxy for the Crohn’s-like lymphocytic reaction at the tumor invasive margin; a second prior, local immune neighborhood encoding, logs the LYM/TUM ratio in nearby tiles. Unlike generic positional encodings, these priors encode disease anatomy. The method is tested on 137 TCGA-COAD slides for training/5-fold validation and 50 external TCGA-READ slides without retraining, using UNI2-h or Virchow2 features. Peripheral distance improves UNI2-h TransMIL to 0.959 MSI AUC and 1.000 MSS specificity on READ, outperforming the best reference and suggesting biologically grounded spatial regularization is a promising path toward site-invariant computational pathology.
papers.abstract: Predicting microsatellite instability (MSI) status from routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) whole slide images (WSIs) offers a practical alternative to molecular testing, but models trained at one institution tend to generalize poorly to slides acquired at a different site. Foundation model representations, despite their generality, still encode site-specific texture alongside the conserved biological morphology underlying MSI. We investigate whether tile-level spatial priors derived from known MSI histology can guide these representations toward more site-invariant features. We introduce a biologically motivated spatial prior based on peripheral distance encoding, reflecting the Crohn's-like peripheral lymphocytic reaction at the tumor invasive margin, and evaluate a secondary local immune neighborhood encoding reflecting the lymphocyte-to-tumor ratio in each tile's immediate spatial neighborhood. Both priors are injected into a TransMIL aggregator before self-attention, allowing the transformer to integrate spatial biological context with UNI2-h or Virchow2 features across all attention layers. We evaluate six foundation model and MIL aggregator combinations as a reference, then assess the effect of each spatial prior. Training on TCGA-COAD (137 slides) and evaluating externally on TCGA-READ (50 slides) without retraining, peripheral distance encoding achieves MSI AUC 0.959 +/- 0.012 on COAD and MSS specificity 1.000 on READ, compared to 0.957 and 0.939 for the strongest reference configuration. Local immune neighborhood encoding achieves comparable internal AUC but lower cross-site specificity, suggesting margin proximity encodes a more site-invariant biological signal than local immune density. Results suggest biologically grounded spatial priors act as regularizers that reduce reliance on site-specific imaging patterns.

papers.title: AcademiClaw: When Students Set Challenges for AI Agents
papers.summary: AcademiClaw: When Students Set Challenges for AI Agents addresses a blind spot in OpenClaw evaluation: existing benchmarks mostly test assistant chores, not the deep, messy academic workflows where agents actually fail. The paper builds a bilingual benchmark of 80 long-horizon tasks scraped from 230 student submissions and filtered by expert review, covering homework, research projects, olympiad problems, full-stack debugging, and GPU-heavy ML work; 16 tasks require CUDA execution and all run inside isolated Docker sandboxes. Its differentiator is bottom-up task sourcing from real student pain points plus a multi-dimensional scoring stack that combines deterministic checks, code execution, LLM-as-judge, vision judging, browser testing, and structured-output validation, alongside a five-category safety audit. Testing six frontier models in the OpenClaw agent framework shows the best system reaches only 55% pass rate, with 23 tasks defeating every model. The analysis exposes sharp domain boundaries, model-specific behavioral phenotypes, and almost zero correlation between token use and score, implying that more deliberation is not equivalent to better reasoning. AcademiClaw points toward agent research that is ecologically grounded, domain-broad, and safety-aware rather than optimized for narrow assistant routines.
papers.abstract: Benchmarks within the OpenClaw ecosystem have thus far evaluated exclusively assistant-level tasks, leaving the academic-level capabilities of OpenClaw largely unexamined. We introduce AcademiClaw, a bilingual benchmark of 80 complex, long-horizon tasks sourced directly from university students' real academic workflows -- homework, research projects, competitions, and personal projects -- that they found current AI agents unable to solve effectively. Curated from 230 student-submitted candidates through rigorous expert review, the final task set spans 25+ professional domains, ranging from olympiad-level mathematics and linguistics problems to GPU-intensive reinforcement learning and full-stack system debugging, with 16 tasks requiring CUDA GPU execution. Each task executes in an isolated Docker sandbox and is scored on task completion by multi-dimensional rubrics combining six complementary techniques, with an independent five-category safety audit providing additional behavioral analysis. Experiments on six frontier models show that even the best achieves only a 55\% pass rate. Further analysis uncovers sharp capability boundaries across task domains, divergent behavioral strategies among models, and a disconnect between token consumption and output quality, providing fine-grained diagnostic signals beyond what aggregate metrics reveal. We hope that AcademiClaw and its open-sourced data and code can serve as a useful resource for the OpenClaw community, driving progress toward agents that are more capable and versatile across the full breadth of real-world academic demands. All data and code are available at https://github.com/GAIR-NLP/AcademiClaw.

papers.title: Fuzzy Fingerprinting Encoder Pre-trained Language Models for Emotion Recognition in Conversations: Human Assessment and Validity Study
papers.summary: Fuzzy Fingerprinting Encoder Pre-trained Language Models for Emotion Recognition in Conversations: Human Assessment and Validity Study tackles a specific ERC bottleneck: RoBERTa-style encoders are strong on DailyDialog but collapse minority emotions into the dominant Neutral class and cannot justify their predictions. The paper keeps a context-dependent PLM encoder, but replaces the usual classifier with Fuzzy Fingerprints built from the pooled [CLS] activations: training embeddings are aggregated per emotion, ranked by latent-feature salience, pruned to top-k cells, and fuzzified into class prototypes whose overlap with a test utterance fingerprint is scored by fuzzy-set intersection similarity. This is unusual compared with nearby ERC work because the decision rule is prototype matching in latent space rather than a learned softmax boundary, making class evidence inspectable and instance-level explainable. On DailyDialog’s highly imbalanced 7-label setup, the method reduces Neutral overclassification and reaches 51.89 macro-F1 at K=300, close to the baseline’s 51.23 while remaining lightweight. A human validity study on 69 baseline-vs-FFP disagreements, judged by three computational linguists, favored the FFP label 59% of the time (Fleiss’ κ=0.47), suggesting a path toward human-aligned, interpretable ERC through fuzzy prototype learning.
papers.abstract: In Emotion Recognition in Conversations (ERC), model decisions should align with nuanced human perception and ideally provide insights on the classification process. Standard encoder pre-trained language models (PLMs) are the state-of-the-art at these tasks but offer little insight into why a certain prediction is made. This is especially problematic in imbalanced datasets, where most utterances are labeled as neutral, making these models frequently misclassify minority emotions as the majority neutral class. To tackle this issue, we introduced a novel, interpretable approach to ERC by combining PLMs with Fuzzy Fingerprints (FFPs). FFP provide class-specific prototypes that reflect the characteristic class activation patterns in the PLM's latent space. They are derived by ranking and fuzzifying the activations of the pooled conversational context-dependent embeddings across training instances for each emotion. At inference time, each input utterance is similarly fuzzy fingerprinted and matched to the emotion prototypes using a fuzzy similarity function based on the aggregation of the intersection of the fuzzy sets that define each FFP. Experimental results show that FFP integration reduces overclassification into the neutral class and human evaluation further supports the adequacy of FFP predictions. Our proposed method thus bridges the gap between deep neural inference and human perception, performing at state-of-the-art level while simultaneously offering valuable insights into the classification procedure.

papers.title: AnchorD: Metric Grounding of Monocular Depth Using Factor Graphs
papers.summary: AnchorD: Metric Grounding of Monocular Depth Using Factor Graphs targets the robotics failure mode where RGB-D sensors become unreliable on transparent, specular, and otherwise non-Lambertian surfaces, while monocular depth foundation models remain dense but mis-scaled in metric units. The key idea is a training-free factor-graph optimization that fuses raw sensor depth with off-the-shelf monocular predictions by jointly solving for dense depth and patch-wise affine alignment parameters, rather than applying a single global scale. Unary sensor factors anchor valid measurements, ternary monocular factors tie each patch to the foundation-model prior, and logarithmic slope factors preserve relative geometry across missing or corrupted regions; a Gaussian-weighted smoothing step removes patch seams without retraining. To benchmark the problem realistically, the paper introduces SprayD, a real-world dataset with dense scene-wide ground truth obtained by matte diffuser spray plus multi-camera fusion, avoiding object-only CAD supervision. Across SprayD, ClearPose, and ScanNet++ aggregation, AnchorD consistently improves MAE/RMSE/REL and yields denser, more geometrically coherent reconstructions, pointing toward zero-shot optimization-based depth grounding for manipulation and navigation.
papers.abstract: Dense and accurate depth estimation is essential for robotic manipulation, grasping, and navigation, yet currently available depth sensors are prone to errors on transparent, specular, and general non-Lambertian surfaces. To mitigate these errors, large-scale monocular depth estimation approaches provide strong structural priors, but their predictions can be potentially skewed or mis-scaled in metric units, limiting their direct use in robotics. Thus, in this work, we propose a training-free depth grounding framework that anchors monocular depth estimation priors from a depth foundation model in raw sensor depth through factor graph optimization. Our method performs a patch-wise affine alignment, locally grounding monocular predictions in metric real-world depth while preserving fine-grained geometric structure and discontinuities. To facilitate evaluation in challenging real-world conditions, we introduce a benchmark dataset with dense scene-wide ground truth depth in the presence of non-Lambertian objects. Ground truth is obtained via matte reflection spray and multi-camera fusion, overcoming the reliance on object-only CAD-based annotations used in prior datasets. Extensive evaluations across diverse sensors and domains demonstrate consistent improvements in depth performance without any (re-)training. We make our implementation publicly available at https://anchord.cs.uni-freiburg.de.

papers.title: An explainable hypothesis-driven approach to Drug-Induced Liver Injury with HADES
papers.summary: An explainable hypothesis-driven approach to Drug-Induced Liver Injury with HADES reframes DILI from a brittle binary label task into mechanistic hypothesis generation for late-stage attrition compounds, where the real bottleneck is not just ranking risk but explaining whether a molecule, metabolite, or pathway is causally responsible. The paper introduces DILER Benchmark, a literature-grounded dataset of 223 test compounds plus 34 post-2021 molecules annotated with 929 stepwise hepatotoxicity or safety hypotheses, each tied to LiverTox-style severity and often a follow-up assay. HADES is an agentic LangGraph pipeline that first uses anonymized, tool-driven reasoning on the query structure—Boltz-1 embeddings, similarity retrieval, metabolite generation via BioTransformer, and an AOP predictor over MIE/KE proxies—then hands a de-identified dossier to DeepResearch for cited literature synthesis, preventing name-based recall and evidence contamination. Unlike standard QSAR/GNN DILI models or frontier LLMs that memorize drug profiles, HADES is evaluated on both ROC-AUC and hypothesis alignment. It reaches 0.68 ROC-AUC on the held-out test set and 0.59 on the post-2021 set, while establishing a mechanistic alignment baseline (fuzzy Jaccard 0.16), pointing predictive toxicology toward auditable, pathway-level, agentic reasoning.
papers.abstract: Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) remains a leading cause of late-stage clinical trial attrition. However, existing computational predictors primarily rely on binary classification, a framing that limits generalization and yields no mechanistic insight to guide translational decisions. We argue that DILI prediction is better posed as an explainable hypothesis-generation problem. To support this shift, we introduce the DILER Benchmark, a dataset that extends beyond binary labels by augmenting a curated set of molecules with mechanistic hepatotoxicity hypotheses derived from biomedical literature. We further present HADES, an agentic system designed to generate transparent and auditable reasoning traces. By combining molecular-level predictions, metabolite decomposition, structural understanding, and toxicity pathway evidence, HADES mechanistically assesses DILI risk. Evaluated on the DILER Benchmark, HADES outperforms existing models in binary classification, achieving a ROC-AUC of 0.68 on the Test Set and 0.59 on the challenging Post-2021 Set, compared with 0.63 and 0.50 for DILI-Predictor, respectively. More importantly, we establish a baseline for mechanistic hypothesis generation, where HADES achieves a Hypothesis Alignment Fuzzy Jaccard Index of 0.16. This result underscores the inherent complexity of the task while highlighting the need for advanced explainable approaches in predictive toxicology.

papers.title: The 2026 ACII Dyadic Conversations (DaiKon) Workshop & Challenge
papers.summary: The 2026 ACII Dyadic Conversations (DaiKon) Workshop & Challenge targets a blind spot in conversational affect research: most benchmarks score isolated speakers, while real dyads involve reciprocal influence, turn coordination, and rapport growth over time. It introduces three linked tasks on the shared Hume-DaiKon corpus of 945 naturalistic audiovisual conversations (743.4 hours) in German, English, Spanish, Dutch, and Polish: directional interpersonal influence prediction over 10 emotion dimensions, turn-taking prediction for next speaker and time-to-next-speech, and rapport trajectory prediction across full interactions. The benchmark is unusual in combining multilingual, face-to-face, dual-speaker recordings with fixed train/validation/test splits, standardized CCC/Pearson/Macro-F1/MAE evaluation, and released baselines built from Whisper-small audio embeddings and FaceNet video embeddings. Rapport supervision is especially distinctive: continuous window-level labels are pseudo-generated with Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct from transcripts plus acoustic-emotional tags, then smoothed into trajectories. Baselines show audio consistently outperforms video and naive multimodal pooling rarely helps, while best test scores remain modest, underscoring that directional dependence and long-horizon interpersonal dynamics are still hard and pointing the field toward dyad-aware, temporally grounded, culturally sensitive affect modeling.
papers.abstract: The 2026 ACII Dyadic Conversations (ACII-DaiKon) Workshop & Challenge introduces a benchmark for modeling interpersonal affect and social dynamics in dyadic conversations. Although conversational affect modeling has advanced rapidly, most benchmarks remain speaker-centric and underrepresent coupled, time-evolving processes between partners, including directional influence, conversational timing coordination, and rapport development. To address this gap, ACII-DaiKon presents three coordinated sub-challenges built on a shared dataset: (1) directional interpersonal influence prediction, (2) turn-taking prediction (next-speaker and time-to-next-speech), and (3) rapport trajectory prediction across full interactions. The challenge is built on the Hume-DaiKon dataset, comprising 945 dyadic conversations (743.4 hours of audiovisual data) collected under naturalistic conditions across five languages. The benchmark supports multimodal modeling, temporal reasoning, and cross-context generalization through fixed train/validation/test splits, standardized metrics, and released baseline systems. Evaluation uses Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC), Pearson correlation, Macro-F1, and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) depending on the sub-challenge. Baseline experiments establish initial reference performance, with best test results of 0.40 CCC and 0.50 Pearson for influence prediction, 0.66 Macro-F1 and 1.50~s MAE for turn-taking, and 0.68 CCC and 0.70 Pearson for rapport trajectory modeling. These results indicate that while current methods capture coarse dyadic patterns, robust modeling of directional dependence and long-horizon interpersonal dynamics remains challenging. The workshop provides a shared platform for rigorous comparison and cross-disciplinary discussion on data validity, evaluation protocols, and culturally aware modeling for dyadic interaction.

papers.title: Online Generalised Predictive Coding
papers.summary: Online Generalised Predictive Coding reframes real-time perception as online triple estimation: simultaneously tracking latent states, learning unknown parameters, and inferring state/observation precisions from each observation once, without revisiting past data. The key mechanism is Online Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (ODEM), a predictive-coding/DEM specialisation built on a separation of temporal scales: fast D-step belief updates over generalised coordinates of motion, plus slower E- and M-step updates that accumulate gradients over a window and refresh parameters and precisions only every inter-EM interval. Unlike particle or SMC schemes, ODEM keeps constant per-step cost and avoids closed-form transition densities by exploiting smooth higher-order derivatives of the trajectory. The paper validates this on a nonlinear, potentially chaotic Generalised Lotka-Volterra generative process with coloured state and observation noise, inverting both a matched GLV model and a mismatched Lorenz model across 1,512 prior/precision settings, precision ratios C, and motion orders kx=2,3. Results show that higher-order motion lets the Lorenz model track GLV data, with stable posterior precision estimates and improved state inference, pointing toward biologically inspired online inference for dynamic environments.
papers.abstract: This paper introduces an extension of generalised filtering for online applications. Generalised filtering refers to data assimilation schemes that jointly infer latent states, learn unknown model parameters, and estimate uncertainty in an integrated framework -- e.g., estimate state and observation noise -- at the same time (i.e., triple estimation). This framework appears across disciplines under different names, including variational Kalman-Bucy filtering in engineering, generalised predictive coding in neuroscience, and Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (DEM) in time-series analysis. Here, we specialise DEM for ``online'' data assimilation, through a separation of temporal scales. We describe the variational principles and procedures that allow one to assimilate data in a way that allows for a slow updating of parameters and precisions, which contextualise fast Bayesian belief updating about the dynamic hidden states. Using numerical studies, we demonstrate the validity of online DEM (ODEM) using a non-linear -- and potentially chaotic -- generative model, to show that the ODEM scheme can track the latent states of the generative process, even when its functional form differs fundamentally from the dynamics of the generative model. Framed from a neuro-mimetic predictive coding perspective, ODEM offers a biologically inspired solution to online inference, learning, and uncertainty estimation in dynamic environments.

papers.title: The Design and Composition of Structural Causal Decision Processes
papers.summary: The Design and Composition of Structural Causal Decision Processes tackles how to model decision-making agents inside economics of computing systems, where subsystems interact, resources are bounded, and discounting may itself be endogenous. The paper defines Structural Causal Decision Models (SCDMs) as an extension of Structural Causal Influence Models that adds explicit decision constraints, permits open root variables with no distribution or structural equation, and separates policy optimization from omniscient action choice. Its differentiator is composability: SCDMs can be glued along bridge variables, and under an orthomodularity/d-separation condition the resulting sequential decomposition supports backwards induction and tighter solution complexity. Building on this, the authors define Structural Causal Decision Processes (SCDPs) as recurring SCDMs with a discount variable and end-state mapping, turning causal graphs into dynamic stochastic optimization problems (P-blocks). SCDPs are claimed to be strictly more expressive than POMDPs because they do not assume rational belief formation; they can encode memory formation, resource-rational agents, habit formation, portfolio choice, and variable discount factors. The framework is positioned for policy simulation in the digital economy, mechanism design for information systems, and digital twins of cyberinfrastructure.
papers.abstract: We present two new classes of causal models of decision-making agents. Our approach is motivated by the needs of modeling the economics of computing systems. These systems are composed of subsystems and can exhibit endogenous limits on cognitive resources and value discounting. Structural Causal Decision Models (SCDMs) expand on Structural Causal Influence Models. Like SCIMs, they explicitly represent the causal relationships between model variables and the payoffs of agent decisions. Additionally, agent decisions can be constrained by their causal antecedents, and SCDMs can have open root variables for which no probability distribution or structural equation is given. We show that SCDMs have a well-defined and computationally useful property of composability. Building on SCDMs, we then define a Structural Causal Decision Process (SCDP) as a recurring SCDM with a discount variable. SCDPs benefit from the useful composition properties of SCDMs. Moreover, SCDPs are strictly more expressive than POMDPs because they do not assume rational belief formation. Indeed, an SCDP can endogenously model the memory-formation process, and is thus useful for modeling resource rational agents in dynamic settings. SCDPs are also capable of modeling variable discounting, a tool used widely in social scientific modeling. We pose that SCDPs are a useful framework for policy simulation for the digital economy, mechanism design for information systems, and digital twin modeling of cyberinfrastructure.

papers.title: Hybrid Inspection and Task-Based Access Control in Zero-Trust Agentic AI
papers.summary: Hybrid Inspection and Task-Based Access Control in Zero-Trust Agentic AI tackles the gap between OAuth-style delegated authorization and the true intent of LLM agents that invoke tools over multi-turn conversations. The paper argues that structural checks alone miss attacks such as system-prompt injection, tool swapping, parameter tampering, and result falsification, while prior TBAC handled only single-turn requests. Its CASA runtime enforces a zero-trust interception layer with five deterministic checks on tool-definition integrity, request authorization, action alignment, parameter integrity, and data fidelity, plus a semantic layer split into conversation task extraction and task-tool matching. To evaluate multi-turn intent recovery, the authors extend ASTRA with curated task-tool labels and generate new conversation-tool benchmarks through two pipelines: a deterministic augmented generator that creates focused/drifted user and assistant variants, and a three-agent multi-agent simulator with cooperative or malicious assistants and synthetic tool responses. Experiments with GPT-5.2/5.4 and Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.6 show strong single-turn F1 and lower but still practical multi-turn performance, with false positives rising on harder Multi-Agent settings. The work points toward hybrid, intent-aware access control for distributed agent collaboration.
papers.abstract: Authorizing Large Language Model (LLM)-driven agents to dynamically invoke tools and access protected resources introduces significant security risks, and the risks grow dramatically as agents engage in multi-turn conversations and scale toward distributed collaboration. A compromised or malicious agentic application can tamper with tool calls, falsify results, or request permissions beyond the scope of the subject's intended tasks, which could go unnoticed with current delegated authorization flows given their lack of visibility into the original subject's intent. In light of this, we make the following contributions towards Continuous Agent Semantic Authorization (CASA). First, we propose a hybrid runtime enforcement model that combines deterministic and semantic controls enabled by a zero-trust interception layer. Five deterministic controls enforce structural and data-integrity guarantees over the message flow, while a semantic inspection layer evaluates whether tool call choices align with the intended tasks commissioned to the agent. Second, differently from prior Task-Based Access Control (TBAC) techniques that operate on single-turn interactions, we decompose the semantic layer into two stages: i) a task-extraction step that distills the subject's objectives from multi-turn conversations at the interception layer, and ii) a task-tool semantic matching step at the authorization server that evaluates whether the requested tools are appropriate for the extracted tasks. Third, we extend the ASTRA dataset that we introduced in a prior work, by generating novel conversation-tool datasets with multi-turn interactions containing relevant and irrelevant tool calls for a given task. Lastly, we provide the first experimental results for TBAC under multi-turn conversations.

papers.title: Spectral Model eXplainer: a chemically-grounded explainability framework for spectral-based machine learning models
papers.summary: Spectral Model eXplainer: a chemically-grounded explainability framework for spectral-based machine learning models tackles the mismatch between feature-attribution XAI and spectroscopy, where SHAP/PFI/VIP assign importance to isolated energies or wavenumbers instead of chemically meaningful spectral zones and ignore continuity/collinearity. SMX is a post-hoc, global, model-agnostic explainer that first compresses each expert-defined zone with PCA, then turns zone-score quantiles into logical predicates, scores each predicate by perturbing only that zone on stochastic subsamples, and aggregates repeated bagwise rankings into a directed weighted graph summarized by Local Reaching Centrality. Its differentiator is threshold spectrum reconstruction: predicate cutoffs are back-projected into the original measurement units so a rule like a Ca kα threshold becomes an overlayable spectral boundary, not just a numeric score. The framework was tested on eight real XRF/GRS binary datasets plus a synthetic ground-truth benchmark, explaining PLS, RBF-SVM, and MLP classifiers. Compared with SHAP, PFI, and VIP, SMX was broadly faithful, often more domain-aligned and more stable than PFI, and substantially simpler than PFI/VIP. The soil-fertility case showed Ca, Mn, Si, and Fe boundaries mapping to actionable chemistry, pointing toward spectral-native, decision-support XAI for analytical science.
papers.abstract: Spectral-based machine learning models have been increasingly deployed in chemometrics and spectroscopy, where predictive accuracy is as important as explainability. Current employed eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods are largely adapted from tabular or generic multivariate domains, assigning relevance to isolated spectral variables rather than to the chemically meaningful spectral zones. Widely adopted tools such as SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), Permutation Feature Importance (PFI), and Variable Importance in Projection scores (VIP) were not designed for the physical continuity and high collinearity of spectral data, and their variable-level outputs require post-hoc aggregation to recover zone-level information. This study introduces the Spectral Model eXplainer (SMX), a post-hoc, global, model-agnostic XAI framework that explains spectral classifiers through expert-informed spectral zones. SMX summarizes each zone via PCA, defines quantile-based logical predicates, estimates predicate relevance with perturbation in stochastic subsamples, and aggregates bag-wise rankings in a directed weighted graph summarized by Local Reaching Centrality. A key component is threshold spectrum reconstruction, which back-projects predicate boundaries to the original spectral domain in natural measurement units, enabling direct visual comparison with measured spectra. The method was evaluated on eight real spectral datasets (six based on X-ray Fluorescence--XRF and two based on Gamma-ray Spectrometry) and one synthetic benchmark with known gr

papers.title: MSMixer: Learned Multi-Scale Temporal Mixing with Complementary Linear Shortcut for Long-Term Time Series Forecasting
papers.summary: MSMixer: Learned Multi-Scale Temporal Mixing with Complementary Linear Shortcut for Long-Term Time Series Forecasting attacks the mismatch between long-horizon forecasting targets and lightweight MLP forecasters that only see one temporal resolution, so they underfit fast oscillations, mid-scale periodicity, or slow trend simultaneously. The core idea is a channel-independent three-branch MLP over fixed average-pooled scales {1×, 4×, 16×}, where each branch specializes at a different bandwidth, then a learned softmax gate adaptively weights the scale outputs instead of hard selecting a resolution. What makes the design distinctive is the explicit DLinear complementary shortcut: a full-window trend/seasonality path restores global context lost by pooling, so the multi-scale mixer does not have to infer long-range structure only from compressed inputs. On ETTh1/2 and ETTm1/2 with standard chronological splits, look-back 336, horizons 96–720, and three seeds, the model reaches 112K parameters and O(T) inference, with lowest average MSE among lightweight models (0.357), beating DLinear and NLinear and matching or surpassing PatchTST on several settings while using about 5× fewer parameters. This points toward compact, interpretable multirate forecasters that combine scale specialization with linear global shortcuts.
papers.abstract: Long-term time series forecasting requires models that simultaneously capture rapid oscillations, medium-range periodicities, and slowly evolving macro-trends from a fixed look-back window. Existing lightweight MLP-based models typically operate on a single temporal resolution, limiting their ability to explicitly model patterns at multiple scales. We propose MSMixer, a channel-independent multi-scale MLP architecture that addresses this limitation through three complementary innovations: (i) three parallel scale branches at down-sample factors {1x, 4x, 16x} with independent MLP blocks, (ii) a learnable softmax gate that dynamically weighs branch outputs, and (iii) a DLinear complementary shortcut that provides full-window trend and seasonality context. MSMixer contains only 112K parameters at H=96 and runs at O(T) complexity. Evaluated on four ETT benchmarks with standard chronological splits and three random seeds, MSMixer achieves the lowest average MSE (0.357) among lightweight models, outperforming DLinear (0.386, -7.4%) and NLinear (0.365, -2.1%), winning 12 of 16 configurations. Against five Transformer-based baselines from the literature, MSMixer achieves best or second-best MSE in 9 of 16 configurations while using 5x fewer parameters than PatchTST. Ablation and sensitivity analyses confirm the complementary contributions of the multi-scale branches and the DLinear shortcut.

papers.title: Caliper-in-the-Loop: Black-Box Optimization for Hyperledger Fabric Performance Tuning
papers.summary: Caliper-in-the-Loop: Black-Box Optimization for Hyperledger Fabric Performance Tuning tackles the operational bottleneck that Hyperledger Fabric throughput depends on hundreds of interacting peer, orderer, channel, batching, and state-database settings, making manual tuning slow, brittle, and hard to reproduce. The paper turns Fabric benchmarking into a noisy black-box objective by wiring an end-to-end closed loop that materializes a candidate configuration, deploys the network, runs Hyperledger Caliper, extracts TPS, and feeds the observation back to Bayesian optimization. Its distinctive move is to study BO in a 317-dimensional configuration space and explicitly pair four acquisition functions (EI, MPI, UCB, DYCORS) with four dimensionality-reduction strategies (PCA, REMBO, sensitivity ranking, SHAP), rather than relying on a single tuner or abstract simulator. Experiments run in a Yandex Cloud testbed on a five-organization Fabric network with FabCar workloads, 300-evaluation budgets, Latin hypercube initialization, and a random-search baseline. The best method, DYCORS-PCA, improves throughput by 12% over the first evaluated configuration; MPI-REMBO reaches 9%. The main implication is that high-dimensional, benchmark-in-the-loop optimization is practical for enterprise blockchain tuning, but conclusions remain sensitive to measurement noise and baseline choice, pointing toward noise-aware, constraint-aware, and transfer-capable configuration optimization.
papers.abstract: Hyperledger Fabric performance depends on many interacting configuration parameters, making manual tuning difficult. We study automated throughput tuning by treating benchmarking as a noisy black-box optimization problem and applying Bayesian optimization (BO) with dimensionality reduction (DR). We implement an end-to-end Caliper-in-the-loop pipeline that deploys candidate configurations, benchmarks them, and updates the optimizer from observed throughput. The search space, derived from Fabric configuration files, has 317 dimensions. In a cloud testbed, we evaluate 16 BO+DR variants and a random-search baseline. The best method, DYCORS-PCA, achieves a 12% TPS improvement relative to the first evaluated configuration, while MPI-REMBO achieves 9%. These results suggest that BO with DR is a practical approach for high-dimensional Hyperledger Fabric tuning, while also highlighting the role of measurement noise in interpreting gains.

papers.title: ParaRNN: An Interpretable and Parallelizable Recurrent Neural Network for Time-Dependent Data
papers.summary: ParaRNN: An Interpretable and Parallelizable Recurrent Neural Network for Time-Dependent Data attacks the standard RNN bottleneck in statistical time-dependent modeling: vanilla recurrent nets are expressive but opaque, sequential to train, and hard to relate to classical time-series structure. The paper’s central idea is to replace one dense recurrent matrix with several small recurrent units run in parallel, then aggregate them additively; through block-diagonal and real-Jordan decompositions, the hidden dynamics split into interpretable “recurrence features” governed by real or complex eigenvalue blocks. These map cleanly to exponential-decay Type R features and damped oscillatory Type C features, making the model more legible than diagonal-only recurrent variants that lose periodic behavior. Unlike many efficiency-focused RNN simplifications, ParaRNN is designed to preserve rich recurrence while parallelizing over hidden-state groups rather than time. The authors prove density of 2×2 block approximations, approximation capacity, and non-asymptotic prediction-error bounds for nonparametric regression on time-dependent data, and argue that ds=2 is often sufficient. Experiments on three sequential tasks, including forecasting and permuted sequential MNIST, show performance comparable to vanilla RNNs with better speed and interpretability, pointing toward statistics-compatible recurrent modeling.
papers.abstract: The proliferation of large-scale and structurally complex data has spurred the integration of machine learning methods into statistical modeling. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs), a foundational class of models for time-dependent data, can be viewed as nonlinear extensions of classical autoregressive moving average models. Despite their flexibility and empirical success in machine learning, RNNs often suffer from limited interpretability and slow training, which hinders their use in statistics. This paper proposes the Parallelized RNN (ParaRNN), a novel model composed of multiple small recurrent units. ParaRNN admits an additive representation that decouples recurrent dynamics into interpretable components, whose behavior can be characterized through recurrence features. This interpretability enables its applications in nonparametric regression for time-dependent data, while the design also allows efficient parallelization. The approximation capacity and non-asymptotic prediction error bounds in a nonparametric regression setting are established for ParaRNN. Empirical results on three sequential modeling tasks further demonstrate that ParaRNN achieves performance comparable to vanilla RNNs while offering improved interpretability and efficiency.

papers.title: Random-Effects Algorithm for Random Objects in Metric Spaces
papers.summary: Random-Effects Algorithm for Random Objects in Metric Spaces tackles the lack of a general mixed-effects baseline when repeated measurements on the same subject are not vectors but metric-space objects such as functions, probability distributions, or graphs. The key move is an anchor-based Fréchet surrogate: for each observed response anchor Aℓ, the method logs squared distances log(d²(Yij,Aℓ)+δ) and fits a scalar mixed-effects model with subject-specific random intercepts, using boosted trees plus GPBoost so the fixed effect can be nonlinear and the anchor regressions can be run in parallel. Unlike Hilbert-space functional mixed models, it never forces an embedding of the object space; instead, it reconstructs the personalized prediction by choosing the observed response whose transformed anchor-distance profile best matches the estimated subject profile. The paper proves pointwise consistency for the metric-space prediction target under an M-estimation working model. Experiments on synthetic data and digital-health cohorts—NHANES activity curves, CGM glucose trajectories, 3D glucose distributions in Wasserstein space, and NHANES Laplacian graphs—show gains from random effects in three of four benchmarks, with the largest reduction on distributional glucose prediction, pointing toward scalable personalized inference for non-Euclidean longitudinal data.
papers.abstract: Across many scientific disciplines, multiple observations are collected from the same experimental units, and in modern datasets these observations often arise as non-Euclidean random objects. In such settings, the incorporation of random effects is a critical modeling step for efficient estimation and personalized prediction. Although mixed-effects models are well established for scalar outcomes and, more recently, for functional data in Hilbert spaces, general random-effects frameworks for objects in metric spaces remain underdeveloped. In this paper, we propose a nonlinear Fréchet-based algorithm for random-effects modeling of arbitrary random objects defined on a metric space. Using M-estimation theory, we establish conditions under which the proposed metric-space prediction target is consistently estimated under a working random-effects formulation. We then evaluate the empirical performance of the proposed method using both synthetic data and digital health datasets that require practical tools for analyzing random objects in metric spaces, such as multivariate probability distributions and random graphs. We show that, although our method is developed beyond Hilbert spaces, it can outperform existing Hilbert space-based methods.

papers.title: mdok-style at SemEval-2026 Task 9: Finetuning LLMs for Multilingual Polarization Detection
papers.summary: mdok-style at SemEval-2026 Task 9: Finetuning LLMs for Multilingual Polarization Detection tackles the POLAR shared task’s harder-than-binary setting: detecting whether text is polarized, then classifying its social type and rhetorical manifestation across 22 languages spanning multilingual, multicultural, and multievent posts. Instead of training small task-specific encoders, the team transfers their mdok robustness recipe from machine-generated text detection to sequence classification with mid-size multilingual LLMs, using QLoRA 4-bit parameter-efficient finetuning and combining all languages into one training pool to bias the model toward polarization rather than language identity. A key differentiator is adversarial-style augmentation: each training text is duplicated as anonymized, lower-cased, upper-cased, and homoglyphified variants, directly targeting social-media obfuscation and tokenization brittleness. They selected Qwen3-32B for subtask 1 and Gemma-3-27B-pt for subtasks 2–3, trained for one epoch with validation-based checkpointing, and released code for replication. The submitted system was strongest on binary detection, more mixed on multi-label type/manifestation labels, and suggests a broader direction toward robust, multilingual, low-rank finetuning plus cognitively grounded signals for polarization modeling.
papers.abstract: SemEval-2026 Task 9 is focused on multilingual polarization detection. Specifically, it covers the identification of multilingual, multicultural and multievent polarization along three axes (in subtasks), namely detection, type, and manifestation. Online polarization presents a concern, because it is often followed by hate speech, offensive discourse, and social fragmentation. Therefore, its detection before it escalates is crucial for a safer and more inclusive online space. We have coped with this SemEval task by finetuning mid-size LLMs for the sequence-classification task using the QLoRA parameter-efficient finetuning technique. The training data augmented the multilingual (22 languages) training sets by anonymized, lower-cased, upper-cased, and homoglyphied counterparts, making the detection more robust.
