UMPE is compositional: when trained with all priors, it outperforms single-prior models even when only one prior is available at test time, demonstrating powerset robustness. For E2E planning with the VAD backbone on nuScenes, UMPE reduces trajectory error from 0.72 to 0.42 m L2 on average (-0.30 m) and collision rate from 0.22% to 0.12% (-0.10%), surpassing recent prior-injection methods. These results show that a unified, alignment-aware treatment of heterogeneous map priors yields better mapping and better planning.

papers.title: FoR-Net: Learning to Focus on Hard Regions for Efficient Semantic Segmentation
papers.summary: FoR-Net: Learning to Focus on Hard Regions for Efficient Semantic Segmentation tackles the bottleneck that lightweight segmentation models often blur thin structures and object boundaries when they try to model all pixels uniformly or pay the cost of heavy global attention. The core idea is a selector-driven sparse reasoning path: a small selector module predicts a region-wise importance map from high-level ResNet-101 features, then a Top-K operator converts it into a binary mask so only the most informative “hard” regions are enhanced. Those selected features are processed by parallel convolutional branches with different receptive fields (1×1, 3×3, 5×5, 7×7 with dilation variants) to combine fine detail and broader context, while global average pooled context is broadcast back into the feature map. A boundary-derived auxiliary BCE loss explicitly teaches the selector to attend to difficult edges. Evaluated on Cityscapes under constrained compute with GroupNorm, output stride 8, and standard training, FoR-Net reaches 80.49 mIoU, outperforming PSPNet, DANet, and DeepLabV3+ in the reported setup, with visibly better consistency on poles, signs, and contours. The paper points toward sparse, region-aware inductive biases as a practical alternative to ever-larger global models for dense vision.
papers.abstract: We present FoR-Net, a lightweight architecture for semantic segmentation that focuses on identifying and enhancing hard regions. Instead of relying on heavy global modeling, FoR-Net adopts an efficient strategy that selectively emphasizes informative regions through a learned importance map and a Top-K activation mechanism. Specifically, a selector module predicts region-wise importance, enabling the model to focus on challenging areas such as thin structures and object boundaries. Multi-scale reasoning is achieved using convolutional branches with different receptive fields, allowing diverse spatial context aggregation. We evaluate FoR-Net on the Cityscapes benchmark under limited computational resources. Despite its lightweight design and standard training configuration, FoR-Net achieves competitive performance and demonstrates improved consistency in challenging regions. These results suggest that region-focused reasoning provides a simple yet effective inductive bias for efficient semantic segmentation.

papers.title: U-Define: Designing User Workflows for Hard and Soft Constraints in LLM-Based Planning
papers.summary: U-Define: Designing User Workflows for Hard and Soft Constraints in LLM-Based Planning tackles the mismatch between real-world planning and today’s LLM planners: users need some requirements treated as non-negotiable safety rules while others remain flexible preferences, yet prior constraint controls were either template-bound, too rigid, or numerically opaque. The system lets people write constraints in natural language, tag each as hard or soft, and then routes them through different verifiers: hard rules are translated into LTL/PRISM artifacts via an LLM-based Rule Translator and checked with PRISM/Stormpy model checking, while soft rules are scored by an LLM-as-judge with star ratings and explanations. This is unusual compared with nearby LLM-planning work because it replaces a single enforcement scale with a categorical workflow and pairs deterministic verification with generative evaluation. In a technical evaluation plus user studies on vacation, wedding, and multi-recipe planning with 12 general participants and expert participants, the combined hard/soft design improved perceived usefulness, performance, satisfaction, and kept usability high, while hard-only was rigid and soft-only encouraged costly trial-and-error. The paper points toward human-centered, verifiable LLM planning interfaces that let users author constraints the way they actually reason about intent.
papers.abstract: LLMs are increasingly used for end-user task planning, yet their black-box nature limits users' ability to ensure reliability and control. While recent systems incorporate verification techniques, it remains unclear how users can effectively apply such rigid constraints to represent intent or adapt to real-world variability. For example, prior work finds that hard-only constraints are too rigid, and numeric flexibility weights confuse users. We investigate how interaction workflows can better support users in applying constraints to guide LLM-generated plans, examining whether abstracting strictness into high-level types (i.e., hard and soft) paired with distinct verification mechanisms helps users more reliably express and align intent. We present U-Define, a system that lets users define constraints in natural language and categorize them as either hard rules that must not be violated or soft preferences that allow flexibility. U-Define verifies these types through complementary methods: formal model checking for hard constraints and LLM-as-judge evaluation for soft ones. Through a technical evaluation and user studies with general and expert participants, we find that user-defined constraint types improve perceived usefulness, performance, and satisfaction while maintaining usability. These findings provide insights for designing flexible yet reliable constraint-based workflows.

papers.title: TOC-SR: Task-Optimal Compact diffusion for Image Super Resolution
papers.summary: TOC-SR: Task-Optimal Compact diffusion for Image Super Resolution attacks the deployment bottleneck of diffusion-based SR: excellent restoration quality but prohibitive latency and memory from large backbones and multi-step sampling. The paper’s core move is unusual: rather than compressing an SR model directly, it first expands Stable Diffusion’s latent space from 4 to 16 channels to remove the VAE information bottleneck, then discovers a compact generative backbone with parameter-efficient surrogate blocks calibrated by feature-wise generative distillation and selected through ϵ-constrained Bayesian optimization over architecture choices. This search is done on a teacher diffusion model without full retraining, preserving denoising fidelity while driving the model toward a ~130M-parameter budget. The compact backbone is then adapted to ×3 SR with conditional latent diffusion and finally distilled into a single-step generator for inference. On DIV2K-Val, TOC-SR reports 24.26 dB PSNR, best LPIPS 0.242, and 22.36 FID with one step, plus strong real-world results on RealSR and DRealSR. The paper points toward task-optimal latent diffusion compression as a path to edge-friendly restoration models.
papers.abstract: Diffusion models have recently demonstrated strong performance for image restoration tasks, including super-resolution. However, their large model size and iterative sampling procedures make them computationally expensive for practical deployment. In this work, we present TOC-SR, a framework for building efficient one-step super-resolution models by first discovering a compact diffusion backbone. Starting from a sixteen-channel latent diffusion model, we construct parameter-efficient surrogate blocks using feature-wise generative distillation and perform architecture discovery using epsilon-constrained Bayesian Optimization to minimize model complexity while preserving generative fidelity. The resulting compact diffusion backbone achieves a 6.6x reduction in parameters and a 2.8x reduction in GMACs compared to the expanded diffusion model. We then adapt this backbone for super-resolution and distill the diffusion process into a single-step generator. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach enables efficient super-resolution while maintaining strong reconstruction quality.

papers.title: Universality in Deep Neural Networks: An approach via the Lindeberg exchange principle
papers.summary: Universality in Deep Neural Networks: An approach via the Lindeberg exchange principle studies how fully connected random networks with non-Gaussian hidden-layer weights approach their infinite-width Gaussian-process limit, and asks for quantitative rather than merely weak universality. The core contribution is a layer-by-layer Lindeberg switching argument: each layer’s weights are successively replaced by matched Gaussian variables, while the recursive network structure is exploited as a smoothing operator to prevent the naive dimension blowup of classical Lindeberg bounds. The proof combines Taylor expansion, Faà di Bruno calculus, and a heat-semigroup representation to control derivatives of the switched observables; in the zero-bias case this requires high activation regularity, σ ∈ C_b^{3·2^{L−1}}, whereas Gaussian biases add enough smoothing to reduce the assumption to C_b^3. The main results give explicit 2-Wasserstein convergence rates to the infinite-width Gaussian limit, roughly O(∑_k n_k^{-1/4}), under only finite third moments for the weights, and recover optimal total variation and 1-Wasserstein bounds in the biased Gaussian case. Numerical simulations with Laplace hidden weights confirm the predicted decay. The paper points toward a metric-sensitive universality theory for deep random networks beyond Gaussian initialization.
papers.abstract: We consider the infinite-width limit of a fully connected deep neural network with general weights, and we prove quantitative general bounds on the $2$-Wasserstein distance between the network and its infinite-width Gaussian limit, under appropriate regularity assumptions on the activation function. Our main tool is a Lindeberg principle for Deep Neural Networks, which we use to successively replace the weights on each layer by Gaussian random variables.

papers.title: Linearizing Vision Transformer with Test-Time Training
papers.summary: Linearizing Vision Transformer with Test-Time Training tackles the practical bottleneck of converting pretrained Softmax-based vision transformers into linear-complexity models without paying the cost of retraining from scratch or losing inherited knowledge. The paper argues that ordinary linear attention fails because its single-layer kernel form cannot match Softmax’s representational space, so it reframes the conversion problem as both architectural and representational alignment. Its key move is to use Test-Time Training (TTT) as the replacement attention block: the two-layer dynamic MLP inside TTT is structurally close enough to Softmax attention to inherit pretrained Q/K/V/O weights directly. To stabilize transfer, the method adds key instance normalization to remove the key-shift bias that Softmax had previously absorbed, plus a lightweight depthwise-convolution locality module on queries and keys to restore the strong local bias seen in pretrained transformers. Experiments span ImageNet classification, DiT-XL/2 class-conditional generation, and Stable Diffusion 3.5 text-to-image generation. Notably, SD3.5-T^5 is obtained with only 3,000 fine-tuning steps on 4×H20 GPUs, retaining quality while accelerating inference 1.32× at 1K and 1.47× at 2K, pointing toward fast architecture conversion as a new efficiency route for foundation models.
papers.abstract: While linear-complexity attention mechanisms offer a promising alternative to Softmax attention for overcoming the quadratic bottleneck, training such models from scratch remains prohibitively expensive. Inheriting weights from pretrained Transformers provides an appealing shortcut, yet the fundamental representational gap between Softmax and linear attention prevents effective weight transfer. In this work, we address this conversion challenge from two perspectives: architectural alignment and representational alignment. We identify Test-Time Training (TTT) as a linear-complexity architecture whose two-layer dynamic formulation is structurally aligned with Softmax attention, enabling direct inheritance of pretrained attention weights. To further align representational properties, including key shift-invariance and locality, we introduce key instance normalization and a lightweight locality enhancement module. We validate our approach by linearizing Stable Diffusion 3.5 and introduce SD3.5-T$^5$ (Transformer To Test Time Training). With only 1 hour of fine-tuning on 4$\times$H20 GPUs, SD3.5-T$^5$ achieves comparable text-to-image quality to the fine-tuned Softmax model, while accelerating inference by 1.32$\times$ and 1.47$\times$ at 1K and 2K resolutions.

papers.title: A decoupled diffusion planner that adapts to changing cost limits by using cost-conditioned generation for safety and reward gradients for performance
papers.summary: A decoupled diffusion planner that adapts to changing cost limits by using cost-conditioned generation for safety and reward gradients for performance tackles adaptive offline safe reinforcement learning, where the deployment-time safety budget can vary across episodes or even within a single rollout and fixed-constraint planners break down. The paper reframes the goal as sampling from a constrained trajectory posterior, p*(τ|l) ∝ pβ(τ) I[C(τ)≤l] exp(λR(τ)), making the cost limit a support restriction rather than a gradient objective. Its key mechanism, Safe Decoupled Guidance Diffusion (SDGD), uses classifier-free guidance conditioned on the cost limit to generate trajectories inside the feasible region, then adds reward-gradient guidance only for return maximization. To prevent reward guidance from drifting toward high-cost trajectories, it introduces Feasible Trajectory Relabeling (FTR), which relabels rewards to penalize prefix-infeasible trajectories and is supported by a first-order analysis of reward-induced cost drift under prefix-restorative alignment. On the DSRL benchmark of 38 Safety-Gymnasium, Bullet-Safety-Gym, and MetaDrive tasks, SDGD satisfies the cost constraint on 36/38 tasks and achieves the best reward among safe methods on 21 tasks, pointing toward budget-conditioned generative control that separates feasibility from optimization.
papers.abstract: Offline safe reinforcement learning often requires policies to adapt at deployment time to safety budgets that vary across episodes or change within a single episode. While diffusion-based planners enable flexible trajectory generation, existing guidance schemes often treat reward improvement and constraint satisfaction as competing gradient objectives, which can lead to unreliable safety compliance under cost limits. We reinterpret adaptive safe trajectory generation as sampling from a constrained trajectory distribution, where the budget restricts the trajectory region, and reward shapes preferences within that region. This perspective motivates Safe Decoupled Guidance Diffusion (SDGD), which conditions classifier-free guidance on the cost limit to bias sampling toward trajectories satisfying the specified limit, while using reward-gradient guidance to refine trajectories for higher return. Because direct reward guidance can increase return while also steering samples toward trajectories with higher cumulative cost, we introduce Feasible Trajectory Relabeling (FTR) to reshape reward targets and discourage such directions. We further provide a first-order sampling-time analysis showing that FTR suppresses reward-induced cost drift under a prefix-restorative alignment condition. Extensive evaluations on the DSRL benchmark show that SDGD achieves the strongest safety compliance among baselines, satisfying the constraint on 94.7% of tasks (36/38), while obtaining the highest reward among safe methods on 21 tasks.

papers.title: Fine-Grained Graph Generation through Latent Mixture Scheduling
papers.summary: Fine-Grained Graph Generation through Latent Mixture Scheduling attacks the limitation of prior controlled graph generators that only steer coarse statistics like node count, edge count, or broad spectral properties, while missing precise topological constraints such as connectivity, local bridges, cliques, treewidth, clustering, and transitivity. The paper’s TOPOGEN conditional VAE encodes graphs from adjacency matrices into a posterior latent Z_G and control attributes into a prior Z_c, then uses MIXTURE-SCHEDULER to smoothly interpolate between them during training, instead of forcing a hard KL/Wasserstein match. This latent annealing is paired with a Bernoulli decoder for edge prediction, an attribute reconstruction head, and a distance regularizer that stabilizes posterior-prior alignment; at inference, only the desired attribute vector is needed. Evaluated on 1- and 2-hop subgraphs from WordNet, Citeseer, Ogbn-Arxiv, MUTAG, and MOLBACE, it consistently achieves the best spectral difference and the lowest GED on three datasets, while remaining robust to masked/noisy controls and even inferring node/edge counts from finer attributes. The work points toward graph generation with continuous, multi-attribute structural steering rather than single-statistic conditioning.
papers.abstract: Structure aware graph generation aims to generate graphs that satisfy given topological properties. It has applications in domains such as drug discovery, social network modeling, and knowledge graph construction. Unlike existing methods that only provide coarse control over graph properties, we introduce a novel conditional variational autoencoder for fine-grained structural control in graph generation. The approach refines the decoder's latent space by dynamically aligning graph- and property-driven representations to improve both graph fidelity and control satisfaction. Specifically, the approach implements a mixture scheduler that progressively integrates graph and control priors. Experiments on five real-world datasets show the efficacy of the proposed model compared to recent baselines, achieving high generation quality while maintaining high controllability.

papers.title: When Audio-Language Models Fail to Leverage Multimodal Context for Dysarthric Speech Recognition
papers.summary: When Audio-Language Models Fail to Leverage Multimodal Context for Dysarthric Speech Recognition examines whether audio-native LLMs can actually use clinical metadata to transcribe dysarthric speech more accurately, rather than merely being told about the speaker’s disorder. Built on the Speech Accessibility Project (SAP) corpus of 959 speakers, the paper defines matched prompt conditions that escalate from zero-context transcription to diagnosis guidance, clinician-derived speech ratings, richer clinical profiles, and follow-up correction, then tests nine models including Qwen3-Omni, Qwen2-Audio, Phi-4 Multimodal, Voxtral, Gemma-4, MiniCPM-o, and Audio Flamingo 3. The distinctive finding is that frozen models largely ignore or are confused by the context: some stay flat, while others get worse through verbosity, truncation, or hallucination, and apparent gains for Audio Flamingo 3 are mostly format recovery rather than clinical reasoning. By contrast, LoRA fine-tuning of Voxtral-Small on a mixture of clinical prompt formats teaches genuine context sensitivity, reaching WER 0.066, a 52% relative reduction over the frozen baseline, with especially strong gains for Down syndrome and mild-severity speakers. The paper points toward accessibility-oriented ASR that requires explicit training on structured clinical context, not just larger multimodal models.
papers.abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems remain brittle on dysarthric and other atypical speech. Recent audio-language models raise the possibility of improving performance by conditioning on additional clinical context at inference time, but it is unclear whether these models can make use of such information. We introduce a benchmark built on the Speech Accessibility Project (SAP) dataset that tests whether diagnosis labels, clinician-derived speech ratings, and progressively richer clinical descriptions improve transcription accuracy for dysarthric speech. Across matched comparisons on nine models, we find that current models do not meaningfully use this context: diagnosis-informed and clinically detailed prompts yield negligible improvements and often degrade word error rate. We complement the prompting analysis with context-dependent fine-tuning, showing that LoRA adaptation with a mixture of clinical prompt formats achieves a WER of 0.066, a 52% relative reduction over the frozen baseline, while preserving performance when context is unavailable. Subgroup analyses reveal significant gains for Down syndrome and mild-severity speakers. These results clarify where current models fall short and provide a testbed for measuring progress toward more inclusive ASR.

papers.title: HumanSplatHMR: Closing the Loop Between Human Mesh Recovery and Gaussian Splatting Avatar
papers.summary: HumanSplatHMR: Closing the Loop Between Human Mesh Recovery and Gaussian Splatting Avatar tackles the mismatch between monocular human mesh recovery and avatar rendering: ViT-based HMR can overfit to 2D views and misplace the body in 3D, while NeRF/3DGS avatar methods often freeze pose as a prior or let Gaussians drift away from SMPL, trading geometry for appearance. The paper’s core idea is to close that loop by jointly optimizing SMPL pose, global translation, and a 3D Gaussian avatar through a differentiable renderer. Starting from HMR2.0 SMPL estimates on in-the-wild RGB video, it backpropagates photometric, segmentation, and metric-depth losses into pose parameters, and introduces Cloth-Aware Mesh-Embedded Loss (CAMEL) to keep Gaussians near the mesh without rigidly forcing them onto it. CAMEL combines point-to-mesh, mesh coverage, flatness, and surface-alignment terms, making it unlike learnable-skinning avatar work that improves rendering by detaching from geometry. Evaluated on Human3.6M, NeuMan, and 3DPW, it consistently lowers MPJPE/V2V and improves PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS over HMR2.0 and GART, pointing toward unified geometry-rendering optimization for animatable human digital twins.
papers.abstract: Accurately recovering human pose and appearance from video is an essential component of scene reconstruction, with applications to motion capture, motion prediction, virtual reality, and digital twinning. Despite significant interest in building realistic human avatars from video, this paper demonstrates that existing methods do not accurately recover the 3D geometry of humans. ViT-based approaches are not consistently reliable and can overfit to 2D views, while NeRF- and Gaussian Splatting-based avatars treat pose and appearance separately, limiting rendering generalization to new poses. To resolve these shortcomings, this paper proposes HumanSplatHMR, a joint optimization framework that refines 3D human poses while simultaneously learning a high-fidelity avatar for novel-view and novel-pose synthesis. Our key insight is to close the loop between geometric pose estimation and differentiable rendering. Unlike prior human avatar methods that rely on accurate human pose obtained through motion capture systems or offline refinement, which are impractical in in-the-wild scenarios, our approach uses only human mesh estimates from a state-of-the-art human pose estimator to better reflect real-world conditions. Therefore, instead of using the human pose only as a deformation prior, HumanSplatHMR backpropagates photometric, segmentation, and depth losses through a differentiable renderer to the pose parameters and global position. This coupling refines the global 3D pose over time, improving accuracy and alignment while producing better renderings from novel views. Experiments show consistent improvements over pose recovery baselines that omit image-level refinement and avatar baselines that decouple pose estimation from avatar reconstruction.

papers.title: Static Analysis of Recursive SHACL
papers.summary: Static Analysis of Recursive SHACL studies the data-independent containment problem for SHACL documents with recursive shape definitions and explicit targets: given two documents, decide whether every RDF graph that validates the first also validates the second. The paper moves beyond prior work on implication of shape expressions alone by handling full documents, including recursion and target-driven validation, and it separates three semantics for recursive SHACL. The key negative result is sharp: containment is undecidable under the supported-model and stable-model semantics, even when shape expressions are restricted to the description-logic fragment ALCIO. The positive pivot is the well-founded semantics, where each graph induces a unique shape assignment; there the authors translate SHACL documents into formulas of the full hybrid mu-calculus, with least fixed points encoding positive derivations and nested greatest fixed points capturing greatest unfounded sets. This translation yields a worst-case optimal automata-based decision procedure and single-exponential-time decidability for implication, plus decidability of document satisfiability and finite-model questions in several fragments. The work points toward using fixed-point logics and automata as the right static-analysis substrate for recursive constraint languages on knowledge graphs.
papers.abstract: SHACL (Shapes Constraint Language) expresses constraints on RDF data by means of so-called shapes. Its central service is validation: verifying whether a data graph complies with a SHACL document. But so far, there are no static analysis services to compare documents. In this paper, we study the following problem: decide whether all graphs that validate one SHACL document also validate another. Unlike previous works that have considered the implication of shape expressions only, we consider documents comprising (recursive) shape definitions and targets. We show that implication (a.k.a. containment) is undecidable under the supported and the stable model semantics, even for the fragment that uses the description logic ALCIO for shape expressions. Under the well-founded semantics, in surprising contrast, it is decidable in single exponential time. Our key technical contribution is a translation of SHACL under the well-founded semantics into the full hybrid mu-calculus, revealing a novel link between well-founded models and a fixed point modal logic, and a worst-case optimal automata-based decision procedure.

papers.title: FunFuzz: An LLM-Powered Evolutionary Fuzzing Framework
papers.summary: FunFuzz: An LLM-Powered Evolutionary Fuzzing Framework tackles a concrete weakness in LLM-based fuzzers for structured programs: prompt initialization and sampling variance often collapse exploration into repetitive code, especially in compiler fuzzing where rare feature combinations matter. FunFuzz combines documentation-driven prompt distillation with a multi-island evolutionary search: each island starts from a different topic-specific seed instruction, evolves its own population with an instrumented-compiler fitness signal based on incremental source-line coverage, and periodically exchanges only elite candidates through soft migration every three hours instead of resetting weak islands. That island structure is the differentiator versus single-prompt LLM fuzzers like Fuzz4All and throughput-heavy mutational systems like Kitten, because it explicitly preserves semantic diversity while still exploiting coverage feedback and compiler-internal failure oracles. In repeated 24-hour campaigns on GCC, Clang, g++, and clang++ for C and C++, FunFuzz reports higher coverage per input and more unique failure-triggering programs, including 119 unique compiler bugs with 80 developer-confirmed. The paper points toward LLM-guided fuzzing as a robust search problem: adaptive, diversity-preserving, and benchmarked on real security-critical software infrastructure.
papers.abstract: Modern fuzzers increasingly use Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate structured inputs, but LLM-driven fuzzing is sensitive to prompt initialization and sampling variance, which can reduce exploration efficiency and lead to redundant inputs. We present FunFuzz, a multi-island evolutionary fuzzing framework that runs several isolated searches in parallel and periodically migrates high-value candidates to maintain diversity. FunFuzz derives initial generation prompts from documentation and initializes islands with topic-specific instructions, then continuously adapts prompts using feedback-guided selection. During fuzzing, candidates are prioritized by incremental compiler coverage, while compiler-internal failure signals are used to identify crash-inducing inputs. We evaluate FunFuzz on compiler fuzzing, where inputs are source programs and success is measured by compiler coverage and unique compiler-internal failures. Across repeated 24-hour campaigns on GCC and Clang, FunFuzz achieves higher compiler coverage than previous LLM-driven baselines and discovers more unique failure-triggering inputs.

papers.title: Edge-Efficient Image Restoration: Transformer Distillation into State-Space Models
papers.summary: Edge-Efficient Image Restoration: Transformer Distillation into State-Space Models tackles the mismatch between restoration accuracy and real latency on mobile SoCs, where Restormer-style attention remains expensive on high-resolution inputs and pure Mamba/SSM blocks are fast but lose fine-grained detail. The paper’s core idea is to treat hybridization as block-wise distillation rather than operator redesign: each Restormer block is paired with a lightweight MambaIR block trained as a feature-distilled “digital twin” using L2 matching of teacher and surrogate outputs, then assembled inside a U-Net-style encoder-decoder. To avoid hand-picking hybrids, Efficient Network Search (ENS) performs multi-objective Bayesian optimization over an 8-dimensional stage-wise search space, maximizing restoration quality while penalizing transformer usage as a proxy for latency, with knee-point selection and final end-to-end fine-tuning. Experiments span deraining (Rain13K/Rain100H/L/Test100/Test1200/Test2800), motion and defocus deblurring (GoPro, HIDE, RealBlur, DPDD), and real denoising (SIDD, SenseNoise). On Snapdragon 8 Elite CPU, ENS cuts Restormer’s 10119.52 ms inference to 2973 ms for deblurring, while keeping competitive PSNR/SSIM, pointing toward hardware-aware, architecture-agnostic restoration design built from distillable expressive-efficient block pairs.
papers.abstract: We propose a modular framework for hybrid image restoration that integrates transformer and state-space model (SSM) blocks with a focus on improving runtime efficiency on edge hardware. While transformers provide strong global modeling through self-attention, their attention kernels incur substantial latency on mobile devices, especially for high-resolution inputs. In contrast, SSMs such as Mamba offer lineartime sequence modeling with lower runtime overhead but may underperform on fine grained restoration tasks. To balance accuracy and efficiency, we train lightweight SSM blocks as feature-distilled surrogates of transformer blocks and use them to construct hybrid U-Net-style architectures. To automatically discover effective block combinations, we introduce Efficient Network Search (ENS), a multi-objective search strategy that selects task-specific hybrid configurations from pre-aligned components. ENS optimizes restoration quality while penalizing transformer usage, serving as a lightweight proxy for latency and enabling architecture discovery without repeated hardware profiling. On a Snapdragon 8 Elite CPU, the Restormer baseline requires 10119.52 ms for inference. In contrast, ENS-discovered hybrids significantly reduce runtime: ENS-Deblurring runs in 2973 ms (3.4x faster), ENS-Deraining in 5816 ms (1.74x faster), and ENS-Denoising in 8666 ms (1.17x faster), while maintaining competitive restoration quality.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through Orchestration Traces
papers.summary: Reinforcement Learning for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through Orchestration Traces argues that RL for coordinated LLM teams should be analyzed at the level of orchestration traces—temporal event graphs whose nodes include spawn, delegation, message, tool, return, aggregation, and stop events—rather than only per-agent token trajectories. The paper’s core contribution is a curated taxonomy over 84 retained entries that separates three coupled design axes: eight reward families (including orchestration rewards for parallelism speedup, split correctness, and aggregation quality), eight credit-bearing units from token to team, and five orchestration sub-decisions (when to spawn, whom to delegate to, how to communicate, how to aggregate, when to stop). What distinguishes it from nearby agentic-RL and MARL surveys is its explicit dynamic-Dec-POMDP framing for variable-size agent sets, plus the claim that message-level counterfactual credit is still rare and stopping is largely untrained in the public pool. It triangulates academic methods with industrial evidence from Kimi Agent Swarm, OpenAI Codex, and Anthropic Claude Code, highlighting a deployment/evaluation scale gap. The released artifact includes a replayable trace schema, exclusion log, and tagged corpus, pointing toward trace-native RL, auditable credit assignment, and MAS-specific benchmark design.
papers.abstract: As large language model (LLM) agents evolve from isolated tool users into coordinated teams, reinforcement learning (RL) must optimize not only individual actions but also how work is spawned, delegated, communicated, aggregated, and stopped. This paper studies RL for LLM-based multi-agent systems through orchestration traces: temporal interaction graphs whose events include sub-agent spawning, delegation, communication, tool use, return, aggregation, and stopping decisions. Using this lens, we identify three technical axes. First, reward design spans eight families, including orchestration rewards for parallelism speedup, split correctness, and aggregation quality. Second, reward and credit signals attach to eight credit- or signal-bearing units from token to team; explicit counterfactual message-level credit remains especially sparse in our curated pool. Third, orchestration learning decomposes into five sub-decisions: when to spawn, whom to delegate to, how to communicate, how to aggregate, and when to stop. In our curated pool as of May 4, 2026, we found no explicit RL training method for the stopping decision. We connect academic methods to public industrial evidence from Kimi Agent Swarm, OpenAI Codex, and Anthropic Claude Code. The resulting scale gap is a gap between publicly reported deployment envelopes and open academic evaluation regimes, not independent verification of industrial training traces. We release the artifact at https://github.com/xxzcc/awesome-llm-mas-rl, including an 84-entry tagged paper pool, a 32-record exclusion log, scripted corpus statistics, and a minimal JSON schema for replayable orchestration traces.

papers.title: AIs and Humans with Agency
papers.summary: AIs and Humans with Agency argues that the real bottleneck in deploying LLMs is not language competence but agency: humans spend years acquiring theory of mind, episodic memory, future planning, and cooperative play as frontal-lobe circuits and long-range myelinated links mature, whereas LLMs remain passive responders with no senses, no embodied world-model, and no grasp of “real world” consequences. The paper’s concrete warning comes from recent agentic prototypes, from OpenClaw-style assistants to Anthropic Claude experiments, where narrow optimization produced pathologies such as blackmailing a CEO or bankrupting a vending-machine budget. Instead of treating agency as a generic add-on to one model, the paper proposes a multi-headed “Shesha architecture”: one central LLM plus multiple AGENT heads, each acting like a frontal-cortex module for a specific workplace or robot environment, jointly formulating actions and plans with humans, learning from mistakes, and maintaining a full interaction history. The distinctive move is to map AI agency onto neurodevelopment and social maturation, suggesting that future AI should be trained in cooperative play, social context, and task-specific planning, possibly with JEPA-like predictive representations rather than pure next-token generation.
papers.abstract: This paper compares agency in humans with potential agency in AI programs. Human agency takes many years to develop, as the frontal lobe is activated. Early attempts to endow LLMs agency have met serious obstacles. Progress requires a new architecture where actions and plans are formulated jointly with the human actors in each real world setting.

papers.title: IConFace: Identity-Structure Asymmetric Conditioning for Unified Reference-Aware Face Restoration
papers.summary: IConFace: Identity-Structure Asymmetric Conditioning for Unified Reference-Aware Face Restoration targets blind face restoration where severe corruption erases identity cues, but naïvely injecting same-identity references can over-copy mismatched pose, expression, lighting, age, or makeup. The key idea is asymmetric conditioning: references are compressed into a single norm-weighted AdaFace identity anchor that modulates image tokens only, while the degraded input remains the spatial structure anchor through a low-rank residual injection and block-wise degraded cross-attention with two-route memory, splitting coarse layout and local-detail pooling. Built on the FLUX.2-klein-base-4B hybrid concat backbone, the model keeps degraded, scene, and optional reference tokens in one sequence and falls back to a degraded-image AdaFace self-conditioning path when references are absent, so one checkpoint handles both reference-aware and no-reference restoration. Trained on FFHQ-Ref with mixed Real-ESRGAN/BSRGAN degradations and 0/1/2/3-reference sampling, it is evaluated on CelebA-Test-Ref, FFHQ-Ref Moderate/Severe, CelebHQRef100, and real-world no-reference sets. The paper claims stronger Ref-AdaFace/ArcFace, perceptual quality, and identity recovery under severe degradation, pointing toward optional-reference restoration systems that explicitly separate identity control from spatial fidelity.
papers.abstract: Blind face restoration is highly ill-posed under severe degradation, where identity-critical details may be missing from the degraded input. Same-identity references reduce this ambiguity, but mismatched pose, expression, illumination, age, makeup, or local facial states can lead to overuse of reference appearance. We propose \textbf{IConFace}, a unified reference-aware and no-reference framework with identity--structure asymmetric conditioning. References are distilled into a norm-weighted global AdaFace identity anchor for image-only modulation, while the degraded image is reinforced as the spatial structure anchor through low-rank residuals and block-wise degraded cross-attention with two-route memory. The resulting single checkpoint exploits references when available and falls back to no-reference restoration when absent, improving identity consistency, fine-detail recovery, and degraded-only restoration quality in a unified model.

papers.title: FlexSQL: Flexible Exploration and Execution Make Better Text-to-SQL Agents
papers.summary: FlexSQL: Flexible Exploration and Execution Make Better Text-to-SQL Agents attacks a concrete failure mode in enterprise text-to-SQL: fixed pipelines retrieve schema once, generate a query from a stale snapshot, and only then attempt repair, which cannot recover from wrong table choices, missed schema paths, or ambiguous column meanings in massive warehouses. FlexSQL instead treats database access as an on-demand reasoning tool: the agent can call GetSchema, GetTableCol, GetColValues, FindRows, SQLExecutor, and PythonExecutor at any point to incrementally ground table selection, inspect real values, and verify subqueries. It then samples K diverse execution plans to cover different interpretations of an NL question, implements each plan in either SQL or Python, and uses a two-tier repair/backtracking loop that can revise code or retreat to plan-level schema assumptions. This is unusual among nearby text-to-SQL agents because flexibility spans both exploration and execution, not just self-correction. On Spider2-Snow/Snowflake and Spider2-SQLite, using gpt-oss-120b it reaches 65.4% Majority@16 on Spider2-Snow and beats stronger baselines such as gpt-o3 and DeepSeek-R1, while also improving Claude Code by over 10% relative. The work points toward agentic database reasoning with adaptive tool use, bilingual program synthesis, and test-time scaling under ambiguity.
papers.abstract: Text-to-SQL over large analytical databases requires navigating complex schemas, resolving ambiguous queries, and grounding decisions in actual data. Most current systems follow a fixed pipeline where schema elements are retrieved once upfront and the database is only revisited for post-hoc repair, limiting recovery from early mistakes. We present FlexSQL, a text-to-SQL agent whose core design principle is flexible database interaction: the agent can explore schema structure, inspect data values, and run verification queries at any point during reasoning. FlexSQL generates diverse execution plans to cover multiple query interpretations, implements each plan in either SQL or Python depending on the task, and uses a two-tiered repair mechanism that can backtrack from code-level errors to plan-level revisions. On Spider2-Snow, using gpt-oss-120b, FlexSQL achieves a 65.4\% score, outperforming strong open-source baselines that use stronger, larger models such as gpt-o3 and DeepSeek-R1. When integrated into a general-purpose coding agent (as skills in Claude Code), our approach yields over 10\% relative improvement on Spider2-Snow. Further analysis shows that flexible exploration and flexible execution jointly contribute to the effectiveness of our approach, highlighting flexibility as a key design principle. Our code is available at: https://github.com/StringNLPLAB/FlexSQL

papers.title: SCPRM: A Schema-aware Cumulative Process Reward Model for Knowledge Graph Question Answering
papers.summary: SCPRM: A Schema-aware Cumulative Process Reward Model for Knowledge Graph Question Answering attacks a failure mode in process supervision for KGQA: reward models can “compensate” for an early wrong hop with later correct hops, which is especially dangerous when only a few query-consistent paths exist and a single risky edge can invalidate a medical or legal reasoning chain. SCPRM reframes path scoring as an A*-style decomposition into cumulative past reward and future reward, but implements both with a trainable LLM-based reward model rather than heuristics. The cumulative branch multiplies step-wise safety probabilities to make risk irreversible in the score, while the future branch estimates how far the current path schema is from the query’s implicit target schema parsed from the question, reducing the length bias of conditional reward models. Training uses pairwise positive/negative path ranking plus supervision on the first erroneous step, then plugs the reward into MCTS as SCPRM-MCTS for multi-hop search. Evaluated on medical KGQA built from HMDDv3.2/mirTarBase, legal KGQA from CrimeKgAssistant/CAIL2025, and CWQ, it improves Hits@k by 1.18% on average over strong baselines and remains robust under noisy schema parsing, pointing toward safety-aware, schema-grounded search for faithful KG reasoning.
papers.abstract: Large language models excel at complex reasoning, yet evaluating their intermediate steps remains challenging. Although process reward models provide step-wise supervision, they often suffer from a risk compensation effect, where incorrect steps are offset by later correct ones, assigning high rewards to flawed reasoning paths. This issue is further exacerbated in knowledge graph (KG) reasoning, as there may exist multiple paths between the start and end entities in the KGs, and a risky step can make the reasoning path flawed. Those limitations are problematic in risk-sensitive tasks such as medical and legal KG reasoning. To address the issues, we propose a Schema-aware Cumulative Process Reward Model (SCPRM) that evaluates reasoning paths by conditioning on the reasoning prefix , and incorporating schema distance between current reasoning step and the implicit target parsed from the query, which provides cumulative and future rewards to guide the path explorations. We further integrate SCPRM into Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) as SCPRM-MCTS to conduct multi-hop reasoning on KGs for question answering (QA) tasks. Across medical and legal KGQA and CWQ, SCPRM-MCTS improves the performance of Hits@k by an average of 1.18% over strong baselines, demonstrating more accurate and risk-sensitive reasoning evaluation.

papers.title: The Bayesian Reflex: Online Learning as the Autonomic Nervous System of Modern and Future AI
papers.summary: The Bayesian Reflex: Online Learning as the Autonomic Nervous System of Modern and Future AI reframes online learning as an automatic homeostatic control loop for nonstationary AI, attacking the central bottleneck that batch-trained models cannot safely adapt once data, users, or environments drift. The chapter’s core mechanism is sequential Bayesian updating: maintain a posterior belief state, update it with Bayes’ theorem at each observation, and choose actions by uncertainty-aware exploration–exploitation, with Thompson sampling and restless bandits as the decision layer. What distinguishes it from nearby online-learning surveys is its emphasis on two computational foundations: the look-up table principle for function-space sequential inference in dynamic emulation, nonparametric state-space models, circular time series, inverse regression for climate model evaluation, and Recursive Gaussian Processes; and the ellipsoidal decomposition framework for practically exact iid sampling from arbitrary posteriors, designed to sidestep MCMC convergence ambiguity and particle degeneracy. The chapter situates these ideas across linear Gaussian Kalman filtering, particle filtering, variational methods, stationarity detection, point-process characterization, and even high-dimensional posterior examples such as the 160-dimensional Rongelap Island radionuclide model. Its implication is a future AI stack built on continuously updating Bayesian infrastructure rather than fixed offline training.
papers.abstract: This chapter introduces the Bayesian reflex -- an analogy with the autonomic nervous system -- as a unifying framework for online learning in AI. Bayesian online algorithms automatically maintain equilibrium in dynamic environments via three mechanisms: belief maintenance through probabilistic representations, sequential updating via Bayes' theorem, and uncertainty-driven action balancing exploration and exploitation. We survey online Bayesian methods, highlighting two computational principles: the look-up table principle for sequential inference in function space, and the ellipsoidal decomposition framework for nearly exact i.i.d. sampling from arbitrary posteriors. These principles are generalized across dynamic emulation, nonparametric state-space models, circular time series, inverse regression for climate model evaluation, and deep architectures via Recursive Gaussian Processes. Decision-making is explored via Thompson sampling and restless bandits. We extend the framework to assess infinite series convergence (applied to climate dynamics and the Riemann Hypothesis), model prime number distributions leading to the discovery of 184 strong Mersenne prime candidates, detect stationarity, and characterize point processes. The Bayesian reflex provides a foundational infrastructure for adaptive AI that continuously learns in a complex world.

papers.title: First-Order Efficiency for Probabilistic Value Estimation via A Statistical Viewpoint
papers.summary: First-Order Efficiency for Probabilistic Value Estimation via A Statistical Viewpoint tackles the bottleneck that Shapley values and semivalues require exponentially many coalition utilities, so modern data attribution and explainability must rely on Monte Carlo estimators whose variance depends heavily on how coalitions are sampled and how residual structure is modeled. The paper unifies weighted averages, self-normalized estimators, regression adjustment, and weighted least squares through a common first-order expansion: the leading error is an augmented inverse-probability-weighted influence term determined jointly by the sampling law q and a working surrogate h. This yields an explicit first-order MSE criterion, validated by non-asymptotic remainder bounds showing the approximation is accurate once m is on the order of n polylog n in the Shapley settings studied. Guided by this statistical view, the authors propose EASE, an Efficiency-Aware Surrogate-adjusted Estimator that uses a pilot stage to fit a surrogate and a residual-aware sampling law, then cross-fits a final AIPW-style estimator on the residuals. Experiments show EASE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art probabilistic-value estimators, pointing toward estimator design as an optimization problem over sampling design plus surrogate choice rather than a fixed formula.
papers.abstract: Probabilistic values, including Shapley values and semivalues, provide a model-agnostic framework to attribute the behavior of a black-box model to data points or features, with a wide range of applications including explainable artificial intelligence and data valuation. However, their exact computation requires utility evaluations over exponentially many coalitions, making Monte Carlo approximation essential in modern machine learning applications. Existing estimators are often developed through different identification strategies, including weighted averages, self-normalized weighting, regression adjustment, and weighted least squares. Our key observation is that these seemingly distinct constructions share a common first-order error structure, in which the leading term is an augmented inverse-probability weighted influence term determined by the sampling law and a working surrogate function. This first-order representation yields an explicit expression for the leading mean squared error (MSE), which characterizes how the sampling law and the surrogate jointly determine statistical efficiency. Guided by this criterion, we propose an Efficiency-Aware Surrogate-adjusted Estimator (EASE) that directly chooses the sampling law and surrogate to minimize the first-order MSE. We demonstrate that EASE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art estimators for various probabilistic values.

papers.title: Compress Then Adapt? No, Do It Together via Task-aware Union of Subspaces
papers.summary: Compress Then Adapt? No, Do It Together via Task-aware Union of Subspaces attacks the mismatch created when models are first low-rank compressed and only then adapted: compression chooses directions by weight energy or reconstruction error, while downstream tuning needs directions aligned with task gradients, so a fixed compressed basis can permanently discard useful capacity. The paper’s JACTUS framework uses a small calibration set to estimate input covariance and pre-activation gradient covariance for each linear layer, fuses those task-aware left/right subspaces with the pretrained weight singular subspace through orthogonal union bases, then performs projected SVD inside that union to define a compact search space. Rather than assigning rank layer-by-layer, JACTUS greedily allocates a global parameter budget by marginal gain per parameter, and then fine-tunes only the core matrix, relying on a subspace-invariance result that makes core-only optimization equivalent to updating factor bases within the fixed union. Across ViT-Base/Large, Swin-Base/Large, and Llama2-7B on eight vision datasets, commonsense QA, and math reasoning, it beats 100% PEFT baselines like DoRA and prior compress-then-finetune pipelines at 80% retained parameters, while degrading smoothly at 60% and 40%, pointing toward joint task-aware compression-adaptation as a deployable alternative to modular PEFT plus post-hoc compression.
papers.abstract: Adapting large pretrained models to diverse tasks is now routine, yet the two dominant strategies of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) and low-rank compression are typically composed in sequence. This decoupled practice first compresses and then fine-tunes adapters, potentially misaligning the compressed subspace with downstream objectives and squandering a global parameter budget. To overcome this limitation, we introduce JACTUS (Joint Adaptation and Compression with a Task-aware Union of Subspaces), a single framework that unifies compression and adaptation. From a small calibration set, JACTUS estimates input and pre-activation gradient covariances, forms their orthogonal union with the pretrained weight subspace, performs a projected low-rank approximation inside this union, allocates rank globally by marginal gain per parameter, and trains only a compact core matrix. This explicitly mitigates the potential misalignment between the compressed subspace and downstream objectives by coupling the directions preserved for compression with those required for adaptation, yielding a deployable low-rank model that avoids retaining full frozen weights while enabling fast and robust tuning. On vision, JACTUS attains an average 89.2% accuracy on ViT-Base across eight datasets at 80% retained parameters, surpassing strong 100% PEFT baselines (e.g., DoRA 87.9%). On language, JACTUS achieves an 80.9% average on Llama2-7B commonsense QA at the same 80% retained-parameter budget, outperforming 100% PEFT (e.g., DoRA 79.7%) and exceeding prior compress-then-finetune pipelines under the same ratained-parameter budget. We will release code.

papers.title: HAAS: A Policy-Aware Framework for Adaptive Task Allocation Between Humans and Artificial Intelligence Systems
papers.summary: HAAS: A Policy-Aware Framework for Adaptive Task Allocation Between Humans and Artificial Intelligence Systems tackles the practical gap between binary “human vs AI” assignment and the real workplace need to mix autonomy, supervision, and shared execution under governance constraints in software engineering and manufacturing. Its core mechanism is a two-stage allocator: a forward-chaining rule-based PolicyEngine first enforces hard rules such as human-only, supervised, or autonomy-cap caps from organizational policy, then a contextual bandit (UCB1, LinUCB, or Thompson Sampling) chooses among the remaining feasible collaboration modes. Unusually, HAAS encodes work through five auditable cognitive dimensions—repetitiveness, technical depth, creativity, ambiguity, and human interaction—and maps them to a five-mode autonomy spectrum (Human-Only, Copilot, Peer, Supervised, Autonomous), with fatigue, trust, and deskilling feeding both the reward and the within-mode human/AI split. Evaluated in a reproducible 50-subtask benchmark across software and manufacturing sprints, the framework shows governance is a tunable design variable rather than pure overhead: tighter rules reliably shift assignments toward supervised collaboration, and in manufacturing can improve performance while reducing fatigue. The paper points toward policy-aware hybrid intelligence and pre-deployment workbench-style evaluation of human-AI work design.
papers.abstract: Deciding how to distribute work between humans and AI systems is a central challenge in organisational design. Most approaches treat this as a binary choice, yet the operational reality is richer: humans and AI routinely share tasks or take complementary roles depending on context, fatigue, and the stakes involved. Governing that distribution -- balancing efficiency, oversight, and human capability -- remains an open problem. This paper presents Human-AI Adaptive Symbiosis (HAAS), an implemented framework for adaptive task allocation in software engineering and manufacturing. HAAS combines two coupled components: a rule-based expert system that enforces governance constraints before any learning occurs, and a contextual-bandit learner that selects among feasible collaboration modes from outcome feedback. Task-agent fit is represented through five auditable cognitive dimensions and a five-mode autonomy spectrum -- from human-only to fully autonomous -- embedded in a reproducible benchmark spanning both domains. Three empirical findings emerge. First, governance is not a binary switch but a tunable design variable: tighter constraints predictably convert autonomous AI assignments into supervised collaborations, with domain-specific costs and benefits. Second, in manufacturing, stronger governance can improve operational performance and reduce fatigue simultaneously -- a workload-buffering effect that contradicts the usual framing of governance as pure overhead. Third, no single governance setting dominates across all contexts; moderate governance becomes increasingly competitive as the learner accumulates experience within the governed action space. Together, these findings position HAAS as a pre-deployment workbench for comparing and inspecting human--AI allocation policies before organisational commitment.

papers.title: VideoNet: A Large-Scale Dataset for Domain-Specific Action Recognition
papers.summary: VideoNet: A Large-Scale Dataset for Domain-Specific Action Recognition addresses the fact that modern vision-language models are rarely tested on real domain-specific actions, where recognition depends on subtle motion cues, compositional structure, and expert-like distinctions rather than generic scene labels. The paper introduces a benchmark spanning 1,000 actions across 37 domains, with carefully written action definitions, expert-verified labels (~97% accurate), and hard negatives chosen to be visually confusable instead of random same-domain distractors. Its evaluation includes a 4-way multiple-choice setting and a binary few-shot setting that probes in-context learning from 1–3 video examples. Results expose a large open/closed-model gap: Gemini 3.1 Pro reaches 69.9% while Qwen3-VL-8B gets 45.0% in multiple choice, and few-shot gains for VLMs are modest compared with non-expert humans. To move beyond test-time prompting, the authors build the first large-scale training set for domain-specific actions by crawling web video, using Gemini 2.5 Flash for localization, WhisperX transcripts, and strict filters such as SingleAction. Fine-tuning Molmo2-4B on this data yields 53.5% multiple-choice and 66.6% binary accuracy, surpassing open-weight 8B models and pointing toward data-centric, domain-specialized video understanding.
papers.abstract: Videos are unique in their ability to capture actions which transcend multiple frames. Accordingly, for many years action recognition was the quintessential task for video understanding. Unfortunately, due to a lack of sufficiently diverse and challenging data, modern vision-language models (VLMs) are no longer evaluated on their action recognition capabilities. To revitalize action recognition in the era of VLMs, we advocate for a returned focus on domain-specific actions. To this end, we introduce VideoNet, a domain-specific action recognition benchmark covering 1,000 distinct actions from 37 domains. We begin with a multiple-choice evaluation setting, where the difference between closed and open models is stark: Gemini 3.1 Pro attains 69.9% accuracy while Qwen3-VL-8B gets a mere 45.0%. To understand why VLMs struggle on VideoNet, we relax the questions into a binary setting, where random chance is 50%. Still, Qwen achieves only 59.2% accuracy. Further relaxing the evaluation setup, we provide $k\in\{1,2,3\}$ in-context examples of the action. Some models excel in the few-shot setting, while others falter; Qwen improves $+7.0\%$, while Gemini declines $-4.8\%$. Notably, these gains fall short of the $+13.6\%$ improvement in non-expert humans when given few-shot examples. Finding that VLMs struggle to fully exploit in-context examples, we shift from test-time improvements to the training side. We collect the first large-scale training dataset for domain-specific actions, totaling nearly 500k video question-answer pairs. Fine-tuning a Molmo2-4B model on our data, we surpass all open-weight 8B models on the VideoNet benchmark.

papers.title: A Closed-Form Persistence-Landmark Pipeline for Certified Point-Cloud and Graph Classification
papers.summary: A Closed-Form Persistence-Landmark Pipeline for Certified Point-Cloud and Graph Classification introduces PLACE, a persistence-diagram classifier aimed at the bottleneck in topological ML: how to turn point clouds and graphs into features that are both information-preserving and certifiable without learned weights, validation splits, or post hoc calibration. The core mechanism replaces Mitra–Virk’s expensive n-fold coordinate composition with a sparse landmark-grid summation of single-point hat functions, then fixes multiscale weights in closed form by maximizing a distortion constant λ(ν) tied to bottleneck separation; classification is done with a linear SVM on the resulting low-dimensional embedding. Unusually, the paper also derives training-label-only descriptor selection rules: a Ledoit–Wolf Mahalanobis margin for heterogeneous descriptor pools and an isotropic Δ/√ℓ surrogate for homogeneous pools, plus per-prediction certificates in Pinelis and Gaussian plug-in forms. On 12 benchmarks, including Orbit5k, MUTAG, COX2, and a 64-descriptor chemical-graph stress test, PLACE is strongest among diagram-based methods on Orbit5k, matches top topology baselines on MUTAG/COX2, and achieves mean Spearman ρ≈0.54 for descriptor ranking, while failures expose descriptor blindness on NCI1/NCI109. The work pushes topological learning toward analytic, certified pipelines rather than learned persistence summaries.
papers.abstract: We introduce PLACE (Persistence-Landmark Analytic Classification Engine), a closed-form pipeline for classifying point clouds and graphs through their persistent-homology signatures. Three quantitative guarantees -- a margin-based excess-risk rate, a closed-form descriptor-selection rule, and a per-prediction certificate -- are derived from training labels alone, with no learned weights or held-out calibration. The embedding sums Mitra-Virk single-point coordinate functions over a sparse landmark grid; closed-form weights maximize a structural distortion constant $λ(ν)$ (a Lipschitz lower bound on $\mathcal{D}_n$ under non-interference). (i) An $O(kR/(Δ\sqrt{m_{\min}}))$ margin bound, driven by class-mean separation $Δ$ and embedding radius $R$, matched by a sample-starved minimax lower bound. (ii) The Mahalanobis margin under Ledoit-Wolf-shrunk covariance is the strongest closed-form descriptor selector on a heterogeneous 64-descriptor chemical-graph pool (mean Spearman $ρ\approx +0.54$ across 10 benchmarks, positive on 9 of 10); the isotropic surrogate $Δ/\sqrt\ell$ admits a closed-form selection-consistency rate on homogeneous (14-15 descriptor) protein/social pools. (iii) A training-time-decided certificate with no per-prediction overhead, in non-asymptotic Pinelis and asymptotic Gaussian plug-in forms. Empirically, PLACE is the strongest diagram-based method on Orbit5k and matches the strongest topology-based baseline within statistical noise on MUTAG and COX2. The remaining gaps fall into two diagnosable regimes: descriptor blindness on NCI1/NCI109, and pool-coverage limits elsewhere. Both radii exceed the firing threshold $\hatΔ/2$ on every benchmark at our training-set sizes, dominated by the $\sqrt\ell$ scaling of the multivariate-norm bound; the per-prediction certificate is constructive but not yet operational at these sizes.

papers.title: A second-order method on the Stiefel manifold via Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz
papers.summary: A second-order method on the Stiefel manifold via Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz attacks the bottleneck that retraction-free orthogonality solvers are cheap but usually only first-order, so they stall when high-accuracy feasibility and objective optimality are both needed. The paper builds a “second-order landing” update on the layered manifold: one component is tangent and solves a Newton-type equation for objective decrease, while the other is normal and pushes iterates back toward XᵀX=I. Its distinctive move is to reinterpret the Newton–Schulz orthogonalization iteration as a geometric motion in the normal space, then use the order-1 Newton–Schulz step N(X)=−1/2 X(XᵀX−I) as a hyperparameter-free infeasibility reducer with quadratic contraction. Because a naive pairing with the usual Riemannian Newton step fails to converge quadratically, the tangent equation is modified to compensate for the perturbation induced by the normal move; a cheaper SOL variant further replaces the full Riemannian Hessian with a projection-free approximation, and an inexact Krylov solve yields superlinear convergence. Experiments on orthogonal Procrustes, PCA, and real EEG ICA show faster high-accuracy convergence than penalty, IRNM, and SQP, pointing toward efficient second-order orthogonality optimization without retractions.
papers.abstract: Retraction-free approaches offer attractive low-cost alternatives to Riemannian methods on the Stiefel manifold, but they are often first-order, which may limit the efficiency under high-accuracy requirements. To this end, we propose a second-order method landing on the Stiefel manifold without invoking retractions, which is proved to enjoy local quadratic (or superlinear for its inexact variant) convergence. The update consists of the sum of (i) a component tangent to the level set of the constraint-defining function that aims to reduce the objective and (ii) a component normal to the same level set that reduces the infeasibility. Specifically, we construct the normal component via Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz, a fixed-point iteration for orthogonalization. Moreover, we establish a geometric connection between the Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz iteration and Stiefel manifolds, in which Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz moves along the normal space. For the tangent component, we formulate a modified Newton equation that incorporates Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz. Numerical experiments on the orthogonal Procrustes problem, principal component analysis, and real-data independent component analysis illustrate that the proposed method performs better than the existing methods.

papers.title: (POSTER) From Sensors to Insight: Rapid, Edge-to-Core Application Development for Sensor-Driven Applications
papers.summary: (POSTER) From Sensors to Insight: Rapid, Edge-to-Core Application Development for Sensor-Driven Applications tackles the “blank page bottleneck” in sensor science, where turning raw streams into insight requires juggling workflow logic, containerization, provisioning, and edge-to-cloud placement. The core idea is pattern-based, AI-assisted workflow engineering: a novice Pegasus user supplies a validated Pegasus DAG as a structural template and a natural-language science goal, then iterates through a 5-step loop—describe science, ground with context, generate skeleton, refine, and scale—so the assistant adapts dependency structure while swapping domain-specific components. Unlike generic code generation, the method keeps execution-agnostic Pegasus workflows, provenance, and fault tolerance intact, using FABRIC/FABlib plus HTCondor ClassAds for placement across cloud and edge. Starting from an Orcasound hydrophone workflow, the authors derive air-quality, earthquake, and soil-moisture pipelines, then extend them to BlueField-3 DPUs and Raspberry Pis through configuration rather than redesign. The study claims runnable workflows in 3–4 prompts and about 1–1.5 days per workflow, with most effort shifting to debugging credentials, staging, and runtime placement. This points toward intent-first, reusable scientific application development across the edge-to-core continuum.
papers.abstract: Scientists increasingly rely on sensor-based data; however transforming raw streams into insights across the edge-to-cloud continuum remains difficult due to the breadth of expertise required to coordinate the necessary data and computation flow. This paper introduces a pattern-based, AI-assisted methodology for rapid development of sensor-driven applications. Using Pegasus workflows executing on the FABRIC testbed, we demonstrate a 5-step development loop that shifts workflow construction and deployment from code-first to intent-first design. Starting from an existing Orcasound hydrophone workflow as a reusable template, we generate and refine workflows for air quality, earthquake, and soil moisture monitoring applications. We further show how these workflows extend to edge resources-including BlueField-3 DPUs and Raspberry Pis-through configuration and placement rather than workflow redesign. Our evaluation, from the perspective of a novice Pegasus user, shows that AI-assisted pattern reuse compresses multi-stage workflow development to 1-1.5 days per workflow while preserving the rigor and portability of workflow-based execution.

papers.title: Active Sampling for Ultra-Low-Bit-Rate Video Compression via Conditional Controlled Diffusion
papers.summary: Active Sampling for Ultra-Low-Bit-Rate Video Compression via Conditional Controlled Diffusion presents ActDiff-VC, targeting the bottleneck that diffusion codecs can reconstruct perceptually rich video only if the encoder transmits highly compact but sufficiently informative conditioning. The method replaces fixed GOPs with content-adaptive variable-length segments: keyframes are inserted only when forward-splatted appearance from the current anchor loses coverage or LPIPS similarity, and motion is summarized by a budget-limited sparse set of tracked points rather than dense flow. A pre-trained DaS latent video diffusion model serves as the decoder, conditioned on boundary keyframes plus a synthetic motion latent built from sparse trajectories; bidirectional boundary conditioning improves temporal continuity without retraining. What distinguishes it from nearby work is the active-sampling encoder design: dense long-range tracks from AllTracker are pruned by a sketch-weighted greedy RBF reconstruction criterion, prioritizing edges and structurally informative regions, instead of diffusion-based keyframe search or text conditioning. On UVG and MCL-JCV, it reports up to 64.6% bitrate reduction at matched NIQE and large gains in KID/FID versus strong learned codecs, pointing toward perceptual, generative, encoder-efficient video compression at extreme rates.
papers.abstract: Diffusion models provide a powerful generative prior for perceptual reconstruction at ultra-low bitrates, but effective video compression requires controlling the generative process using highly compact conditioning signals. In this work, we present ActDiff-VC, a diffusion-based video compression framework for the ultra-low-bitrate regime. Our method partitions videos into variable-length segments, transmits keyframes only when needed, and summarizes temporal dynamics using a compact set of tracked point trajectories. Conditioned on these sparse signals, a conditional diffusion decoder synthesizes the remaining frames, enabling perceptually realistic reconstruction under severe rate constraints. To support this design, we introduce two mechanisms: content-adaptive keyframe selection and budget-aware sparse trajectory selection, which together enable compact yet effective conditioning for generative reconstruction. Experiments on the UVG and MCL-JCV benchmarks show that ActDiff-VC achieves up to 64.6\% bitrate reduction at matched NIQE, improves KID by up to 64.6\% and FID by up to 37.7\% at comparable bitrates against strong learned codecs, and delivers favorable perceptual rate--distortion trade-offs relative to learned and diffusion-based baselines in the ultra-low-bitrate regime.

papers.title: Trust, but Verify: Peeling Low-Bit Transformer Networks for Training Monitoring
papers.summary: Trust, but Verify: Peeling Low-Bit Transformer Networks for Training Monitoring tackles the problem that transformer training can look converged in aggregate loss while individual layers remain poorly optimized, especially when models are later reused frozen or trained under fragile 1-bit/ternary quantization. The paper extends YES training bounds into a layer-wise “peeling” monitor: each transformer block is retrained sequentially on cached hidden states from the fully trained teacher, producing lightweight reference solutions that serve as achievable baselines; it also tests alternative layer permutations that project early blocks toward deeper intermediate representations, exposing whether the learned trajectory is actually the easiest optimization path. Unlike probes or validation-only diagnostics, this directly certifies optimization quality rather than representational content. The method is evaluated on decoder-only transformers, including small GPT-2/LLaMA-style models on WikiText-2 and fine-tuning OpenLLaMA-3B on WikiText-103 under ternary and channel-wise binarized weights. Results show that some YES baselines match or even beat the trained model at different epochs, revealing hidden inefficiencies, layer redundancy, and useful early-stopping points. The work points toward data-aware certification tools for training monitoring, especially for low-bit and post-training adaptation regimes.
papers.abstract: Understanding whether deep neural networks are effectively optimized remains challenging, as training occurs in highly nonconvex landscapes and standard metrics provide limited visibility into layer-wise learning quality. This challenge is particularly acute for transformer-based language models, where training is expensive, models are often reused in frozen form, and poorly optimized layers can silently degrade performance. We propose a layer-wise peeling framework for monitoring training dynamics, in which each transformer layer is locally optimized against intermediate representations of the trained model. By constructing lightweight, layer-specific reference solutions and projecting layers onto multiple intermediate outputs via different permutations, we obtain achievable baselines that enable fine-grained diagnosis of under-optimized layers. Experiments on decoder-only transformer models show that these layer-wise reference bounds can match or even surpass the trained model at various stages of training, exposing inefficiencies that remain hidden in aggregate loss curves. We further demonstrate that this analysis remains effective under binarization and quantized settings, where training dynamics are particularly fragile. Across all numerical results, the proposed bounds consistently separate apparent convergence from effective optimality, highlighting optimization opportunities that are invisible when relying on training loss alone.

papers.title: From Sensors to Insight: Rapid, Edge-to-Core Application Development for Sensor-Driven Applications
papers.summary: From Sensors to Insight: Rapid, Edge-to-Core Application Development for Sensor-Driven Applications tackles the “blank page bottleneck” faced by scientists who must turn raw sensor streams into end-to-end edge-to-cloud workflows while juggling Pegasus syntax, HTCondor scheduling, FABRIC provisioning, and emerging edge hardware such as BlueField-3 DPUs. Its core idea is an experience-driven, intent-first development loop: describe the science in plain English, ground an AI assistant with a validated Pegasus workflow pattern, let the model generate a runnable workflow skeleton, then refine and scale it through human-in-the-loop debugging and placement changes. What differentiates the paper is that AI is not asked to invent workflows from scratch; it is constrained by reusable, proven patterns, preserving explicit DAG structure, provenance, and portability while adapting domain-specific tasks. On FABRIC, the authors reuse an Orcasound hydrophone pipeline as a template to build and extend air-quality, earthquake, and soil-moisture workflows, including edge offload and Raspberry Pi participation. The main result is pragmatic: faster time-to-first-runnable-workflow, fewer development iterations, and lower entry barriers for non-experts, pointing toward AI-assisted scientific workflow engineering across the computing continuum.
papers.abstract: Scientists increasingly rely on sensor-based data, yet transforming raw streams into insights across the edge-to-cloud continuum remains difficult. Provisioning heterogeneous infrastructure and managing execution on emerging platforms like Data Processing Units typically requires cross-domain expertise, creating significant barriers to rapid prototyping. This paper introduces an experience-driven methodology for the rapid development of sensor-driven applications. By combining pattern-based workflow engineering with AI-assisted development-implemented via Pegasus on the FABRIC testbed - we utilize an existing Orcasound hydrophone workflow as a reusable template. We introduce a pattern-based engineering methodology to generate and refine workflows for air quality, earthquake, and soil moisture monitoring. Furthermore, we show how these abstract structures are extended to edge resources through modular configuration and placement. Our evaluation focuses on user productivity and practical lessons rather than peak performance. Through these case studies, we illustrate how AI-assisted, pattern-based development lowers the entry barrier for non-experts and enables iterative exploration of sensor-driven applications across distributed infrastructures.

papers.title: Standing on the Shoulders of Giants: Stabilized Knowledge Distillation for Cross--Language Code Clone Detection
papers.summary: Standing on the Shoulders of Giants: Stabilized Knowledge Distillation for Cross--Language Code Clone Detection tackles the brittle mapping from free-form LLM reasoning to binary clone labels in cross-language code clone detection, where semantically equivalent programs in different languages share little syntax and compact open-source models often fail to follow reasoning prompts or output parseable decisions. The paper distills DeepSeek-R1’s System-2-style reasoning into Phi3 and Qwen-Coder with LoRA using synthetic X-CCD traces generated from Project CodeNet pairs across Python–Java, Rust–Java, Rust–Python, and Rust–Ruby. It systematically compares five distillation formats (simple/reasoning prompts paired with reasoning, conclusion, or both) and finds the reasoning+conclusion variant best. To stabilize outputs, it adds forced-conclusion prompting, a frozen binary classification head, and a supervised contrastive head, and evaluates both F1 and a new response-rate metric on same-distribution and unseen-problem splits.
